INSTRUCTIONS FOR LIFE ON EARTH

Author: FreemanAV, a resident of planet Earth

  • REVIEW

A review that can't be bought

(Since the author of this book has renounced the world and retreated into the shadows, seeking neither fame nor recognition, I, a fellow inhabitant of planet Earth, have taken it upon myself to write these few lines. The author may never even know about them. But I must leave them.)

I read this manuscript in three nights. The first night, I was angry. Too much truth. Too harsh. The author dared to call a spade a spade—where it's customary to smile and pretend "it's all complicated."

On the second night, I started counting. My life cost. My "touches." My man-hours. It turned out I wasn't an "entrepreneur," but simply a very tired artisan who sold his time at retail, proudly calling it a "business."

On the third night, I calmed down. I understood the main thing FreemanAV wanted to say:Mathematics doesn't lie. And freedom isn't a gift, but a deal with oneself.

There are no "secrets to success" here. There are blueprints.
There's no motivation here. There's NNNN and the understanding that failure is just a statistical unit.

If you're looking for a book that will pat you on the head and tell you you're great, then close this book. You're not ready.
If you feel like you're trapped in someone else's manual, and you lost yours at birth, read. Slowly. With a pencil.

This book is dangerous. It cuts the strings that the puppeteers hold you by.
But it's also a lifesaver. Because it provides a map. And, as we all know, you still have to go it alone.

Wanderer Zero
An inhabitant of planet Earth.

P.S. I'm very grateful to Space for a friend who showed me that very website. There, deep in the abandoned pages, the author's contact information was open for a few days. I managed to save it. But when I visited again a week later, it was all gone. No trace. Just the book.

I write these words not knowing whether FreemanAV will read them. But even if he doesn't, let them remain here. As a testimony.

I now call myself "a man from planet Earth." The material in this book has deeply penetrated me. I've turned my life around 180 degrees. Thank you, friend. This all resonates with me.

*P.S. I'd give the book a 9/10. I took off one because I felt too quiet inside after reading it. But it seems that this is the very Freedom he writes about.* 

INTRODUCTION

Chapter B-1. Climbing Everest

Imagine this: you just got a call from a law firm. Some distant relative, whose existence you never even knew existed, left you a huge inheritance. Not just an inheritance, but the key to your future.

But there's a condition. The key is in a titanium container at the summit of Everest—the highest point on the planet, 8,848 meters above sea level. The condition is simple and strict: you must personally reach the summit. No helicopters, no porters. Just you and the mountain. The deadline is 12 months from today. If you make it, the key is yours. If not, you lose everything.

Question: Will you do it?

Think about it. Climbing Everest is not a hopeless endeavor. Thousands of people have reached the summit. But not one of them did it spontaneously, simply waking up one morning. Everyone who has stood at the top has gone through the same thing.

Preparation of thinking.Fear of heights, self-doubt, the voices in your head that said, “You can’t do it” – all of this had to be turned off before you took the first step.

Studying the route.Everest doesn't forgive mistakes. You need to know every meter of the route, the danger zones, and the campsites.

Team.No one climbs an eight-thousander alone. You need someone who's been there before, someone who can back you up, someone who can carry some of the load.

Equipment.Oxygen tanks, ice axes, crampons, ropes – the right tools multiply your chances.

Understanding the stages.First base camp, then camp 1, 2, 3, 4. And only then – the final push.

Why is this metaphor about you?

Building a successful business is exactly the same as climbing. Only the summit here isn't geographical, but financial and personal. And the problem with most people is that they try to scale Everest in flip-flops and with a sandwich in their pocket.

They see only the summit, but not the path. They want the result, but they're not ready for the steps.

I'm here to be your guide. I know where avalanches lurk, where crevasses are, and where the perfect spot for a breakthrough is. I don't promise you an easy ride. I promise a map, gear, and mental support.

Ready to take the first step?

Then let's move on to the main thing.

FORMULA FOR SUCCESSFUL BUSINESS

Every minute, someone in the world starts a business. Every day, someone becomes richer, freer, and more powerful. For some, it took years of trial and error. Others got there faster because they learned from others' mistakes, not their own.

There's a formula that works no matter what kind of business you're building. I've broken down the climb to the top into five essential components.

Successful Business = Mindset + Systems + Communities + Levers + Scaling

The formula only works when ALL five parts are in place. If even one is missing, you'll either remain stuck or fall off the wagon halfway.

Let's look at each element of the formula.

1. THINKING (Entrepreneurial thinking).This is your inner core. The base camp from which your ascent begins. You see people not as drama, but as statistics. You treat projects as hypotheses. You understand your inner roles and know when to activate each one.

2. SYSTEMS (Business systems and mathematics).This is your map, your route, and your rules for surviving on the slope. You know your numbers, you build closed money chains, you understand the laws of large numbers and critical mass.

3. COMMUNITIES.They are your support group, your fellow travelers, and those who will follow you. You understand that the most valuable asset of the 21st century is the people who trust you.

4. LEVERS.It's your gear that amplifies every move. People, money, technology, content—all of it multiplies your efforts.

5. SCALING.This is the final push to the top and the ability to not stop there.

Ready to climb?

The formula is before you. This isn't a textbook theory, but a distillation of real-life experience (both personal and others')—with ups, downs, and new attempts.

Now the choice is yours. You can stay at the foot, peering at the summit through binoculars and contemplating how awesome it must be to be standing at the top. Or you can start preparing.

Right now. From the first step.

Forward. The summit awaits.

Chapter B-2. Life is not just business
(Important warning before starting)

Before we dive headfirst into formulas, systems, and strategies, I need to tell you one important thing. Something that business book authors often forget.

Business isn't life. It's just a part of it.

Yes, we'll talk a lot about money. About how to count it, how to grow it, how to build systems that generate income. But if you think money is the most important thing, you've already lost before you've even started the game.

What really matters.

Money is a tool. Like a hammer. You can build a house with a hammer, or you can break a window or, if you really try, bash in your neighbor's head. A hammer in itself is neither good nor bad. It's just a tool. What matters is who uses it and for what purpose.

It's the same with money. In itself, it's neutral. But it becomes either a blessing or a curse depending on your intentions.

The Wheel of Life (or Why Business Is Only One Spoke).

If you want to visualize it, imagine a wheel. It has eight spokes:

  • Business/Career
  • Finance
  • Health
  • Family
  • Friends/Environment
  • Recreation/Entertainment
  • Personal growth
  • Spirituality

A wheel only runs smoothly when all the spokes are roughly the same length. If one spoke, say "Business," becomes gigantic and the others are short stubs, the wheel becomes an oval. You won't get very far (in life) on that. It will shake so much that every bone will crumble.

Your task is not to grow one spoke to the sky, but to maintain balance.

But why isn't there a separate "Money" knitting needle on this list?

Money isn't a spoke. Money is the fuel in the tank. Without fuel, a car won't move. But if you fill the tank but forget about the steering wheel, the engine, or the wheels, you still won't get anywhere. Your job isn't to chase fuel, but to build a good car and learn how to drive it.

What is this book for then?

If business and money aren't the most important thing, why are we here at all?

The answer is simple: because without money it is very difficult to maintain balance.

When you lack a financial foundation, you can't afford quality healthcare for your family. You can't find time to relax because you're constantly thinking about survival. You can't help your friends because you don't have enough yourself. You lack the resources for spiritual exploration—you're simply looking for where to get food for tomorrow.

Honestly earned money, combined with good intentions, gives you freedom. Freedom to choose how to spend your time. Freedom to be a better family man, a better friend, a better member of society. Freedom to not think about survival 24/7.

I didn't write this book to turn you into a miser who counts every penny and sees people only as wallets. I wrote it so you can stop worrying about money and finally start living.

Your task on this path

By following the material in this book, you're almost certain to build a successful business. All the tools are right there in front of you, and they're proven. I'm not asking you to believe in yourself right now—I'll believe in you for both of us until you've accumulated enough victories to believe in yourself.

But, as you go along this path, keep one main thing in mind:

You build a business to live well. You don't live to build a business.

Ready?

Then move on. Your Thinking is ahead.

Chapter B-3. Why do I want you to become a wealthy man?

I ask myself this question every time I sit down to write another chapter. And the answer isn't money. At least, not my money.

The answer is in the air we breathe.

Oxygen metaphor.

Tell me: are you willing to share oxygen? Do you mind if someone nearby takes an extra breath?

Of course not.

Why? Because there's more than enough oxygen around. There's enough for everyone. When something is abundant, you don't think about how to hide it. You just breathe and let others breathe.

Now apply this metaphor to money.

A wealthy person is not someone who has a lot of zeros in his account.
A wealthy person is one who has more than enough money.

More than enough to survive. More than enough to be comfortable. More than enough to cover basic needs.

And this is where the magic happens.

When you have more than enough, you begin to share naturally. Not because you "have to." Not because it's the norm. But because abundance itself seeks an outlet. It overflows. Money in the hands of a person with an abundance mindset is a tool for creation. It's an opportunity to help those who are still on their journey.

Why?

Because when millions of people start sharing their wealth, money, knowledge, experience, and opportunities, it will change everything. It will create a win-win economy. It will transform competition into cooperation. It will make the world a bigger, kinder, and fairer place.

How do I know this path?

The path to wealth exists. It consists of ups and downs, periods of poverty and moments of triumph. I know this path to wealth. With all its bumps, traps, and highways. I know where to take shortcuts and where it's best to slow down. I know which tools work and which ones just waste time.

And now I show this path to you.

But there is one important condition.

Your role in this journey

I can give you a map. I can supply you with tools. I can highlight the most dangerous places and show you the shortest routes.

But you will have to go.

No one will walk this path for you. No one will take your steps. No one will accept your decisions.

This is your ascent. Your summit. Your oxygen.

This book isn't just a theory, but a proven system that will help you break out of the vicious cycle of work, debt, and fatigue and enter a space of freedom and abundance.

Let's walk this path together.

Are you ready?

Then move on. Ahead lies your new Thinking.

Chapter B-4 How is this book organized?

This book is not quite ordinary.
In fact, these are two full-fledged books under one cover.

Book 1 (for everyone)This is the version you're reading right now, and the very first one. This is the foundation. All the basic laws, principles, tables, and algorithms. This is what everyone should start with, regardless of age or mindset.

Book 2 (for children and the soul)— it's waiting for you a little further. It contains no tables or algorithms. Only living examples, real stories, parables, and metaphors. So that the growing generation can understand complex terms through simple images.

Why do I need this hybrid?

People learn differently. There's an important pattern: everyone has their own "learning style," their own dominant half of the brain.

Conventionally, I divide all people into two categories.

The first category is "Techies".They are left-brain dominant (logical). They need diagrams, numbers, algorithms, clear instructions. They say, "Give me a structure, and I'll figure it out myself."

The second category is “Those who think in images.”They are right-brain dominant (images). They need stories, examples, emotions, real people. They say, "Show me through someone else's experience—I'll understand how to apply it to myself."

Neither of these types is better or worse. They are simply—different.And to reach everyone, you need to speak two languages.

That's why I created this hybrid.

My goal is not just to inform you. My goal istransformAnd transformation requires a comprehensive approach: both the head and the heart must be involved in the process.

THE STORY OF THE BIRTH OF THIS BOOK

Initially, everything looked different.
I was planning to createthree separate books: for mathematicians, for humanities students, and for children. Each has its own language and presentation. For mathematicians – formulas and tables. For humanities students – the same formulas, but in simple language. For children – stories and parables.

I started with the first one—for mathematicians. I wrote it, filling it with formulas, graphs, and rigorous proofs. And at some point, I caught myself thinking, "Why is it so complicated? Why all this complexity when the same idea can be expressed more simply, more humanely, while still retaining all its depth?"

I started simplifying. I removed the complicated constructions, leaving the essence. Some tables remained—they were needed for structure. The algorithms remained—they were convenient to follow. But that "mathematical arrogance" that makes books clever but dead disappeared.

And suddenly I realized: a book for mathematicians and a book for humanities… became twin sisters. They talked about the same thing. They conveyed the same truths. Just in different words, but in the same style.

Then I made a decision:combine them.

This is how this book was born -Book 1 (for everyone)It contains tables, structure, and clear principles. But at the same time, it's written in simple, human language. It will be understood by a techie, a visual thinker, and anyone who wants to understand how this world works.

And the second book remained.for children and the soulBecause the growing generation needs to be taught complex things through simple things, and laws and concepts through stories and parables.

Two books. One meaning. Different languages.

What awaits you next?

After the introductory part, after the “Introduction”, the book is divided into two halves.

BOOK 1 (FOR EVERYONE)

This is the part you're reading now. It's all built on:

  • Principles.Each chapter is one law, expressed in a simple, understandable form.
  • Clear algorithms.Step 1, step 2, step 3 - do it and get results.
  • In human language.No abstruse language, just the essence.

If you like things laid out neatly, this section is for you. You can read it like a textbook, returning to relevant chapters, and checking the algorithms.

BOOK 2 (FOR CHILDREN AND THE SOUL)

If you've reached this point but feel like the structures and algorithms are giving you a headache, don't rush to close the book. And if you're with children who want to explain complex concepts in simple terms, even more so, linger.

In this book, you'll find the same information, but presented in a completely different way. No tables, no numbers, no algorithms. Just living examples, real stories, parables, and metaphors.

You'll see how Pareto's laws work in an ordinary family. How critical mass accumulates in a group of friends. How the power of community saves in a critical situation.

This book can be read together with parents or grandparents.
This is wisdom conveyed through images.

How to read this?

You have two options.

The first option is sequential.You read the Basic Part for everyone first, then the Children's Part. As a result, you compare how the same idea sounds in different languages. This gives you a three-dimensional, stereoscopic understanding.

The second option is selective.You know why you came. If you want depth and structure, read the first part. If you want warmth and want to explain it to your children, open the second. The result is that you save time, but get the main thing.

Choose the method that is convenient for you.

Why is this important?

Because each of us contains both an adult and a child. An adult needs clear instructions and an understanding of the laws. A child needs a warm story and a vivid image.

This book gives youfull arsenalAnd logic, and emotions. And head, and heart.

Now, move on and choose your path.

MAIN:
One truth, two languages. Choose the one you need now. Or learn to speak both.

BOOK ONE
(FOR EVERYONE)

(Formulas, tables, algorithms)

Author: FreemanAV, a resident of planet Earth

Note #1:Some laws and concepts in this book are presented from different angles: first through the lens of thought, then through the lens of mathematics and systems. This is not a repetition, but a deliberate deepening—so that you can see the truth in its entirety.

Note #2:As you read, you may encounter unfamiliar words, terms, or concepts. Don't stop and don't get discouraged. There's a "Glossary of Terms" at the end of the book—it contains 200 entries—and the word you need is likely already explained there. If you don't find it there, it's your personal challenge: activate self-study mode and find an explanation yourself—on the internet, in other books, or from a trusted expert.

Introduction to Book 1

Have you ever wondered why at school they teach us everything except the most important things?

We're taught to solve equations, memorize war dates, and write essays about Ostrovsky's "The Storm." But no one—do you hear?—nobodydoes not teach us how life really works.

How money works. How to make decisions. How to overcome the fear of mistakes. How to build relationships with people. How to understand who you really are.

We weren't given this instruction manual at birth. And most people live like that—by trial and error, learning their lesson, resenting the world, and not understanding why some succeed and others don't.

I wrote this book to fill this gap.

But a warning right away:this is not the ultimate truthIt's just experience (mine and others): victories, mistakes, conclusions. I'm not asking you to take my word for it. I'm asking youcheck.

Take every idea, every principle and test it on your own skin.
What works, keep it. What doesn't, discard it without regret. This book will only become yours when you experience it.

In Book 1, everything is built on logic, formulas, and tables. No fluff, no fluff. Just the essence.

If you like everything to be in its place, then this part is for you.

Ready?

Then move on. Ahead lies the ascent of Everest.

VOLUME 1. BUSINESS

SECTION 1. THINKING
(Setting up the main tool)

Introduction to the section

Before you begin, remember:don't believe a single word I sayEverything written here is a generalized experience. Take it, test it out for yourself, keep what works, throw out what doesn't.

Why do most people fail?

Because they are trying to change the consequences without changing the cause.
Imagine a tree with fruit. These are your results: money, success. If the fruit is rotten, shaking the branches is useless. The quality of the fruit is determined by the roots—what's hidden underground. Your beliefs, fears, and deep-seated programming.

The external is a reflection of the internal.

We live on four levels:

  • Physical (actions, money)
  • Mental (thoughts, beliefs)
  • Emotional (feelings, fears)
  • Spiritual (values, mission)

There's an error in the "file" (internal settings), and you're trying to fix the "printout" (results). You change jobs, niches, courses—but the program inside is the same. And the results are the same.

Why is this book different?

It starts not with numbers and strategies, but with the mind. First the roots (thinking), then the trunk (systems), then the fruits (money).

The principle that will change everything: 
Your income grows as long as your aspirations grow.
It's not "I want it," but an inner readiness to handle large sums of money and responsibility. The rich person's program cannot be lost, even if you've lost everything.

What awaits you? 

Setting up an internal file. How to turn chaos into statistics, emotions into data. NNNN, NNN, and the price of freedom.

Check it out for yourself.

MAIN:
If you want to change the fruits, change the roots. We're going to the roots. 

Chapter 1.1 Contacts = Statistical Units

The essence of the phenomenon.
All business contacts and interactions with people should be treated as events without emotional overtones, as statistics. Every interaction has only two outcomes: "yes" or "no."

Operating principle.
Imagine you're a salesperson at a store. 100 people came in one day. 5 bought, 95 left. If you cried over every single person who left, you'd be exhausted by lunchtime. But if you understand that a 5% conversion rate is normal, you can continue working calmly, knowing that the more people came in, the more they bought.

Any business works the same way. You look for partners. If you know that out of 10 meetings, only 2 yield results, you don't get mad at the 8 who declined. You just go on to the next 10 meetings.

This isn't cynicism. It's mental hygiene. You stop asking questions like, "Why did he turn me down?", "What did I do wrong?", "Am I just a bad person?"

Instead, you think, "Another statistic said no. Moving on."

What does it consist of?
Any interaction with people in business can be broken down into three simple elements.

  1. Incoming flow- all the people you came into contact with: called, wrote, met.
  2. Conversion— the percentage of those who said “yes” from the total number of contacts.
  3. Result— the result of multiplying the first by the second.
    The more contacts, the greater the result.
    The higher the conversion, the greater the result.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Count.Every day, write down how many contacts you made, how many said "yes" and how many said "no." Don't judge, don't rejoice, don't get upset. Just write it down.
  2. Look.At the end of the week, look at your numbers. Calculate your average conversion rate. For example: 50 contacts, 5 "yes" responses – 10% conversion. This is your personal conversion rate.
  3. Plan.If you need to get 10 yeses in the next week, and your conversion rate is 10%, you know: you need to make 100 contacts.
  4. Do it.Go and make 100 contacts. No hard feelings towards those who said "no." No unnecessary emotions.
  5. Repeat.Look at the numbers every week. If your conversion rate is up, you're getting better. If it's down, something's gone wrong, and you need to figure it out.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Perceiving rejection as a personal tragedy. People think, "I was rejected, so I'm bad," "I wasn't appreciated, so I'm worthless." This causes them to worry, get angry, get upset, and ultimately stop trying.

Mistake 2: Not counting at all. Living on gut feelings: "I think there are a lot of bounces," "I think the conversion rate is low." Until you see the numbers, you have no control over the process.

Example.
Alexey was looking for partners for his startup. The first 10 meetings ended in rejections. He almost gave up, thinking that "nobody needed his idea."

But then he decided to run some statistics. It turned out that out of 10 meetings, two actually yielded results. He just hadn't noticed it in the heat of the moment. He changed his approach: he stopped worrying about rejections and started planning the number of meetings based on his conversion rate.

Within three months he had 10 partners.
He realized that "Entrepreneurial Thinking" is not only a positive outlook, but also the ability to count, analyze, and not be offended by rejections.

Question:
Think back to your last rejection.
Did you analyze it or were you just upset?

Practical task.
This week, whenever you agree with someone about anything, mentally tick the box: “YES” or “NO”.
At the end of the week, look at the statistics. Just look. No judgment.

MAIN:
A refusal isn't a death sentence. It's simply data for analysis.

Chapter 1.2 Projects = Hypotheses

The essence of the phenomenon.
Any project you conceive isn't the ultimate truth. It's simply a hypothesis. An assumption that it will work. Your job isn't to fall in love with the idea, but to quickly and cheaply test whether it works.

Operating principle.
In science, there's a concept called "trial and error." You put forward a hypothesis, conduct an experiment, and observe the results. If the hypothesis isn't confirmed, you discard it and move on. No hard feelings, no drama. It's the same in business. Don't build a factory right away. First, check if people need your product.

What does it consist of?
The hypothesis testing cycle consists of several stages.

  1. Hypothesis selection— what is worth checking and what is not.
  2. Order of precedence- what we launch first, what next.
  3. Creating a minimum version of the product— the simplest and cheapest.
  4. Teston real people and collecting feedback.
  5. Solution- either improve or close.
  6. Scalingwhat worked - if the hypothesis was confirmed.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Formulate the hypothesis in simple words.For example: "People in this area will buy fresh baked goods in the morning."
  2. Come up with the cheapest way to check.Don't rent space or buy equipment. Partner with an existing bakery, buy bread wholesale, and sell it from a stand.
  3. Take the test.One day, two, a week. See if they buy or not.
  4. Collect data.How many people came? How many bought? What did they say?
  5. Draw a conclusion.If they buy, the hypothesis is confirmed. You can think about expanding. If not, shut down the project. You spent pennies instead of millions and a minimum of time.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Falling in love with an idea and ignoring the facts. You've nurtured it for so long that it's become "your baby," and you can't admit that no one needs it.

Mistake 2: Creating a perfect version before testing demand. Spending six months developing an app without knowing if anyone will use it.

Mistake 3: Failing to close failed hypotheses. Continuing to invest time and money in something that has already proven to be a failure.

Example.
Marina dreamed of opening her own bakery. Instead of taking out a loan for rent and equipment (a million rubles at risk), she made a deal with a local café: she bakes pies at home and sells them through the window.

The first three days—zero sales. If she'd rented the space right away, she'd have lost a million. As it is, it's only three days and the cost of the groceries.

Marina wasn't upset, but began testing hypotheses: she changed the recipe, added new fillings, and redesigned the packaging. Within a week, the pies were selling. Only then did she rent a space.

She wasn't building a business—she was testing ideas. Fast, cheap, no loans.

Question:
Do you have an idea that you've been carrying around for years but never tested in practice?

Practical task.
Choose one of your ideas.
Come up with the cheapest and fastest way to check if people need it.
Do this in the next 7 days. Don't hesitate. Just do it.

MAIN:
Projects are hypotheses. They need to be tested quickly and cheaply, not invest millions from day one. 

Chapter 1.3 Business is a game with rules

The essence of the phenomenon.
An entrepreneur sees business as a strategic game. It has elements, rules, and moves. The main rule: you must have a strategic plan from the very beginning.

Operating principle.
As in any game, in business you can't rely solely on luck and improvisation. You need to know how many projects you have, what stages you're going through, in what order, by what deadlines, and how you'll maintain discipline. A plan isn't a prison, but a map. Without a map, you won't know whether you've made it or whether you're lost.

What does it consist of?
The strategic plan is built on five elements.

  1. Projects— How many hypotheses do you run in parallel? Beginners are better off with no more than 1-2.
  2. Stages— How many steps from start to result? Break it down point by point.
  3. Order— What do you do first, what do you do next? Don't jump from one thing to another.
  4. Deadlines— clear dates. Not "someday," but "before March 1st."
  5. DisciplineA plan without execution is a dream. Execution without a plan is chaos.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Take a piece of paper.Write your main goal for the year at the top.
  2. Describe the stages.What needs to be done first, what next?
  3. Set deadlines.Write a specific date next to each stage.
  4. Define discipline.How will you monitor the implementation? Daily check? Weekly?
  5. Hang the plan on the wall.So that it is always before your eyes.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: No plan at all. Living by the principle of "as things go."

Mistake 2: An overly complex and detailed plan becomes paralyzing. When every action is scheduled for every hour, any deviation leads to paralysis.

Mistake 3: Lack of process. Launching advertising before the product is created. Hiring employees before there's an idea.

Example.
Igor decided to start a business. He had five ideas: food delivery, phone repair, marketing consulting, his own blog, and an online candle shop. Every week, he threw himself into a new one: he spent a week setting up delivery ads, then switched to repairs, then to blogging, with no results. A year later, Igor hadn't had a single working project. Only fatigue and debt.

His friend took a different approach: he chose one niche (phone repair) and made a plan: one month to find a location, the second to buy tools, and the third to launch advertising. Within a year, he had a stable income and a point of expansion.

Igor ran along all the roads at once. His friend chose one and reached it.

Question:
Do you have a plan for the coming year?
Or are you just "moving the pieces"?

Practical task.
Take a piece of paper.
Write your main goal for the year at the top.
Write it down point by point: stages, order, deadlines.
Hang it on the wall.

MAIN:
Business is a game with rules. A plan, stages, order, deadlines, discipline.
You won't get there without a map. 

Chapter 1.4 Projects = Temporary Money Chains

The essence of the phenomenon.
View business projects not as "lifelong projects," but as temporary cash flows—streams that generate income over a specific period of time. Each chain has a life cycle.

Operating principle.
Every project becomes obsolete sooner or later. The market changes, technologies advance, and clients find new interests.
Your job is to spot the decline in time and calmly replace the dying project with a new one. Don't get attached.
The money chain has ended - build a new one.

What does it consist of?
The life cycle of any project goes through four stages.

  1. Launch— You invest time and money, but there is no result yet.
  2. Height— the project is gaining momentum, income is growing.
  3. Peak— maximum income, stability.
  4. Decline— income falls, the market changes, interest fades.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. I noticed that the project has stopped growing.Don't pretend it's temporary. Face it.
  2. Recognize that this is not temporary, but natural.The market won't return. The clients won't return.
  3. Find a new money chain.Look where your customers are going. What's trending now?
  4. Launch a new project in parallel or instead of the old oneDon't wait until the old one dies completely.
  5. Close the old project without regrets.Say thank you for what he gave and move on.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Holding on to a dead project out of nostalgia. "I've invested so many years into this!" That's a mistake of the past. The future isn't in the past.

Mistake 2: Investing in reviving something that can't be saved. Sometimes it's cheaper to close it down and start over.

Mistake 3: Not realizing that the market has shifted. Focusing on one thing after customers have already switched to another product.

Example.
Dmitry had a popular YouTube channel about Minecraft. For five years, he generated a steady income from advertising, integrations, and subscriptions. But as the game became outdated, its audience grew, and viewers lost interest.

Many bloggers in this situation began to whine, trying to win back their old audiences, and making nostalgic episodes. Their income dropped, and they burned out.

Dmitry acted differently.
He closed the old channel without regret and launched a new one—about new games. Then another—about adult life, finances, and moving. His income didn't fall, but grew. He didn't get attached to any of the projects.

He built temporary money chains and replaced them in time.

Question:
Do you have a project that is no longer generating the same income as before, but you are still holding on to it?

Practical task.
Look at your current projects.
Rate each one on a scale: growth, peak, decline.
For those that are on the decline, figure out how to replace them with new ones.

MAIN:
All projects are temporary. The money chain has ended—build a new one.
No regrets or nostalgia. Don't get attached.

Chapter 1.5 Build a System - Forget About Goals
(short - full inChapter 2.9)

The essence of the phenomenon.
A system is a conveyor belt. People flow in, money flows out. The goal of mathematics is to make this conveyor belt predictable. So you can sleep soundly, knowing the result will happen.

Operating principle.
Any business can be broken down into simple factors. There are only four of them: how many people came to you, how many of them bought, how much money they spent, and how often they return. By changing any of these factors, you change the end result. The system works like a control panel.

---

Example.
Maxim owned a small online store selling sporting goods. He was chasing revenue: running ads, offering discounts, and participating in sales. He had money, but each month he couldn't understand where it came from or why it was higher one month and lower the next.

He once broke down his business into four parameters. It turned out: website traffic was 1,000 per day, conversion rate was 1 percent (10 purchases), and the average order value was 3,000 rubles. Daily revenue was 30,000 rubles.

Maxim noticed that his conversion rate had dropped from 2 percent to 1 percent within six months. He stopped increasing his advertising budget and focused on conversion. He simplified the checkout process, added reviews to the product page, and set up a live chat. Within a month, his conversion rate had risen to 2.5 percent. Revenue grew to 75,000 rubles per day with the same traffic.

He didn't spend more money on advertising. He simply found a weak link in the system and strengthened it.

Question:
Tell me three numbers about your business right now: how many people come in a day, how many of them buy, and how much money they leave.
If you can't name it, you're in the fog.

Practical task.
This week, break down your income into four components.
Write them down on an A4 sheet of paper and hang them in front of you.

MAIN:
Don't guess—calculate. Improve one parameter—change the result.
Don't fuss—improve. The system prefers order, not fuss.
Give the system time to show the result.

Chapter 1.6 The Master and His Preparatory Work: A Lesson from Andrei Mironov

The essence of the phenomenon.
A high-level professional isn't someone who "creates from scratch" every time in a state of inspiration. They're someone with a vast arsenal of tools, developed, honed, and ingrained in their subconscious. The audience sees only the brilliant result. Behind the scenes, however, are hundreds of hours of tedious, routine work creating these very "blanks."

Operating principle.
In an interview, Andrei Mironov was asked if he had any pre-prepared roles. He responded:
If an actor has 5 blanks, he is an amateur.
If 50 - a good specialist.
If 500 (like Mironov himself) – Master.
The difference between an amateur and a master is not in the presence of “gift”, but in the number of completed blanks.

What does it consist of?
The master system is built on three levels.

  1. Accumulation.A master doesn't wait for inspiration. He constantly expands his arsenal. For an actor, it's hundreds of roles played. For a businessman, it's hundreds of negotiations, thousands of contacts.
  2. Sharpening.A master doesn't simply accumulate; he refines his craft. Each instrument is perfected through analysis of mistakes and repetition.
  3. Freedom.When you have 500+ blanks to work with, the right tool pops out of your subconscious automatically. Behind the ease lies a tremendous amount of hidden work.

Step-by-step plan (How to accumulate your 500+ blanks).

Stage 1. Accumulation.Do a lot. Don't evaluate quality at the start. Build quantity. Your arsenal grows from 5 to 50.
Result: a base appears, fear disappears.

Stage 2. Selection.Analyze what worked and what didn't. Keep the tools that work. The arsenal is filtered from 50 to 150.
Result: efficiency appears.

Stage 3. Sharpening.Repeat the same tools hundreds of times until they become a reflex. Your arsenal turns from 150 to 500+ reflexes. The result: ease, improvisation, and mastery.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Disdain for "pre-made ideas." Thinking that using ready-made ideas is "uncreative." Sticking to the 5-pre-made-ideas level.

Mistake 2: Stopping at 50. You've accumulated a decent arsenal and become complacent. You've stopped learning. Your tools become obsolete.

Mistake 3: Confusing "accumulation" with "chaotic piling." You need to do something, get results, analyze, select, and refine.

Example.
Think of any master in your field: a jeweler, a surgeon, a negotiator. From the outside, it seems like an innate talent, a gift from the gods. But look behind the scenes.

The jeweler ruined hundreds of blanks until he learned to feel the metal.

The surgeon spent thousands of hours training before taking on the complex operation.

The negotiator failed dozens of deals before developing his techniques.

The difference between an amateur and a master isn't the presence of a "gift." It's the number of presets they've developed and the ability to use them at the right moment. An amateur has five—they break down at the first glitch. A master has five hundred. They've become reflexes that operate automatically. A master doesn't wait for inspiration. They've built their arsenal over the years.

And one more thing.
*Remember:Never become arrogant about your skill. Because there are always, without exception, Masters who are at least one level higher than your "skill."*

Question:
How many “blanks” do you have in your professional arsenal?
5, 50 or are you already aiming for 500?

Practical task.
Take one key task in your business.
Write down all the ways to solve it.
Find 3 new ways to add them to your arsenal.
Practice each at least 3 times.
Repeat monthly.

MAIN:
Genius/Mastery is not talent, but thousands of hours of preparation and hundreds of honed “blanks”.

Chapter 1.7 Three Types of Scaling
(short - full inChapter 2.18)

The essence of the phenomenon.
You have a working system. Money is flowing, processes are streamlined.
Question: What's next? Answer: If the system works, your job is to scale it up. There are three different ways to scale, and it's important not to confuse them.

Operating principle.
You can't scale something that doesn't work. Before you step on the gas, make sure the engine is assembled. Growth should be based on data, not emotion. There are three scenarios: accelerate the current model, change the model, or change the business. Each has its own moment.

---

Example.
Let's take the owner of a small flower shop in a residential area.

First, he opened one location, and it began to generate a steady profit. He realized there was no ceiling yet. He stepped up his game, opening a second identical location in the neighboring area, then a third. Everything kept growing.

A year later, he notices that the new locations aren't paying for themselves as quickly, and social media advertising has stopped generating the same growth. He's hitting the ceiling of his current model. He doesn't panic and shut down the business. He "changes the model"—launches a subscription for weekly flower deliveries to offices, adds an auto-funnel to Instagram, and partners with coffee shops. Growth returns.

Two years later, the flower market in his city is oversaturated, with large national chains emerging with discounted prices. The niche is practically exhausted for a single player. He decides to "change his business." He doesn't close down, but rather transforms: from a regular flower shop, he turns into a floristry studio, hosts master classes, and sells training and materials for florists. The business remains, but its essence has changed.

Question:
What stage is your business at?
What scaling scenario is needed right now?

Practical task.
Evaluate your business.
Determine what stage you are at.
Choose one of the three paths and make an action plan for the month.

MAIN:
Don't fix what's broken—scale it. There are three scaling scenarios: step up the pace (increase traffic), change the model (improve conversion/ticket), or change the business (enter a new niche).
Everyone has their own moment. Don't confuse them.

Chapter 1.8 Focus ONLY on the result

The essence of the phenomenon.
Your approach must be strictly results-oriented—using the methods that will deliver it in the shortest possible time. Everything else is noise, the illusion of action, a waste of time.

Operating principle.
Choose the course of action that is already working 300%, or can start working in the near future without a long buildup.
Don't confuse process with results. You can be very busy and still get nothing. Sweat on your forehead doesn't mean you're moving forward.

What does it consist of?
Focus on results is based on three principles.

  1. The result is more important than the process.It doesn't matter how you did it. What matters is whether you did it.
  2. Speed ​​is more important than perfection.Better fast and crooked than perfect, but never.
  3. Do only what brings you closer to the result.Cross out or delegate everything else.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Before every task ask:“Does this bring me closer to the result or just create the appearance of work?”
  2. If it brings you closer, do it.Don't think, don't doubt. Just do it.
  3. If not, cross it out or delegate it.Don't waste your time on this.
  4. If you don't know, stop and think.Don't start until you understand.
  5. At the end of the day, check: what have you done today?Did this bring you closer to the result?

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Confusing activity with productivity. You can run, shout, and fuss, but still not get anywhere.

Mistake 2: Doing perfectly what shouldn't be done. Polishing a website that no one will see. Writing perfect copy for an audience of three.

Mistake 3: Studying for years instead of starting and doing it poorly now. Endless courses, books, and webinars are often just a way to avoid real action.

Example.
Two people wanted to create a website for their small business.

The first one took a two-year programming course. He studied HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and databases. After two years, he knew how to code, but he never launched a website—the business had closed by then.

The second one bought a ready-made template for 2,000 rubles and set it up in three days. The website worked, and orders started coming in.

If the goal is simply to create a website, the second one acted smarter.
If the goal is to become a programmer, the first one has chosen the right path.

The problem is that the first one confused the process with the result. He needed a website, but he was learning programming. Don't confuse one with the other.

Question:
What process are you currently focusing on instead of results?

Practical task.
Look at your current tasks.
For each one, ask: “Does this action bring me closer to the result or just create the appearance of work?”
Anything that falls into the second category, cross out or put aside.

MAIN:
Focus on the result, not the process.
Don't do perfectly what doesn't need to be done at all.
Don't confuse activity with productivity.

Chapter 1.9 The Closed Loop of the Money Chain

The essence of the phenomenon.
Focus on the chain of actions that ultimately yields monetary results. And ensure that this cycle is never interrupted. Money should work, not sit idle.

Operating principle.
Every time you make a profit, immediately use part of it to attract new clients. That way, the process never stops.
The cycle must be closed:
entrance→action→result→reinvest→new entrance.
If the loop is broken, you start from scratch every time.

What does it consist of?
The closed loop operates according to the diagram.

  1. Login- You attract clients.
  2. Action- You sell, you provide a service.
  3. Result- You get money.
  4. Reinvest- You invest part of the money back into attraction.
  5. New entrance— new clients appear.
  6. The cycle repeats itself endlessly.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Count it, what percentage of your income are you willing to reinvest.
    Minimum 30%, better 50%.
  2. Immediately after receiving the moneyTransfer this amount to advertising or development. Don't wait until the end of the month.
  3. Set up an auto funnel,So that it happens without your participation. Automatic payments, automatic campaigns.
  4. Make sure the cycle does not break.If you stop reinvesting, the system will stop.
  5. Monitor efficiency.If reinvestment doesn't bring in new clients, change channels.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: I received the money and immediately spent it all on myself. The car, the apartment, the vacation. There was nothing left for development.

Mistake 2: Reinvesting without tracking the effectiveness of investments. Throwing money into advertising without monitoring whether it's paying off.

Mistake 3: There's no reserve, and the cycle breaks at the first failure. You can't reinvest every penny. The majority should be kept in reserve.

Example.
Dmitry owned an online store. He earned 300,000 rubles net each month. And every month he spent everything on himself: a new car, restaurants, vacations. The next month, he'd start from scratch again—looking for clients, running ads, hustling. There was no growth. He had the money, but no confidence in the future.

One day, he decided to change his approach. He told himself, "I'm not touching 30 percent of the profits. They're going toward advertising and expansion." In the first month, he invested 90,000 rubles in contextual advertising. There were no results—only five new clients arrived. Dmitry almost quit, but remembered the "no-evaluation period" and continued.

In the second month, he changed the channel, launching targeted social media campaigns. He gained 20 new clients. In the third month, he invested 120,000 rubles and gained 50 new clients. The cycle was complete: the money was working, the business was growing, and Dmitry stopped fussing.

Question:
Where does the money you earn go?
Do they continue to work or just get spent?

Practical task.
Calculate what percentage of your income you reinvest in your business. Set a goal to increase this figure to 30-50%.

MAIN:
Closed loop - input→action→result→reinvest→new entrance.
Without reinvestment, business comes to a standstill.

Chapter 1.10 Knowledge Minimalism (Occam's Razor in Action)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Why do "simple-minded" people often achieve greater results than the smart and educated? Because they don't need to know much. They only need to know the amount of information and skills they need.to maintainselected money chain.

Operating principle.
"Do not multiply entities unnecessarily." Keep only what is essential to the system. Everything superfluous is a drag. Everything necessary and sufficient is a lever. Knowledge without action is just a collection.

What does it consist of?
The minimum set of knowledge is divided into three categories.

  1. What you need to know— only that without which the system will die.
  2. What is useful to know— what the system works with more efficiently.
  3. What you might not know— everything else (99% of the information in the world).

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Describe your current system.What do you do, how do you do it?
  2. Find what you need.What will the system die without? This is your bare minimum.
  3. Find what is sufficient.What does it take to achieve perfect work? This is your benchmark.
  4. Cut off the excess.Everything else is without pity. It is unnecessary, not useful, not critical.
  5. Check it out.Is the system working without any extras? That means you did everything right.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Confusing what's necessary with what's sufficient. Thinking that without "what's sufficient," the system will die. No, it will die without "what's necessary." The rest can be added later.

Mistake 2: Getting carried away with learning "just in case." "What if it comes in handy?" It won't. The only thing that comes in handy is what you use.

Mistake 3: Using a lack of knowledge as an excuse for inaction. "I don't know everything yet, so I'm not starting." This is a trap. Start with the essentials, and you'll learn the rest as you go.

Example.
The manager was launching an online store. He hadn't spent 20 years studying marketing, hadn't mastered SEO, and hadn't read programming textbooks. He mastered a website builder in a day, set up simple social media ads, and started selling. Within three months, the store was consistently profitable.

His neighbor, who also wanted to open a store, took a different approach: he enrolled in marketing courses, studied analytics, completed training on targeting, and read 15 books. A year later, he still hadn't started selling—he still wasn't "prepared enough."

Question:
How much extra knowledge are you dragging around with you instead of just doing it?

Practical task.
Write down everything you have studied over the past year.
Divide it into two columns: “Useful in practice” and “Just interesting”.
Look at the proportions. If the second column is larger than the first, you're overtraining.

MAIN:
To start, you only need to know what is necessary, and not everything.
The rest is learned as we go.

Chapter 1.11 The Low-Hanging Fruit Concept
(short - full inChapter 2.34)

The essence of the phenomenon.
In any area of ​​life and business, there are simple, quick, and accessible sources of results. These are the "low-hanging fruits"—things that are ready to fall into your lap and require little effort. First, tackle the easiest and quickest tasks that yield maximum results with minimal effort..

Operating principle
You make a list of all possible actions to achieve the goal, and evaluate each one on two axes:effort (time, money, resources) And results (revenue, efficiency, clients)Then you select the tasks that require minimal effort and yield the greatest results. You do these first. Complex and resource-intensive tasks are done later or after analyzing their necessity.

The concept of low-hanging fruit teaches a simple sequence. First, you take the easiest and quickest actions that are most likely to yield results. You fill your basket with small victories, gaining resources, energy, and confidence. And only then, with a safety margin, decide whether to invest in complex and risky projects.

Many entrepreneurs make the mistake of immediately climbing to the top of the tree for a single, beautiful fruit, ignoring the dozens of ripe ones hanging on the lower branches. As a result, they either lose the fruit or expend so much energy that even the result they achieve brings no joy.

---

Example.
Elena was launching an online course on time management. She had a plan: make a professional video, hire a designer, and launch targeted advertising. The budget was 200,000 rubles. She had already started looking for contractors.

The mentor said, "Stop. Pick the low-hanging fruit first." Elena didn't believe it, but she decided to give it a try. She made a list: write to 10 old clients, ask friends to repost, record a short video on her phone and post it on social media, and give a free Zoom webinar to five friends.

She completed the entire process in three days. Existing clients placed three pre-orders. Friends reposted, gaining 50 new subscribers. The short video was viewed by 200 people. Five people attended the free webinar, two of whom purchased the course.

Elena made five sales and 50,000 rubles in revenue in three days, with virtually no expenses. She put her complex advertising campaign on hold and continued harvesting the low-hanging fruit: creating simple posts, responding to comments, and offering a discount for reviews. A month later, she had 20 sales, and she spent her advertising budget on promoting only the simple posts—5,000 rubles.

Question:
What three low-hanging fruits can you harvest in your project today?

Practical task.
Write down 10 ideas for improving any business indicator (sales, speed, quality, costs).
Next to each idea, give two ratings (1–10):
Efforts(1 - easy, 10 - very difficult)
Result(1 is almost zero, 10 is huge)
Divide a sheet into 4 quadrants:
Low effort + High results= do it tomorrow.
Low effort + Low result = don't do it, it's distracting.
High effort + High results = strategic plan.
High effort + Low result = never do it.
Take 3 tasks from the first quadrant and complete them within 3 working days.
Record the actual result. Compare it with the expected one.

MAIN:
Don't climb to the top for just one fruit. First, gather everything that hangs low. Once your basket is full, you'll have the strength and resources to tackle the difficult stuff.
But remember: when the easy fruits are gone, it's time to climb the ladder. The concept doesn't replace complex strategic work, it merely sets the correct order of actions.

Chapter 1.12 Know your Archetype and Psychotype
(short - full inChapter 2.4)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Before choosing a business, profession, or model, you need to honestly answer one question: "Who am I?" Not in a philosophical sense, but in the most practical, everyday sense. What is my personality like? What comes easy to me, and what drains my soul?

Operating principle.
A mismatch between your work and your nature is the main cause of burnout. It's like trying to make a cat bark or a dog meow. They may try, but they'll be miserable and achieve nothing. If you're an introvert who recharges in silence but choose a profession that requires 24/7 public exposure, you'll burn out within a month.

*Important.Don't try to remake yourself, but find a job that suits you.*

---

Example.
Vera worked as a cold sales manager. Her job involved 80 calls a day, harsh scripts, and rejections. Every evening she came home exhausted, unable to even talk. She thought she was simply tired from work and tried to pull herself together.

A friend advised her to take a personality test. It turned out that Vera is a classic melancholic. Her strengths include analysis, deep detail processing, and systemic thinking. Cold selling, on the other hand, entails stress, quick reactions, and frequent rejections. Her psyche expended enormous resources maintaining this mode.

She asked to be transferred to the analytics department. Within two months, Vera had become a top performer, processing large amounts of data and feeling satisfied rather than fatigued.

Question:
Is your current job or business a good fit for you?
Do you go there in the morning with desire or through force?

Practical task.
Take a piece of paper and honestly answer four questions: Who am I?
How did I act in the past?
How will I act in the future?
What is my strength?

MAIN:
Conformity of the task to your nature = speed and ease.
Mismatch = internal sabotage, burnout and drain.
Know yourself and your right path.

Chapter 1.13 Human Factor
(short - full inChapter 2.36)

The essence of the phenomenon.
We live among people, and even the most beautiful mathematical model can collapse if the human factor isn't taken into account. People aren't robots. They get tired, they make mistakes, they get offended, they get sick, they have bad days, they can get tired, angry, or simply change their mind.

Operating principle.
Don't ignore the human factor. Don't try to overcome it. Account for it consciously, with a margin. Build your system so that it's resilient to a single human failure. Always factor a 20-30% margin for human error into your plans. People will make mistakes—it's not a disaster, it's just data for planning.

---

Example.
Sergey, the owner of a small logistics company, hired a driver, Dmitry. For the first two weeks, Dmitry worked like clockwork: on time, without complaints, and delighted clients. Sergey was already mentally planning to expand his staff.

In the third week, problems started. Dmitry was late for a delivery, then got the address wrong, then scratched the car. Sergey was furious: "How can this happen? I pay him well! Is he kidding me?"

Instead of firing Dmitry, Sergey decided to have a talk. It turned out Dmitry was having problems at home—his son was sick and hadn't slept for three nights. Sergey gave him two days off and reassigned his orders. A week later, Dmitry was back working perfectly.

If Sergei fired him, he would lose a good driver and waste time looking for a new one.

Question:
Think of a situation where someone else let you down.
How much of your resentment was his fault, and how much was your inflated expectations?

Practical task.
This week, when someone makes a mistake, instead of the automatic reaction, “They’re bad,” ask yourself, “What’s their current state?”
Maybe he didn't get enough sleep, he's having problems, or he just didn't understand the problem?"

MAIN:
There are no perfect people. The human factor is not a reason for emotion, but a reason for planning. A pro has a backup plan.

Chapter 1.14 Any system is working
(short - full inChapter 2.16)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There are only three conditions for any business system to work. If they are met, the system will work, even if everything is done poorly. If even one is missing, the system will not work, even if the idea is brilliant and the product is perfect.

Operating principle.
The most common mistake a beginner makes is not believing in the system and constantly changing it. Someone launches one system. If there's no results for a week, they quit. They launch a second one. No money for a month, they quit again. They launch a third, a fourth, a tenth. Ultimately, no system ever reaches the point where it was supposed to work.

---

Example.
A young entrepreneur, Vadim, decided to promote his travel blog. He chose one strategy: posting on Instagram every day. Within a week, he had ten subscribers, including his mother and two friends. "It's not working," Vadim thought, and switched to TikTok. A week later, he had fifteen subscribers. "That's not it either," he said, and started making YouTube videos. In three months, he tried five platforms. None of them produced results. He became exhausted and abandoned the blog.

His friend Denis did it differently. He chose one platform—YouTube. For the first two months, his videos were garnering 100 views. He didn't flinch, continuing to shoot weekly. In the third month, one video accidentally popped up in recommendations—it got 50,000 views. Subscribers started coming in, and the system started working automatically.

Denis simply gave the system time. Vadim, meanwhile, ran from one platform to the next, never giving any of them time to mature.

Question:
Does your system have all three components?
Or do you change it too often without giving it time to warm up?

Practical task.
Describe your current system.
Check three points.
If something is missing, don't change the system, but build in what's missing.

MAIN:
Don't touch the system. Give it time and an influx of people. And it will work over time, reaching a critical mass of people.

Chapter 1.15 Timeframe: 2-3 years maximum
(short - full inChapter 2.17)

The essence of the phenomenon.
We live in an era of instant gratification. Click a button, get a pizza. Swipe, approve, find a partner. Order, get it delivered tomorrow. This kills an entrepreneur's most important skill: patience. Most people quit their businesses after 3-6 months, before they even reach the point where they should be profitable. They simply don't know the statistics.

Any business can be built and brought to its maximum potential in 2-3 years. This isn't theory, but practice, confirmed by thousands of examples. Those who give up after 3-6 months simply don't know the statistics. A great business isn't built in a month.

Operating principle.
The first six months are assembly and errors.
The next year is one of active growth and marketing.
The third year – reaching a plateau and stabilization.
From now on, it's just maintenance and scaling.
For those who survive the first three years, business becomes a habit and a way of life.

But in order to walk this path not by touch, but consciously, a coordinate system is needed.

The best analogy for understanding the process of achieving a big goal is downloading a file from the internet. It provides not just comfort, butmanagement tools.

Imagine,Your business project is a file. It has an objective "size." For example, creating a stable flow of clients through an affiliate network might require 1,000 units of effort. Creating a marketplace requires 100,000 units. File size isn't an abstraction, but the total amount of work, which can be calculated through decomposition: how many contacts, meetings, presentations, and trained partners are needed.

A process has a "loading speed." In business, this is yourrate of action per unit of timeNumber of calls per day, meetings held per week, posts published per month. Unlike the internet, where speed depends on your provider, here you control this parameter yourself. Increase your quota—the speed increases. Hire assistants—the speed multiplies by the number of assistants. Automate the process—the speed no longer depends on your personal time.

There's "progress"—a volume already loaded. This is your accumulated critical mass. The number of clients in your database, partners in your structure, subscribers in your community. Progress doesn't disappear when there are glitches. Even if you pause the project for a month, your client base won't disappear. This is the key difference between a systematic approach and working for hire: you're creating an asset that accumulates.

And there is a “completion” criterion. On the internet, it is 100 percent loading. In business, it ispredetermined numerical indicatorsNot "when the business becomes successful," but "when the monthly net profit reaches 500,000 rubles." Not "when I get a lot of subscribers," but "when the community reaches 10,000 people." Without a clear benchmark, you won't know when you've reached your goal, and you'll be endlessly grinding away, wasting energy on pointless improvements.

This analogy turns the abstract “patience” into something concrete.process managementYou stop being a passive observer waiting for things to "work out." You become an operator who sees.threelever.

The first lever— the scope of the work. Have you assessed the scale correctly? Perhaps you're trying to download a file of 100,000 units, although 1,000 is enough to get you started. Break the big goal into stages. First, reach the intermediate point, gain resources and confidence, and then move on.

Second lever— Speed ​​of action. What's your average quota for the last month? If, at your current rate, it takes 10 years to complete the workload, not three, you have only two systemic solutions: increase your personal quota or leverage your resources—hire people, automate processes, or use existing tools.

The third lever— the completion criterion. Is it defined numerically? If not, you'll never know when the file has been downloaded. You'll endlessly improve the product, change the design, add text, but you'll never reach the point of achieving results.

---

Example.
Two entrepreneurs launched an online English school simultaneously. The first, Alexey, panicked after three months of no sales: "The format isn't working, people don't want to learn!" He shut down the project and launched a new one—marketing courses. Three months later, he shut it down again and launched design courses. Over the course of two years, he tried eight different niches, achieving no results in any of them.

The second, Mikhail, chose one topic—English for programmers. For the first six months, sales were almost nonexistent—2-3 students per month. He kept his cool: he refined the program, recorded webinars, and built a database. In the second year, he started receiving regular clients—20-30 per month. By the third year, the school had reached a stable income—150,000 rubles per month.

Mikhail simply gave the business time to grow, but Alexey quit every time before reaching the point where everything was supposed to work.

Question:
How long are you willing to wait?
Three months or three years?

Practical task.
If you're just starting out, plan a budget for 3 years in advance (either from your reserves or some money from your hired work).

MAIN:
A great business isn't built in a month. Three years is the norm. Six months is just a warm-up. Valuable things mature slowly. Give them time to mature.

Chapter 1.16 Three Questions That Determine Everything

The essence of the phenomenon.
Everything that happens in your life and business—how much you earn, how free you are, how happy you are—comes down to just three questions. Not luck, not start-up capital, not connections. But three simple questions you must honestly ask yourself.

Operating principle.
These three questions only work together.
The outcome is determined by how well your self-esteem, your ambitions, and your willingness to pay the price match each other.
If at least one of the factors is zero, the result is set to zero.

What does it consist of?
Three questions that determine everything.

  1. Who am I? (Internal price).This is your foundation. How you view yourself. Do you consider yourself valuable, worthy of respect, a good salary, and an interesting life? Or is there a voice inside that whispers, "I'm average, I won't earn much"?
  2. Where am I going? (Size of ambition).This is your superstructure. Your true, honest inner desire. Not the one imposed by parents or advertising, but the one that makes your eyes light up. What do you really want?
  3. What am I willing to do? (Willingness to pay the price).These are your legs. How many years am I willing to build? What habits am I willing to give up? What discomfort am I willing to tolerate?

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Take a piece of paper.Divide into three columns.
  2. Column A (Who am I).Describe your self-esteem in words. High or low? Expensive or cheap? Valuable or not?
  3. Column B (Where I'm going).Write down your true ambitions. In terms of money, in terms of lifestyle. Not what you "should" have, but what you really want.
  4. Column B (What you are willing to give).Write down how many years you are ready to build, what habits you are ready to give up, what discomfort you are ready to accept.
  5. Check for congruence (correspondence). 
    If column A has low self-esteem and column B has high ambitions, you are in an illusion.
    If your ambitions are high and your willingness to pay the price is low, you are deluding yourself.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: confusing mere "wants" with true ambitions. "Wants" are "it would be nice." Ambition is "I'll do it, no matter what it takes."

Mistake 2: Not understanding that every dream has a price. Dreaming of a house by the lake, but not being prepared to save, work, and wait.

Mistake 3: Thinking you can get everything without giving anything. Living in the illusion that "someone will come and give it to you."

Example.
Two friends dreamed of a house by the lake. Both earned 50,000 rubles.

The first one said, "I'll buy it someday." But every weekend he spent 10,000 on restaurants, new clothes, and gadgets. Ten years later, he had nothing—only loans.

The second one told himself, "My goal is a house worth 3,000,000. That means I need to set aside and save 20,000 a month for 12 years." He gave up restaurants, sold his old car, and took on part-time jobs in the evenings. His friends laughed: "You're missing out on life." He persevered.

Twelve years later, he moved into the house by the lake. The first one came to congratulate him and asked, "How did you do it?"

"I knew what I wanted and was willing to pay for it. You just wanted it."

Question:
Have you ever honestly answered these three questions for yourself on a piece of paper?

Practical task.
Take a piece of paper.
Divide it into three columns and answer all three questions honestly, without lying.
Check if there is any contradiction between the columns.

MAIN:
Ambition without the willingness to pay the price is just dreams and wishes.
A true goal requires sacrifice.

Chapter 1.17: Looking into the Future and Asking the Right Questions

The essence of the phenomenon.
The quality of your life is determined by the quality of the questions you ask yourself. Most people live within a week-month timeframe and ask, "How can I make it to payday?" Those building Freedom live within a 10-100-year timeframe and ask, "What can I build today that will work in 50 years?"

Operating principle.
When you look 100+ years into the future, your attitude toward the first two or three years of construction changes dramatically. You stop panicking about the lack of quick cash. You begin to see discomfort, time, and effort as an investment, not a loss.

What does it consist of?
The questions you ask yourself fall into four types.

  1. Questions and complaints:"Why can't I do anything?"
    The result is paralysis and negative energy.
  2. Tactical questions:"How do I do this task?"
    The result is an immediate result.
  3. Strategy questions:"What system should I build so that tasks are completed automatically?" The result is a long-term asset.
  4. Mission Questions:"What mark will I leave?" The result is a legacy that will last 100+ years.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Write down all your questions for three days,that you ask yourself.
  2. Classify them into 4 types(complaints, tactics, strategy, mission).
  3. Calculate the percentage of each type.Where do you live? In tactics? In strategy?
  4. On the fourth day, start your morning consciouslyfrom a question from the “mission” category (horizon 100+ years).
  5. Compare in a weekCompare your condition and results with a "normal" week. Notice a difference?

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Living in "fire brigade" mode, keeping your head down. Only tactics, only solutions to current problems. Strategy and mission are put off until later.

Mistake 2: Thinking that questions about the future are empty philosophy. "I need money now, and you need your 100-year horizon."

Mistake 3: Not realizing that the quality of your life directly depends on the quality of your questions. Ask petty questions, and you'll get a petty life.

Example.
Two entrepreneurs were building a business.

The first one asked every morning, "How can I afford a new car?" He chased quick cash, took on dubious orders, and skimped on quality. Ten years later, he had a new car—and the same standard of living. He still asked the same question.

The second asked differently: "What system am I creating for my descendants?" He built processes, trained the team, and thought years ahead.

Ten years later, his business was running without him. His children were left with an asset, not a pile of scrap metal in the garage.

Question:
What questions do you ask yourself in the morning?
About how to survive until the evening, or about what to build for eternity?

Practical task.
Complete the question change diagram.
Start your morning with a mission question.

MAIN:
The quality of life depends on the quality of questions.
Small questions lead to small results. Questions about legacy lead to freedom. Ask questions on a 100+ year scale, and live on a 100+ year scale.

Chapter 1.18 The Main Goal of the First Stage

The essence of the phenomenon.
When you don't have a stable basic income, you're constantly in a survival mode. Your mind is preoccupied with one thing: "How will I survive until payday?", "What if everything collapses tomorrow?", "What will I do if I lose this source?" In this state, it's impossible to think strategically. It's impossible to make long-term plans. It's impossible to be calm and free.

Operating principle.
Look at people who have a guaranteed monthly income stream that doesn't require daily involvement. These could be retirees with a pension, rentiers from rent, people with established businesses, or partners in a deep-seated MLM. What do they have in common? Peace of mind. Confidence in the future. The opportunity to do what they want, not what they have to do.

THE MAIN GOAL OF THE FIRST STAGE

Not a million. Not a yacht. Not an empire.

500-1000 dollars per month - automatically (per person).

An amount that covers your basic needs. That gives you the right to breathe. That switches your brain from "survival" mode to "strategy" mode. Once you've done that, you're no longer lagging behind. You can choose.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Learn to create content.Text, video, images. With modern tools, this is accessible to everyone.
  2. Learn to drive traffic.Content + regularity + platforms = flow of people.
  3. Enter a self-reproducing business.A model where you get credit for the entire revolution of your depth.
  4. Gain a critical mass of people.1000-3000 active people in the structure = your guaranteed flow.
  5. Once you reach the basic flow, choose.If you want, stay and grow. If you want, build your own platform. If you want, invest in assets.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Chasing millions without creating a baseline. Jumping over your head without a safety net.

Mistake 2: Choosing a business model without a self-reproduction mechanism. Creating work for yourself instead of an asset.

Mistake 3: Not having a plan. Throwing yourself into one project after another, never bringing any of them to critical mass.

Example.
Marina worked as an accountant for 12 years. Every morning—the same road, the same office, the same numbers in the reports. She dreamed of freedom, but didn't know where to start.

She set a specific goal: to earn $500-700 in passive income per month—to cover basic expenses and breathe freely.

She chose an affiliate program in the financial sector. It took her three years to build the structure. Rejections, mistakes, doubts. The first year, $100 a month. The second, $300. The third, $700. But she didn't quit.

Now, $700-$900 comes into her account every month without her involvement. She quit her job, is traveling, and is deciding whether to continue growing or build her own platform.

The main thing is that she has a base. And with a base, you can choose.

Question:
How many months does it take you to create this “basic flow”?
Are you ready to work for 2-3 years and then live peacefully for 20-30-50 years?

Practical task.
Write your base flow goal in numbers.
How much do you need per month to feel at peace?
What business model will help you achieve this?
Make a plan for 2-3 years.

MAIN:
A stable monthly income stream isn't about retirement. It's about freedom of choice.
First, create a basic flow that meets your needs. Then choose a path. 

Chapter 1.19 The Formula to BE→ACT→ HAVE

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's an ironclad law for achieving any goal. It works always and everywhere. Most people try to reverse it and wonder why nothing works.

Operating principle.
First, you make a decision and become the person who has already achieved the goal. Your actions automatically follow from this new state. And the result becomes an inevitable consequence. The order remains unchanged.
You can't first get results and then become the one who deserves them. First you become. Then you do. Then you have.

What does it consist of?
The formula consists of three links.

  1. BE— your internal state, identity, conviction. You tell yourself: “I am the one who has already achieved the goal.”
  2. ACT— actions that naturally follow from this state. You act as someone who has already achieved their goal would.
  3. HAVE- a result that becomes an inevitable consequence of the correct state and actions.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Take your main goal.Write it down on paper.
  2. Answer yourself honestly:Who must I be for this goal to become my reality? Not "who I will become later," but "who I must be now."
  3. Make a list of 10 characteristicsThis state. Habits, beliefs, reactions to events. For example: "I am calm," "I am not afraid of rejection," "I act every day."
  4. Over the next 21 days, consciously turn on this state.Every morning, tell yourself: "I am that person." Make decisions the way he would. Act the way he would.
  5. After 21 days, evaluate,How your actions and initial results have changed. You'll see that "having" comes naturally, when you first "were."

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Trying to achieve results so you can become someone else. "First I'll have money, then I'll act like a rich person, then I'll become rich." This is a dead end.

Mistake 2: Acting without the appropriate internal state. Imitating the actions of a rich person while remaining poor inside. This imitation doesn't work.

Mistake 3: Not noticing that the results aren't sticking because you're still the same inside. Money comes and goes, but you stay where you are.

Example.
Oleg dreamed of becoming a leader in the company, but he reasoned, "First, give me a team and a high salary—then I'll become a leader." A year passed, and there was no team. He waited for the external trappings, which never materialized.

The mentor said, "You've got the order mixed up. First, you need to become a leader internally: in your thinking, habits, and how you treat people. Then the team and income will follow."
