  5. Launch it.Put the assembled model to work for 30 days. Don't improve or tweak it along the way. Just do it as you've seen from the masters. After 30 days, evaluate the result and only then begin adding your own elements.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Blindly copying without analysis. Someone sees a competitor's red button on their website and then installs one too, without understanding why it's there or whether it even works. This relates to Step 2. Instead, you need to understand the logic behind it: why it's designed that way, what problem it solves.

Mistake 2: Trying to copy everything from one master at once. A person finds a great player and tries to imitate them exactly. But the master has a different audience, a different budget, and different experience. Blind copying doesn't work. This relates to Step 3. Instead, you need to take one strong trick from different masters and create your own combination.

Mistake 3: Starting to refine the model before it's produced results. Someone launches the assembled model, and after three days, they feel like they "need improvement," so they start changing elements. Ultimately, they can't figure out what works and what doesn't. This relates to Step 5. Instead, let the model run for at least 30 days without modification and only then analyze it.

Mistake 4: Stopping at copying and not adding your own. Someone has created a working model, it produces results, and then they work on it for years without developing it. Gradually, the model becomes outdated, and competitors gain ground. This applies to the stage after Step 5. Instead, once you've achieved stable results, you should start adding your own elements and make the model unique.

Example.
Anna was launching an online English school for adults. She took several promotional courses, but her own efforts were weak. Subscribers were few, and sales were sluggish. She decided not to reinvent the wheel, but to model successful projects.

Anna chose three schools that were growing the fastest in her niche. She studied the first for its content: which topics generated the most engagement, what format they filmed their videos in, and how often they published. She studied the second for its funnel: how they guided subscribers from the first touchpoint to purchase, what free materials they offered, and how they nurtured them. She studied the third for its packaging: how they designed their social media, what their course presentations looked like, and what tone of voice they used.

From the first, she adopted the format of short videos analyzing one specific mistake. From the second, a two-day free intensive training before the launch of sales. From the third, a friendly, non-motorizing communication style.

Anna assembled her model from these three elements and launched it for 30 days. She didn't improve or tweak it—she simply did what she'd seen. Within a month, she had 400 new subscribers and eight sales of the main course. Previously, she'd been getting 50 subscribers and one or two sales per month.

Three months later, when the model was operating reliably, Anna began adding her own elements: she introduced a signature section, adjusted the structure of the intensive to suit her audience, and added personal stories. The model became unique, but the foundation was built on best practices.

Question:
Which successful players in your niche are already doing what you want to do?

Practical task.
Today, choose three successful players in your niche.
Identify each person's one strongest feature. Write them down.
Assemble your model from these three chips and launch it into operation for 30 days without changes.
Evaluate the results in a month.

MAIN:
Don't reinvent the wheel. Find the best, copy their strengths, and build your own model. Launch it. Get the first results. Uniqueness will come later.

Chapter 2.60 Be Consistent in Your Sequence

The essence of the phenomenon.
Success in business isn't a once-a-month heroic achievement. It's a predictable, daily routine. Masters don't look for a "magic pill." They do the same things every day, in the same order. They've turned success into a habit.

Operating principle.
Without consistency, even the right actions don't yield results. Without time, they don't accumulate. Heroes work in bursts: once a month, they achieve a feat—they burn out, then take two weeks off. Consistent people do a little bit every day and reach the top within a year.

What does it consist of?
Comparison of two approaches.

  1. Hero.Once a month, he achieves a feat. Result: burnout, unstable results, fluctuating between 0 and 100.
  2. Consistent.Every day, he does the bare minimum. The result: it grows like a snowball, steadily and predictably.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose one key action, which leads to the result.
  2. Determine the minimum you can do every daywithout burnout.
  3. Do this every dayat the same time for 21 days.
  4. Evaluate the result after 21 days.Has it become easier? Have you gotten used to it?
  5. Add the next action.Repeat the cycle.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Waiting for "inspiration" to start. Inspiration is for amateurs. Habit is for professionals.

Mistake 2: Making big leaps and then taking weeks off. Two calls a day are better than 50 a month.

Mistake 3: Skipping two days in a row breaks the system. One skip is no big deal. Two skips break the system.

Example.
The sales manager changed his scripts every week. Sometimes it was energetic pressure, sometimes gentle persuasion, sometimes aggressive spam. Results fluctuated between 5 and 20 sales per week, but there was no consistency. He grew tired, didn't understand what was working, and was constantly searching for a "magic pill."

A colleague had been working with the same script for three years. Every morning, 50 calls using the same template. He was bored, but he didn't change it. He simply perfected his intonation, pauses, and objections.

A year later, my colleague became the department's top salesperson, consistently making 40-45 sales a week. His conversion rate increased to 20%—he knew what the customer would say at every turn.

The first manager remained average - 10-15 sales, because he was chasing innovation, not results.

Question:
Are you a hero or a follower?
What do you do every day?

Practical task.
Complete the implementation diagram of the sequence.
Choose one action and do it for 21 days in a row.

MAIN:
Inspiration is for amateurs. Habit is for professionals.
Magic is in the little things you do every day.
Don't look for miracles. Perfect one technique, and it will always work.

Chapter 2.61 Self-Reproduction is the Only Path to Freedom

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's only one way to build a business or asset that will operate without your constant involvement and provide true freedom: self-replication. This is the ability of a system (people, platform, or capital) to grow without a proportional increase in your personal efforts.

*Important.If your business model doesn't replicate itself, you've created a job, not an asset. You'll trade your time for money until you stop.*

Operating principle.
Self-replication is a mathematical transition from linear to nonlinear growth. Without it, the following formula applies:
Your income = Your hours x Your rate.The ceiling is 24 hours a day.

Self-reproduction changes the formula: 
Your income = other people's hours x their number x your share.
You don't sell your time - you get a share from the sale of someone else's.

The key mechanism is separating your earnings from your personal time.
As long as these two values ​​are tightly linked, you're not the asset's owner, you're its prisoner. As soon as the link is broken, you're free.

What does it consist of?
Three types of self-reproduction.

  1. Self-reproduction of people (affiliate programs and MLM). 
    You find someone, they find the next one. The chain is continuous. The entry barrier is minimal—you can start from scratch. You can start making money as early as the first month.
    Main limitation: requires leadership qualities and the ability to work with people.
  2. Self-reproduction of the system (network effect). 
    Each new participant increases the value of the system for everyone else. More sellers mean more choice—more buyers mean even more sellers. The system grows on its own.
    The main limitation: it requires a high starting threshold – developing the platform, attracting the first users on both sides.
  3. Self-reproduction of capital (compound interest). 
    You invest money, the profit stays in business and generates more profit. Money works for you 24/7. Over the long term (10-20-30 years), it produces exponential growth.
    The main limitation: it requires start-up capital and time. To earn $500 per month at 20% annual interest, you need approximately $30,000 in capital.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Test your business model.Does it have a self-replicating mechanism? If not, you've created a work, not an asset.
  2. Determine which of the three types is closer to you.People – you need leaders, training, and motivation. Systems – you need technology, launch, and attracting first users. Capital – you need start-up capital and time.
  3. Make a plan for 2-3 years.Develop a self-reproduction mechanism, not just "manual work." Check every month: is the self-reproduction coefficient increasing or decreasing?

Possible errors.

Mistake #1: Building a business that relies solely on you. If you drop out, everything collapses. It's not an asset, it's a job.

Mistake 2: Not building a self-replication mechanism into the model from the start. Rebuilding it later is difficult or impossible.

Mistake 3: Relying on compound interest without starting capital. This is a dead end. First, you need to build capital through the other two methods of self-reproduction.

Mistake 4: Confusing clients with leaders (in affiliate programs) or users with network effects (in platforms). Not every crowd replicates itself.

Example.
Three entrepreneurs started from scratch.

The first one opened a bakery and worked there himself. After a year, he was broke; his income hit a ceiling—he couldn't work more than 12 hours a day.

The second created an affiliate program: he found people who opened their own bakeries using his model, and they found others. Three years later, he had 50 bakeries, and all he did was train and test them.

The third built a food delivery aggregator, connecting bakeries and customers. The more bakeries, the more customers. The more customers, the more bakeries. The system grew on its own.

All three came to freedom, but by different paths.

Question:
Does your business model have a self-replicating mechanism? Which of the three?
Or did you just create a job for yourself?

Practical task.
Write out your current business model.
Determine whether it has self-replication. If not, replace the model or supplement it with one of the three mechanisms. If it does, determine which of the three types works for you.
Make a 2-3 year plan for the development of this particular mechanism.

MAIN:
Three types of self-reproduction—three paths to freedom: people, the system, or capital. Choose yours. But without self-reproduction, you'll always work for money, rather than having money work for you. 

Chapter 2.61.1 Sector "I": Why Compound Interest Isn't a Freebie, but a Deal with Time

The essence of the phenomenon.
The "I" (Investor) quadrant of the Kiyosaki quadrant is when your money works for you. You're not selling your time, managing a team, or creating a product. You simply own assets that generate income. The purest mechanism here is...Compound interest.But this path has its price, and it is important to know it before you embark on it.

Operating principle.
Compound interest occurs when the capital income is not spent but reinvested. In the next period, interest accrues on a larger amount. Over the long term, this results in exponential growth.

Imagine a snowball rolling down a hill. At first, it's small, and its growth is barely noticeable. But the further it rolls, the more snow it accumulates, and the faster it grows. This is how compound interest works:
The first few years – slowly, almost imperceptibly. After decades – like an avalanche.
But for the lump to roll, three things are needed: 
start-up capital (the root ball itself), time (a long slope) and skills (the ability to choose the right slope and not crash into a tree).

Pros and cons of the "I" sector.

Pros:

  • Income doesn't require your daily involvement. The money works while you sleep.
  • Over the long term (20+ years), compound interest works wonders. Even small amounts can turn into fortunes.
  • You don't depend on a team, clients, or partners. You rely solely on your capital and your decisions (based on your competencies).
  • Assets can be passed on by inheritance.

Cons:

  • Start-up capital is required. You can't enter the "I" sector from scratch.
  • It takes time. For the first 10-15 years, growth is almost imperceptible. Most people aren't willing to wait that long. It takes discipline throughout that time.
  • Competence is required. Without an understanding of how investment instruments work, you will inevitably lose capital.
  • There are no guarantees. Any investment involves risk. Responsibility for the outcome is always yours.

What does it consist of?
Three conditions for entering the "I" sector.

  1. Start-up capital.Money you're prepared to hold onto for decades. Not your last money, not borrowed money, not "savings for a rainy day." Just cash you can spare, the loss of which won't ruin your life.
  2. Time.The horizon is 10 years or more. Compound interest isn't for those looking for a quick fix. The first few years' growth is almost imperceptible, and only the patient reap the rewards.
  3. Competencies.It's not about "read three books and become an investor." It's about deep immersion. You need a wealth of experience—hundreds of studied cases, dozens of books read, thousands of hours of analysis. Like an artist who copied a hundred works by great masters before finding his own style. Only when you develop a sense of direction will you be able to make informed decisions.

Important difference: Group 0 and sector "I".

Don't confuse two different concepts.

  • Group 0 (Payment systems and capital storage facilities)— is a BUSINESS. You're creating a payment system, a bank, a crypto exchange. You're an entrepreneur in the financial sector. This is the "B" sector, not the "I" sector.
  • Sector "I"This is when you don't create a business, but rather invest in existing businesses. You buy stocks, bonds, and stakes in companies. You're an owner, not a creator. An investor might invest in a Group 0 company (for example, buy Visa shares). But they don't build the company. They simply own a part of it.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Create capital.The "I" sector isn't for starting from scratch. First, earn money in the "P," "C," or "B" sectors. Accumulate an amount you're willing to invest long-term.
  2. Develop your visual acuity.Study not three, but dozens of books. Analyze hundreds of case studies. Understand how different investors lost money and how they made it. Just as an artist copies the masters, so too, absorb the experience of others to develop your own intuition.
  3. Use the BM Hierarchy as a filter.Look at the business model map (Chapter 5.2). The most resilient ones are Group 2 (two-sided platforms), Group 1 (ecosystems), and Group 0 (payment systems). People will always look for housing, take taxis, order delivery, and transfer money. Investments in such "evergreen" groups have a better chance of surviving crises.
  4. Start small.Invest a small amount in an instrument you understand. Not for profit, but for experience. Experience what it's like to see market fluctuations without panicking.
  5. Reinvest.Return all income received to your capital. Don't spend a penny for the first 10 years. This is the fuel for compound interest.
  6. Build your competencies.The larger your capital, the deeper your knowledge should be. Learn new instruments, diversify, consult with professionals, but make your own decisions.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Jumping into the "I" quadrant without start-up capital. Investing your last money, taking out loans, hoping for a quick buck. This is a path to debt, not freedom.

Mistake 2: Trusting managers' "guarantees." There are no guarantees in investing. Anyone who promises "a stable, risk-free income" is either lying or doesn't know what they're talking about.

Mistake 3: Not developing competencies. Handing over money to someone else to manage and forgetting about it. Sooner or later, such an investor is left without capital because they can't distinguish between reasonable risk and gamble.

Mistake 4: Expecting quick results. Compound interest only begins to noticeably impact your earnings after 10-15 years. Most people aren't willing to wait that long and quit right at the start of exponential growth.

Mistake 5: Confusing the "I" quadrant with Group 0. Thinking, "I'm an investor, so I should start my own bank." No. Starting a bank is entrepreneurship. Investing is owning a share of an existing bank.

Example.
Imagine that at age 25, you started saving 10,000 rubles a month and investing it at 12 percent annual interest (the average historical stock market return). You do this every month, without fail. At age 35, you stop adding to the account and simply let the savings grow.

Your friend wasn't saving anything at 25, but at 35, he came to his senses and started investing the same 10,000 rubles a month at the same 12 percent interest rate. He continued doing this until he was 60—a full 25 years.

By age 60, you'll have about 46 million rubles in your account. Your friend, who's been investing for two and a half times longer, has about 19 million.

The $27 million difference arose solely because you started 10 years earlier. You invested $1.2 million of your own money, your friend $3 million. But compound interest meant that the smaller effort put in earlier outpaced the greater effort put in later.

This is the magic of compound interest.
Not doubling in one step, but quiet, imperceptible growth that, over the long term, turns modest sums into fortunes.

Question:
Are you ready to wait 20-30 years for your capital to start working for you?
Or do you need freedom earlier, and then your path is to sector "B"?

Practical task.
Calculate how much you need to save monthly to receive passive income equal to your current life cost in 20 years.
Use a conservative rate of return of 10 percent per annum.
Compare this amount with your current capabilities.
If the figure seems unattainable, think about how to increase your income in the “C” or “B” sectors, and then move on to the “I” sector.

MAIN:
The "I" sector isn't for everyone. It requires start-up capital, expertise, and decades of patience. But if you're ready, it's the quietest and most reliable path to freedom. Choose wisely. 

Chapter 2.62 The Mathematics of Man-Hours

The essence of the phenomenon.
Self-reproduction gives you access to the main resource -someone else's time.
And time is the only thing that can't be replenished. When you work alone, your output is limited to 24 hours a day. When you create a self-replicating system, you gain access to the combined time of all participants.

Operating principle.
Every person who enters your structure adds their working hours to the general pot.
If you are alone, you have 8-10 hours a day.
If you have 100 people, that’s already 300-400 hours a day.
And if there are 10,000 people, that’s 30,000 hours daily.

You stop working with your hands. You start working.through peopleAnd your personal contribution becomes not physical, but organizational.

What does it consist of?
Mathematics of man-hours.

  1. You are alone— 1 person x 8 hours = 8 man-hours per day.
    Leverage: 1× (baseline).
  2. A small team— 10 people x 6 hours = 60 man-hours per day.
    Lever: 7.5×.
  3. Active structure— 100 people x 4 hours = 400 man-hours per day.
    Lever: 50×.
  4. Critical Mass (MLM)— 1000 people x 3 hours = 3000 man-hours per day. Lever: 375×.
  5. Large-scale system— 10,000 people x 3 hours = 30,000 man-hours per day. Lever: 3750×.

Your personal productivity is limited to 24 hours. The productivity of a self-replicating system is limited only by the number of people within it. And that number can be "infinite."

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Findthe first people ready to work in the system.
  2. Train them.so that they can teach the next ones.
  3. With each new levelThe total time does not grow linearly, but multiple times.
  4. Controlnot actions, but results.
  5. The more people,the higher your leverage.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that your personal efficiency is the most important thing. Even if you work as hard as 10 people, you still hit 24 hours.

Mistake 2: Trying to control everyone manually. This is impossible when there are many people.

Mistake 3: Not understanding that 30,000 man-hours a day isn't magic, but mathematics. It's the result of a properly designed system.

Mistake 4: Training performers, not leaders. Performers don't create new performers. Leaders create leaders.

Example.
You work alone—8 hours a day, 40 hours a week. Your income hits a ceiling: you can't physically handle more than 24 hours a day. Even if you're a genius, you won't earn more than 100 conventional units a month.

Your friend has created a network of 1,000 people. Each person works just 3 hours a day—that's a total of 3,000 hours daily. Even if each person brings in 1 unit of income per day, that's 1,000 units. You won't be able to collect that much in a month.

He's not stronger than you. Not smarter. Not more talented. He simply has leverage—other people's hands, other people's time. You sell your watch. He sells watches to thousands of people. The difference isn't in strength—the difference is in scale.

Question:
Where can you get access to 10,000 man-hours per day?
In what system are people motivated to work without your constant supervision?

Practical task.
Calculate how many man-hours per day you can personally provide.
Calculate how many man-hours it will take to achieve your goal.
Imagine how many people would be needed to cover this figure.
Start looking for the first three.

MAIN:
Leverage is a way to multiply your results through someone else's resources.
Without leverage, you sell your time. With leverage, you scale. 

Chapter 2.63 THE BIG SECRET. Traffic is King

The essence of the phenomenon.
If you can create a flow of people (traffic), you can do anything!
The rest will take care of itself. The law of large numbers and double Pareto will do 90% of the work for you. Traffic is the only parameter that has no ceiling.

Operating principle.
You create content. Content attracts people. People log in. Then the math works: out of 100 people, 20 will become clients, out of 20 clients, 4 will become leaders, and those 4 will account for 80% of your total turnover. It doesn't matter what the product is. It doesn't matter what the niche is.
If there are 10,000 people per month at the input, there will be results at the output.
Traffic cures everything: product errors, weak leaders, lack of money.

What does it consist of?
Traffic system.

  1. Content- something that attracts attention.
  2. Platform— where you post (YouTube, Telegram, VK, Zen).
  3. I.E.— content creation accelerator.
  4. Automation— so that the system works without you 24/7.
  5. Analytics— to understand what works.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Select a platform(one, don't spread yourself too thin).
  2. AI Tuningto create content.
  3. Create a content plan for the month.30 posts - one per day.
  4. Publish regularly(3-5 times a week).
  5. Collect analytics,Remove what is not working, strengthen what is working.
  6. Repeat,until you reach 10,000+ reach.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not having traffic and expecting miracles. You've created a product, but no one knows about it.

Mistake 2: Spreading yourself thin across all platforms at once. It's better to stick to one platform where you're a regular.

Mistake 3: Not using AI and automation. Doing everything manually, slowly, and burning out.

Example.
The owner of a carpentry workshop made perfect custom furniture. High-quality, expensive, and full of passion. But his clients were few—only those who found him by word of mouth. He decided the problem lay in his craftsmanship and further improved the quality—he bought German machinery for 2 million and hired a designer. But his clientele didn't increase.

Another carpenter made simple, solid furniture, no frills. But he started a social media page, posted videos of the process, showed how a cabinet was created from a piece of wood, answered questions in the comments, and talked about wood species. People came to look, stayed because they were interested. Then they ordered.

A year later, the second carpenter has a waiting list of six months ahead and prices are 40% higher than the first.

And the first one was still waiting for someone to find out about him one day.

Question:
What if tomorrow you learned how to attract 10,000 people a month on any topic?

Practical task.
Choose one platform.
Set up the AI. Create 30 posts (one per day). Publish.
Analyze which ones came in.
Do this for 3 months in a row.

MAIN:
Traffic cures everything. No sales – little traffic. No leads – little traffic. No money – little traffic. Drive traffic – and build your freedom.


SUMMARY OF SECTION 2. SYSTEMS
Self-assessment checklist.

  • I understand the difference between assets and liabilities.
  • I know my life cost down to the last penny.
  • I understand which quadrant I am in and where I want to go.
  • I build my business according to the CON-TON-FON scheme.
  • I test ideas through MVP before investing money.
  • I know how to break down big goals into small steps.
  • I understand the laws of large numbers and Pareto.
  • I know what critical mass is and I strive for it.
  • I understand the difference between KPI and ROI.
  • I build systems, I don't put out fires.
  • I use levers to scale.
  • I understand that passive income is a myth, and I am ready to work ahead of the curve.
  • I take the human factor into account in my plans.
  • I know about the existence of "Black Swans".
  • My system is anti-crisp.
  • I tested my business model for self-replication.
  • I understand the man-hours math (8 vs. 30,000).

MAIN:
Business math never lies. Calculate the numbers, build systems, don't confuse scaling scenarios, consider the human factor, remember Black Swans, and build antifragile systems. And most importantly, traffic heals everything.

SECTION 3. COMMUNITIES
(Your main asset in the 21st century)

Introduction to the section

In the 21st century, the most important asset isn't money, or machines, or real estate. The most important asset is "Community." A group of people who trust you, follow you, and are willing to buy from you again and again. A product can be copied, a technology can be stolen, and prices can be driven down. But no one can copy a loyal community.

Of your thousands of subscribers, according to the Pareto law, 4-5% will "buy" everything you offer. This isn't fortune-telling; it's business mathematics. It works without fail. Your goal is not just to build an "Audience," but to transform it into a "Community": connecting people with each other, building trust and loyalty.

In this section, we'll explore the difference between an Audience and a Community, how 10,000 subscribers work, two types of promotion, personal branding, the 3-message method, Six Touches, Six Handshakes, Six Priorities and Five Golden Questions, content powered by neural networks, and the art of feedback.

MAIN:
Don't chase butterflies - plant a garden, take care of it, and the butterflies will settle in themselves. 

Chapter 3.1 The Power of Community
(short - full inChapter 2.15)

The essence of the phenomenon.
In the 21st century, the most important asset isn't money, or machines, or real estate. The most important asset is communities. A group of people who trust you, follow you, and are willing to buy from you again and again.

Operating principle.
A product can be copied in a week. Technology can be stolen. Prices can be undercut. But a loyal community that trusts you can't be copied. People don't come for a product. They come to you. Your job is not just to build an audience, but to transform it into a community: to connect people with each other, to build trust and loyalty.

Of the 100% of the flow of people, 20% will become clients, and 4% will become leaders and generate 80% of the total turnover. Your task is to find and unite these 4%.

---

Example.
In one area there were two fitness clubs with the same equipment and prices.

At the first club, clients came, worked out, and left. Attrition was very high—after two or three months, people stopped renewing their memberships. The owner didn't know the names of the clients and didn't communicate with them.

There was always a line at the second club. Owner Irina knew everyone by name, remembered injuries and limitations, and offered personalized nutrition advice. She created a private chat where members shared their successes, arranged joint runs, and helped each other. Clients stayed for years, even when new clubs with modern equipment opened nearby.

Irina didn't sell memberships—she created a community. Clients paid for the equipment, not the accessories.

Question:
Do you have your own community?
People who listen to you, read you, watch you and trust you?

Practical task.
Set a goal: 1000 subscribers in 6 months.
Break it down into weeks and do it.

MAIN:
Don't chase butterflies - plant a garden, take care of it, and the butterflies will settle in themselves. 

Chapter 3.2 Two Types of Promotion
(short - full inChapter 2.47)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Any business grows in only two ways. The first is when you personally invest your time and effort. The second is when the system works for you, even when you sleep. The problem with most is that they choose only one. You need both, but in the right proportions.

Operating principle.
You have two resources: your personal time (limited to 24 hours) and your reputation with content (works indefinitely).
The first engine gives a quick but local result.
The second one takes a long time to accelerate, but then works for you forever.
The task of the strategist is not to pit them against each other, but to arrange them so that one feeds the other.

---

Example.
Imagine two bakers.

The first one gets up at 3 a.m., kneads the dough, bakes 20 loaves, sells them before lunch, and collapses exhausted. He works only with his hands. If he's sick, there's no money. If he's tired, there are no sales. He sells his time.

The second one also started at 3 a.m. But he also blogged, recorded recipes, and filmed his baking process. A year later, he had 50,000 subscribers. Now he sells courses, books, and merchandise, and the bakery employs hired bakers. He built a second engine that feeds him even when he's not at the oven.
The first baker still gets up at 3 a.m. The second one sleeps until 8.

Question:
What do you do with your hands every day that you can write down, film, or describe once so that it works for you forever?

Practical task.
Choose one action that you do regularly.
Record it on video or write step-by-step instructions.
This is the first brick of your automatic engine.

MAIN:
Active promotion (do it yourself) + passive (content, brand) + team = freedom.

Chapter 3.3 Personal Brand and Target Audience
(short - full inChapter 2.53)

The essence of the phenomenon.
In today's world, people buy from people. Products can be copied, prices can be lowered, but no one can copy a personal brand. A personal brand is your reputation and recognition, which works like a magnet. People buy people, not services.

Operating principle.
Regular sales (non-branded): You → find a client → persuade → sell. Low efficiency, high rejection rates.
Branded sales: The client → recognizes you → trusts you → buys. The efficiency is high, and there are almost no rejections. A personal brand takes years to build, but it always works for you.

---

Example.
The young photographer shot weddings, but clients found him only through targeted advertising. He made enough money, but competition was growing, and clicks were becoming more expensive.

He decided to build a personal brand. He started a blog: he posted not just a portfolio, but stories from each shoot, showed his editing process, and shared life hacks. He answered questions in the comments. Literally six months later, people started coming to him through recommendations. Women brought their friends.
mothers - daughters.

A year later, his advertising budget had fallen by 70%, but his client base had grown. People weren't buying photos—they were buying the confidence that their wedding would be remembered and beautifully showcased. A product can be copied, but a personal brand cannot.

Question:
Do you have a personal brand?
Do the people who need you know you?

Practical task.
Describe your target audience.
Assess your personal brand.
Choose one platform and start creating content regularly.

MAIN:
A personal brand isn't just a big name. It's attention, memory, care, reputation, and attitude. People buy from people.

Chapter 3.4 The 3-Message Method
(short - full inChapter 2.48)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The most environmentally friendly and effective method of promotion on any platform (social media, messenger) for any business. It respects people's privacy but is guaranteed to spark a conversation. Don't sell to strangers. First, become familiar, then sell.

Operating principle.
Standard direct sales: "Hi! Buy from me..." The person immediately closes down, blocks the offer. The three-message method works differently: first you provide value, then you demonstrate expertise, and only then you make an offer. The person comes to their own decision.

---

Example.
A girl wanted to meet a guy at the gym. Instead of immediately approaching him and saying, "Hey, let's be friends," she took a different approach.

My first message (I wrote in the comments under his post): "You did a great job squatting 100 kg today. I can't do that myself, but I'm trying." He replied: "Thanks, it took me a long time to get there."

Three days later, I came up to him at the gym and asked, "Could you tell me how to do a deadlift correctly? I'm afraid I'll throw out my back." He showed me and helped.

A few days later, she wrote: "Thanks for your help! I couldn't have figured this out without you. Hey, maybe we could go get a protein shake after my workout?" He agreed.

Help first, invite later.

Question:
How do you usually start communicating with new people online?

Practical task.
Complete the application plan for 5 people.

MAIN:
Don't sell to strangers. Become familiar first, then sell.

Chapter 3.5 Six Touches
(short - full inChapter 2.50)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Most marketers make the same mistake: they show an ad once, nobody buys, and then they give up. They don't understand the "Six Touches" principle. For the average person to buy something, it takes at least six touches of product information.

Operating principle.
A touch is any contact a potential customer has with you or your product: seeing an ad, reading a post, watching a video, hearing a review from a friend, visiting a website, receiving an email. With each touch, awareness, trust, and willingness to buy grow. People don't buy on the first try. They buy when they're ready.

---

Example.
Ekaterina was launching a consulting project. She compiled a list of 40 potential clients and began working with them using the Six Touches system.

Touch 1-2: left detailed comments on their posts on social media, simply showed interest.

Cases 3-4: A few days later, I sent short audio messages via private message explaining their situation—free, no requests. Four responded.

Case 5: I offered to conduct a free diagnostic of their business. Three agreed.

Case 6: After the diagnostics, she asked, "Would you like me to show you how to fix this problem systematically? There's a monthly plan." Two out of three bought it.

She didn't write a single sales pitch. She provided value, answered questions, and demonstrated her expertise. By the sixth contact, people were willing to pay.

Question:
How many touchpoints do you have with your potential clients? Have you counted?

Practical task.
Count how many touches you make.
If less than 6, plan a 6-step funnel.
Don't give up after the first failures.

MAIN:
People don't buy the first time. They buy when they're ready. Your job is to wait for them to be ready.

Chapter 3.6 Six Degrees of Separation
(short - full inChapter 2.51)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's a famous theory: any two people on planet Earth are separated, on average, by six levels of mutual acquaintance. This means you can meet anyone—be it a president, a Hollywood star, or a billionaire—through a chain of no more than six people. Anyone on the planet is no more than six handshakes away. Just start looking.

Operating principle.
If each person has an average of 100 acquaintances, then at step one you have 100 people, at step two – 10,000, at step three – 1,000,000, at step four – 100,000,000, at step five – 10,000,000,000 (more than the population of the Earth). By step five, you already cover the entire planet.

---

Example.
The student dreamed of getting into graduate school with a renowned professor. There was no direct contact.

He went to his supervisor: "Ivan Petrovich, help me. I want to see Professor Sokolov, but I don't know how to contact him."

Ivan Petrovich said: "Oh, I studied under Sokolov! I can give you the phone number of his former graduate student; he's in Moscow now."

The student called his former graduate student. He said, "Sokolov rarely answers his phone these days. But I know his wife; we were at a conference together. I can give you her email."

A student wrote to a professor's wife. She replied, "My husband is very busy right now, but if you like, I'll give him your letter."

A week later, Professor Sokolov himself called the student: "Hello, my wife gave me your letter. Tell me about the work."

The professor liked it.
A year later, the student was already in graduate school. Three handshakes are not six. It's faster in a community.

Question:
Which of the "unreachable" people do you want to meet?

Practical task.
Write the name of the person you want to meet.
Write a list of your acquaintances (at least 20).
Ask everyone: "Who do you know from the sphere of...?"
Follow the chain.

MAIN:
The Six Degrees of Separation Theory: You can reach anyone through connections. Don't be shy about asking for help.

Chapter 3.7 Six Priorities + Five Golden Questions
(short - full inChapter 2.49)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The most powerful sale is the one a person makes “to himself.”
Your job isn't to persuade, but to help them formulate their own desires. Don't sell—help them choose. 6 priorities + 5 questions help people formulate their own motivations.

Operating principle.
First, you give the person a list of priorities so they can identify their own "hot button." Then you ask five questions that help them formulate their true desires. The proposal becomes their own, not yours.

---

Example.
A mother wanted to buy her child a laptop. The store clerk showed her three models. The mother was at a loss—she didn't know which one to choose.

Instead of overwhelming her with technical specifications, the seller asked five golden questions.

"What will the child do on the laptop?" - "Study, watch videos, sometimes play."

"Why is this so important to you?" - "So that he doesn't fall behind his classmates."

"What happens if you buy a weak laptop?" — "You'll have to buy a new one in a year. That's wasted money."

“Why does this bother you?” “Because the budget is limited, I want to buy it once and for a long time.”

The salesperson understood that maximum performance wasn't what was needed, but a balance between price and durability. He offered a model with a mid-range processor but good build quality and a three-year warranty. Mom bought it.

Question:
Have you ever bought something that you “sold” to yourself inside your head?

Practical task.
Remember the list.
Next time you're talking to a potential client, use this technique.

MAIN:
6 priorities + 5 questions help people formulate their own motivations. Don't sell – help them choose.

Chapter 3.8 Tools: Content, Neural Networks, and Automation
(short - full inChapter 2.55)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Modern business requires modern tools. Manual labor is becoming a thing of the past. Those who don't use tools are slowed down by 10-100 times. The three main tools of the 21st century are content, neural networks, and automation.

Operating principle.
Each of these tools is a lever that multiplies your efforts.
Content works for you 24/7, even when you sleep.
Neural networks do in a minute what used to take hours and days.
Automation replaces your manual labor with software work.
Together they give superpowers.

---

Example.
The culinary blogger created new recipes, took photos, wrote texts, and edited videos. She no longer had the energy to manage her social media accounts—she had 500 subscribers, and they were barely growing.

Her tech-savvy husband set up a neural network for her. The AI ​​began generating ideas for posts, writing recipe drafts, selecting hashtags, and even doing preliminary color correction of photos. The author only polished the results, added personal comments, and tested tastes.

Six months later, she had 15,000 subscribers. Her time for cooking and filming hadn't increased—it was just that the neural networks had taken over the routine. The number of orders for custom menus had increased fivefold. And she had even more free time than before.

Question:
Which of these tools do you already use?
And which ones are you afraid of?

Practical task.
Choose one instrument and start mastering it.

MAIN:
Tools (neural networks and automation) multiply your efficiency.
Don't do with your hands what automation can do.

Chapter 3.9 Feedback and the Art of Building Bridges
(short - full inChapter 2.58)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Most entrepreneurs ask clients the wrong question. They say, "Do you like our product?" and get a polite, meaningless "yes." Experts ask differently. They build bridges between the client's problem and their solution.

Operating principle.
The right question is: "What irritates you most about our industry?" The person isn't defensive (you're not asking about yourself). The person is honest (they're complaining about the industry, not you). You get real insights—what's truly important to the client. "Building bridges" means establishing a connection between the person's problem and your solution.

---

Example.
A design studio launched a new landing page for selling courses. Within a week, it had 500 visitors and two applications. The owner wanted a complete redesign of the website for 200,000 rubles.

The marketer said, "Stop, let's ask those who left first." They set up an exit survey: "What stopped you from applying?" 68 percent responded, "It's unclear what I'll get after payment. There's no course outline."

They didn't redesign the website. They simply added a 5-page PDF with a detailed program to the homepage. Applications increased to 15 per week. Costs were zero rubles. The result was the same as replacing the entire website.

Question:
How do you ask clients about their problems?
Or just whether they like your product?

Practical task.
Complete the feedback collection scheme.
Conduct 5 in-depth interviews.

MAIN:
Don't build bridges no one wants to cross. Find out first where people want to cross. 


SECTION 3 SUMMARY: COMMUNITIES
Self-assessment checklist.

  • I understand that community is the greatest asset of the 21st century.
  • I know that 4% of people make 80% of the turnover.
  • I have my own community (Telegram channel, blog, group).
  • How many subscribers do I have? My goal is 10,000.
  • I use both types of promotion - active and passive.
  • I'm building a personal brand. People know me as an expert.
  • I use the "3 messages" method when meeting people.
  • I understand that it takes at least six touches to make a sale.
  • I use the six degrees of separation theory to find the right people.
  • I know how to ask the right questions (6 priorities + 5 questions).
  • I use content and neural networks to grow my community.
  • I ask clients not "do you like it?", but "what annoys you?"

Key findings of the section:

  1. Community is an asset.An audience is capital. A product can be copied, technology can be stolen, but a dedicated community cannot be copied.
  2. 4% make 80%.Out of 100 people, 20 will become clients, and 4 will become leaders. Don't try to be good for everyone. Find your 4% and work with them.
  3. 10,000 subscribers = any product.If you have 10,000 loyal fans, you can sell them almost anything.
  4. Make friends first, then sell.The 3-message method, the Six Touches, and the right questions—all of this is about respecting a person and building trust.
  5. Content + neural networks = superpower.Content works for you 24/7, and neural networks make its creation fast and easy.

MAIN:
Community is your greatest asset. Build it, respect it, and nurture it. And math will do its job. 

SECTION 4. LEVERS
(How to strengthen yourself tenfold)

Introduction to the section

Leverage is what allows you to achieve great results with little effort. One person with leverage is more powerful than a thousand without it. It's the most important element of scaling!

In this section, we'll explore five types of leverage: people, money, systems, content, and AI. We'll also discuss delegation, the success triangle, the power of teamwork, the five faiths, diversification, big money, and copying the masters.

MAIN:
10,000 people working 3 hours a day = 30,000 man-hours.
This is incomparably more than your personal 3-4 hours. Find leverage. 

Chapter 4.1 Levers
(short - full inChapter 2.32)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Leverage is what allows you to achieve great results with little effort. In business, leverage is the only way to escape the "selling your time" trap. One person with leverage is more powerful than a thousand without it.

Operating principle.
In physics, a lever allows you to lift a heavy load with minimal effort. In business, a lever is a way to multiply your actions.
You can work 8 hours a day.
But if you find the lever, you can get the result of 80, 800 and 8000 man-hours - per day!

---

Example.
The lawyer ran a private practice. She worked 10-12 hours a day: consulting with clients, drafting contracts, handling correspondence, and preparing court documents. Her income was limited by her personal time.

Instead of working more, she found an assistant for three hours a day. She took care of correspondence, scheduling meetings, and preparing templates. The lawyer concentrated only on complex consultations and court cases.

Two months later, she hired a second assistant—one with a legal background. He began preparing preliminary versions of documents; she merely reviewed and finalized them. The time spent per client was cut threefold.

Within six months, she had a team of three. She worked four to five hours a day, and her clientele doubled. Her income quadrupled.

She didn't work harder. She simply found leverage—other people's hands and heads—that augmented her own work.

Question:
What levers do you use?
People, money, systems, content, AI?

Practical task.
Complete the lever implementation diagram.

MAIN:
Give me a place to stand and I will move the Earth.
Find your footing.
One man with a lever is stronger than a thousand without it.

Chapter 4.2 Delegation and Why It Matters
(short - full inChapter 2.56)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The most common mistake entrepreneurs make is trying to do everything themselves. This is a dead end, leading to selling your time. An entrepreneur is not a craftsman. A craftsman sells his hands. An entrepreneur sells a system.

Operating principle.
If you do everything yourself, your income is limited to 24 hours a day.
If you delegate, your income = (total team time) x (average efficiency) x (margin). There is no ceiling.
Delegation is not a loss of control, but a reconfiguration of it.

---

Example.
The owner of a small event planning agency personally called clients, negotiated with contractors, supervised installation, and prepared estimates. He prided himself on having "everything under control." He worked 12-14 hour days, seven days a week.

He once ended up in the hospital for two weeks. When he returned, he expected to find the place in disrepair. But his assistant, whom he never trusted with client management, conducted three negotiations, signed two contracts, and coordinated estimates with contractors during that time. The clients didn't even notice the owner was gone.

He realized: all this time, he wasn't a businessman, but the busiest employee in his company. He stopped doing everything himself and hired a manager, an accountant, and an administrator. Now he works four hours a day, and his income has doubled. Because he's learned to let go.

Question:
What things do you do yourself that you can delegate today?

Practical task.
Complete the delegation scheme.
Hire your first person within the next month.

MAIN:
Delegation is the key to scaling. If you do everything yourself, you're not an entrepreneur, but the busiest employee.

Chapter 4.3 The Triangle of Successful Business
(short - full inChapter 2.8)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Do you want to end up with a lot of money and a lot of free time? There's only one way: building big teams.
The formula is simple: a large team = a lot of money + a lot of time.

Operating principle.
A solo worker is always limited by their personal time. Even if their hour is worth 10,000 rubles, there are only 24 hours in a day. The ceiling is strict. A businessman hires people, and their income begins to grow nonlinearly, because hundreds and thousands of man-hours are being worked.

---

Example.
The head of the sales department did everything himself: calling clients, negotiating, issuing invoices, and sending documents. He worked from 8 a.m. to 10 p.m., seven days a week. The department grew, but he didn't hire assistants—"no one could do it better."

He once ended up in the hospital for two weeks. He returned expecting to find everything in disarray. But his deputy, whom he never trusted, had personally negotiated the deal during that time, signed three contracts totaling 5 million rubles, hired an intern, and trained him in invoicing. The clients didn't even notice the manager was gone.

He realized that all these years he hadn't been the boss, but the busiest employee. He stopped doing everything himself and hired a manager and a secretary. Within a month, he was working four hours a day, and his income had increased by 40 percent.

Question:
Are you trying to do everything yourself or are you already building a team?

Practical task.
Complete the team building diagram.
Hire your first person within the next month.

MAIN:
Big team = a lot of money + a lot of time.
One man in the field is no match for another. One man in business is no match for a businessman.

Chapter 4.4 The Power of Teamwork
(short - full inChapter 2.45)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Nothing significant is achieved by one person. Even the smallest success requires the contribution of an entire team. A lone achiever is always limited by their time and energy. A team removes this limitation and creates synergy. A lone achiever goes fast. A team goes far.

Operating principle.
Synergy is when 1 + 1 = 3. People don't just work together, they reinforce each other. One came up with the idea, another built it, a third sold it, a fourth tested it. Together, they accomplish what would be impossible on their own. Everyone matters in a team.

---

Example.
The construction company employed three foremen. The architect conceived the design, the foreman organized the work, and the builder erected the walls.

One day, they got into an argument over who was in charge and split up. The architect drew the perfect house, but didn't know how to lay bricks. The foreman bought the materials, but without a blueprint, he built haphazardly—the walls turned out crooked. The builder started laying the walls, but the foundation was in the wrong place.

A year later, they met, tired and broke. They looked at each other and laughed. "Let's work together," said the foreman. The architect drew, the foreman organized, and the builder erected. A year later, the house was ready.

They stopped arguing about who was in charge. What mattered was what they did together, as a team.

Question:
Do you have a team? Or are you still a loner?

Practical task.
If you are a single worker, choose the first task to delegate.
Find someone to give it to. Start small.

MAIN:
Teamwork produces results that cannot be achieved alone.
Everyone matters. One person – one result. A hundred people – a hundred results. Choose.

Chapter 4.5 Five Faiths
(short - full inChapter 2.46)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The foundation of any great undertaking is five levels of faith. If even one is missing, the entire structure collapses. You can believe in yourself but not believe in the product. Or believe in the product but not believe in the team. Ultimately, things don't work out, and it's unclear why.

Operating principle.
The five levels of faith act as multipliers.
Overall belief = belief in yourself × belief in the company × belief in the system × belief in the team × belief in the product.
If even one faith is zero, the overall faith is zero. For sustainable forward movement, all five pillars are necessary. Like five fingers on a hand.

---

Example.
Oleg launched an online course on advertising setup. He did everything right: high-quality content, a user-friendly platform, and invested in targeted advertising. Sales were good, but slow.

He stumbled across an article about the five faiths. Oleg sat down and honestly rated himself on a scale of 0 to 10. Faith in himself was an 8, faith in the company was a 9, and faith in the team was a 9. Faith in the product was a 4, and faith in the system was a 3.

He suddenly realized: everything he was doing was right, but he didn't believe in it. Subconsciously, he considered the course unpolished and the sales funnel unworkable. He reviewed the lessons, added practical exercises, and wrote new modules. His confidence in the product rose to a 7. He tested his traffic channels, found one that worked, and wrote a script. His confidence in the system rose to a 6.

Sales picked up. Oleg stopped rushing around.
He understood: if you don’t believe in the product, no one will buy it.

Question:
What is your weakest faith?

Practical task.
Rate each of the Five Faiths on a scale of 0 to 10.
Find the weakest one.
Make a plan to strengthen it in the next month.

MAIN:
All Five Faiths are important. Like five fingers on a hand. If you break one, the hand is no longer the same.

Chapter 4.6 Tools: Content, Neural Networks, and Automation
(short - full inChapter 2.55)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Modern business requires modern tools. Manual labor is becoming a thing of the past. Those who don't use tools are slowed down by 10-100 times. The three main tools of the 21st century are content, neural networks, and automation.

Operating principle.
Each of these tools is a lever that multiplies your efforts.
Content works for you 24/7, even when you sleep.
Neural networks do in a minute what used to take hours and days.
Automation replaces your manual labor with software work.
Together they give superpowers.

---

Example.
The copywriter wrote copy for days, but still didn't have enough clients. There was no time left for finding orders or promoting himself.

The programmer set up a neural network for him. The AI ​​began generating topics, structures, drafts—and even suggesting which services were currently trending. The copywriter merely refined the texts to suit his needs.

In six months, he took on three times as many orders while spending half as much time on work. And most importantly, for the first time in a long time, he had free evenings. He stopped doing manually what a neural network could do.

Question:
Which of these tools do you already use? Which ones are you afraid of?

Practical task.
Choose one instrument and start mastering it.

MAIN:
Tools (neural networks and automation) multiply your efficiency.
Don't do with your hands what automation can do.

Chapter 4.7 Competencies + Resources + Money
(short - full inChapter 2.54)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Success rests on three legs. If one leg is short, the stool falls. Three legs: expertise, resources, money. I used to think two were enough. Life has shown: a stool on two legs doesn't stand up.

Operating principle.
Competencies are the engine.
Resources (connections, reputation, time, energy) are the map and the navigator.
Money is fuel.
You can fill up the tank, but without an engine and a map, you'll get nowhere. You need all three. And they only work together.

---

Example.
Igor worked as a project manager for a construction company for five years. He had a thorough understanding of all stages of the project, was skilled in negotiating with contractors and clients, and understood estimates. His competencies were rated 8 out of 10.

When he decided to start his own business, it turned out that wasn't enough. He didn't have the start-up capital to rent an office or hire a team. His money was a 2 out of 10. And he had no connections among suppliers—all his contacts were with his former employer. His resources were a 3 out of 10.

He returned to working for another two years. But this time, he did things differently. He saved money, met suppliers, and established direct contacts with foremen. Within two years, he had 600,000 rubles in start-up capital, a base of seven vetted contractors, and a reputation among clients. His capital had grown to 6, and his resources to 7.

The second launch was a success. He hadn't gotten any smarter in those two years. His competencies remained the same. But now he had the resources and the money. All three components came together—and things took off.

Question:
Which leg is your shortest?

Practical task.
Create a circuit for amplifying three components.
Start with the weakest.

MAIN:
Success depends on three components: competence, resources (connections), and money. If even one is weak, the ceiling is low.

Chapter 4.8 Diversification
(short - full inChapter 2.38)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Don't put all your eggs in one basket, even if it seems safe. This is called "diversification"—spreading the risk.
The main rule when planning any business model: first consider the risks. Then consider the benefits.

Operating principle.
Professionals first calculate how much they can lose, and only then how much they can earn. You distribute your assets so that the collapse of one doesn't destroy everything. The more independent points you have, the lower the risk. If you have one source of income, you're at risk. If you have three, you're safe.

---

Example.
The entrepreneur kept all his savings in one bank. The bank's license was revoked, and his funds were frozen for a year. Business ground to a halt.

His partner held funds in three banks, in two currencies, and invested some in his own business. When one bank went bust, the remaining assets saved him.

One lost everything. The other didn't even notice the crisis. The difference is in the number of baskets. One basket means one path to disaster. Many baskets mean many paths to safety.

Question:
How many baskets do you have?
What happens if one of them crashes tomorrow?

Practical task.
Write down all your current points.
For each one, ask: “What would happen if this disappeared tomorrow?”
Create a diversification plan for the year.

MAIN:
One basket - one path to disaster.
Many baskets - many paths to safety.
Diversification is the distribution of risks.

Chapter 4.9 Big Money = Big Problems
(short - full inChapter 2.41)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's a myth: "Just make a million and all my problems will be solved." The truth is, big money doesn't solve problems. It creates new problems—problems of a different order. And if you're not prepared for these problems, money will crush you.

Operating principle.
There's no such thing as passive money. Any money, especially large amounts, requires energy to acquire, preserve, and grow. With each new level of income, new layers of complexity emerge that you never even imagined. Money isn't a finish line, but a new start with new rules.

---

Example.
A young entrepreneur sold his startup for a large sum. He'd been dreaming about it for three years. The money landed in his account, and chaos ensued.

First came "old friends" he'd long forgotten. Everyone needed help: some asked for business support, some for medical treatment, some just "until payday." He couldn't refuse—he gave away almost half.

Then some "investors" showed up with mountains of gold. They convinced a friend to invest in the project. The project failed within three months.

The tax office sent a notice—he'd forgotten that large sums were subject to payment. Fines ate up another third.

Six months later, he had neither money nor business left. And the friends who had received help had vanished. He wasn't prepared for the fact that big money wasn't just a joy, but also a huge headache.

Question:
Are you ready for the challenges that come with big money?

Practical task.
Complete the Big Money Preparation Plan.
Write a list of problems and ways to prevent them.

MAIN:
Money doesn't solve problems. It creates new ones.
Be prepared.

Chapter 4.10 Study and Copy from the Masters (short - full inChapter 2.59)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's no need to reinvent the wheel when you can look at how the best have done it and make it even better. Experts in any field seek out those who have already achieved what they want and literally copy their actions, thoughts, and beliefs. This is called "modeling." Copying isn't a sign of weakness or lack of talent. It's the fastest way to learn and shortcut your path to success. Uniqueness comes later, when you've mastered the basics and begin to add your own.

Operating principle.
When you try to invent something from scratch, you waste a huge amount of time and energy on trial and error. You're walking the same path that someone else has already taken and making the same mistakes. It's ineffective.

"Modeling" works differently. You select 3-5 successful players in your niche and carefully study their actions. What do they do? How do they do it? What tools do they use? How do they communicate with clients? How do they build funnels? You don't just observe—you dissect their system into its component parts.

Then you take the best elements from each and assemble your own model from them. From the first, the content structure, from the second, the method for handling objections, from the third, the product packaging format. This isn't blind copying, but a conscious assembly of a construction kit from proven components.

Once the model is assembled, you put it to work and observe the results. Some things work perfectly, while others require some tweaking to suit your specific needs. You adjust, add your own elements, and gradually the model becomes your own. Uniqueness doesn't emerge from nothing, but from a combination of best practices and your personal experience.

---

Example.
A budding entrepreneur was launching his first online store. Instead of inventing a unique sales funnel, he studied three market leaders. He wrote down their techniques: how they designed product cards, how they wrote emails, how they communicated with customers. He took the best from each and created his own system.

Within six months, his store was generating steady sales. Competitors were surprised: where did he get all this experience?

He simply realized that there's no need to reinvent the wheel. It's enough to copy the best—and add your own touch.

Question:
Which successful players in your niche are already doing what you want to do?

Practical task.
Today, choose three successful players in your niche.
Identify each person's one strongest feature. Write them down.
Assemble your model from these three chips and launch it into operation for 30 days without changes.
Evaluate the results in a month.

MAIN:
Don't reinvent the wheel. Find the best, copy their strengths, and build your own model. Uniqueness will come later.


SECTION 4 SUMMARY: LEVERS
Self-assessment checklist.

  • I understand what levers are and what types there are.
  • I delegate routine tasks.
  • I build a team, rather than trying to do everything myself.
  • I have people who work instead of me.
  • I use team synergy.
  • I have all Five Faiths.
  • I use modern tools (content, AI, automation).
  • I develop all three components: competencies, resources, money.
  • I diversify risks.
  • I understand that big money brings big problems.
  • I study and copy successful strategies.

MAIN:
10,000 people working 3 hours a day = 30,000 man-hours.
This is incomparably more than your personal 3-4 hours. Find leverage.

SECTION 5. SCALING
(How to Grow Without Breaking the System)

Don't look at the scale of others' results. Build your own vector of movement. Look and move forward.

Introduction to the section

What you see before you is a hierarchy of business models. This ismapyour personal growth. And it has one important feature: you don't have to go step by step. You can enter the game at any level. Want to build an ecosystem right away? Great. Set aside 10 years, assemble a team, learn programming, security, payments, AI—and build. It's your choice.

If you're just starting out and lack resources, it's smarter to enter Group 7 or 5, earn some initial capital, develop competencies, and then move up. But you need to know the whole map from the start to understand where to grow.

In this section, we'll explore three scaling paths, a hierarchy of 8 groups (from one-dimensional businesses to payment systems), each group separately with examples, and the concept of a "lamp you build yourself."

MAIN:
Group 7 is fine. Group 0 is awesome. But the main thing is to move up.

Chapter 5.1 Three Types of Scaling
(short - full inChapter 2.18)

The essence of the phenomenon.
You have a working system. Money is flowing, processes are streamlined.
Question: What's next? Answer: If the system works, your job is to scale it up. But there are three different ways to scale, and it's important not to confuse them.

Operating principle.
You can't scale something that doesn't work. Before you step on the gas, make sure the engine is assembled. Growth should be based on data, not emotion. There are three scenarios: accelerate the current model, change the model, or change the business. Each has its own moment.

---

Example.
The owner of a furniture workshop made luxury custom kitchens. The waiting list stretched for six months. Instead of investing in CNC machines and hiring two assemblers, he decided to improve the quality: he switched to a more expensive hardware supplier, redesigned the designs, and added lighting.

While he spent six months "perfecting" one model, competitors launched production with automated lines. They produced kitchens in two weeks and at a price 30% lower.

Clients stopped waiting. The workshop lost ground. Instead of scaling up, he focused on cosmetics.

Question:
What stage is your business at?
What scaling scenario is needed right now?

Practical task.
Evaluate your business.
Determine what stage you are at.
Choose one of the three paths and make an action plan for the month.

MAIN:
Don't fix what's broken—scale it. There are three scaling scenarios: step up the pace (increase traffic), change the model (improve conversion/ticket), or change the business (enter a new niche). Each has its own moment. Don't confuse them. 

Chapter 5.2 Business Model Hierarchy (Terrain Map)

The essence of the phenomenon.
All businesses in the world can be arranged in a single hierarchy—from the simplest to the absolute pinnacles that rule the markets. Understanding this hierarchy gives you the most important thing: you see where you are now and where you can grow. You stop stumbling blindly and start consciously choosing your level of play.

Operating principle.
This hierarchy isn't a ladder you climb step by step. It's a map of the landscape. You can enter the game at any level. Want to build an ecosystem right away? Great. But if you're just starting out, it's smarter to enter Group 7 or 5, earn your first capital, and then move up.

What does it consist of?
Eight groups of business models.

  1. Group 7 - All others.They sell a specific product or service. It's easy to enter, but dependent on a single product. Example: a shoe store, a hair salon.
  2. Group 6 - Process automation.Machines and programs are replacing people. Create it once and sell it endlessly. Example: n8n, factory robots.
  3. Group 5 - Affiliate programs.Selling other people's products for a percentage. Low entry costs, you can start from scratch. Examples: MLM, CPA networks.
  4. Group 4 - Affiliate centers.Affiliate program aggregators. People come to compare and choose. Examples: Bestchange, Aviasales.
  5. Group 3 - Tool bases.Selling "shovels" to gold miners. Millions need the tool. Examples: Adobe, Canva.
  6. Group 2 - Double-sided platforms.They bring together buyers and sellers. Network effect—the system grows on its own. Examples: Avito, Uber, Wildberries.
  7. Group 1 - Ecosystems.A group of related services that meet all user needs. Examples: Yandex, Apple, Amazon.
  8. Group 0 — Payment systems and capital storage facilities.The financial infrastructure through which money from all other groups flows. Examples: Visa, Mastercard, SWIFT, banks, crypto networks.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Determine where you are now.Take an honest look at your business. What group? Don't fool yourself.
  2. Ask yourself three questions. 
    How many resources do I have? (money, time, skills, connections).
    What is my character? (Am I a leader-person or a systems architect?)
    What do I want in 10 years? (modest stability or global scale?).
  3. Select the direction of movement.What group do you want to be in? Write it.
  4. Write a plan for the first three steps.What specifically will you do to get things moving?
  5. Remember:Even if you're currently in Group 7 (selling pies), you can build a network of stores (Group 7, but on a larger scale), create a franchise (closer to Group 5), build a delivery platform (Group 2), and in 20 years, build an ecosystem around it (Group 1). And always, at any level, you'll use the services of Group 0—banks and payment systems.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that Group 7 is bad, while Group 1 is cool and you should definitely try it. No. Each group makes sense for its stage and its type of person.

Mistake 2: Not seeing the maps and stumbling around blindly. Jumping from one model to another without even knowing where you're going.

Mistake 3: Getting stuck in the lower tiers because you don't know what's up there. Selling pies for 20 years without realizing you can build a chain, a franchise, or a delivery platform.

Example.
Anna started with a small bakery in a residential area. She baked her own bread, worked behind the counter, and did the bookkeeping herself. It was a classic Group 7 business—a one-dimensional business tied to her personal time.

Two years later, she opened a second location, then a third. She hired bakers and salespeople. Revenue grew, but Anna was still tied to the operations—she was now managing three locations instead of one. It was still Group 7, just scaled up.

Then she went further. She developed a franchise: she packaged the technology, recipes, standards, and design. She sold the first franchise, then five more. Now her business has moved into Group 5—an affiliate model where others pay her for the right to operate under her brand.

Ten years later, Anna launched a platform for delivering fresh baked goods from local bakeries. Bakeries registered on the platform, customers ordered through the app, and couriers delivered. This was Group 2—a two-sided platform with a network effect.

Anna is currently building an ecosystem: in addition to delivery, she's added a marketplace for pastry chefs, an online baking school, and a subscription service for rare ingredients.

She started with pies in one neighborhood, and now her business is an entire universe for those who bake and love baked goods. And at every stage, she used the services of Group 0—banks, payment systems, and acquiring.

Question:
What level are you at now?
Where do you want to be in 5-10 years?

Practical task.
Determine where your current business is located (Group 7, 5, 3, 2 or other).
Think about which group you want to move to.
Write a plan for the first three steps.

MAIN:
There's a map. Choose your path.

Chapter 5.3 Group 0. Payment systems and capital storage

The essence of the phenomenon.
Every business, from a shawarma stand to a powerful ecosystem, faces the same question at the end of each day: "Where to put the money? How to process it? Who to trust?"

Payment systems and places of capital storage arefinancial infrastructure, which holds everything together. It's not just another business model. It'spower over the movement of all the world's money.

Operating principle.
Group 0 does not create goods, does not provide services, does not bring sellers and buyers together. It createspipes, through which everyone else's money flows. And it takes a microscopic commission on each transaction. But when these transactions amount to billions a day, the microscopic commission turns into astronomical profits.

What does it consist of?
Group 0 includes several types of structures.

  1. Payment systems— networks for transferring money between participants. Examples: Visa, Mastercard, SWIFT, PayPal, Stripe.
  2. Banks (as repositories)— places where money is stored and used. Examples: Sber, JPMorgan, ICBC.
  3. Cryptocurrency networks— decentralized value transfer protocols. Examples: Bitcoin, Ethereum.
  4. National payment systems— state-owned equivalents. Examples: SBP, Mir, UnionPay.
  5. Clearing centers— clearing houses that facilitate transactions.

Why do Ecosystems create their own payment systems?

When turnover reaches billions of dollars, trusting your money to an "outsider" becomes mortally dangerous. The "outsider" (bank, government, payment system) can at any time:

  • freeze accounts
  • block transactions
  • go bankrupt
  • submit to sanctions

That's why Ecosystems (Group 1) spend billions to createtheir ownpayment systems. Tinkoff Pay, Apple Pay, Amazon Pay—they're not just convenience for the customer. They'rean attempt to break free from the control of Group 0.

But the real top is those whoinitially owns these "money makers" and "storages"Those who control the movement of all the money in the world.

Step-by-step diagram (for those aiming for Group 0):

  1. Obtain all necessary licenses.These are years and billions. There is no other way.
  2. Build trust on a global level.People must believe that their money will not be lost.
  3. Create infrastructure,Resistant to hacking and failures. This is nuclear reactor level.
  4. Connect thousands of banks and millions of users.Without this, the system is useless.
  5. Ensure security at the highest level.Mistakes are not forgiven here.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking it can be done on the fly. No. Group 0 requires resources, time, and national expertise.

Mistake 2: Underestimating regulatory risks. Laws change, licenses are revoked, sanctions are imposed. You can't survive without a team of lawyers.

Mistake 3: Skimping on security. A payment system hack is the end of a business and a career.

Mistake 4 - Not understanding that Group 0's main asset istrustLose trust - lose everything.

Example.
Visa doesn't bake pies, build platforms, or create ecosystems. It simply facilitates the flow of money between them all. And for this, it earns a penny from every transaction. But these pennies are so numerous that Visa is one of the most valuable companies in the world.

Question:
Who do you trust with your money?
Who controls the pipe they pass through?
Do you understand that every ruble you spend brings someone a penny just for the fact of moving?

Practical task.
List all the payment systems you use.
Think about who is behind them.
Realize: Group 0 is always there, even if you don’t notice it.

MAIN:
Money must flow. Those who build the riverbeds always get their share, regardless of what business flourishes on the banks. 

Chapter 5.4 Group 7. All Others (One-Sided Businesses)

The essence of the phenomenon.
These are all business models that promote their own "one-dimensional" and "narrow" products or services. They sell a specific product, not a meeting opportunity, tools, or automation. Examples: grocery stores, hair salons, car repair shops, furniture manufacturers, freelancers, and cafes.

Operating principle.
How much a typical business—for example, a store or a hair salon—earns depends on three simple things.
First: how many people come per day.
Second: how much money does each buyer leave on average?
Third: how often do they return? Multiply one by the other and by the third to get income.

These are simple, straightforward businesses that are easy to get started in. But they are vulnerable because they depend on a single product, a single niche, and a single market.

What does it consist of?
Vulnerabilities of a one-sided business.

  1. Product dependence.If people stop buying the product, everything will stop. Example: a video store in 2025.
  2. There is no network effect.Every new client doesn't attract more. A hair salon doesn't grow on its own.
  3. Constant investment in advertising.As soon as you stop advertising, you run out of clients.
  4. Limited market.Only those who are nearby (for offline business).
  5. Competition.It’s easy to open the same business nearby.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Admit that you are in group 7.If this is so, don’t deceive yourself.
  2. Assess your vulnerabilities.For each item from the list above.
  3. Choose a strategy.Either become the best in your niche (so much so that clients come by word of mouth), or start moving up the hierarchy.
  4. If you chose "become the best"— focus on quality, service, uniqueness.
  5. If you chose "move up"— think about which next group is closer to you (5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0).

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking this is the only type of business. Not seeing other options.

Mistake 2: Not seeing other options. Staying in Group 7 forever, without trying to move up.

Mistake 3: Not taking into account that Group 7 is normal at the start, but it is not the ceiling.

Example.
A private eyelash extension artist saw clients at home. She worked from morning until night, sometimes seven days a week. Clients loved her, and she was booked up months in advance. But she was constantly burning out; her income was hitting a ceiling—she couldn't physically handle more.
