A beauty studio opened nearby. Three lash artists worked there, each with their own schedule. The owner didn't do lashes; she hired, trained, promoted, and managed appointments. The quality of work was slightly lower than that of a private lash artist, but there were plenty of clients for everyone. The owner herself worked three to four hours a day and earned more than a private lash artist would in 12 hours.

She wasn't the best master. She simply built a system. And the private master remained the best, but the most tired.

Question:
Are you in group 7? Do you feel vulnerable?

Practical task.
Determine which group you are in now (honestly).
If you are in group 7, assess your vulnerabilities.
Think about which group you would like to move to.

MAIN:
Group 7 is normal. But it's not the ceiling. There's room for improvement.

Chapter 5.5 Group 6. Process automation platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
It's a business that replaces people with machines, robots, and software. You don't sell a product or provide a service. You create a tool that does the work for people.

Operating principle.
There are two branches of automation:
offline (physical) - robots in factories, automatic lines, CNC machines;
and online (digital) – programs, neural networks, automation tools like n8n or Make. The second branch is accessible to everyone, cheap, and fast.

What does it consist of?
Comparison of a human and a machine.

  1. Speed:man - 1×, machine gun - 10-1000×.
  2. Opening hours:person - 8 hours, machine - 24/7.
  3. Errors:A person is there (fatigue, inattention), a machine is a minimum.
  4. Price:The human being grows, the machine falls.
  5. Scaling:It’s difficult for a person, but easy for a machine.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find 3 processes in your business that repeat every day (or every week).
  2. Consider whether they can be automated using software.
  3. Find a tool(n8n, Make, Zapier) or a programmer.
  4. Implement and test.
  5. Spend your free time on development.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that automation is complicated and expensive. Today, there are free and easy-to-use tools.

Mistake 2: Not noticing simple things that can be automated today.

Mistake 3: Automating what doesn't need to be automated. Not everything that can be automated should be automated.

Example.
The entrepreneur installed a kiosk in a business center for charging phones and selling power banks. He came once a week, collected proceeds, and replaced consumables. The equipment operated automatically.

A year later, he installed similar terminals in three other business centers. A year later, in ten more. He hired a courier to drive around to each location and collect the money.

He himself stopped traveling altogether—he only checked reports on his phone. One terminal worked for him 24/7. Ten terminals worked as if they had ten employees, without pay or days off.

Question:
What processes in your life or business can be automated today?

Practical task.
Find 3 processes in your business that can be automated.
Implement one in the next month.

MAIN:
Automation is when you create a system that works without you, and then simply replicate it.

Chapter 5.6 Group 5. Affiliate programs (including MLM)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The company creates a product and offers it to anyone willing to sell it for a commission. You don't produce, store, or deliver. You simply bring in customers and earn a commission.

Operating principle.
The scheme is simple: company (product + payments)→partners (sell and receive %)→clients. You become an agent of the company.
I told a friend about the bank, he got a card and received a bonus.
Sold someone else's online course using my link and received a commission.

What does it consist of?
Types of affiliate programs.

  1. CPA.Pay-per-action (purchase, registration). Percentage: fixed or %. Example: online stores.
  2. Referral.Refer a friend and receive a bonus. Interest: usually a one-time payment. Examples: banks, taxis.
  3. MLM.% of your sales and your team's sales. Percentages: deep. Example: network marketing companies.

Pros and cons.
Affiliate programs have their advantages. The entry level is low—you don't need to create your own product, invent a product, or set up production. You can get started quickly, almost immediately after registering. The company provides ready-made marketing materials and training. Plus, there's ongoing support—you're not alone.

But there are downsides, too. You're completely dependent on the company. They can change the terms, interest rates, or payment rules at any time. You're not creating an asset that can be sold or passed on—you're working in someone else's system. And if the company closes, you'll start from scratch.

Strategy for working in only one Affiliate Program.
Get in and promote. Don't spread yourself too thin. Build your empire there. Become a super partner, build a deep structure, and gain access to exclusive terms.
The price of strategyYou're deeply attached to one company. But even if it closes, no problem. Knowing the business math from this book, you'll be able to build everything back up to the highest level in another affiliate program in the next 2-3 years.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find 3 affiliate programs in your niche.
  2. Analyze the conditions.Interest rates, payment terms, product quality.
  3. Choose one and start promoting it.Don't spread yourself too thin.
  4. Build your own structure in it.Don't just sell, find partners.
  5. When the structure grows, choose.Stay or go your own way.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Avoiding the word "MLM" like the plague. It's just an affiliate program. Not all MLMs are created equal. There are reputable companies with good products.

Mistake 2: jumping headfirst into the first affiliate program you come across without understanding the terms and conditions. Do your research.

Mistake 3: Spreading yourself thin across multiple affiliate programs and failing to build any one deeply. It's better to have one, but with a deep structure.

Example.
The appliance repairman worked alone. Clients were few, and his income was meager.

My nephew suggested, "I'll create a website and a social media group. I'll attract clients and arrange for outreach. You do the repairs. We'll split the cost."

A month later, the nephew had five student assistants. They also sought out clients, took their cut, and gave a portion to the nephew. The mechanic simply repaired the machines. The nephew coordinated the network.
Within six months, they had 20 partners working for them. The foreman couldn't keep up—he hired a second foreman. Everyone was making money.

Question:
Have you ever tried affiliate programs?
Why yes or why not?

Practical task.
Find 3 affiliate programs in your niche.
Analyze the conditions.
Choose one and start promoting it.

MAIN:
An affiliate program is when you don't sell yourself, but find people who sell for a percentage.

Chapter 5.7 Group 4. Affiliate Centers (Aggregators)

The essence of the phenomenon.
These are affiliate program aggregators. You create a hub that brings together multiple affiliate programs in the same or different niches. People come to you to compare and choose, and then leave to buy through your links.

Operating principle.
You don't sell anything directly. You create a platform where people can compare offers from different companies. When they click your link and buy, you get a commission. You help people choose the best and make money from it.

What does it consist of?
Examples of aggregators.

  1. Bestchange— collects all currency exchangers.
    Earns: percentage from each transition.
  2. Aviasales— collects all airlines and ticket agencies.
    Earns: commission on ticket sales.
  3. Compare.ru— collects banks and insurance companies.
    Earns: percentage of completed applications.
  4. Booking.com— collects hotels and inns.
    Earns: commission from each booking.

How is this better than one affiliate program?
When you work with a single affiliate program, you're completely dependent on one company. If they change their terms or shut down, you lose everything. Earnings are limited to a single commission, and you offer only one product to the client. Essentially, you're just an advertiser.

An affiliate center (aggregator) works differently. Risks are reduced through diversification—if one company fails, others will remain. You receive commissions from multiple affiliate networks, and clients can choose the best offer from among them. Your value grows: you're no longer just an advertiser, but a facilitator of choice. People come to you to compare offers and leave with purchases through your links.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a niche with a lot of affiliate programs.Tours, hotels, courses, exchange offices, banks, insurance.
  2. Join all affiliate programs in this niche.
  3. Create a platform(site, channel, bot, table) where people can compare.
  4. Make comparison easy.Prices, conditions, reviews.
  5. Chase traffic.People come to compare and leave through your links.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Joining a single affiliate program and trying to promote only it. It's not an aggregator, it's just an affiliate program.

Mistake 2: Creating a clunky interface that makes comparisons difficult. If users can't quickly compare, they'll leave.

Mistake 3: Not updating information. Prices change, terms and conditions become outdated. If the information is out of date, trust is lost.

Example.
The entrepreneur noticed that people spend hours searching for the best currency exchange rate—opening ten tabs, comparing rates, and wasting time.

He created a simple aggregator website: he collected data from all the city's exchange offices in a single table. Rates were updated automatically.

Users quickly appreciated the convenience. Within six months, the site was receiving 5,000 visits a day. Exchangers themselves were asking to be added to the list—for a small fee.

The entrepreneur didn't sell or change anything. He simply collected in one place what people were already searching for all over the internet.

Question:
In what niche could you create your own aggregator?
Where should people compare?

Practical task.
Choose a niche that has a lot of affiliate programs.
Build a list of 10+ affiliate programs.
Think of how you can help people choose the best (website, Telegram bot, table, channel).

MAIN:
An aggregator is not a seller, but a comparison site. It makes money through commissions or paid connections.

Chapter 5.8 Group 3. Tool Bases

The essence of the phenomenon.
The golden rule: "During a gold rush, the biggest moneymakers are the shovel sellers." You don't pan for gold—you sell gold panning tools. You don't build houses—you sell hammers and nails. You don't create content—you build neural networks to create content.

Operating principle.
You are creating a tool that millions of people need.
Everyone pays you a little (subscription, license), but due to the scale you get a huge income.
The tool is created once, and sold indefinitely.

What does it consist of?
Examples of tool bases.

  1. Microsoft Office— all office workers. Payment model: license/subscription.
  2. Adobe Photoshop— all designers. Payment model: subscription.
  3. Canva— everyone who makes pictures. Payment model: subscription.
  4. 1C— all accountants in the Russian Federation. Payment model: license.
  5. ChatGPT— everyone who writes texts. Payment model: subscription.

Why it's powerful.
Millions of people need these tools—it's a huge market. Users get used to a particular service and don't switch to competitors, even if cheaper alternatives appear.

The more people use a tool, the more valuable it becomes—that's the network effect. A program or platform only needs to be created once, and it can be sold endlessly. The cost of each new copy approaches zero.

And most importantly, tools are not dependent on fashion or season. Yesterday, you needed spreadsheets and text editors, today, neural networks and automation tools, tomorrow, something else. But the need for tools that simplify work always remains.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find the pain.What do people do with their hands, painfully, every day?
  2. Automate.Make a program/service that does this faster.
  3. Make it simple.So that anyone can use it without instructions.
  4. Sell ​​subscriptions.Little money from many.
  5. Constantly improve.Listen to your users and add the features you need.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Building a tool for yourself without thinking about the users. It's difficult, confusing, and inconvenient to implement.

Mistake 2: Creating a complex interface that intimidates newcomers. The tool should be simple.

Mistake 3: Don't sell a subscription, but try to sell it one-time. A subscription is more profitable in the long run.

Example.
When the neural network boom began, thousands of people rushed to create courses, channels, and blogs about AI. They wrote posts by hand, spent hours selecting images, and learned from their mistakes.

One entrepreneur took a different approach. He collected ready-made prompts for ChatGPT, a collection of neural networks for image generation, and scripts for automated posting in one place. He sold access by subscription for 500 rubles per month.

While the "gold diggers" were making money on their courses, he was steadily making money from anyone who wanted to make their work easier.
Minimal risk. Income every month.

Question:
What kind of "shovel" could you create for your niche?
What does everyone need but no one does?

Practical task.
Write down 10 tasks that you do with your hands every day.
Consider whether it is possible to create a tool that automates these tasks for others?
Start small - make a simple tool for yourself, then refine it for sale.

MAIN:
Don't look for gold—sell shovels. The tool base provides a stable income, independent of the success of each individual gold digger. 

Chapter 5.9 Group 2. Double-sided platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
These are business models wheresimultaneouslyThere are both sellers and buyers. The platform doesn't sell its product—it sells the opportunity to meet. It's the top of the business food chain (before ecosystems and payment systems).

Operating principle.
The platform simply creates the space, provides security, and takes a cut for organizing the meeting. The more sellers there are, the more choice there is for buyers. The more buyers there are, the more sellers want to attend. This is called the "network effect."

What does it consist of?
Examples of double-sided platforms.

  1. Notice boards— My father.
    Sellers: anyone who wants to sell. Buyers: anyone who wants to buy.
  2. Marketplaces — Wildberries.
    Sellers: thousands of stores. Buyers: millions of buyers.
  3. Taxi — Uber.
    Sellers: drivers. Buyers: passengers.
  4. Food delivery — Delivery Club.
    Sellers: restaurants. Buyers: hungry people.
  5. Freelance — Kwork.
    Sellers: freelancers. Buyers: clients.
  6. Acquaintance — Tinder.
    Sellers: all users. Buyers: all users.

"Network effect." The main secret of "Self-replication."
More sellers→More choice→More buyers→Even more sellers→Even more buyers. This cycle makes platforms indestructible. The more people use them, the more valuable they become.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find a niche where there is a problem of meeting sellers and buyers.
  2. Create a minimal version(Telegram bulletin board, VKontakte group).
  3. Reach the first critical mass on one side(or sellers or buyers).
  4. Connect the other side,using the first one as bait.
  5. Launch the network effect.The more people, the more valuable the platform.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that only giants can create a platform. No, you can start small.

Mistake 2: Not solving the "chicken and egg" problem (who to recruit first). If there are no sellers, there will be no buyers. If there are no buyers, there will be no sellers.

Mistake 3: Building a platform but not ensuring transaction security. People are afraid of fraud.

Example.
The entrepreneur noted that the city has many private contractors (plumbers, electricians, repairmen), but they are difficult to find—they rely on word of mouth. Meanwhile, city residents are constantly in need of such services.

He created a simple website: repairmen submit requests with prices and reviews, residents choose a suitable one and call. The entrepreneur himself doesn't repair anything—he simply provides a venue for supply and demand to meet.

He takes a small commission from each order. Artists find clients, and clients find artists. The platform is self-sufficient.

Question:
In what niche could you create your own two-sided platform?
Where are there sellers and buyers who have difficulty finding each other?

Practical task.
Think about your niche. Is there a problem with meeting people?
Can you create a platform where they can find each other?
Start small – a bulletin board on Telegram, a VKontakte group.

MAIN:
Become a platform that connects sellers and buyers. 

Chapter 5.9.1 The Secret to the Explosive Growth of Double-Sided Platforms
(Income as fuel)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The success of a two-sided platform—for example, a classifieds board, a marketplace, or a taxi booking service—depends on more than just convenience.
The main hidden driver is the opportunity for ordinary people to earn real money from it! 
People go where the income is. If a platform generates income, it takes off. If not, it fizzles out, even with the perfect design.

Operating principle.
Sellers, drivers, craftsmen, and freelancers are constantly searching for ways to generate income. When they see a platform where they can sell a product, find a job, or receive payment for a service, they're the first to arrive. Their arrival creates a supply: goods, services, trips. This supply attracts buyers. Seller success attracts even more new sellers. A powerful virtuous circle is triggered: earnings lead to increased supply, increased supply leads to increased demand, and increased demand increases earnings.
This isn't just a network effect. It's a money-fueled network effect. It's 10 times more powerful!

Without this mechanism, the platform remains empty. People won't post ads where no one is buying. Drivers won't go out on a route where there are no passengers. Technicians won't register where there are no orders.

What does it consist of?
The growth spiral of the two-sided platform includes five stages.

  1. Opportunity to earn money— the first sellers or performers see that the platform generates real income.
  2. Increase in supply— the range of goods or available services increases, and choice appears.
  3. Growing demand— buyers or customers go where there is a large selection and low prices.
  4. Increase in income— sellers earn more due to high demand and competition.
  5. Influx of new sellers— rumors of easy money attract new participants, and the cycle repeats.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Make entry easy.Don't require sellers to complete complicated registration and documents at the start. The easier it is to start earning, the more people will come.
  2. Set a low commission at the start.High commissions kill the desire to earn money. 5-10% is normal for a launch. 30% is the death of the platform.
  3. Show the successes of the first participants.Create a page or section with testimonials and numbers: "Ivan earned 50,000 in a month." This works better than any advertising.
  4. Ensure fast withdrawal of funds.If someone has earned money, they want to receive it immediately, not in two weeks. Delays destroy trust.
  5. Follow the balance.If there are too many sellers and too few buyers, revenue will fall. You need to attract a second party. If there are too many buyers and too few sellers, you need to simplify the entry process for sellers.

Possible errors.

Mistake #1: Thinking that a user-friendly interface is more important than the ability to make money. You can create a beautiful website, but if it doesn't make money, no one will stay.

Mistake 2: Raising fees from day one. High fees discourage the first, most active participants. Without them, the platform won't reach critical mass.

Mistake 3: Ignoring the other side of the platform. If the platform connects buyers and sellers, you can't develop only one side. Sellers won't come without buyers. Buyers won't come without sellers.

Example.
Two tutor search platforms launched simultaneously in one city.

The first one created a beautiful website with ratings, a portfolio, and video business cards. But the commission per lesson was 30%. Tutors tried it, realized it wasn't profitable, and moved to other platforms. The platform lasted six months and then shut down.

The second one charged a 5% commission at launch. It added easy card withdrawals and a page with success stories: "Sergey, a student, earned 50,000 in a month." Tutors flooded in. Students came for them because the choice was vast. A year later, 2,000 tutors were working on the platform, and the commission was raised to 15%—but no one wanted to leave because there were so many orders.

Question:
Does your platform give people the opportunity to actually earn money or just promise it?

Practical task.
If you're building a two-sided platform, find three potential sellers or contractors. Ask them, "What commission rate would be profitable for you to work with us?"
Write down the answers. Average them. That's your starting commission.

MAIN:
Convenience attracts. And earning keeps people coming back. If people can actually make money on your platform, they'll bring others along.

Chapter 5.9.2 Content for Platform Promotion: All Money and Nothing But Money

The essence of the phenomenon.
When promoting a two-sided platform, all content—posts, videos, newsletters, reviews—should be built around one thing: who has earned on the platform and how much. Don't talk about the service's advantages. Don't describe the user-friendliness of the interface. Don't list features. Only numbers, screenshots of payouts, and stories of real people and their earnings.

Operating principle.
90% of people on the planet are looking for ways to make money. Now and in the future. Consistently. If your platform offers this opportunity, don't bury it under a pile of words like "reliable," "convenient," or "fast." People don't believe promises. They believe evidence.

A screenshot of a 50,000 ruble payout works better than any advertising text. An interview with a seller who sold 100 units in a month will attract more newcomers than a million-dollar banner. Someone who sees someone else making money thinks, "Why not me?" and signs up.

What does it consist of?
The content strategy for promoting a two-sided platform is built on three types of materials.

  1. Screenshots of payments— actual data from users' cards and accounts. The more recent, the better.
  2. Success stories— interviews with sellers and contractors: how they started, how much they invested, how much they earned in the first month, after six months, after a year.
  3. Case studies— how a person came to the platform, what they did, what mistakes they made, what result they arrived at.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find your first successful users.Even if they earned 1,000 rubles, that's history. Interview them. Ask for a screenshot of the payment.
  2. Publish these stories everywhere.On social media, in newsletters, on the blog, in advertisements. On all platforms where potential sellers are present.
  3. The format is as simple as possible.Screenshot + 3-5 sentences: who earned how much, over what period. No fluff.
  4. Repeat regularly.One story a week is the minimum. The more, the better.
  5. Create a separate section or page with success stories.So that a newbie could come in and see: “They actually make money here, not just promises.”

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Talking about the platform, not the earnings. "We have low fees, fast support, and a convenient search." People don't hear that. They only hear, "I can make money here."

Mistake 2: Posting general phrases without numbers. "Our users are successfully earning money." Without specific amounts, this is just empty talk. Just numbers: 50,000, 100,000, 1,000,000.

Mistake 3: Forgetting about the platform's other side. Content about making money attracts sellers. But if there are no buyers, they will leave. You need to find a balance.

Example.
A food delivery platform launched in the city. The marketing department created attractive videos: "Fast delivery, hot food, low prices." The ads were viewed, but the couriers didn't sign up—it was unclear how much money they could make.

A competitor took a different approach. He didn't make videos about food. He filmed short interviews with couriers: "Kolya, how much did you earn last week?" "25,000 rubles." "Show me your phone screen." Kolya did. There were 20 such videos with different couriers. They would be shared in chats, posted on social media, and shown on targeted ads.

Couriers poured in. The question of "how much can you earn" was answered clearly and honestly.

Question:
Your last post about the platform—did it talk about its benefits or how much real people have earned on it?

Practical task.
If you already have a platform, find one user who has earned at least 1,000 rubles.
Have a short interview with him and ask for a screenshot of the payment.
Publish it today.
If you don't have any users with income yet, start with yourself: show how much you've earned.

MAIN:
Don't talk about the platform. Talk about the money people make on it. Numbers sell. Promises don't.

Chapter 5.10 Group 1. Ecosystems

The essence of the phenomenon.
You've gone from selling products (Group 7) to creating two-sided platforms (Group 2). You've learned to connect buyers and sellers. But the game doesn't end there. The next level, the next peak in the hierarchy, is the Ecosystem.

Operating principle.
An ecosystem isn't just a single platform. It's a group of interconnected digital services and platforms owned by a single company and united by shared data, user identification, and the goal of meeting as many user needs as possible, so they never leave the system.

What does it consist of?
Three steps to building an ecosystem.

  1. Anchor product (traffic and data).Create an entry point—a free or freemium service that solves a basic need. Examples: Yandex—search, Tinkoff—maps and apps, Apple—iPod/iTunes.
  2. Expansion of the perimeter (closure).Using trust and data from the anchor product, launch related services. Example:
    Yandex: Maps, Taxi, Food, Music, Kinopoisk.
    Tinkoff: deposits, investments, insurance, SIM card.
  3. Synergy and closed loop (power).Services begin to work in concert with each other. Data from one improves the work of the other. Example: Taxi improves Maps, Maps improves Search, Search sells ads.

Examples of ecosystems.
YandexIt started with search. Over time, I added Maps, Taxi, Food, Market, Music, Kinopoisk, Lavka, and Disk. All services are connected through a single account, sharing data and payments. Log in once and use them all.

TinkoffIt started with a credit card and an app. Now it offers savings, investments, insurance, a SIM card, and legal assistance. The bank knows your spending habits and suggests suitable products—often before you've even considered them.

AppleIt all started with the iPod and the iTunes store. Today, it's the iPhone, iPad, Mac, Apple Watch, the App Store, Apple Music, iCloud, and Apple Pay. All devices work together, and data syncs instantly. You change your phone, and it's just like the old one.

AmazonIt opened as a bookstore. Now it's a massive marketplace, with a Prime subscription, the AWS cloud platform, a Kindle reader, Prime Video, and its own logistics. Prime subscriptions lock in the customer experience: fast delivery, videos, and music—all in one place.

The world's most valuable and powerful companies are ecosystems. Their goal is to become the operating system of your life.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Create an anchor product.A free or shareware service that solves a basic need.
  2. Collect user data.Without data, an ecosystem cannot function.
  3. Launch related services.Using trust and data, expand the perimeter.
  4. Connect services together.Single account, single payments, shared data.
  5. Build synergy.So that services reinforce each other.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking you can build an ecosystem in a year. Nope. It's a decades-long process.

Mistake 2: Trying to address all needs at once, without an anchor product. You need an entry point.

Mistake 3: Not connecting services together, leaving them disjointed. This isn't an ecosystem, but a collection of applications.

Example.
You wake up to your iPhone alarm. You check the weather on Yandex. You take a taxi (Yandex.Taxi) and listen to Yandex.Music. You order food from Yandex.Lavka. You pay with Apple Pay. You watch a movie on Kinopoisk.
You live inside ecosystems without even noticing it.

Question:
What ecosystem do you live in?
What services does it consist of?

Practical task.
Analyze any ecosystem (Yandex, Tinkoff, Apple).
List all of its services.
Think about how they are related.
This understanding will give you a map of where you can grow.

MAIN:
Don't sell a product. Don't sell a meeting. Sell the whole world around a person.

Chapter 5.11 Group 2. Notice Boards - BEST BM #1

The essence of the phenomenon.
A universal platform where you can sell absolutely anything. No restrictions on niches, products, or services. It's one of the simplest and most effective two-sided platforms. Avito, Yula, and similar services earn billions using this model.

Operating principle.
You create a place where people can post ads to buy or sell. You don't fill the catalog—users do that themselves. You don't store the goods—sellers do their own storage. You only arrange meetings and charge for additional services.

What does it consist of?
Advantages of bulletin boards.

  1. Limitless niches.You can sell anything: from a needle to an airplane.
  2. Users create content.You don't fill the catalog - the salespeople do that.
  3. There is no risk of off-season.There is always something on sale.
  4. No production costs.You don't produce goods.
  5. Network effect.The more people, the more valuable the site.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a niche(if you can't do everything at once). For example, "car sales classifieds in your city" or "board for mothers with children."
  2. Make a simple platform.Website, Telegram channel with bots, VKontakte group.
  3. Reach critical mass.Free at first, lure the sellers.
  4. Provide convenience.Categories, search, filters.
  5. Enter paid services.When traffic appears.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that bulletin boards are a thing of the past.
No. It's one of the most resilient business models, at all times, in any

state, under any government.

Mistake 2: Trying to do everything for everyone at once. Start with a narrow niche.

Mistake 3: Introducing paid services before you've built an audience. Gain people first.

Example.
Avito started small. Initially, it was just classifieds, then they added categories, then paid services, then delivery. It's a giant now, but at its core, it's still based on the same simple idea: a meeting place for buyers and sellers.

Question:
In what niche can I create my own classifieds board?

Practical task.
Think about a niche where people are actively buying and selling, but there is no good platform.
Write a plan for creating such a board.

MAIN:
There is no need to reinvent Avito.
We need to find our niche and make Avito fit into it. 

Chapter 5.12 Group 2. Marketplaces

The essence of the phenomenon.
A more structured version of classifieds. There are clear categories, packaging standards, logistics, and returns. This is the next level of trade organization. Examples: Wildberries, Ozon, Amazon, AliExpress, eBay.

Operating principle.
Unlike classifieds, a marketplace takes on more responsibility: moderation, product quality, logistics, and returns.
For this he takes a higher percentage, but the trust of buyers is also higher.

What does it consist of?
Differences from bulletin boards.

  1. Moderation.The classifieds board is minimal. The marketplace is strict.
  2. Quality of goods.Classifieds - any. Marketplace - verified.
  3. Logistics.A classifieds board is where the seller is. A marketplace is where the platform helps.
  4. Returns.Classifieds—the seller decides. Marketplace—the platform regulates.
  5. Customer trust.Classifieds - average. Marketplace - high.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a narrow niche(no need to compete with Wildberries right away).
  2. Find your first sellers and help them with their first sales.
  3. Ensure quality.Check products, collect reviews.
  4. Connect convenient payment and delivery.
  5. Scale to other niches.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that a marketplace is complicated and expensive. You can start small.

Mistake 2: Not ensuring quality and losing trust. One bad product can ruin a reputation.

Mistake 3: Charging too high a commission at the start, scaring off sellers.

Example.
Wildberries started as a small online clothing store. Gradually, they added other sellers, then more, then launched their own delivery service, and then became the number one marketplace in Russia.

Question:
What kind of niche marketplace could you create?

Practical task.
Explore any marketplace as a seller.
Calculate the commissions.
Think about a niche where there is demand but no good marketplace.

MAIN:
Don't build a store. Build a place where stores can trade.

Chapter 5.13 Group 2. Uber-like platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
Platforms that connect customers and service providers in real time. The key feature is immediacy and geolocation. Clients want to receive services here and now, not in a week.

Operating principle.
The client opens the app, sees nearby providers, selects them, and orders. The provider receives the order, arrives, and completes it. The platform takes a commission. Everything happens in real time, based on location.

But the main secret of explosive growthSuch platforms provide an opportunity for ordinary people to quickly start earning money.
Drivers don't have to go through an interview, wait for a salary once a month, or look for clients on their own.
He turned on the app and is already receiving orders.
The money arrives immediately or the next day.
This speed and ease of entry attract hundreds of thousands of performers. And where there are many performers, orders are fulfilled quickly. Where there is fast fulfillment, clients flock to.
The spiral begins: more drivers→faster delivery→more passengers→Even more drivers. This spiral is fueled not just by convenience, but by real money, which the contractor sees today.

What does it consist of?
Examples of Uber-like platforms.

  1. Uber, Yandex.Taxi— Service: trips. Sellers: drivers. Buyers: passengers.
  2. Delivery Club, UberEats— Service: food delivery. Sellers: restaurants, couriers. Buyers: hungry people.
  3. YouDo, Profi.ru— service: services. Sellers: craftsmen (plumbers, cleaners). Buyers: customers.
  4. Citylink (delivery)— Service: delivery of goods. Sellers: couriers. Buyers: buyers.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find a niche where people want to get a service quickly and right now.
  2. Start small:One district, one service, 5 performers.
  3. Involve the first party(for example, performers) by promising orders.
  4. Bring in the other side(customers) with the promise of fast service.
  5. Automate order distribution.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that such a business requires millions in development costs. You can start with a simple chat or spreadsheet.

Mistake 2: Not solving the "chicken and egg" problem (who to hire first). If there are no contractors, the clients won't come. If there are no clients, the contractors won't come.

Mistake 3: Launching across the entire city at once and failing to handle the logistics. Start with one district.

Example.
Uber started with one car in one city. They hired a few drivers and began attracting passengers through a simple website. Once they reached critical mass, they expanded to other cities.

Question:
What service do people want to receive instantly in your city?

Practical task.
Consider whether there's a problem finding a particular service in your city. Could you create a small, local version of Uber for this service?

MAIN:
Fast, here, now – that’s what people are willing to pay more for.

Chapter 5.14 Group 2. Employment platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
Connecting employers and job seekers. A two-way model: employers pay to post job openings, and job seekers receive free access.
It's an eternal market - people will always be looking for work, and companies will always be looking for employees.

Operating principle.
Employers post job openings, search for candidates, and pay the platform for this service. Job seekers post resumes, search for jobs, and use the platform for free (sometimes there are paid services for job seekers, such as resume promotion or highlighting).

What does it consist of?
Examples of employment platforms.

  1. HeadHunter— Who pays: employers. Features: the largest database in Russia.
  2. SuperJob— Who pays: employers. Features: there are free vacancies.
  3. LinkedIn— Who pays: employers + job seekers (premium). Features: a social network for professionals.
  4. Rabota.ru— Who pays: employers. Specifics: mass market segment.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a niche(IT, medicine, construction, specific city).
  2. Build a database of employers(You can start for free).
  3. Build a database of job seekers(advertising on social networks, in educational institutions).
  4. Introduce paid services for employers.
  5. Add additional services(testing, evaluation of candidates).

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking the market is already divided among giants. There are giants, but there's room for smaller niches.

Mistake 2: Making the interface too complicated. People come for simplicity.

Mistake 3: Not monitoring the quality of job postings. Too much spam kills trust.

Example.
The small town lacked a convenient platform for job searching. Employers posted ads on lampposts, and job seekers visited businesses with paper resumes. The process took weeks.

A local enthusiast created a VKontakte group called "Jobs in Our City." He collected job openings and posted them in one place. Within the first three months, he had 100 subscribers. After six months, he had 1,000. Employers began sending him job openings themselves, asking him to post them faster.

The enthusiast introduced a fee—200 rubles per post. Within a year, the group had grown to 10,000 subscribers. It became the city's main recruitment channel. The enthusiast earned 50,000 rubles a month, working two hours a day.

He didn't create anything new - he simply collected in one place what was scattered throughout the city.

Question:
Is there a problem with finding work or employees in your city or in your profession?

Practical task.
Explore HeadHunter as an employer.
See how much it costs to post a vacancy.
Consider whether you can create a cheaper or more specialized alternative.

MAIN:
Employers are looking for employees, employees are looking for jobs.
Bring them together and make money. It's an eternal market.

Chapter 5.15 Group 2. Freelance Exchanges

The essence of the phenomenon.
Specialized platforms for remote work. Clients seek contractors, and contractors seek orders. This is a market for temporary projects and tasks, not permanent employment.

Operating principle.
A client posts a task (write text, design a logo, create a website). Contractors respond, offer a price and deadline. The client chooses. The platform takes a commission from the contractor or the client (or both).

What does it consist of?
Examples of freelance exchanges.

  1. Kwork— Specialization: any services. Commission: fixed.
  2. Upwork— Specialization: international freelancing. Commission: percentage of the transaction.
  3. Freelance.ru— Specialization: Russian-speaking freelance. Commission: percentage of the transaction.
  4. Fl.ru— Specialization: Russian-language freelancing. Commission: percentage + paid account.
  5. Freelancer.com— Specialization: international. Commission: percentage of the transaction.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a narrow specialization(designers, programmers, copywriters, translators).
  2. Recruit your first performers(You can invite freelance friends).
  3. Get your first customers(social media advertising, contextual advertising).
  4. Ensure the security of transactions(escrow accounts, guarantees).
  5. Scale to other niches.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Making the registration system too complicated. The easier the login, the more people will sign up.

Mistake 2: Not ensuring security. Clients are afraid to pay, and contractors are afraid of not getting paid.

Mistake 3: Charging too high a commission at the start. A low commission and high turnover are better.

Example.
Kwork started as a fixed-price microservices exchange. They created a very simple interface: any service costs 500, 1000, or 1500 rubles. This attracted many customers because it was simple and straightforward. It's now one of the largest exchanges in Russia.

Question:
What area of ​​freelancing lacks a good marketplace?

Practical task.
Register as a freelancer on any freelance exchange.
Learn the working mechanism and commissions.
Think about what kind of narrow exchange you could create.

MAIN:
Become a bridge between those who can do it and those who need it. Freelancers come and go, but the marketplace remains, and take your cut from every deal.

Chapter 5.16 Group 2. Financial Platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
A vast class of two-way platforms where buyers and sellers of money, assets, and currencies meet. This is the pinnacle of the financial world. Money is always needed, turnover is huge, commissions are small, but due to volume, profits are enormous.

Operating principle.
Financial platforms bring together those who have money (depositors, investors) and those who need money (borrowers, companies, traders).
The platform charges a commission for transactions, exchanges, and storage.

What does it consist of?
Types of financial platforms.

  1. Banks— Sber, Tinkoff. Sellers: depositors (lend money). Buyers: borrowers (take money). Commission: difference in percentage.
  2. Cryptocurrency exchanges— Binance, Bybit. Sellers: crypto sellers. Buyers: crypto buyers. Commission: % of the transaction.
  3. Stock exchanges— Moscow Exchange, NYSE. Sellers: stock sellers. Buyers: stock buyers. Commission: % of the transaction.
  4. Crowdfunding— Kickstarter, Planeta. Sellers: project authors. Buyers: sponsors. Commission: % of the raised amount.
  5. P2P exchangers— LocalBitcoins. Sellers: Currency sellers. Buyers: Currency buyers. Commission: % of the transaction.

Why is this the top?
Money is always needed. Demand for financial services never declines—not in times of crisis, not in times of prosperity. The turnover on such platforms is enormous: billions of dollars flow through exchanges, banks, and currency exchangers daily.

The commission can be quite small—0.1-1 percent. But due to the enormous volumes, even such a tiny fraction can translate into billions in profit.
And here the network effect comes into play: the more users a platform has, the more liquid it is. Sellers benefit from going to places where there are a lot of buyers. Buyers benefit from going to places where there are a lot of sellers. The platform grows, fueling its own growth.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a niche(P2P transfers, currency exchange, investments, crowdfunding).
  2. Obtain all necessary licenses(this is the most difficult part).
  3. Ensure safety(protection against hacking, insurance of funds).
  4. Make a user-friendly interface.
  5. Attract users(the first ones can be subsidized).

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that financial platforms are only for giants. There are thousands of small P2P platforms and local exchanges.

Mistake 2: Ignoring regulatory requirements. Finance is the most regulated industry.

Mistake 3: Skimping on security. One leak ruins your reputation.

Example.
There are thousands of small P2P platforms, local exchanges, and crowdfunding platforms. You don't necessarily need to build a second Binance. You can create a local exchange in your city or a P2P platform for transfers between your peers.

Question:
What financial niche could you master?

Practical task.
Learn how Binance or any crypto exchange makes money.
Consider whether it is possible to create a small P2P platform in your country or city.

MAIN:
Money loves silence. But it loves it even more when it's combined with other money.

Chapter 5.17 Group 2. Platforms for creativity and content

The essence of the phenomenon.
Two-sided models where content creators meet consumers or buyers. Creativity becomes a commodity, and the platform takes a cut. Photographers, musicians, designers, and writers can all sell their work through such platforms.

Operating principle.
Authors upload their works (photos, videos, music, design).
Users purchase access to these works (download, watch, listen) or order new ones.
The platform charges a commission for arranging the meeting and for the security of the transaction.

What does it consist of?
Examples of creative platforms.

  1. Photo banks— Shutterstock, Adobe Stock. Sellers: photographers. Buyers: designers, editorial offices.
  2. Music platforms— Spotify, Apple Music. Sellers: musicians. Buyers: listeners.
  3. Video platforms— YouTube, Vimeo. Sellers: videographers. Buyers: viewers.
  4. Design platforms— Behance, Dribbble. Sellers: designers. Buyers: clients.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a niche(photography, music, design, video, texts).
  2. Recruit the first authors(You can invite friends and offer free accommodation).
  3. Get your first customers(advertising on social networks, in professional communities).
  4. Provide convenient search and filters.
  5. Add a payment and licensing system.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that creative platforms are only YouTube or Spotify. There's room for niches.

Mistake 2: Not protecting copyright. If authors are afraid their work will be stolen, they won't come.

Mistake 3: Creating a complex deposit and withdrawal system. The simpler, the better.

Example.
The musician had been writing tracks for years, but no one knew about them. He posted them on social media—his friends liked them, but they didn't buy them.

He found a music platform where creators upload tracks, and bloggers, advertisers, and video makers buy them for their projects. Prices range from $5 to $50 per track.
The musician uploaded 20 of his compositions. A month later, three of them were sold. Six months later, he was earning passive income from sales.

The platform takes a 30% commission, but it does the most important thing: connect the artist and the buyer. Without it, the artist would remain unknown.

Creative platforms are turning talent into a commodity, connecting creators with those willing to pay.

Question:
Which creative people in your circle need a platform to sell their work?

Practical task.
If you are a creator, post your work on such platforms.
If you are an organizer, think about what niche platform is missing.

MAIN:
Artists paint, musicians play, platforms sell. Everyone should do their own thing.

Chapter 5.18 Group 2. Gaming and entertainment platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
Casinos, bookmakers, binary options. Here, too, there are two sides: players (buyers of emotion and hope) and owners (sellers of probability). From a business model perspective, this is pure probability mathematics.

Operating principle.
The platform owner creates a game with a fixed mathematical expectation in their favor. Players place bets on the outcome of sporting events. Thousands of people participate, each paying a small fee. The winner takes the prize, and the platform keeps a commission for organizing the event.

The math works in such a way that the sum of collected contributions is always slightly greater than the amount paid out to the winners. This difference is the platform's revenue. It doesn't matter who exactly won today: a newbie or an experienced player, one person or ten. In the long run, the platform always wins because the rules of the game are designed with a mathematical advantage in its favor.

This is called "mathematical expectation" and is always positive for the establishment.

What does it consist of?
Types of gaming and entertainment platforms.

  1. Online casino— slot machines, roulette, cards.
    Examples: Joycasino, Pin-up.
  2. Bookmakers— sports betting. Examples: 1xBet, Fonbet.
  3. Binary options— betting on price movements (prohibited in many countries).
  4. Poker rooms— players play against each other, and the platform takes a commission. Example: PokerStars.
  5. Lotteries— a prize draw. Example: Gosloto.

Step-by-step diagram (purely technical, not a guide to action):

  1. Obtain a license in a jurisdiction where it is permitted (Curacao, Malta, Gibraltar).
  2. Develop a platform (or buy a ready-made solution).
  3. Set up payment gateways.
  4. Attract traffic (usually through aggressive advertising and affiliate programs).
  5. Constantly monitor the math to avoid going into the negative.

Possible mistakes (for those who decide to do it).

Mistake 1: Failure to obtain a license. Criminal liability.

Mistake 2: Not protecting the platform from hacking and fraud.

Mistake 3: Not calculating the math and ending up in the red.

Example.
The slot machine is programmed to have a 95 percent return rate. This means that for every 100 rubles players deposit, 95 rubles are returned as winnings, and 5 rubles remain with the player.

This doesn't mean every player gets back 95 rubles. One might lose a thousand, another might win a hundred thousand. But thousands of players and millions of rubles pass through the machine every day. Over the course of a month, that 5% translates into hundreds of thousands in net profit.

The machine owner doesn't have to wonder, "Will I get lucky today or not?" They just know the number. 5% of the turnover is theirs. Always. The math won't fail.

Question:
Do you understand why the casino always wins?
Are you ready to take responsibility for such a business?

Practical task.
Just take note of the existence of this model.
Whether to use it or not is a personal choice.
But you need to know about it in order to fully understand the hierarchy of business models.

MAIN:
In games of chance, the platform owner always wins in the long run.

Chapter 5.19 Group 2. Logistics and transport platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
P2P delivery of mail, cargo, and packages. The platform connects those who need to ship and those who are ready to deliver. This is a huge market, because the need to move things and people will always exist.
*P2P(Peer-to-Peer) — a “person-to-person” model, without intermediaries.*

Operating principle.
The sender creates an order (pick up the parcel there, deliver it here).
The performer (courier, driver) takes the order, picks it up, and delivers it.
The platform takes a commission and provides security and tracking.

What does it consist of?
Examples of logistics and transport platforms.

  1. Postal operators— Russian Post, SDEK, DHL. Sellers: logistics companies. Buyers: shippers.
  2. Courier services— Yandex.Delivery, Delivery Club. Sellers: couriers. Buyers: online stores, restaurants.
  3. Cargo transportation— Yandex.Delivery (freight), freight exchanges. Sellers: drivers with trucks. Buyers: shippers.
  4. Car sharing— Delimobil, YouDrive. Sellers: car owners. Buyers: renters.
  5. Passenger transportation— BlaBlaCar. Sellers: drivers. Buyers: passengers.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose a niche(city delivery, intercity transportation, ride-sharing, carsharing).
  2. Find the first performers(drivers, couriers).
  3. Make a simple ordering app.
  4. Attract your first customers(You can start with a small business in your area).
  5. Ensure security and tracking.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that logistics is complicated and requires huge warehouses and a fleet of vehicles. No, it's not. You can be an aggregator without all that.

Mistake 2: Not checking contractors. Theft and damage to cargo. A rating system and insurance are needed.

Mistake 3: Not providing 24/7 customer support. In logistics, problems happen at any time.

Example.
BlaBlaCar started as a simple idea: connect drivers traveling to another city with passengers going there too. No cars, no warehouses. Just a platform. Today, it's an international service with millions of users.

Question:
What logistical problems are there in your city?

Practical task.
Think about what people often forward and how you can simplify this process through the platform.

MAIN:
The logistics platform does not own transport – it connects those who travel with those who need it

Chapter 5.20 Group 2. Other double-sided platforms

The essence of the phenomenon.
Other types of platforms, not included in the main categories, operate on the same principle—connecting sellers and buyers, supply and demand. If two groups of people want to find each other, a platform can be a solution.

Operating principle.
Your imagination is your only limitation. Where there's a mismatch between supply and demand, there can be a platform. People are looking for housing, a soulmate, a tutor, a dog for mating, rare spare parts—all these are niches for platforms.

What does it consist of?
Examples of other two-sided platforms.

  1. Acquaintance— Tinder, Badoo, Mamba. People looking for people.
  2. Auctions— eBay. Sellers negotiate with buyers.
  3. Transaction guarantorsEscrow services hold money until the terms are met.
  4. Real estate— CIAN, Domofond, Avito Real Estate. Owners and tenants/buyers.
  5. Car sharing— Delimobil. Car owners and renters.
  6. Education— Profi.ru (tutors), Courses. Teachers and students.
  7. Animal mating— animal owners are looking for partners.
  8. Rare goods— collectors' forums. Sellers and buyers of rare items.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Watch.Observe the people around you. What are they looking for? What are they offering?
  2. Look for inconsistencies.Where is there demand but no supply? Where is there supply but no demand?
  3. Ask.Ask your friends what problems they face when searching.
  4. Check it out.Make a simple version (a bulletin board in Telegram) and see if the traffic goes.
  5. Scale.If it works, develop it.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Limiting your imagination. Thinking that "everything has already been invented." No. The world is vast.

Mistake 2: Thinking that if the niche is small, it's not worth starting. A small niche means there's no competition.

Mistake 3: Making the platform too complex at the start. Start simple.

Example.
In one city with a population of 300,000, dog owners couldn't find partners to breed their pets. Ads were posted on lampposts and in veterinary clinics—information quickly got lost, making it difficult to find the right breed. Owners spent weeks searching, often to no avail.

One of the owners created a VKontakte group called "Dog Mating in Our City." He began collecting ads, sorting them by breed, and publishing contact information. He worked for free for the first few months, building an audience. Within six months, the group had 2,000 members.

Owners began sending ads themselves, asking for them to be posted faster. The creator introduced a fee for featured posts—100 rubles per post. Advertisers—pet food stores, veterinary clinics, and pet grooming salons—also wanted to be featured.

Two years later, the group has 15,000 members. The founder earns 30,000 rubles a month from paid ads and advertising. He doesn't produce any products or provide any services—he simply connects those seeking help with those who can provide it.

Question:
What other human needs can be connected through the platform?

Practical task.
Observe the people around you. What are they looking for? What are they offering?
Where is the disconnect? This is a potential niche for the platform.

MAIN:
The world is full of people looking for something. Help them find it.

Chapter 5.21 Interesting Business Models from Group 7
(Separate analysis)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Although Group 7 is the simplest, it contains interesting models that deserve attention. They allow you to earn money in a variety of ways, even without leaving your one-dimensional niche. These aren't platforms, but they're still worth exploring.

Operating principle.
You don't have to be a platform to be interesting. Even simple niches have room for creativity. Some models from Group 7 can be very profitable if implemented correctly.

What does it consist of?

1. Specialized Associations, Prizes, Awards. 
A status-based business. People pay for membership, for the right to be called "the best."
Income comes from membership fees from all participants plus money from sponsors.
Example: Association of Best Chefs, Golden Site Award.

2. Online platforms with content (subscription). 
People pay monthly for access to content.
Revenue is the number of subscribers multiplied by the subscription price.
Example: Netflix, Patreon, Boosti, private clubs.

3. Delivery networks. 
Business at speed. You collect your goods and deliver them in 15-30 minutes.
Income is the revenue from orders minus all expenses: couriers, packaging, warehouse rent.
Example: "Samokat", "Yandex.Lavka".

4. Payment system centers. 
You become a "cash register" for other businesses.
Income is a small percentage of all the money that has passed through your system.
Example: payment aggregators, terminals.

5. Search engines and social networks. 
They live off advertising and data.
Revenue depends on three things: how many people use the service, how much time they spend there, and how much advertisers are willing to pay for their attention.
Example: Google, Yandex, VKontakte.

6. Community buys and joint purchases. 
People join forces to buy goods together at wholesale prices.
The organizer's income is a share of the discount received by the group.
Example: a joint purchase of food products, a joint purchase of a course.

7. Ticket offices. 
Sale of tickets for events, transportation.
Revenue is a commission on each ticket sold. The more tickets sold, the higher the revenue.
Example: box offices, online ticket aggregators.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Select a model,which is closer to you in terms of skills and interests.
  2. Think about how you can adapt it to your niche.
  3. Write a plan for 3 months.What will you do first, what will you do later?
  4. Launch MVP.Check if the model works in your niche.
  5. Scale it up,if it works.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not seeing that even simple niches have room for creativity. Thinking that if you're in Group 7, you "can't come up with anything interesting."

Mistake 2: Copying a model without adapting it to your niche. Taking someone else's recipe and blindly repeating it.

Mistake 3: Thinking that if a model is simple, there's no money in it. Money is everywhere where there's a need.

Example.
There were about 200 designers working in city N, from freelancers to studios. No one knew who was the best. Clients chose at random, and designers couldn't raise their prices due to a lack of reputation.

One designer decided to organize a citywide "Golden Makeup" award. He gathered sponsors: a printing house, a tablet store, and a design school. He made the statues out of plexiglass—the budget was 15,000 rubles.

Participation in the awards cost 2,000 rubles per application. Fifty designers entered, earning 100,000 rubles in fees. Sponsors awarded an additional 80,000 rubles for logo placement on posters and mentions on social media.

The mayor's office and local media were invited to the ceremony. The winners received statuettes and certificates. A year later, the awards ceremony became a major event for city designers. The organizer spent two weeks launching the event and earned 150,000 rubles. Now, they hold the awards ceremony annually, spending three days a year on coordination. The rest of the time, the money just flows in—designers prepare applications, and sponsors call.

Question:
Which of these models do you prefer?
What can be applied in your niche?

Practical task.
Choose one of the models and think about how you can adapt it to your business.
Write a plan for 3 months.

MAIN:
You don't have to be a platform to be interesting.
Even in simple niches there is room for creativity.

Chapter 5.22 The Lamp You Build Yourself

The essence of the phenomenon.
I have seen many times how different companiesfor yearspromote their "one-sided" businesses. Even mega-large corporations. They're all in the same boat.for decades, without realizing thatbecame hostagestheir own lamp—the one they themselves created. They are Genies, locked in their own lamps.
The larger and more successful the business, the stronger the walls of the lamp and the more difficult it is to escape from it.

Operating principle.
Imagine the Genie from the fairy tale. He lives in a lamp. Someone rubs the lamp—the Genie comes out and grants wishes. And then goes back inside. He's not free. He's bound to the lamp.

It's the same in business. You create your own business—you build your own lamp. When the lamp is small, you can easily come and go. But the bigger it gets, the more it demands your presence. You can't take a vacation. You can't get sick. You can't turn off your phone. You're a genie trapped in your own lamp. You created it to serve you, but in the end, you became its servant.

You can live your whole life inside this lamp: getting up at 6 a.m., spinning, running, solving problems, putting out fires. Or you can one day rewrite the rules. Build not just a lamp, but a system in which the lamp runs itself, and you are free.

What does it consist of?
Comparison of two roles.

  1. A genie trapped in a lampHe works 24/7, solves all problems himself, doesn't delegate, and can't step away from his work. The lamp (business) keeps him inside. The result: there's business, but no freedom.
  2. Free lamp creator.He builds the system so it works without him. He describes processes, hires a team, automates, and delegates. The lamp generates income without requiring his constant presence. The result: business, freedom.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Realize that you are a Genie trapped in your own lamp.If you do everything yourself, can't take more than a week off on vacation, are constantly putting out fires, and your business never lets you go - you're inside a lamp.
  2. Start building a system, not just working.Describe processes, delegate, automate. Stop being the only one who keeps everything on himself.
  3. Delegate everything that can be delegated.Hire assistants, employees, partners. Let the lamp work for them, and not just for you.
  4. Automate everything that can be automated.Use programs, bots, neural networks. The less manual labor, the less you're needed inside the lamp.
  5. Gradually remove yourself from the operating system.Your job is to manage from the outside, not to live within. When you stop being an indispensable performer, you can step out of the lamp and become free.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that's the way it should be. That "a real businessman is always inside his business." No. A real businessman is someone who can leave, but the business continues to thrive.

Mistake 2: Not seeing that you can live differently. Not realizing that there are systems that work without you.

Mistake 3: Building a system without removing yourself from it. Leaving yourself as the key link, without which everything collapses. Then you've simply built a stronger lamp and stayed inside.

Example.
An entrepreneur opened a chain of coffee shops. He worked in each one himself, overseeing purchases, training baristas, and resolving customer conflicts. The business was profitable, but whenever he went on vacation, sales dropped and quality suffered. He worked 12 hours a day and believed there was no other way. He built a large, beautiful lamp and stayed inside it.

The second opened one coffee shop and wrote out all the processes: purchasing, recipes, service standards, shift schedules. He hired a manager and trained him. Then he opened a second, a third, a tenth. The managers reported weekly. He himself came once a month to check that everything was running according to standard.

Ten years later, the first was still working 12 hours a day in his coffee shop. The second had left operations, taken on new projects, and the chain continued to grow without his daily involvement. He didn't live inside a lamp. He built a lamp that worked on its own.

Question:
Are you a Genie trapped in your own lamp, or a free creator who can leave and the lamp continues to work?

Practical task.
Evaluate your current business.
Can you build a platform on it? A classifieds board? An aggregator? A toolbox? An ecosystem?
Write a 3-year plan on how to get out of the lamp and stay free.

MAIN:
The genie lives in a lamp and can't leave it. A free person builds a lamp that works without him. Choose who to be. 

Chapter 5.23 The Way of the Warrior: One Norm, One Discipline, One Freedom

The essence of the phenomenon.
This book has it all: laws, systems, algorithms, tables, checklists. This is a map for the Architect—someone who wants to build their empire, hire people, create systems, and take responsibility.

But there is another way.

It doesn't require you to be a boss. It doesn't require you to manage others. It doesn't require you to delve into complex structures and bear the burden of someone else's responsibility.

He requires only one thing:Do one simple thing every day. Over and over. And over.

This is the Path of the Warrior. The Path of the Pilgrim. The Path of one who has chosen not to possess, but to act and follow their Path (with what they have, here and now, without unnecessary complications, with minimalism in everything).

Operating principle.
Two paths to the same summit. Imagine a mountain. At the summit lies freedom. Income. Time. Peace. Two paths lead to the mountain.

The Way of the Architect.Builds his own trail. Designs, hires, manages. Responsible for everything. Carries the maps, the tools, the plans, the burden of responsibility. The owner. The creator. The architect.
The price: power demands sacrifice. You're always on duty.
Result: if you reach it, the mountain is yours.

The Way of the Warrior.Follows a prepared path. Meets the quota. Repeats. Accountable only for his own actions. Carries only a staff and a compass. Warrior. Pilgrim. Scholar.
The price: freedom requires discipline. Every day is the same.
Result: if you get there, you are free.

What does it consist of?
What is the "Way of the Warrior" in business?

It's not about "being the boss." It's aboutto be a participant in a system that works without you.

You choose a ready-made business model in which:

  • The product already exists (no need to invent it)
  • The system is already running (no configuration required)
  • Payments are automatic (no need to count or distribute)
  • The mechanism of self-reproduction is built in (people can create people)

Your job is not to manage. Your job is todo your quota every day.

How do you know if you're on your path?

Ask yourself three questions:

  1. Is it easier for me to do my own business than to manage someone else's?If yes, you are a Warrior, not a Master.
  2. Do I feel free when I am not responsible for anyone?If yes, you don’t need power, you need autonomy.
  3. Am I willing to do the same thing every day, for years, if it brings results?If yes, you have the main quality for this path.

If you answered “yes” to all three questions, you have found your path.

Step-by-step diagram (Warrior norm):

Every day:

  1. Make 300 contacts (messages, calls, meetings)
  2. Show your model 30 times (tell, invite)
  3. Conduct one school (teach, help, inspire)

Every month:

  • Count the numbers: how many contacts, how many came, how many are left
  • Make sure that the standard is met (or not met - and draw conclusions)

Every quarter:

  • Adjusting the course: what works better, what works worse
  • Change the site if necessary
  • But do not change the norm

The path of the Warrior is based on only one thing: your discipline.

No one will control you.
Nobody will demand.
No one will punish you if you go astray.
You will be alone. With your own norm.
And with business mathematics that doesn't lie.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1 - the temptation to "do less today, catch up tomorrow." You won't catch up. 300 contacts a day isrhythm, not the amount. If you make 150 today, you won't make 450 tomorrow. You'll just mess up the system.

Mistake 2: The temptation to "improve quality, not quantity." Quality grows from quantity. You can't learn sales by making 10 calls. You can't learn persuasion by holding 5 meetings.

Mistake 3: The temptation to "start on Monday. On the new month. On the new year." No. Start today. Tomorrow will be an excuse to put it off again.

Example.
Two travelers were walking towards the mountain.

FirstHe built his own road. He chopped down trees, removed stones, built bridges. Sometimes others joined him, and he taught them. Years passed. He built a strong, beautiful road. But he himself made little progress toward the summit. But many who followed his path did.

SecondThe traveler found an old path that had been trodden before him. He set a rule for himself: every day—a thousand steps. No more and no less. Every day. Without fail. Three years later, he reached the summit.

Question:
Do you already know which road is yours?

If you want to build, manage, take responsibility, and leave behind something that will outlive you, choose the path of the Architect. It's longer, harder, but you will become the Master.

If you want simplicity, discipline, and freedom, choose the path of the Warrior. It doesn't require talent, connections, or start-up capital. It demands only one thing: to do your quota every day. And not to stray from the path/Way.

MAIN:
You can build an empire. Or you can just walk.
Don't choose what's cool. Choose what's yours.

Chapter 5.24 THE BIG SECRET. Traffic is King
(short - full inChapter 2.63)

The essence of the phenomenon.
If you can create a flow of people (traffic), you can do anything!
The rest will take care of itself. The law of large numbers and double Pareto will do 90% of the work for you. Traffic is the only parameter that has no ceiling.

Operating principle.
You create content. Content attracts people. People log in. Then the math works: out of 100 people, 20 will become clients, out of 20 clients, 4 will become leaders, and those 4 will account for 80% of your total turnover. It doesn't matter what the product is. It doesn't matter what the niche is.
If there are 10,000 people per month at the input, there will be results at the output.
Traffic cures everything: product errors, weak leaders, lack of money.

---

Example.
Anna was an excellent English tutor. Her students adored her, and her results were excellent. But she found new students only through word of mouth—two or three a month. Her income was modest, though her teaching quality was excellent.

Her colleague Olga knew English at an intermediate level, but she actively blogged: she posted short videos explaining complex rules, shared life hacks for memorizing words, and showed off her lesson preparation. Her following grew. Within six months, she had a waiting list of students, and her lessons had doubled in price.

Anna was still waiting for word of mouth. Olga had created a flow. An average product with a flow beat a great product without a flow.

Question:
What if tomorrow you learned how to attract 10,000 people a month on any topic?

Practical task.
Choose one platform.
Set up the AI. Create 30 posts (one per day). Publish.
Analyze which ones came in.
Do this for 3 months in a row.

MAIN:
Traffic cures everything. No sales – little traffic. No leads – little traffic. No money – little traffic. Drive traffic – and build your freedom. 


SECTION 5 SUMMARY: SCALING

The main conclusion.

The hierarchy of business models is a map of your future. It consists of eight groups, from the simplest to the absolute pinnacle. The higher you climb, the more you:

  • more resilient to crises
  • less dependent on one product
  • closer to "eternal" money that flows constantly

Three main lessons of the section:

  1. Don't confuse scaling scenarios.Gas, model, business - each has its own moment.
  2. Hierarchy is a map, not a ladder.You can enter at any level.
  3. The highest power lies not in the creation of value, but in control of the flow of money.Group 0 stands above all, because without it, no one works.

Final thought.

Even if you are now in group 7 (selling pies), you can:

  • build a network of points (group 7, but on a larger scale)
  • create a franchise (closer to group 5)
  • make a platform for delivery (group 2)
  • gather an ecosystem around it (group 1)
  • and one day think about controlling the money itself (group 0)

MAIN:
Group 7 is normal. Group 1 is cool. Group 0 is power. But the main thing is to move up. 

SECTION 6. STEP-BY-STEP ALGORITHM

Introduction to the section. Map and legs

We've covered 63 laws of business mathematics, 9 chapters on communities, 10 on leverage, and 23 on scaling. You know how assets and liabilities work, what critical mass is, and why self-replication is the only path to freedom.

Now the most important question:What to do right now?

The answer doesn't start with action. It starts with a plan.

The most successful people aren't the most talented or the luckiest. They're the most consistent. Their secret is simple: a realistic plan and methodical adherence to it over a period of two to three years.

So the first thing you'll do in this section is create a Global Plan. You'll break your life down into sectors. Define the ideal end result for each. Set daily quotas. Set milestones.

And then you just start walking.

This section contains no new theory. Only a clear step-by-step algorithm. From point "zero" to point "freedom." Expanded by weeks, months, and years.

Knowledge without action is dead weight. Action without a plan is chaos. A plan without checkpoints is a dream. Here you'll find it all: plan, action, control.

MAIN:
The secret to success isn't talent or luck. It's a realistic plan and methodical adherence to it. Two or three years, and you won't recognize your life. 

Chapter 6.0 Global Plan.
How to build a life according to CON-TON-FON

The essence of the phenomenon.
CON-TON-FON isn't just about business. It's a universal principle for building any system, including your own life.

You can apply it to one area—health, finances, relationships. Or you can bring it all together and build a Global Plan in which all sectors work in concert.

Operating principle.
The global plan is your life's CON. You define the IFR (ideal end result) in each sector and combine them into a single picture.

TON is a daily snapshot of your tasks. What you do today to advance in all sectors simultaneously. Don't rush between areas, but take a small step in each one every day.

BACKGROUND is maintaining the entire system in working order. Regularly checking checkpoints, adjusting the plan, and maintaining habits.

The planning horizon is at least two years. This is the deadline within which any program in any sector can be developed.

What does it consist of?
The global plan is built on five elements:

  1. IFR (Ideal Final Result).A specific, measurable goal in each sector. Not "become healthier," but "weigh 80 kg, sleep 7 hours, exercise 3 times a week."
  2. Methods and tools.What exactly will you use to achieve your ICR? Tabata, fasting, vitamins based on your blood sugar levels, a sleep schedule—or other tools.
  3. Requirements for yourself.What kind of person should you become to achieve and maintain results? What skills should you develop? What habits should you instill?
  4. Daily norms.Specific actions for each day. What and how much you do to move toward your ICR.
  5. Checkpoints.Stopping and review points: after 1 month, 3, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months. At each point, an honest analysis is performed: what has been accomplished, what is lagging behind, and where correction is needed.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Write out all the sectors of your life.Health, finances, relationships, work, self-development, rest – everything that is important to you.
  2. For each sector, determine the IFR.What do you want to see in two years? Write specifically, in numbers, without vague formulations.
  3. Select methods and tools for each ICR.What will help you? Don't copy someone else's—choose something that suits your personality type, your rhythm of life, and your resources.
  4. Determine what you should become.What habits should I develop? What should I give up? What should I change about myself?
  5. Set daily norms.What do you do every day in each sector? Even if it's just 15 minutes, the key is consistency.
  6. Set up checkpoints.Mark the following on your calendar: 1 month, 3, 6, 12, 18, 24 months from the start. At each point, analyze and adjust.
  7. Proceed according to plan.Don't wait for motivation. Habits will replace it.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Setting abstract goals. "I want more money," "I want to be healthy." Abstraction doesn't provide direction. Only concrete goals: "income 200,000 a month," "weight 75 kg."

Mistake 2: Trying to implement everything at once. Choose 1-2 sectors at the start. Once the habits in those sectors become established, add the next ones.

Mistake 3: Skipping checkpoints. Without them, a plan becomes a dream. A checkpoint is a moment of truth. Unpleasant, but necessary.

Mistake 4: Abandoning the plan at the first sign of a glitch. One missed deadline is no big deal. Two in a row is a reason to adjust, not panic.

Example.
At 35, Alexey decided it was time to take a more systematic approach to his life. He identified four areas: health, finances, relationships, and work.

For each, I determined the ICR for 2 years:

  • Health: weight 80 kg, sleep 7 hours, Tabata 3 times a week.
  • Finances: income 200 thousand per month, two sources.
  • Relationship: spend 10 days of vacation with my wife a year, have dinner together at least 5 times a week.
  • Work: launch your own project, leave employment.

I selected methods for each ICR. I wrote out daily norms. I set checkpoints.

He kept a journal for the first 30 days. It was difficult. But the habits stuck. A year later, he had lost 12 kg, launched his first project, and his relationship with his wife had become warmer. Two years later, he earned his own income, quit his job, and went on vacation with his family.
The global plan turned chaos into system.

Question:
What areas of your life require a Global Plan right now?
Which sector will you start with?

Practical task.
Take a piece of paper. Write down all the sectors of your life.
For each one, write an ICR for 2 years.
Then, for one sector (just one!), set a daily target for the next month.

MAIN:
