A global plan is the CON of your life. Daily standards are the TONE. Checkpoints are the BACKGROUND. Without a plan, you drift. With a plan, you consciously create your life.

Habits are a secret weaponThey replace motivation and make achieving goals natural rather than heroic.

BLOCK 1. PREPARATION (Week 1)

Step 1: Know your Archetype and Psychotype
(short - full inChapter 2.4)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Business is always about people. And people are built differently. There are two important things that determine who you are on a team and what kind of work will come easy to you and what will drain your soul. These are archetypes (deep roles) and personality types (speed and reaction style).

Operating principle.
If you give someone a job that doesn't suit their personality type, their productivity plummets. They'll struggle to do everything they do and quickly burn out. If the job matches their personality type, they'll work at full capacity without tiring.

*ImportantDon't try to remake yourself, but find a job that suits you.*

---

Example.
Maxim worked as an accountant. He crunched numbers, compiled reports, and filed tax returns. The job was stable, but every evening he left feeling drained. He thought it was just a job—that's how it should be.

At a corporate event, he accidentally found himself in the sales department. He enjoyed discussing strategies, persuading clients, and negotiating. He decided to give it a try. It turned out he was a sanguine person by nature; he enjoyed socializing, new challenges, and movement. Meanwhile, the accounting department, with its silence and routine, was pure drudgery.

He retrained as a sales manager. His income increased, and his evening fatigue disappeared. He realized that he could work less and earn more if he focused on his own business.

Question:
Is your current job or business a good fit for you?
Do you go there in the morning with desire or through force?

Practical task.
Take a piece of paper and honestly answer four questions: Who am I? How have I acted in the past? How will I act in the future? What are my strengths?

MAIN:
Conformity of the task to your nature = speed and ease.
Mismatch = internal sabotage, burnout and drain.
Know yourself and your right path.

Step 2. Cost of Living (Lifecost)
(short - full inChapter 2.3)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's one number every adult should know. It's the cost of your life. How much money you need each month just to live the way you do now. Food, housing, transportation, communications, clothing, health, entertainment. Everything you can't imagine your everyday life without.

Operating principle.
This figure becomes your baseline, below which you can't fall. If you earn less than this amount, you're living in the red and going into debt. If you earn just this much, you're surviving and not growing. If you earn more, you have capital for investment and growth. Without knowing your figure, you're living blind.

Example.
A young couple complained about not having enough money. Their salaries were 80,000 rubles a month, but by the end of the month, they had nothing. They couldn't figure out where the money was going. They decided to track every expense for a month.

It turned out that 12,000 rubles were spent on food delivery, 6,000 on taxis instead of the metro, 4,000 on coffee at coffee shops, and 3,000 on subscriptions that were rarely used. In total, 25,000 rubles a month simply disappeared without a trace.

They reassessed their spending: they started cooking at home, bought metro passes, and kept only those subscriptions they actually used. They started putting the money they saved aside. After a year, they had a five-month safety net. They stopped living paycheck to paycheck. And all that changed was that they simply began to see where their money was going.

Question:
Do you know your figure? Not approximately, but up to hundreds of rubles?

Practical task.
Keep track of your expenses for the month.
Just write down everything you spend.
At the end of the month, add up the total. This will be your actual life cost.

MAIN:
Lifecost is the value of your life. Without knowing it, you can't manage your money. Once you see the numbers, you can manage them.

Step 3. Self-reproduction
(short - full inChapter 2.61)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's only one way to build a business or asset that will operate without your constant involvement and provide true freedom: self-replication. This is the ability of a system (people, platform, or capital) to grow without a proportional increase in your personal efforts.

*Important.If your business model doesn't replicate itself, you've created a job, not an asset. You'll trade your time for money until you stop.*

Operating principle.
Self-replication is a mathematical transition from linear to nonlinear growth. Without it, the following formula applies:
Your income = Your hours x Your rate.The ceiling is 24 hours a day.

Self-reproduction changes the formula: 
Your income = other people's hours x their number x your share.
You don't sell your time - you get a share from the sale of someone else's.

The key mechanism is separating your income from your personal time. As long as these two values ​​are tightly linked, you're not the asset's owner, you're its prisoner. As soon as the connection is broken, you're free.

Example.
The appliance repairman worked alone. Clients were few, and his income was meager.

My nephew suggested, "I'll create a website and a social media group. I'll attract clients and arrange for outreach. You do the repairs. We'll split the cost."

A month later, the nephew had five student assistants. They also sought out clients, took their cut, and gave a portion to the nephew. The mechanic simply repaired the machines. The nephew coordinated the network.

Within six months, they had 20 partners working for them. The master couldn't keep up—he hired a second master. Everyone was making money. The nephew had created a self-reproduction system: assistants found new assistants. The master had created work for himself. The difference was in the model.

Question:
Does your business model have a self-reproduction mechanism?
Which of the three?
Or did you just create a job for yourself?

Practical task.
Write out your current business model.
Determine whether it has self-reproduction.
If not, replace the model or supplement it with one of the three mechanisms.
If so, determine which of the three types works for you.
Make a 2-3 year plan for the development of this particular mechanism.

MAIN:
Three types of self-reproduction—three paths to freedom: people, the system, or capital. Choose yours. But without self-reproduction, you'll always work for money, rather than have money work for you.

BLOCK 2. LAUNCH (Month 1-3)

Step 4. MVP (Minimal Visible Product)
(short - full inChapter 2.30)

The essence of the phenomenon.
An MVP is a minimum viable product. It's the simplest, cheapest, and fastest version of your idea that you can show to people and test whether they need it. It's similar to the "5 units" technique. Before you build a skyscraper, build a small house and test whether the land is even worth building on.

Operating principle.
Your goal isn't to do it perfectly, but to get market feedback with minimal investment. Don't invest millions without testing demand. It's better to test quickly and make a mistake than to spend a year building and find out no one wants it.

---

Example.
Sergey wanted to open his own bakery. Instead of taking out a loan for rent and equipment (a million rubles at risk), he made a deal with a local cafe: he bakes the pies at home and sells them through the window.

The first three days—zero sales. If he'd rented the space right away, he'd have lost a million. As it is, it's only three days and the cost of the groceries.

Sergey wasn't upset, but began testing his hypotheses: he changed the recipe, added new fillings, and redesigned the packaging. Within a week, the pies were selling. Only then did he rent a space.

He wasn't building a business—he was testing ideas. Fast, cheap, no loans.

Question:
Do you test your ideas before investing in them?
Or are you hoping for the best?

Practical task.
If you have an idea, come up with an MVP and launch it within the next 7 days.

MAIN:
An MVP is a minimum viable product. Before investing millions, test the idea on a small budget. If you buy it, scale it up. If not, you saved money.

Step 5. The 3-Message Method
(short - full inChapter 2.48)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The most environmentally friendly and effective method of promotion on any platform (social media, messenger) for any business. It respects people's privacy but is guaranteed to spark dialogue.

Don't sell to strangers. First become acquainted, then sell.

Operating principle.
Standard direct sales: "Hi! Buy from me..." The person immediately shuts down and blocks the offer. The three-message method works differently: first you provide value, then you demonstrate expertise, and only then you make an offer.
A person approaches the decision himself.

---

Example.
A freelancer wanted to land a commission from a major client. Instead of sending a portfolio and a proposal, he used the three-message method.

First message (via LinkedIn): "I saw your website redesign project. The carousel on the homepage is an interesting idea." The client replied, "Thanks, I came up with it myself."

Second message (two days later): "I found an example of how another site implemented a similar carousel with a call-to-action button. You don't have one, but maybe it'll be useful." The client thanked him.

Message three (two days later): "I can whip up a prototype of this carousel for you in a couple of hours. Free, just to see what it looks like. Would you like one?" The client agreed. The prototype was well received, and the freelancer received a full contract.

Question:
How do you usually start communicating with new people online?

Practical task.
Complete the application plan for 5 people.

MAIN:
Don't sell to strangers. First become acquainted, then sell. 

Step 6. Feedback
(short - full inChapter 2.58)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Most entrepreneurs ask clients the wrong question. They say, "Do you like our product?" and get a polite, meaningless "yes." Experts ask differently. They build bridges between the client's problem and their solution.

Operating principle.
The right question is: "What irritates you most about our industry?" The person isn't defensive (you're not asking about yourself). The person is honest (they're complaining about the industry, not you). You get real insights—what's truly important to the client. "Building bridges" means establishing a connection between the person's problem and your solution.

---

Example.
The product manager launched a new feature. A week later, users started complaining, but he thought they were "simply not used to it." A month later, users started churning.

He conducted 10 interviews with those who had stopped using it. Eight out of ten said the same thing: "The feature is necessary, but it's implemented illogically. It requires five extra clicks."

He simplified the interface, removing three unnecessary steps. After two weeks, the churn stopped. If he hadn't asked, he would have lost all his users. He was guessing, and his clients knew. All he had to do was ask.

Question:
How do you ask clients about their problems?
Or just whether they like your product?

Practical task.
Complete the feedback collection scheme.
Conduct 5 in-depth interviews.

MAIN:
Don't build bridges that no one wants to cross.
Find out first where people want to go.

BLOCK 3. SYSTEM (Month 6-12)

Step 7. The CON-TON-FON business concept
(short - full inChapter 2.14)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There's a simple formula for a stable, "eternal" business. If you want to make money for years and decades, build your business according to this formula.
It's called "CON-TONE-FON." You can't skip any of these stages. The second stage, TONE, is especially important—without it, even the most brilliant product will remain unknown.

Operating principle.
Every business goes through three stages. In the first, you're just getting started (CON). In the second, you start earning and fine-tuning (TONE). In the third, you reap the benefits and fade into the background (BACKGROUND). The order doesn't change. You can't immediately want BACKGROUND without going through CON and TONE.

---

Example.
Anna launched an online English school. She started with a simple setup: she recorded the first lessons on her phone, put together a program, and hired one teacher. No extra costs.

Then the TON began: she launched advertising, set up an auto-funnel on Telegram, and offered free webinars. For the first three months, sales were almost nonexistent—two or three students. But she didn't give up. She continued to fine-tune her marketing.

When the school reached a stable enrollment of 50 students per month, she switched to the Fund for Social Development (FON): she hired an administrator to answer questions and a methodologist to check lessons. Anna herself writes only strategic emails once a week.

Now she works two hours a day, and the school is growing without her daily involvement. First, assemble. Then set up. Then rest.

Question:
What stage is your project at?
Are you investing enough in promotion?

Practical task.
If you already have a business, determine what stage you are at.
If it's a CON, plan a budget for TON right away.

MAIN:
CON — create. TONE — promote. FON — reap the benefits and nurture and maintain your system for 100+ years.
Don't skip the TONE. Even the best product is useless without marketing.

Step 8. The Power of Community
(short - full inChapter 2.15)

The essence of the phenomenon.
In the 21st century, the most important asset isn't money, or machines, or real estate. The most important asset is communities. A group of people who trust you, follow you, and are willing to buy from you again and again.

Operating principle.
A product can be copied in a week. Technology can be stolen. Prices can be driven down. But a loyal community that trusts you can't be copied. People don't come for the product. They come to you.
Your goal is not just to build an audience, but to transform it into a community: to connect people with each other, to create trust and loyalty.

Of the 100% of the flow of people, 20% will become clients, and 4% will become leaders and generate 80% of the total turnover. Your task is to find and unite these 4%.

---

Example.
Anna ran a travel blog. She had 5,000 subscribers, but engagement was weak: some likes, but almost no comments.

She decided to build a community. She started a Telegram chat and invited her most active subscribers. Every evening, she asked questions like, "Where are you going next weekend?" and "What's the most memorable place you've ever seen?"

Within a month, the chat had 200 people discussing routes, chipping in for group trips, and helping each other with visas. Comments on posts had increased tenfold. Subscribers were answering newcomers' questions themselves, and Anna could only pop in occasionally to offer thanks.

She was no longer just a blogger. She became the center of a travel community. And when she launched a paid course on travel photography, the first 30 spots were sold out in a day—without advertising, just through chat.

Question:
Do you have your own community?
People who listen to you, read you, watch you and trust you?

Practical task.
Set a goal: 1000 subscribers in 6 months.
Break it down into weeks and do it.

MAIN:
Don't chase butterflies - plant a garden, take care of it, and the butterflies will settle in themselves. 

Step 9. Closed loop of the money chain
(short - full inChapter 1.9)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Focus on the chain of actions that ultimately yields monetary results. And ensure that this cycle is never interrupted. Money should work, not sit idle.

Operating principle.
Every time you make a profit, immediately use part of it to attract new clients. Then the process doesn't stop. The cycle should be closed: input→action→result→reinvest→new entry. If the loop is open, you start from scratch every time.

---

Example.
Anna owned a small beauty salon. She earned 200,000 rubles a month. And every month, she spent everything on herself: clothes, restaurants, travel. There was nothing left for expansion. The business was stagnant.

Anna implemented an algorithm. She set up an autopayment: on the day revenue came in, 30 percent (60,000 rubles) would automatically go to a separate account. She forbade herself from touching this money. The first two months were difficult—she wanted to withdraw it and spend it. But the algorithm worked: the money was in a separate account, and she didn't touch it.

In the third month, she invested her savings of 180,000 rubles in social media advertising. A month later, she had 30 new clients. Her income had grown to 280,000 rubles. Anna increased her reinvestment rate to 40 percent.

She didn't have to think every time: "To invest or not to invest?" The algorithm decided for her. Her personal will was required only in one thing: not to meddle with the "For Development" account.

Question:
Where does the money you earn go?
Do they continue to work or just get spent?

Practical task.
Calculate what percentage of your income you reinvest in your business.
Set a goal to bring this figure to 30-50%.

MAIN:
Closed loop - input→action→result→reinvest→new entrance.
Without reinvestment, business comes to a standstill. 

UNIT 4. SCALING (Years 2-3)

Step 10. Levers
(short - full inChapter 2.32)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Leverage is what allows you to achieve great results with little effort. In business, leverage is the only way to escape the "selling your time" trap. One person with leverage is more powerful than a thousand without it.

Operating principle.
In physics, a lever allows you to lift a heavy load with minimal effort. In business, a lever is a way to multiply your actions.
You can work 8 hours a day.
But if you find the lever, you can get the result of 80, 800 and 8000 man-hours - per day!

---

Example.
The sales manager worked with clients manually: calling, sending commercial proposals one by one, and entering data into Excel.

It took 2 hours per client.

He learned about an automated email marketing service. He set it up in a day: clients received emails themselves, downloaded presentations themselves, and filled out forms themselves. Processing each client now took 15 minutes.

Within a week, he had established integration with the CRM: requests from forms were automatically entered into the database, tasks were assigned to managers, and reminders were sent.

He didn't work faster. He stopped manually doing what software could do. He spent the six hours a day he gained on face-to-face meetings with key clients. His income increased. And his time on routine tasks dropped to zero.

Question:
What levers do you use? People, money, systems, content, AI?

Practical task.
Complete the lever implementation diagram.

MAIN:
Give me a place to stand, and I will move the Earth. Find your place to stand.
One man with a lever is stronger than a thousand without it.

Step 11. Three types of scaling
(short - full inChapter 2.18)

The essence of the phenomenon.
You have a working system. Money is flowing, processes are streamlined.
Question: What's next? Answer: If the system works, your job is to scale it up. But there are three different ways to scale, and it's important not to confuse them.

Operating principle.
You can't scale something that doesn't work. Before you step on the gas, make sure the engine is assembled. Growth should be based on data, not emotion. There are three scenarios: accelerate the current model, change the model, or change the business. Each has its own moment.

---

Example.
The owner had a chain of dry cleaners with a good flow of customers. A line formed at each location. Instead of opening another location in a neighboring area (which would increase the demand), he decided to improve the service: add a courier, change the packaging, and expand the price list. While he spent six months fine-tuning the model, competitors rented space in a neighboring area, opened their own dry cleaner with a similar on-site service, and snatched up some of his customers.

If he had stepped up his game and opened a store while demand was high, his competitors simply wouldn't have caught up. He wanted to improve what was already working, rather than scale up his success. And his competitors simply took over the existing lineup.

Question:
What stage is your business at?
What scaling scenario is needed right now?

Practical task.
Evaluate your business.
Determine what stage you are at.
Choose one of the three paths and make an action plan for the month.

MAIN:
Don't fix what's broken—scale it. There are three scaling scenarios: step up the pace (increase traffic), change the model (improve conversion/ticket), and change the business (enter a new niche). Each has its own moment. Don't confuse them. 

Step 12. Diversification
(short - full inChapter 2.38)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Don't put all your eggs in one basket, even if it seems safe. This is called "diversification"—spreading the risk.
The main rule when planning any business model: first consider the risks. Then consider the benefits.

Operating principle.
Professionals first calculate how much they can lose, and only then how much they can earn. You distribute your assets so that the failure of one doesn't destroy everything.
The more independent points, the lower the risk.
If you have one source of income, you are at risk.
If there are three of them, you are safe.

---

Example.
The freelancer worked with a regular client. He paid well, and the orders were steady. The freelancer settled down and stopped looking for other clients.

Six months later, the client ran out of money and moved to another platform. The freelancer was left without income. She had to urgently find new clients, take on cheaper orders, and agree to unfavorable terms.

His colleague worked with three clients: one accounted for 50 percent of the revenue, the second for 30 percent, and the third for 20 percent. When the first client left, the colleague didn't notice a drop. The other two covered the loss.

My colleague wasn't smarter. He simply had three points of support. When one breaks, you don't fall.

Question:
How many baskets do you have?
What if one of them crashes tomorrow?

Practical task.
Write down all your current points.
For each one, ask: “What would happen if this disappeared tomorrow?”
Create a diversification plan for the year.

MAIN:
One basket - one path to disaster.
Many baskets - many paths to safety.
Diversification is the distribution of risks.

Step 13. Exit point

The essence of the step.
You've built a system that works without you. Now you can think about exiting. Sell the business, pass it on to your heirs, or cash out.

Operating principle.
A good business can be sold. A great one can be passed on. A brilliant one can be both.
The cost of a business is usually 2-4 times the annual profit (depending on the niche and risks).
If you've built an asset that works without you, you have a choice.

What does it consist of?
The exit point includes three stages.

  1. Asset valuation.How much is your business worth? Formula: annual profit x 2-4 years.
  2. Preparing for sale.We clean up the reports, pack them, and look for a buyer.
  3. Sale or transfer.We either take profits and cash out, or pass them on to our children/partners.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Calculate the approximate value of your business today. Annual profit x 3 (average ratio).
  2. Think about what price you want in 3 years. Multiply the current price by 5-10.
  3. Create a plan to increase the value of your business. Increase profits, reduce dependence on yourself, and streamline your processes.
  4. Research the market. Who might be the buyer? Competitors? Investors? The team?
  5. Start preparing. Clean reporting, described processes, a trained team—all this increases value.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not thinking about exiting at all. Building a business that can't be sold (because it's only supported by you).

Mistake 2: Thinking that selling a business is easy. It's not. It's a process that takes 1-2 years.

Mistake 3: Not preparing your business for sale in advance. When a buyer comes along, you're left with a mess of reporting and processes.

Example.
The owner of a chain of cleaning companies built a business that operated without his daily involvement. Managers reported weekly, processes were defined, and finances were transparent.

Three years later, he decided to exit. He found a buyer—a large national chain that wanted to expand into his region. The business was valued at four times its annual profit.

He spent six months preparing: putting contracts in order, resolving all legal issues, and transferring key employees to full-time positions with guarantees. The sale was completed in two months. He invested the proceeds in dividend-paying stocks and real estate.

At 48, he retired from the operating business. His monthly income remained the same, but his free time increased. He didn't just build a business—he built a transferable asset. And he did it just in time.

Question:
Are you building a business to sell or to leave to your children?

Practical task.
Calculate the approximate value of your business today.
In 3 years it should grow 5-10 times.

MAIN:
A good business can be sold. A great one can be passed on. A brilliant one can be both.

BLOCK 5. SUPPORT (Continuous)

Step 14. Human factor
(short - full inChapter 2.36)

The essence of the phenomenon.
We live among people, and even the most beautiful mathematical model can collapse if the human factor isn't taken into account. People aren't robots. They get tired, they make mistakes, they get offended, they get sick, they have bad days, they can get tired, angry, or simply change their mind.

Operating principle.
Don't ignore the human factor. Don't try to overcome it. Account for it consciously, with a margin. Build your system so that it's resilient to the failure of a single person. Always factor a 20-30% margin for human error into your plans. People will make mistakes—it's not a disaster, it's simply data for planning.

---

Example.
Elena managed a small development department. She had created a perfect sprint plan—two weeks, clear objectives, no delays. But on the third day, programmer Mikhail called in sick, designer Anna had a falling out with the client and was working at half-stress, and layout designer Pavel took time off.

Elena could have snapped, demanded reports, and forced overtime. Instead, she redistributed tasks, moved the deadline forward by three days, and hired an external freelancer to handle minor tasks.

The project was delivered two days late, but the team was not fired up. They caught up a month later. If Elena had started pushing, half the department could have quit.

Question:
Think of a situation where another person let you down.
How much of your resentment was his fault, and how much was your inflated expectations?

Practical task.
This week, when someone makes a mistake, instead of the automatic reaction, “They’re bad,” ask yourself, “What’s their current state?”
Maybe he didn't get enough sleep, he's having problems, or he just didn't understand the problem?"

MAIN:
There are no perfect people. The human factor is not a reason for emotions, but a reason for planning. A pro has a backup plan.

Step 15. KPIs and ROI
(short - full inChapter 2.29)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There are two main indicators that need to be monitored in any business.
One is about you personally, the other is about the business as a whole. KPI is your personal performance indicator. ROI is the return on investment.

Operating principle.
KPIs show how many useful actions you've taken. ROI shows whether the effort and resources invested in your business are paying off.
Good KPIs (lots of calls) should lead to good ROI (profit).
If the KPIs are high but the ROI is low, then the problem is not in the quantity of actions, but in the quality.

---

Example.
The owner of an online shoe store launched advertising across three channels: contextual advertising, social media, and a teaser network. After a month, he had no idea which channels were working and which weren't. He simply looked at his overall revenue and was happy when it grew.

A friend taught him how to calculate ROI. It turned out that contextual advertising invested 30,000 rubles and received 90,000 (an ROI of 200 percent). Social media invested 20,000 rubles and received 20,000 (an ROI of 0 percent, a break-even point). Teaser advertising invested 10,000 rubles and received 2,000 (an ROI of minus 80 percent).

He immediately shut down the teaser network, disabled social media a month later, and tripled his contextual advertising budget. Overall revenue increased by 50 percent, while the advertising budget remained the same. He stopped guessing which button to press. He started counting.

Question:
What are your KPIs?
What is the ROI?
Is there a connection between them?

Practical task.
Complete the KPI and ROI implementation plan.

MAIN:
If you're not counting numbers, you're not running a business. You're just present in it. KPIs are how much you produce. ROI is how much you earn. Keep track of both.

Step 16. Life Balance

The essence of the phenomenon.
Business is only part of life. Not the main one. The wheel of life must be balanced, otherwise you won't get very far.

Operating principle.
A wheel runs smoothly only when all the spokes are approximately the same length.
If one spoke, say "Business," becomes gigantic and the others are short stubs, the wheel becomes an oval. You won't get very far on that. It will shake so much that all the bones will crumble.

What does it consist of?
Balance wheel (8 spheres).

  1. Business/Career
  2. Finance
  3. Health
  4. Family
  5. Friends/Environment
  6. Recreation/Entertainment
  7. Personal growth
  8. Spirituality

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Draw your balance wheel.8 spokes, from center to edge. Rate each sphere from 1 to 10.
  2. Look at the shape of the wheel.Where are the dips? Which spokes are shorter than others?
  3. Make a plan for the monthto eliminate failures. Don't try to fix everything at once. Choose 2-3 areas where the scores are the lowest.
  4. Make time.Not “whenever it works out,” but strictly in the calendar: time for family, for sports, for sleep, for friends.
  5. Remember NNNN.No one owes anything to anyone. Including you, the business.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that business is everything. "Once I earn a million, then I'll focus on my family and health." You won't. The million won't come, or it will, but your family and health will already be ruined.

Mistake 2: Not setting aside time for rest. Living like a hamster in a wheel. Burnout is inevitable.

Mistake 3: Not checking the balance wheel regularly. Once a year isn't enough. Once a quarter is the minimum.

Example.
The owner of an IT company worked 12-14 hours a day, seven days a week. The business was growing, new offices were opening, and profits were rising. But he was rarely seen at home. His wife had left, his children had stopped talking to him, and his health was failing: high blood pressure, insomnia, and constant fatigue.

He drew a balance wheel. He rated each area from 1 to 10. Business and finance – 9. Health – 3. Family – 2. Friends – 1. Leisure – 2. The wheel wasn't round, but curved – like an oval.

He recalibrated his priorities. He hired a CEO and handed over the operations to him. He himself began finishing work at 6:00 PM. He made a doctor's appointment and started going to the pool. He bought his son football tickets and started going to matches with him.

A year later, the business grew by another 30%—without his 24/7 presence. His health returned to normal, he and his wife began spending time together, and he and his son went on vacation. The wheel straightened out. He realized that business is only one spoke. If it gets too long, the wheel won't move.

Question:
Do you live to work or work to live?

Practical task.
Draw your balance wheel.
Rate each area from 1 to 10. Where are the gaps?
Make a monthly plan to eliminate them.

MAIN:
You're not a battery. You can't function without recharging. Rest consciously.

Chapter 6.1 Case Study: Marina

The essence of the example.
Theory is theory, but a real-life example is always clearer. Here's the story of Marina, who walked this path from start to finish. A real person, real numbers, real results.

Initial data.

  • Name: Marina
  • Age: 32 years
  • Original job: Accountant for hire
  • Lifecost: 50,000 rubles per month
  • Ambition: 150,000 rubles per month
  • Psychotype: sanguine + choleric (loves communication, quickly gets excited)
  • Free money to start: 100,000 rubles

What she did.

  1. Week 1 (Preparation). 
    I realized that accounting irritates her.
    I chose a niche—an affiliate program in the financial sector (credit cards, deposits). I gained a clear direction.
  2. Month 1-3 (Launch). 
    Created an MVP — a Telegram channel about personal finance.
    Using the 3-message method, I agreed with 10 friends to subscribe.
    First sales - commission 2000 rubles.
    I received my first money and understood that the idea was working.
  3. Month 6-12 (System). 
    I've accumulated 500 subscribers, 20 of whom have become clients.
    I started looking for partners and trained 3 people.
    A team has appeared.
  4. Year 2-3 (Scaling). 
    A team of 15 partners, 3 of whom have already recruited partners themselves.
    Income is 200,000 rubles per month, of which 70% is passive (percentage from partner sales).
    I hired an assistant.
    The system works without it.
  5. Now. 
    Works 3-4 hours a day.
    Lives in Thailand.
    Balance wheel - all spokes 8-9.
    Freedom.

Why it worked.

  1. Choosing the right niche.Finance is her subject, she is an accountant and understands it.
  2. MVP.I didn’t build a platform right away, I started with a channel.
  3. Community.I gathered subscribers, then partners.
  4. Self-reproduction.The partners found their partners.
  5. Delegation.I hired an assistant and freed up my time.
  6. Life balance.She left for Thailand and works 3-4 hours a day.

MAIN.
Marina is not a fiction. There are thousands of Marinas like her. Why can't you be next?

Question:
What from Marina’s journey can you repeat today?

Practical task.
Write your initial data as Marina did.
Make the same plan for 3 years.

MAIN:
Marina was an accountant. Now she's a free person. You can do the same.


FINAL CHECKLIST
"START ON MONDAY"

This checklist is your plan for the next three years. Print it out and hang it on your wall. Check off the items as you complete them.

Week 1 (Preparation).

  • I know my number (Lifecost)
  • I know my psychotype and archetype
  • I chose a business model and tested it for self-replication.

Month 1-3 (Launch).

  • I made an MVP and got my first sales/rejections
  • I wrote to the first 50 clients using the 3-message method
  • I've collected feedback and know the top 3 pain points.

Month 6-12 (System).

  • I have described the system (CON)
  • I have 500-1000 followers on social media.
  • I reinvest 30-50% of profits

Year 2-3 (Scaling).

  • I have my first employee.
  • I chose the scaling path (gas/model/business)
  • I have 2-3 sources of income
  • I calculated the value of my business

Constantly (Support).

  • I monitor KPIs and ROI
  • I duplicate key people
  • I'm checking the balance wheel

MAIN:
We have the map. We have the shoes. Now it's your turn. See you at the top.

VOLUME 2. ENVIRONMENT AROUND

SECTION 7. THE STRUCTURE OF REALITY
(A map of the area to help you understand where you are)

Introduction to the section

We've figured out how business works—your personal earning system. That was the first floor. Now let's go higher and look atWednesday, in which this system operates. On the world around.

There's no politics, no appeals, no slogans in this section. Just cold, calm analysis. Imagine you're planning a big trip. You can train your muscles and buy all the gear you want, but if you don't study the map, you'll get stuck in the first mudhole.

Reality doesn't have to be convenient. It just is. Your task is not to deny it or panic, but to integrate into it so that you can live by your own rules. Where are the mountains? Where are the rivers? Where are the danger zones? Where can you set up camp, and where is it better not to linger?

In this section, we will examine two worlds (the microcosm and the macrocosm), the matryoshka principle, the laws of aquariums, the illusion of choice, four main questions, the state as a system, digital traces, global players, and survival strategies.

MAIN:
The world is complex, but knowable. Don't be afraid, don't deny it, study it. And build your life within.

Chapter 7.1 The Two Worlds You Exist In

The essence of the phenomenon.
You live in two worlds simultaneously. Understanding this is the first step to awareness. People often confuse these worlds, and this leads to suffering.

Operating principle.
There is a microworld (inner) - your thoughts, feelings, beliefs, fears, dreams. Something that cannot be touched, but it exists.
And there is the macroworld (external) – family, home, city, country, planet. That which can be touched or measured.

Two people with different microcosms will perceive the same event in the macrocosm differently. One will say, "Hurray, opportunity!" The other, "A nightmare, a catastrophe!" It's all about the internal settings.

What does it consist of?
Microworld (internal)— thoughts, feelings, beliefs, fears, dreams. It cannot be measured, but it is real.
Macrocosm (external)— family, home, city, country, planet. It can be touched or measured.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Write down what is happening in your microworld now.Thoughts, feelings. Don't judge, just write them down.
  2. Write down what is happening in your macrocosm right now.Events around, circumstances.
  3. Understand what you can influence and what you cannot.In the microworld, you can change everything. In the macroworld, only a small part.
  4. Don't confuse one with the other.Don't think that your inner experiences are reality. And don't think that the outside world completely determines your state.
  5. Remember:The outside world influences you. The inner world determines how you react to it.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that your inner experiences are reality. "I'm scared, so the world is dangerous." No. You're scared, but the world could be safe.

Mistake 2: Thinking that the outside world completely determines your state. "I'm in a bad way because I'm in a crisis." No. Your reaction to the crisis determines your state.

Mistake 3: Not noticing how thoughts influence your perception of events. Living on autopilot.

Example.
Two people are stuck in traffic. The first is angry, cursing, and getting on his nerves. The second turns on an audiobook and listens calmly. Same event, different reactions. It's all about the microcosm.

Question:
What's happening in your microworld right now?
And in the macro world?
Is there a connection?

Practical task.
Write down what is happening in your microworld now (thoughts, feelings).
Write down what is happening in your macroworld (events around you).
Understand what you can influence and what you cannot.

MAIN:
The events are the same, but the reactions are different. Choose your reaction.

Chapter 7.2 The Matryoshka Principle (The Structure of the Macroworld)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The outside world can be imagined as a nesting doll or aquariums, one inside the other. You are at the center, and around you are layers: family, home, neighborhood, city, country, planet, galaxy.

Operating principle.
The further a level is from you, the less influence you have on it, but the more it influences you. You can't change the laws of a country with a single wish, but they influence you every day. Understanding this removes the illusion of omnipotence and reduces frustration.

What does it consist of?
Hierarchy of Matryoshka dolls (from you outwards).

  1. Level 1 - You.You are in complete control.
  2. Level 2 - Family.You can have a great influence.
  3. Level 3 - House, neighbors.Less influence.
  4. Level 4 - City District.Even less.
  5. Level 5 - City.You have almost no influence.
  6. Level 6 - State.Affects you every day.
  7. Level 7 - Planet.You don’t influence her in any way, but she does.
  8. Level 8 - Galaxy.It’s hard to even think about such a scale.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Draw your own matryoshka doll.From yourself to the galaxy.
  2. At each level, write down what is important that is happening now.What events, what people, what circumstances?
  3. Determine which levels you can influence.Most likely levels 1-3. Maybe a little level 4.
  4. Determine which levels affect you.Most likely, these are all levels from 2 to 8.
  5. Don't fight with levels that are higher than you.Take them into account. And build your life within.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Trying to influence what's uninfluenceable. Writing angry posts about politics when you can't change the country's laws.

Mistake 2: Ignoring the influence of distant levels on your life. Not taking into account that government laws, the planet's climate, and the economy affect you every day.

Mistake 3: Thinking you're the center of the universe and everything revolves around you. It's not true.

Example.
People get angry at the government, post angry messages on social media, but nothing changes. They could simply accept that the state is a distant layer of the Russian doll and build their lives around that, rather than fighting it.

Question:
What levels can you influence?
And which ones influence you?

Practical task.
Draw your own matryoshka doll: from yourself to the galaxy.
At each level, write down what is important that is happening now.
Determine which levels you can influence and which ones influence you.

MAIN:
You are the smallest matryoshka doll.
Take the big ones into account, but don't try to change them.

Chapter 7.3 Who Lives in These Worlds

The essence of the phenomenon.
Each aquarium (level of the macrocosm) has its own inhabitants with their own roles. There are champions, average performers, and laggards. Understanding this structure helps us avoid expecting everyone to behave the same way.

Operating principle.
In every aquarium—a family, a company, a city—people naturally assign roles. This happens spontaneously, without anyone's dictate, simply because people are different.

Some are naturally blessed with more energy, ambition, and ability—they become champions. Others have just enough to live a stable life and stay within the system—they are average. Still others haven't found their niche, haven't fit in, or haven't wanted to fit in—they are laggards.

The main thing here is this:You can't change this distribution.No matter how much you train or motivate, the ratio will remain roughly the same. Tomorrow you fire the laggards, and new ones will take their place. Take away the champions, and the average performers will be redistributed, and new leaders will rise from their ranks.

Understanding this removes two painful questions.
The first one: “Why are some successful and others not?” – because that’s how any aquarium is designed.
Second: “How can we make everyone successful?” – no way. You can only work with those who are there and take the right position.myposition in this structure.

What does it consist of?
Roles at any level (according to Gauss from Chapter 2.23).

  1. Champion— 5-10% of the total. The best in this aquarium. They set the standards and receive the main bonuses.
  2. Middle class— 80% of the total. The majority. They simply live, work, and pay taxes.
  3. Lagging behind— 5-10% of the total number. They don't fit in, they can't find their place.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose your 3 main aquariums.Work, family, hobbies, city.
  2. Determine what role you play in each.Champion? Average? Underperforming?
  3. Think about where you want to change roles.Maybe you're average at work and want to be a champion? Maybe you're a champion at home and want to relax?
  4. Make a plan.How do I change my role? What do I need to do?
  5. Accept that you may have different roles in different aquariums. This is fine.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Expecting everyone to be a champion. Thinking that "everyone around you should be successful." They won't. That's statistics.

Mistake 2: Labeling those who are lagging behind without understanding the context. Thinking that "they're just lazy." Perhaps they simply haven't found their place.

Mistake 3: Not noticing that you have different roles in different fishbowls. Thinking that if you're a champion at work, you must be a champion everywhere.

Example.
The same person can be a respected professor at the university (a champion), a bad father (a laggard), and not take an active part in the life of his city, but go to elections (an average person).
This doesn't make it good or bad, they're just different games.

Question:
What role do you play in your main aquarium (work, family, city)? Are you satisfied with this role?

Practical task.
Choose your 3 main aquariums (work, family, hobby, city).
Determine what role you play in each.
Think about where you want to change roles.

MAIN:
Each aquarium has its own hierarchy.
You can be a champion in one thing and a laggard in another.

Chapter 7.4 Aquarium Laws

The essence of the phenomenon.
Every aquarium has its own rules—written and unwritten. To survive and thrive, you need to know them. Ignoring the aquarium's laws doesn't exempt you from the consequences.

Operating principle.
You may not know it's dangerous to walk around this area at night, but if you get robbed, you'll see the real consequences. The law is the law, even if you haven't read it. Your job is to learn the rules of the game in the aquariums you find yourself in.

What does it consist of?
Types of laws.

  1. Written.Official rules, violations of which result in punishment. Example: state laws, school rules.
  2. Unwritten.Traditions, customs, "that's the way it's done." Example: they don't walk like that in this area, they don't speak like that here.
  3. Market.Laws of supply and demand. Example: prices, wages.
  4. Physical.Laws of nature. Example: gravity, aging.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. For your main aquarium (for example, work), write out the laws of all types.Written (labor code, company rules).
    Unwritten (how people communicate, who is friends with whom).
    Market (how much they pay, what are the prices).
    Physical (if the work is physical, what kind of loads).
  2. Which ones are you breaking (even without knowing)?Admit it to yourself honestly.
  3. What needs to be changed to live according to the laws and not against them?Maybe change the behavior? Maybe change the aquarium?
  4. Don't argue with the laws.You won't change gravity. You won't change market prices with your discontent.
  5. If the law seems stupid, find another aquarium where it is different.But while you're at it, observe.

Possible errors.

Mistake #1: Thinking that if you don't know the law, it doesn't apply to you. It does. Ignoring it doesn't absolve you of the consequences.

Mistake 2: Thinking that if a law seems stupid, you can break it. Yes, you can. But there will be consequences.

Mistake 3: Ignoring unwritten rules and wondering why people don't want to deal with you.

Example.
A man moved to a new country and continued to behave as he had in his home country. He spoke loudly in public places, didn't greet neighbors, and didn't observe local customs. People avoided him and didn't want to interact with him, even though he hadn't broken any laws.

Question:
What are the unwritten laws in your main aquarium?
Do you observe them?

Practical task.
For your main aquarium (for example, work), write out the laws of all types.
Which ones are you breaking (even without knowing)?
What needs to be changed to live according to the laws and not against them?

MAIN:
There are rules everywhere. If you want to play, follow them. If you don't, find another game.

Chapter 7.5 The Illusion of Choice

The essence of the phenomenon.
We think we choose how to live. But in reality, 90% of our decisions are predetermined by the environment we were born into. Language, country, religion, profession, lifestyle—all of this was given to us, not chosen.

Operating principle.
You didn't choose where you were born. You didn't choose the language you speak. You didn't choose your religion (if you have one). You didn't choose your profession—your parents, your school, your environment chose it for you. Realizing this is the first step to true freedom.

What does it consist of?
Degree of predetermination.

  • Language— 100% (the language my parents speak)
  • Page— 100% (where born)
  • Religion— 90% (what parents profess)
  • Profession— 80% (which is prestigious in your circle)
  • Lifestyle— 70% (how they live around)
  • Opinion about the world— 90% (what the media, school, and family say)

Quote from Schopenhauer:
"As soon as you're born, your name, country, faith, and identity are already chosen for you. And then you spend your whole life defending what you never chose."

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Realize.Understand that your "personal" beliefs are often the beliefs of your aquarium.
  2. Doubt it.Ask the question: “Why do I think that? Is this mine or was it given to me?”
  3. Explore alternatives.See how people live in other aquariums. Read books, watch movies, talk to people from other cultures.
  4. Choose consciously.Keep what suits you, discard what suits others.
  5. Remember:You didn't choose where you were born. But you can choose who you become.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Sincerely believing that your opinion is your opinion.

Mistake 2: Not noticing that you think this way because everyone around you thinks this way.

Mistake 3: Being afraid to step outside the bounds of predetermined behavior. Thinking that "it's wrong," "it's not accepted."

Example.
The man considered himself a patriot of his country all his life, attended rallies, and wore symbols.

One day he wondered: "Why am I a patriot? Because I decided to be one or because I was raised that way?"
He began to study history, travel to other countries, and ultimately made a “conscious” choice to remain a patriot, but by choice, not by inertia.

Question:
Which of your beliefs are not really yours, but are given to you by the environment?

Practical task.
Take your 5 main beliefs (about money, family, work, country, religion).
Answer honestly: where did they come from?
Did you come yourself or were you given it?
If they “gave” you something, think about whether you want to keep it.

MAIN:
You didn't choose where you were born. But you can choose who you become.

Chapter 7.6 Four Questions for Awareness

The essence of the phenomenon.
There are four questions, the answers to which determine your entire worldview. Most people never ask themselves these questions. And they should. The answers to these questions determine the rules by which you live and the meaning you give to your life.

Operating principle.
If you don't ask these questions, someone else has already answered them for you. Your parents, your school, your church, the government, the television. You're living by someone else's instructions without even realizing it. Asking these questions means starting to write your own instructions.

What does it consist of?
Four questions.

  1. Who created people?
  2. For what purpose were people created?
  3. Why did we appear on planet Earth?
  4. Is there any instruction on how to live on planet Earth correctly or incorrectly?— which we did NOT receive at the moment of our birth?

Answer options (to give you something to work from).

To the question "Who created people?" ?" can be answered in various ways: God or gods, evolution, alien intelligence, no one. Or simply say "I don't know."

To the question "For what purpose were humans created?"The options are also varied: worship God, reproduce, explore the world, be happy. Or—nothing at all.

To the question "Why did we appear on planet Earth?" ? " Some say: the conditions are right here. Others: this is a prison or a school. Others: it's an accident. Still others: this is our mission.

To the question "Where is the instruction manual for life?"The answers differ: there are no instructions in religious texts, in the laws of nature. Or—you're holding this book in your hands.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Sit down and write out your answers to these 4 questions.If there are no answers, write: “I don’t know.”
  2. Start looking for answers.Books, philosophy, science, religion - whatever is closer.
  3. Explore other points of view.Read about different religions, about science, about philosophy.
  4. Formulate your working hypothesis.She can change. That's okay.
  5. Remember: It's okay not to know the answers.Not to look for them is a crime against oneself.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1 is thinking these questions are unimportant. "What difference does it make who created it? The main thing is to live." There is a difference. Everything depends on the answers.

Mistake 2: Accepting other people's answers as your own. Believing what your parents believe without asking "why."

Mistake 3: Fearing the lack of answers, you stop looking for them. "It's too difficult, I won't think about it."

Example.
A man lived for 50 years, went to church, believed in God, but never asked himself, "Why do I believe?" One day, he honestly asked himself, "Is this my faith, or was it taught to me?" and began his search.

Question:
Do you have answers to these questions?
Or have you never thought about it?

Practical task.
Sit down and write out your answers to these 4 questions.
If there are no answers, write: “I don’t know.”
Start looking for answers (books, philosophy, science, religion - whatever is closest).

MAIN:
Not knowing the answers is normal. Not looking for them is a crime against yourself.

Chapter 7.7 The State as One of the Aquariums
(cold analysis - no rebellion)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The state is one of the aquariums in your Matryoshka doll. It exists, it influences, it sets the rules. This is a fact that shouldn't be judged as "good" or "bad." It's simply a given that must be accepted and taken into account.

Operating principle.
The state is an organization that operates within a given territory, establishes laws, enforces them (through the police, courts, and army), and collects taxes. Your job is not to fight it, but not to deify it, either.considerin their plans.

What does it consist of?
The state from the point of view of the system.

  • Territory.Valid within a specific territory.
  • Laws.Sets rules for everyone in the area.
  • Control of execution.Monitors the implementation of laws (police, courts, army).
  • Taxes.Collects money for his needs.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Calculate how much you pay to the state.Taxes, duties, fees. Everything that goes into the budget.
  2. Calculate what you get in return.Roads, police, education, medicine (if you use it). What else?
  3. Decide if this balance suits you.If yes, good. If no, choose one of the strategies.
  4. Choose a strategy. 
    Observe(pay taxes, live according to the laws).
    Optimize(use benefits, legal ways to reduce taxes).Distance yourself(minimize contacts, live modestly, reduce publicity)(More details on this in Section 9, Scenarios A, B, D).
    Change the aquarium    (move to another country)(For more details, see Section 9, Scenario D).
  5. Take action.Don't waste your energy complaining. Choose a strategy and live.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Falling into extreme patriotism, considering the state a deity. "Our state is the best." This is an illusion.

Mistake 2: Falling into extreme nihilism while trying to fight it. "The state is the enemy." This is a road to nowhere.

Mistake 3: Not factoring the government into your plans and then being surprised by the consequences. "Why did the tax office come to me?"

Example.
Two neighbors received notice of a property tax increase.

FirstI wrote an angry post on social media and calmed down.

SecondI studied the law, found a benefit for pensioners, submitted an application, and got back three years' worth of overpayment.

The neighbor saved 15,000 rubles, the first one didn’t.

Question:
What's your current strategy? Are you happy with it?

Practical task.
Calculate how much you pay to the government (taxes, duties, fees). Calculate what you get in return (roads, police, education, healthcare). Decide if this balance suits you.
If not, choose one of the strategies (from section 9).

MAIN:
The state is neither a father nor a mother. It is a system.
Choose your strategy and live by it.

Chapter 7.8 Digital Traces and the New Reality

The essence of the phenomenon.
The 21st century has seen the emergence of a new level of macrocosm—the "digital" one. You live not only in a physical city, but also in a digital space. And this digital identity is often more important than the physical one. Your digital footprints will help you find your way anywhere in the world.

Operating principle.
All information about you that exists online remains there forever. Your likes, comments, search queries, purchases, geolocation, and conversations. Even if you delete a post, it will likely remain in other people's screenshots.

What does it consist of?
Composition of the digital footprint.

  • Social media.Posts, photos, likes, friends. Who sees them: everyone.
  • Search queries.What you search for on Google. Who sees it: Google, intelligence agencies.
  • Purchases.What, where, and when bought. Who sees it: banks, marketplaces.
  • Geolocation.Where you were and when. Who can see it: phone, apps.
  • Correspondence.What you wrote to whom. Who sees it: companies, intelligence agencies.
  • Banking operations.Your income and expenses. Who sees them: banks, tax authorities.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Google your first and last name.What is visible? Would you like your children, employer, parents to see this?
  2. Go through your social networks.Delete anything you don't want the world to see. Old photos, questionable comments, political posts.
  3. Set up two-factor authentication wherever possible.So that it doesn't get hacked.
  4. Don't write anything in messengers that you wouldn't say out loud in front of everyone.The correspondence may surface.
  5. Use different passwords.So that everything doesn't get hacked at once.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that what you write online doesn't matter to anyone. It does. Intelligence agencies, scammers, and employers alike.

Mistake 2: Thinking that deleting a post means erasing it forever. No. It could have been saved in the cache, screenshots, or archives.

Mistake 3: Not considering the consequences of your digital actions. "I was just kidding."

Example.
In her youth, Olga kept a blog with harsh comments about her former employers. Eight years later, she rose to the rank of top manager.

When moving to a large company, HR found old posts.

The offer/proposal was revoked. Olga couldn't erase the past—the internet remembers everything.

Question:
What do people see if they Google your name?
Would you like your children, employer, parents to see this?

Practical task.
Google your first and last name. What do you see?
Go through your social media and delete anything you don't want the world to see. Set up two-factor authentication wherever possible.

MAIN:
The Internet remembers everything. Even what you forgot.
Think before you write.

Chapter 7.9 Global Players (Large Aquariums)

The essence of the phenomenon.
Beyond states, there are structures that operate on a global scale: transnational corporations, financial groups, international organizations. They influence our lives no less than governments, yet we often overlook them.

Operating principle.
These structures are not elected by the people and do not report to voters, yet their decisions impact the lives of millions of people. They can be more powerful than individual states. They must be known and taken into account.

What does it consist of?
Global players.

  1. Transnational corporations.Apple, Google, Microsoft, Amazon. They determine what technologies we use and where our data is stored.
  2. Financial groups.The World Bank, the IMF, and private investment funds influence the economies of entire countries, provide loans, and set conditions.
  3. International organizations.UN, WHO, WTO. They establish international rules, standards, and recommendations.
  4. Informal associations.The G7, the Bilderberg Group. They discuss global issues behind closed doors.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Read about any global corporation.For example, Apple. How does it make money? Who owns it? How does it affect your life?
  2. Think about how you interact with global players every day.What services do you use? Which banks do you trust?
  3. Don't fall for conspiracy theories.There are global players, but “world government” is an oversimplification.
  4. Don't panic.Panic doesn't help. Just be aware that they exist.
  5. Use this knowledge.Integrate yourself into these processes. Or at least don't interfere with them.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Falling into conspiracy theories, seeing a global government under every bush. "The Illuminati runs everything." This is not constructive.

Mistake 2: Ignoring global players altogether, thinking the world consists only of states. This is naive.

Mistake 3: Trying to fight something you can't defeat. "I'll close Google!" You won't.

Example.
People buy iPhones, use Google, watch YouTube, and order from Amazon. They interact with global corporations every day, but they don't even consider their scale and influence.

Question:
What global players are influencing your life right now?
Have you noticed this?

Practical task.
Read about any global corporation (for example, Apple).
How does she make money?
Who owns it?
Think about how you interact with global players every day.

MAIN:
Global players exist and will always exist, at all times.
Study them and monitor them, but don't panic. Panic doesn't help.

Chapter 7.10 Survival Strategy in the Macroworld

The essence of the phenomenon.
How can you live in a world with all these levels, aquariums, and players, and still remain free? There are general principles that will help you stay sane and avoid losing.

Operating principle.
You won't change the world. But you can change your place in it.
Don't fight the system—embrace it. Don't try to beat it—integrate it.
Live your own life, not the one that is imposed on you.

What does it consist of?
Five principles of survival.

  1. Understand, but don't dramatize.You won't overthrow the system. But you can navigate it.
  2. Take the laws into account.Don't fight windmills - just know where they are.
  3. Minimize dependence.The less you need from the system, the freer you are.
  4. Create reserves.Stocks of food, money, and connections for a rainy day.
  5. Live your life.Not the one that is imposed, but the one that you choose yourself.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Study the map.We have already done this in this part.
  2. Determine which aquariums you are in.What levels of Matryoshka are important to you?
  3. Understand the laws of these aquariums.Written and unwritten. What is allowed and what is not?
  4. Choose a strategy for each level.Comply? Optimize? Distance yourself? Change?
  5. Build your life within, not against.Don't try to remake the world. Build your fortress within it.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Trying to fight the entire system. "I'll overthrow the government!" You'll lose.

Mistake 2: Submitting yourself completely to the system. You'll lose yourself.

Mistake 3: Ignoring reality and hoping it will change on its own. It won't.

Example.
The intercity bus driver had studied all the accident-prone sections of the road. He knew where the ice was, where the potholes were, where overtaking was dangerous. He didn't speed, didn't take risks—he simply drove with reserve. He had a first aid kit, a tow rope, and a spare gas can. In ten years, he hadn't had a single accident. Passengers valued him.

Question:
Which principle do you need to strengthen?
Understanding? Compliance with laws? Independence? Reserves?

Practical task.
Rate yourself on five principles (0-10).
Where are your failures?
Make a plan to strengthen your weak areas for the coming year.

MAIN:
Don't speed on a dangerous road. Learn it and drive with reserve. 


SUMMARY OF SECTION 7. THE STRUCTURE OF REALITY
Self-assessment checklist.

  • I understand the difference between my thoughts and reality.
  • I know there are levels I have no control over.
  • I understand that there are different roles in each group.
  • I follow the rules or look for another game.
  • I understand that 90% of my choices are not mine.
  • I asked myself the main questions.
  • I have a strategy for communicating with the government.
  • I follow digital footprints.
  • I know about global players.
  • Am I going with the flow or fighting the river?

MAIN:
The world is complex, but knowable. Don't be afraid, don't deny it, study it. And build your life within. 

SECTION 8. THE CONCEPT OF A FREE MAN
(Who are you really and where do you get your rights)

Introduction to the section

In the previous part, we discussed how the world around us works. Now let's talk aboutwho are youIn this world, most people live with the conviction: “I am a citizen of such-and-such a country,” “I am an employee of such-and-such a company,” “I am a husband/wife/son/daughter.” They wear these labels and don’t even think: who am I without them?

Imagine that all the labels have been removed from you.
No passport. No job title. No marital status. No nationality. No religion. Who will remain? This question is frightening. Because there is often no answer. In this section, we will search for an answer. Not one that is imposed, but one that is born within.

In this section, we'll explore the fact that you're an inhabitant of planet Earth, where governments come from, that freedom of choice is your innate right, delve into four questions, understand that no one gave you instructions, accept loneliness as a basic reality, and see that there are no boundaries or rules except those in your head, and that you need to create the meaning of life yourself.

MAIN:
You are not your job. You are not your passport.
You are not your religion. You are you. 

Chapter 8.1 You are an inhabitant of planet Earth

The essence of the phenomenon. 
The most important thing to understand and remember forever: you were born on planet Earth. Your only true citizenship is planet Earth. Everything else is labels slapped on you later.

Operating principle.
States, nationalities, religions—all of these were invented by people. They didn't exist forever. You didn't sign a treaty with the country you were born into. Your rights are granted to you by the fact of being born on this planet, not by the fact of having a passport.

But living on the territory of a state means that you accept its laws -while you are in this territory. 
Rights are given by birth, responsibilities by location.

What does it consist of?
Logical proofs.

  • You were born on the territory of planet Earth. →Your homeland is the planet.
  • States appeared later than people →they did not exist forever.
  • Borders are drawn by people →This is a convention, not a law of nature.
  • You didn't sign a contract with the state. →You didn't agree to his rules.

What does this mean in practice?

By birthright, all the planet's resources belong to you. You have the right to live anywhere on Earth. No self-proclaimed states have power over you without your consent.
You are free by the fact of birth.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Say it out loud:"I am a resident of planet Earth." Feel the difference with the phrase "I am a citizen of..."
  2. Write down what feelings this caused.Fear? Relief? Unfamiliarity?
  3. Think about it:What is more important to you: citizenship of a country or belonging to the planet?
  4. Don't identify yourself with labels.You are not a "Russian," not an "American," not a "Muslim." You are a human being living on Earth.
  5. Remember:Your only true citizenship is planet Earth.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Identifying yourself with labels. "I'm Russian," "I'm American," "I'm Muslim." These are part of your identity, but not your whole identity.

Mistake 2: Forgetting that behind the labels lies a common human essence. We are all human.

Mistake 3: Being willing to kill and die for labels, forgetting that we are all equal people on one planet.

Example.
Sergei was born in the USSR. When the country collapsed, he became a Russian citizen. He then moved to Germany, received a German passport, and then a residence permit in Montenegro. When asked, "Who are you?" he answered differently each time: Russian, German, European. Irritation grew within him—he didn't understand who he really was.

One day at a conference, he met an old man who had traveled to 80 countries. Sergei complained, "I'm confused by my passports." The old man pulled out a globe and said, "You're an inhabitant of planet Earth. Everything else is just paperwork for officials."

Sergei calmed down. He stopped tormenting himself with the question of his nationality and began to simply live.

Question:
Who are you - a citizen of a country or an inhabitant of the planet?
What is more important to you?

Practical task.
Say out loud: “I am a resident of planet Earth.”
Feel the difference with "I am a citizen..."
Write down what feelings this caused.

MAIN:
Your only true citizenship is planet Earth. Everything else is just pieces of paper and labels created by humans. Don't forget who you are.

Chapter 8.2 Where did states come from?

The essence of the phenomenon.
States haven't always existed. They're a relatively recent invention of humanity. Understanding this removes their aura of "naturalness" and "eternity."

Operating principle.
Long ago, people lived without borders. Then powerful men with weapons came and said, "This land is ours. You will live on it by our rules." Then they began to negotiate among themselves and draw borders (the strong against the strong). This is how the first states emerged. Centuries passed, and people forgot that the borders were drawn by men with swords.

What does it consist of?
History of the emergence of states.

  • 200,000 years ago— tribes. They roamed, no borders.
  • 10,000 years ago— the first settlements. People began to settle, and private property appeared.
  • 5,000 years ago— the first kingdoms. One ruler captured the territory.
  • 2,000 years ago— empires. They conquered their neighbors.
  • 300 years ago— modern states. Borders, passports, laws.

How states consolidated power.
Borders separate "us" from "them." Passports confirm your belonging to a specific country. Laws determine what is permitted and what is not. Taxes take a portion of your income. The army and police control and enforce obedience. The system of any state will always use and justify any means for the sake of national security. And education instills from childhood that this is how it must be, otherwise there will be no order.

The question isn't whether this is good or bad. The question is to understand this mechanism. Then you can live within this system consciously, and not like a sleepwalker.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Realize:States are not eternal. They appeared—they can also disappear.
  2. Don't build your life around something that may not exist tomorrow.Don't put the state at the center of your identity.
  3. Imagine that tomorrow all people stop believing in the state.What will happen? Realize: states are held together by people's faith in their legitimacy.
  4. Do not fight with states.It's useless. Just consider their existence.
  5. Remember:People drew the boundaries. People can erase them.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that states have always existed. No. They are a relatively new phenomenon.

Mistake 2: Thinking that life without a state means chaos and anarchy. Perhaps. But that doesn't mean the state is the only possible form of organization.

Mistake 3: Not understanding that states are simply one form of organization that can disappear.

Example.
In 1991, citizens of the USSR woke up in a different country. Just yesterday, they had a single passport, a single currency, and a single border. Today, there were fifteen new states, new laws, new rules. People were confused: how could this be? After all, the USSR had seemed eternal. It was called a "bulwark of stability." But it disappeared within a few months. Not because enemies had arrived. But because the agreements between people had ceased to work.

The same thing happened with Yugoslavia and Czechoslovakia. Borders that seemed permanent were erased and redrawn. One person lived in the same house, but changed the country in his passport three times without ever moving.

Question:
If states appeared not so long ago, does that mean they can also disappear?
What then?

Practical task.
Imagine that tomorrow all people stop believing in the state.
What will happen?
Realize that states are built on people's faith in their legitimacy.

MAIN:
States are not eternal. They appear and disappear. These are not laws of nature, but agreements between people. Borders are created and removed by people.

Chapter 8.3 Freedom of Choice as an Innate Right

The essence of the phenomenon.
You always have freedom of choice. It's not the state that gave it to you—it's a fact of your existence. You can think what you want, say what you think, do what you think is right, join with whomever you want, and leave where things are bad.

Operating principle.
Freedom isn't a gift. It's a responsibility you accept. The more you accept other people's restrictions, the less freedom you have. Your freedom ends where someone else's begins.

What does it consist of?
Levels of freedom.

  1. Lagging behind.You do what you're told, even if you don't want to.
    Degree of freedom: 0%.
  2. Obedient citizen.You do what you're told because "that's how it should be."
    Degree of freedom: 10%.
  3. Conscious.You understand what they tell you, but you choose to obey.
    Degree of freedom: 30%.
  4. Using.You are using the system for your own purposes.
    Degree of freedom: 60%.
  5. Free.You live by your own rules, taking into account the system.
    Degree of freedom: 90%.
  6. Absolutely free.You live outside the system (very rarely).
    Degree of freedom: 100%.

What is freedom of choice?

You can think whatever you want.
You can say what you think (considering the "aquariums" that are around).
You can do whatever you think is right.
You can team up with whoever you want.
And you can leave where you feel bad.
This is freedom of choice.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Write down 10 limitations in your life.What you don’t do because “it’s not allowed”, “it’s not accepted”, “it’s not supposed to”.
  2. Opposite each one write:“It’s really not possible” or “I just decided so.”
  3. For the “I just decided” items, think aboutCan you change this?
  4. Don't expect the state to make you free.Freedom begins with your inner decision.
  5. Remember:Freedom is not a gift. It is a choice. And this choice has a price.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that freedom is granted by the state. Waiting for the Constitution to include "freedom of speech" and then it will appear.

Mistake 2: Thinking that just because the Constitution says "freedom of speech" means it exists. No. Freedom of speech means you can say what you think without consequences.

Mistake 3: Not understanding that freedom comes with responsibility. Wanting freedom, but not wanting to pay for it.

Example.
The engineer had worked at the plant for 15 years. He had a stable salary, a clear schedule, and a benefits package. He didn't like the job, but he was afraid to change anything. He had gotten used to this "cage." The feeder was always full, the perches were comfortable.

One day, he was offered a job at a private IT project with uncertain prospects. Six months without guarantees, he might have to scrape by. But the opportunities were many times greater. He could remain in a secure but stifling cage. Or he could be thrust into the unknown.

He quit. The first few months were scary and hungry. Clients came and went, and the money was barely enough to survive. But he studied, searched, and didn't give up.

Within two years, his design company had grown into a stable business with a team of seven. He chose his own schedule, partners, and tasks. Freedom wasn't free—it cost two years of discomfort and fear. But he paid the price. And he didn't regret it.

Question:
What prohibitions have you taken for granted, although in fact they are not necessary?

Practical task.
Write down 10 limitations in your life.
Opposite each one, write: “This is really impossible” or “I just decided so.”
For the “I just decided” items, think about whether you can change it.

MAIN:
Freedom isn't free. And you'll have to answer for every step you take.
Your freedom ends where another's begins. Everything else is your right.

Chapter 8.4 On the Types of Law

The essence of the phenomenon.
In all educational institutions of the world - from Harvard to Moscow State University, from the Sorbonne to Peking University - they study in the course "Theory of State and Law"types of lawThis is not my invention. It is classical legal science that is hundreds of years old.

Operating principle.
There are four main types of law.

  1. Natural law.Rights that belong to a person by birth. They don't need to be earned, and they are not granted by the state. Every person on the planet has them, regardless of citizenship, religion, skin color, or place of residence. The right to life, the right to liberty, the right to movement, the right to one's own body.
  2. Positive (state) law.Laws adopted by a specific state within its territory. They are binding on everyone within that territory, regardless of whether you agree with them or not. Tax code, traffic regulations, criminal code.
  3. International law.Agreements between states. The UN, the Geneva Conventions, the WTO, climate agreements. States sign them voluntarily and can withdraw from them.
  4. Corporate (local) law.Rules within organizations: schools, companies, clubs. You voluntarily enter into these relationships and voluntarily leave them.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Realize:You are simultaneously a subject of several legal systems.
  2. As a "human"— you have natural rights that no one gave you and no one can take away (but they can be violated - this is called a crime).
  3. As a "citizen" (or foreigner)— you are subject to the positive law of the country where you are.
  4. As a ‘participating communities’— you follow their internal rules.
  5. Remember:Knowledge of the different types of law is not a call to rebellion. It's information. Whether or not you use it is your own business.
