  2. Realize:Your attention makes things real. If you constantly think about the bad, you make the bad real.
  3. Start consciously directing your attention to what you want to receive.Not just “don’t think about the bad,” but think about the good.
  4. Focus.Choose what to look at.
  5. Remember:Reality is clay. Your thoughts are your hands. Sculpt what you want.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Thinking that thoughts are unimportant. "It's all esoteric." No. It's quantum physics.

Mistake 2: Focusing on problems rather than solutions. Thinking about what you don't want instead of what you do want.

Mistake 3: Expecting reality to change on its own, without your intervention. Thoughts without action are just fantasies.

Example.
Two people came to an interview at the same company.

The first one spent the entire journey thinking, "I don't want to say something stupid, I don't want to forget the numbers, I don't want to be late." He arrived tense, answered monosyllabically, and apologized constantly. They didn't hire him.

The second one thought, "I've prepared, I have something to say, this is my chance to prove myself." He walked calmly, smiled, answered confidently, and asked questions. He was hired at a higher salary than expected.

Same situation. Different thoughts – different results.
The focus of attention determined reality.

Question:
What do you look at more often - problems or opportunities?

Practical task.
Remember what you've been thinking about for the last 3 days.
Which of these thoughts came true?
Start consciously directing your attention to what you want to receive.

MAIN:
Reality is clay. Your thoughts are your hands. Sculpt what you want.

Chapter 11.6 What to read: "The Power of Thought" by William Atkinson

The essence of the phenomenon.
There are books that change your worldview. One of them is"The Power of Thought" by William AtkinsonThis is the foundation for understanding how our thoughts work and how to manage them. The book was written over 100 years ago, but it's still relevant today.

Operating principle.
Atkinson was one of the first to systematize knowledge about how thought influences reality. His book is not esotericism, but a practical guide to managing your consciousness. If you want to learn to control your thoughts rather than be their slave, this book is for you.

What does it consist of?
The main ideas of the book.

  1. Thought is energy.It is real and can be directed.
  2. Thought materializes.What you constantly think about comes into life.
  3. Suggestion and autosuggestion.You can program yourself.
  4. Thought forms.Thoughts create invisible structures.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find the book "The Power of Thought" by William Atkinson.It is freely available.
  2. Read slowly. Do not swallow, but think.
  3. Read with a pencil.Write down what's important. Take notes.
  4. Read with the desire to apply.Theory without practice is dead.
  5. Apply.After each chapter, do the exercises from there.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not reading at all. "I already know everything." You don't know.

Mistake 2: Read and forget. A book isn't just for show, but for life-changing reasons.

Mistake 3: Reading but not applying. Leaving knowledge in your head without translating it into action.

Example.
The manager spent years buying business bestsellers. He read two books a week, took notes, and felt smart. But the reality remained the same: the salary was the same, the problems were the same.

A colleague recommended a thin book, "The Power of Thought." He said, "Don't read it too fast. A couple of pages a day. And do the exercises."

The manager tried it. The first week seemed uninteresting. By the second, he noticed he'd stopped responding to criticism. By the third month, he'd changed his habits. A year later, he quit and started his own business.

He only read one book for real. But he did it.

Question:
When was the last time you read a book that actually changed your life?

Practical task.
Find the book "The Power of Thought" by William Atkinson.
Read the first chapter.
Apply one exercise from there.

MAIN:
Read the right books. Read to change.

Chapter 11.7 Mathematics and miracle: how do they fit together?
(short - full inChapter 1.21)

The essence of the phenomenon.
The book has two levels. The first is cold mathematics, calculations, systems, statistics. The second is self-confidence, the power of thought, superpowers, the concept of the superhuman. They are not contradictory. They are different floors of the same house.

Operating principle.
Mathematics is a map of the terrain. You know where the mountains are, where the rivers are, where the danger zones are.
A superman is your legs, your will, your ability to walk, even when your legs hurt, when it’s raining, when you see on the map that the goal is still far away.
Can you reach your destination without a map? Sometimes, if you're lucky. But more often, you'll end up in a swamp.
Can you reach your destination without legs, even with a perfect map? No. You'll lie there staring at the map, but you won't move anywhere.

---

Example.
The athlete was training for a marathon. He studied the theory of running, knew everything about heart rate, breathing, and nutrition. But he never went out to train. He said, "I'll study everything first, then I'll run."

His opponent didn't know the theory. But he ran every day—in the rain, in the snow, when he was sick, when he didn't feel like it. He didn't have a map. He just ran.

They met at the start of the marathon. The first ran 10 kilometers and lost his breath because he knew the theory, but his body wasn't ready. The second ran the entire distance. His legs knew how to run, even if his head didn't.

And the one who could combine the map and legs would become the champion.

Question:
Do you have a map?
Do you have the strength to walk?
What's missing?

Practical task.
Complete the 5-step connection diagram.
Write down your goal mathematically, and then find a source of faith and energy to achieve it.

MAIN:
A "miracle" is preparation meeting opportunity.
Without a foundation (preparation) there is no miracle.

Chapter 11.8 Psychics, shamans, witches - who are they?

The essence of the phenomenon.
We observe psychics, witches, and shamans—for them, their power is clearly manifest. For the rest, it lies dormant. They are not the chosen ones, they are simply those who were not afraid to develop what everyone has. These are people who have managed to awaken their inner giant.

Operating principle.
The difference between an ordinary person and a psychic isn't the presence of abilities, but that one develops them while the other doesn't. It's like muscles: everyone has them, but some are toned and some aren't.

What does it consist of?
What do all "special" people have in common?

  1. Sensitivity.They feel the world more subtly and notice what others miss.
  2. Faith.They believe in their abilities and have no doubts.
  3. Practice.They regularly train their gift and don’t give up.
  4. Courage.They are not afraid to be “strange” and do not look back at the opinion of the crowd.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Try to guess who is calling on the phone before you look.Write it down. Check it.
  2. Write down your dreams and check if they come true.Pay attention to the repeating images.
  3. Pay attention to the intuitive decisions that turned out to be correct.What did you feel? When did it happen?
  4. Train.Meditation, observation, silence.
  5. Don't go crazy.Don't abandon reality. Develop your abilities alongside your everyday life. Balance is key.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Denying the existence of such abilities. Considering all psychics to be frauds.

Mistake 2: Idolizing their owners. Thinking they're "chosen ones" and you're not.

Mistake 3: Giving up real life to "develop a superpower." Retreating to the forest, forgetting about family and work.

Example.
Two acquaintances shared a strange trait: one could often guess who was calling before even looking at the phone screen. The other could sense when a loved one was feeling ill, even thousands of miles away.

The first considered himself "just a lucky guy." The second decided to develop this ability: he kept a diary of his premonitions, tested them, analyzed his mistakes, and meditated.

Three years later, the second one could predict events down to the day. The first one remained "sometimes lucky."

They were asked: “Why can you do it, but he can’t?” The second one answered: “Because I train every day, but he doesn’t.”

Question:
Have you ever had a situation where you knew the answer even though logically you couldn't know it?

Practical task.
Try to guess who is calling on the phone before you look.
Write down your dreams and check if they come true.
Pay attention to the intuitive decisions that turned out to be correct.

MAIN:
Everyone has abilities. Like muscles. Some are toned, some aren't. The only difference is in the training.

Talent is 1% of nature. The remaining 99% is quiet, daily work on yourself. No days off.

Chapter 11.9 Critical Situations as Awakening

The essence of the phenomenon.
Superpowers often awaken at critical moments. When "normal" methods no longer work, when life or death is at stake, when there's no turning back. The body removes internal blocks and produces results that are impossible under normal conditions.

Operating principle.
In a critical situation, the brain switches off its inhibitions, mobilizes all resources, leaves no time for doubt, and activates intuition. What is usually blocked comes to the surface.

What does it consist of?
Why strength awakens in crisis.

  1. Disabling limiters.The brain removes the brakes, allowing what is usually prohibited.
  2. Full mobilization.All resources are thrown at the task.
  3. There is no time for doubts.You act, but don’t think.
  4. Intuition is turned on.Rationality doesn’t have time, ancient knowledge comes into play.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Remember your best state.When were you at your peak? What caused it?
  2. Analyze it.What was happening? What were the conditions? How did you feel?
  3. Try to reproduce the conditions.Don't wait for a critical situation. Learn to enter this state consciously.
  4. Practice entering a resourceful state.Breathing (deep, fast).
    Movement (physical activity to the limit).
    Risk (do something scary within safe limits).
    Deadline (set strict deadlines).
    Responsibility (put something important on the line).
  5. Don't wait for life to kick you in the ass.Learn to get up on your own.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Waiting for a critical situation to wake up. "When things get really tough, then..." It won't.

Mistake 2: Not learning to access the resource consciously. Hoping that it will "happen on its own."

Mistake 3: Overindulging in artificial stress. Constantly living in deadline mode without restoring your energy.

Example.
An ordinary office worker was driving his wife to the maternity hospital. She started having contractions on the highway, and the ambulance got stuck in traffic for an hour.

He delivered the baby himself. In the car. Without doctors. Without instruments. Everything went fine.

A year later, his wife asked, "Can you do it again?" He laughed, "No. I don't remember how I did it. I just did it. It was impossible not to do it."

He's not a doctor, not a rescuer, not a hero. He's an ordinary man. But at a critical moment, his body activated a reserve he didn't even know he had.

And then the reserve fell asleep again. Until next time.

Question:
In what situations have you felt a surge of incredible strength?

Practical task.
Remember your best state (when you were at your peak).
Analyze what caused it.
Try to reproduce the conditions.

MAIN:
He who trains in silence wins at the critical moment.

Chapter 11.10 The Limits of Superman

The essence of the phenomenon.
Yes, we have power. But we also have limitations. It's important to know both. Superhuman doesn't mean omnipotent. There are real boundaries (the laws of physics, biology, time), and there are imaginary ones (fears, beliefs, "that's not how it's done").

Operating principle.
The impossible is what you stillI didn't take it on.But there are real limitations, too. You can't live forever, you can't fly without technology, you can't overturn the laws of physics with your mind. It's important to distinguish real boundaries from imaginary ones.

What does it consist of?
Real limitations.

  1. Physical body.You can’t live forever, you can’t fly without technology.
  2. Laws of physics.They cannot be cancelled by the power of thought.
  3. Other people.They have their own will and cannot be controlled.
  4. Time.There is no turning back.

Imaginary restrictions (which can be removed).

  1. "I can't".It is removed by the experience of success.
  2. "That's not the way it's done."It is removed by courage.
  3. "I have no talent."It is removed by discipline.
  4. "It's too late to start."It features examples of success at any age.
  5. "This is impossible".It is removed by the facts that others have done it.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Write down your "impossibles".
  2. Opposite each one, write why it is actually impossible.Real law or just fear?
  3. For the "fear" items, make an action plan.What needs to be done to make this possible?
  4. For the "real law" points - accept.Don't waste your energy fighting gravity.
  5. Remember:Know your boundaries so you can expand them. But don't confuse boundaries with illusions.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not believing in yourself at all. Thinking that everything is "impossible."

Mistake 2: Thinking you can do anything, ignoring reality. Trying to fly without a plane.

Mistake 3: Confusing real boundaries with imaginary ones. Treating fear as a law of nature.

Example.
The startup founder became convinced of his own genius. He declared to his partners, "I can do anything! We'll take over the market within a year; we have no competition."

He stayed up nights, pressuring the team, demanding the impossible. People left. The money ran out. Six months later, the startup closed.

He came to his mentor: “I believed in myself and my abilities. Why didn’t it work out?”

"You've confused self-confidence with the abolition of reality," the mentor replied. "You can't change the laws of the market with the power of thought. But you can become more disciplined, more resilient, and smarter. A superhuman isn't someone who smashes through a wall with their forehead. It's someone who finds a door or builds a bridge."
The point: the superhuman is not omnipotent. His strength lies not in overriding the laws of nature and the market, but in his ability to grow, adapt, and find solutions.

Question:
Which of your “impossibles” are real and which are imaginary?

Practical task.
Write down your "impossibles".
Opposite each one, write why it is actually impossible (real law or just fear).
For the "fear" items, make an action plan.

MAIN:
You can only expand boundaries once you've discovered them. You can't expand what you can't see.

The only way to set the boundaries of the possible is to dare to go a little beyond them, into the impossible.

Chapter 11.11 Your Power Is in Your Hands

The essence of the phenomenon.
Everything that is written in this part is not so that you believe in your majesty and stop making calculations. But so that you know:you have the resourceYou can do more than you think. But this resource only works in conjunction with math and action.

Operating principle.
Your power isn't a gift to be waited for, nor a secret to be sought. It's a tool already lying before you.

You already have a head to think (Knowledge). You already have a heart to believe (Faith). You already have hands to do (Action). This isn't something you need to "earn" or "find." It's something you just need to take and apply.

The problem isn't a lack of power. The problem is that you're waiting for someone to put it in your hands. And it's already there.

What does it consist of?
Three components of strength.

  1. Knowledge.Mathematics, laws, systems. Where they come from: books, experience, mentors.
  2. Faith.Confidence that you can. Where does it come from: from victories, from within, from God.
  3. Actions.What you do every day. Where it comes from: discipline and habits.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Rate yourself on three parameters (knowledge, faith, actions) from 1 to 10. Honestly.
  2. Find the weakest one.The one with the lowest rating.
  3. Make a plan to strengthen it for the next month.
  4. If knowledge is weak— learn. Read, listen, study.
  5. If faith is weak— accumulate small victories. Start with easy tasks.
  6. If weak actions— Introduce discipline. Take small steps every day.
  7. Remember:Strength is not in knowledge. Not in faith. Not in actions. Strength is in their combination.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Relying solely on knowledge. Becoming a dry person who knows everything but does nothing.

Mistake 2: Relying solely on faith. Becoming a dreamer who believes but doesn't act.

Mistake 3: Relying solely on actions. Becoming a mindless workaholic who does a lot but doesn't understand why.

Example.
Three partners started a business.

The first was a "theoretician": he knew everything about the market, strategies, and competitors. But he didn't do anything—he just gave advice.

The second was a “believer”: he believed in success, charged with energy, but did not understand numbers and processes.

The third was a "worker": he toiled for days, but did not understand why and where they were going.

At first, we were moving along somehow. Then we started arguing: "Without my knowledge, you're nothing!" - "Without my faith, everything will fall apart!" - "Without my work, you're just chatterboxes!"

They dispersed to their corners. Business came to a standstill.

A mentor came to them and said, "We need all three of you. Knowledge without action is dead. Faith without knowledge is blind. Action without faith is meaningless."

They united. Business took off.

Question:
What is your weakest multiplier?

Practical task.
Rate yourself on three parameters (knowledge, faith, actions) from 1 to 10.
Find the weakest one.
Make a plan to strengthen it for the next month.

MAIN:
Knowledge, faith and action work only together.
No single component is more important than the others.


SUMMARY OF SECTION 11. SUPERMAN
Self-assessment checklist.

  • We are all superhumans.Everyone has power, it’s just sleeping.
  • Testimonies.The mother lifts the car - that's a fact.
  • Bio-suit.The body is the instrument, the soul is the operator.
  • The ladder of evolution.A person is not a peak, but a step.
  • The power of thought.Attention changes reality.
  • Atkinson's book.Read to change.
  • Mathematics and miracle.Two levels of one reality.
  • Psychics.They just developed what everyone has.
  • Critical situations.Learn to enter the resource consciously.
  • Borders.Knowing where it is really impossible.
  • Your strength.Knowledge x Faith x Actions.

MAIN:
You are superhuman. You are just sleeping. Wake up.

SECTION 12. HEALTH AND TOOLS
(How to keep your bio-suit in working order)

Introduction to the section

We've covered business, space, people, and superpowers. But all of this becomes meaningless if your "bio-suit" (body) breaks down.
Health is a basic asset. First-order zero.

You can be a brilliant entrepreneur, but if you don't get enough sleep, you'll make stupid decisions. You can be a free person, but if your body is falling apart, freedom is no fun. You can believe in superpowers, but if you don't drink water and breathe properly, that power won't awaken.

This section is all about practice. No philosophy. Specific tools: discipline, sleep, exercise (Tabata), fasting, morning exercises, vitamins, habits, productivity tools, water and breathing, stress and recovery.

MAIN:
Health is a fundamental asset. Without it, everything else is meaningless. Take care of it.

IMPORTANT WARNING
(Before the health section)

Everything written in this section is —my personal experienceI'm not a doctor, a nutritionist, or a trainer. I'm simply someone who has tried these methods on myself and am describing how they work for me.

Your body is not my body.

What works for me may be bad for you. Before you start fasting (Chapter 12.4), changing your sleep schedule (Chapter 12.2), or implementing intense exercise (Chapter 12.3) —consult a doctor.

I'm not responsible for your experiments with your health. You're an adult and responsible person. You make your own decisions. You're responsible for the consequences.

If you have chronic diseases, problems with your heart, blood pressure, stomach, endocrine system -Do not repeat my protocols without consulting a specialist..

I'm sharing my experience. Whether to use it or not is your choice and your responsibility. 

Chapter 12.0 The Main Rule of Health: Habits are more important than motivation

Health as a sector of the Global Plan

Health isn't a separate issue. It's one of the sectors of your Global Plan, just like finances, relationships, or self-development.

And the approach to it is the same: CON-TON-FON.

  • HORSEIn health, this is your ideal result: weight, sleep patterns, energy levels, exercise frequency. Not just "feeling good," but specific numbers and indicators.
  • TONE— these are your daily actions: exercise, nutrition, sleep, water, fasting. What you do today to achieve results.
  • BACKGROUNDIt's about maintaining the level you've achieved. When you're already healthy, already in shape, you simply make sure the system doesn't break down.

Habits are a secret weapon

Motivation is an emotion. It's here today, gone tomorrow. You can't build your health on motivation.

Health can only be built on habits.

A habit is an action that, after 30 days, no longer requires energy. You do it not because you "have to," but because it's already part of your day. Like brushing your teeth. Like drinking water in the morning.

Habits are the secret weapon for achieving any goal. Joyfully. Without force. Without the search for eternal motivation. You simply live according to a well-established daily routine—and the results will come naturally.

Body analyzer

The body is not the enemy. But the body is not the commander either. The body is a source of data.
It always, by default, strives for peace. This is a legacy of survival: the brain conserves energy.

He perceives any effort as a threat and will incline you to inaction.

So they don't "listen" to your body in the sense of "What do you want today, my dear?" They analyze your body. You collect data: how much you sleep, how much you eat, how much you move, what your heart rate is, what your energy level is. And based on that data, you adjust your plan. You don't cancel it. You adjust it.

How Training Works: The Mechanics of Momentum

Imagine a large steel ball. It's standing still. To get it rolling, you need a powerful impulse.

Training is the impetus. You give your body a jolt, kick-start all the metabolic processes, and put it into the right mode.

One workout lasts 3-4 days. Then the body craves rest again. So, it's time to give it another boost—2-3 times a week.

It's a cumulative effect: the more regularly you stimulate your body, the faster it recharges and the longer it holds its charge. The internal battery lasts longer.

Two or three Tabata workouts a week are enough.
Morning exercises are not necessary with this regimen—you've already charged your body with training.

Daily routine as a framework for health

Health isn't maintained by heroic efforts. It's maintained by daily routine.

Your habits are your routine. Wake up, drink water, eat, work, sleep—all at the same time, without straining yourself. This is the BACKGROUND to health: keeping your system running smoothly.

If your daily routine is structured correctly, you don’t need to exercise willpower.
Everything is going according to plan. You're just living. And your health is growing.

MAIN:
Health isn't about motivation, it's about habits. Your body needs to be tuned.
A boost workout will give you a boost for 3-4 days. Do it 2-3 times a week.

Chapter 12.1 On discipline (again, but about the body)

The essence of the phenomenon.
A quote from Bruce Lee encapsulates the entire concept of body care: "Discipline is not the restriction of freedom. It is the cutting away of all that is superfluous." Discipline in body care is not self-abuse, but self-care. It is the removal of what is destructive and the addition of what is strengthening.

Operating principle.
Health Result = (Good Habits) minus (Bad Habits). The bigger the gap, the healthier you are. Discipline helps to widen this gap. But it's important: there should be no violence against the body.
The body is your partner/friend, not your slave/enemy.

I used to think I had to get up at 4 or 5 a.m. to my alarm, through sheer force, with the thought, "I have to." That's a false strategy. THE BODY SHOULD NOT BE VIOLENTLY ABUSED! If you force it, sooner or later it will rebel—with illness, fatigue, depression.

What does it consist of?
Proper discipline is built on four principles.

  1. Listen to your body. Don't force it, but negotiate.
  2. Negotiate with him. Find a compromise between "need" and "want."
  3. Create habits. So that what's beneficial becomes natural.
  4. Notice what works. And if something disgusts you, look for another way.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Stop itdoing something that disgusts you, but you do it “you have to.”
  2. Find a wayDo the same with pleasure. If you don't like running in the morning, run in the evening. If you don't like running, swim. If you don't like the gym, dance.
  3. Don't force yourself.If an exercise causes pain, stop. If a routine causes stress, change the routine.
  4. Build habits.Small, but every day.
  5. Remember:Discipline isn't about beating yourself. It's about caring.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Confusing discipline with violence. Forcing yourself, hating every minute.

Mistake 2: Forcing yourself to do something you detest. Looking for the "right" way instead of your own.

Mistake 3: Not listening to your body's signals. Ignoring pain, fatigue, and discomfort.

Example.
One man decided to take up morning running. He forced himself to get up at 6 a.m., ran through sheer force, hated every minute. He quit after a month. Another man found a comfortable time—evenings—and ran for pleasure, and still does. The difference isn't in willpower, but in the fact that the second man found a way that doesn't force his body, but brings only joy from the process.

Question:
What do you do "by force"?
Maybe this can be done differently?

Practical task.
Stop doing what you feel disgusted about, but you do it “because you have to.”
Find a way to do the same with pleasure.

MAIN:
Respect for yourself begins with respect for your body.
And respect isn't violence. It's care, discipline, and attention.

Chapter 12.2. Sleep and Regime

The essence of the phenomenon.
Sleep is the primary recovery mechanism. Without quality sleep, all other health efforts are meaningless. You can eat right, exercise, and take vitamins—but if you don't get enough sleep, your body will deteriorate.

Operating principle.
Recovery = (Sleep Quality) x (Number of Hours).
Sleep is the time when the body repairs itself, the brain processes information, and the immune system is restored.
Lack of sleep kills productivity, immunity, the brain and the nervous system.

What does it consist of?
My sleep schedule is based on two principles.

  1. I go to bed at 10:00 PM to keep up with my biorhythms.
  2. I'm getting upwithoutalarm clock. The body itself knows how much it needs.

Why no alarm clock?
The alarm clock interrupts the sleep cycle.You wake up only when your body is ready.Sleep deprivation accumulates unnoticed. You may wake up at different times: 4 a.m., 5 a.m., 9 a.m.…that's normal.

Lunchtime nap (that's my thing). 
I'm a proponent of the 30+ minute "lunchtime nap." A 30-minute nap during the day gives you about an extra 3 hours of productivity in the evening. Take a 30-minute nap. It resets your brain, and you'll be just as productive in the evening as you were in the morning.

I have 2 lunchtime nap modes. 
First— according to plan: from 14:00 to 14:30 (or from 14:30 to 15:00).
Second— at random, whenever your body asks, at any time, sometimes twice a day. Listen to your body.

Upon awakening:If your body asks for another 5-10 minutes of sleep, do so calmly. It's better to wake up feeling "pleasure" rather than "violence" over your body and will.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. this weekTry going to bed at 10:00 pm.
  2. Put away the alarm clock,Get up without him (if you can).
  3. Add a midday nap30 minutes.
  4. Follow your sensations.Are you getting enough sleep? Do you have energy?
  5. Adjust it to suit yourself.Some people need 8 hours, some 7. Find your norm.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Considering sleep a waste of time. "I'd rather work and sleep later." You won't get enough sleep.

Mistake 2: Sleeping only 5-6 hours, hoping to get more done. Productivity drops, and your health deteriorates.

Mistake 3: Not taking your biorhythms into account. A night owl tries to become a morning person and suffers.

Example.
One person slept for five hours, drank gallons of coffee, and by age 40 had developed a plethora of ailments: high blood pressure, gastritis, and insomnia. Another slept for eight hours, including 30 minutes during the day, felt great, and accomplished more. The difference isn't in the number of tasks, but in the quality of recovery.

Question:
How much do you sleep?
Are you getting enough sleep?

Practical task.
This week, try going to bed at 10:00 pm.
Remove the alarm clock, wake up without it (if you can).
Add a 30-minute nap during the afternoon.

MAIN:
Sleep is sacred. Don't rob yourself of restoration.

Chapter 12.3 Physical Education: Tabata

The essence of the phenomenon.
I use Tabata—a series of short, high-intensity exercises developed by Japanese exercise specialist Izumi Tabata. It's the most effective way to tone your body in the shortest amount of time.

Operating principle.
Tabata is an interval training routine: 20 seconds of maximum effort, 10 seconds of rest. Repeat 8 times (4 minutes). That's one circuit.
After each circuit, rest for 2 minutes. You can do 2-3 circuits.

In 16-20 minutes you get a load comparable to an hour in the gym.

After these minutes you should feel like a squeezed lemon.
If you don’t feel it, it means you didn’t do it to your full potential.

What does it consist of?
The Tabata routine consists of three rounds.

  1. Round 1. 4 minutes: 20 seconds exercise, 10 seconds rest, repeat 8 times.
  2. Rest. 2 minutes.
  3. Round 2. 4 minutes: 20 seconds exercise, 10 seconds rest, repeat 8 times.
  4. Rest. 2 minutes.
  5. Round 3. 4 minutes: 20 seconds exercise, 10 seconds rest, repeat 8 times.
  6. Total: 16 minutes.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Find it in your phone"Tabata timer". There are many free apps.
  2. Choose exercises.Burpees (full body), squats (legs, butt), push-ups (chest, arms), lunges (legs), plank (abs), mountain climber (cardio, abs).
  3. Do 1 circle (4 minutes)Right now. Without thinking.
  4. Add it tomorrowsecond round.
  5. A week later - the third.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not doing it at full strength. If you're not tired and out of breath after 20 seconds, you're not at your maximum.

Mistake 2: Skipping days. Tabata is only effective if done consistently.

Mistake 3: Doing the same exercises over and over again. Your muscles get used to them, and their effectiveness decreases. Change it up.

Example.
Two people decided to take up exercise. The first went to the gym for two hours, but only once a week.

The second one did Tabata for 20 minutes, but three times a week. A month later, the second one was in better shape than the first: a toned body, more energy, and a better mood. And he spent six times less time.

Question:
When was the last time you worked out so hard you couldn't breathe?

Practical task.
Find "tabata timer" on your phone.
Do 1 lap (4 minutes) right now.
Add the second circle tomorrow.

MAIN:
Better 20 minutes of thoughtful work than two hours of useless effort. Quality over quantity.

Chapter 12.4 Fasting

The essence of the phenomenon.
Fasting isn't a weight-loss diet. It's a way to cleanse and reset your body. It's like a spring cleaning, only on the inside. Fasting triggers cleansing processes that simply don't work when you're constantly eating.

Operating principle.
When you don't eat, your body stops wasting energy on digestion and begins to cleanse itself. The process of autophagy kicks in.cells eat their own garbageThe Nobel Prize was awarded for this discovery in 2016.

What does it consist of?
My regimen (tested over the years): 1.5-day fast once every 2-3 weeks. Water only. Friday 5:00 PM to Sunday 10:00 AM.
(after this, you can eat anything, regardless of calories)

Scheme:

  1. Friday until 5:00 PM - eat normally.
  2. Friday 17:00 – last meal.
  3. Friday evening - only water.
  4. Saturday all day - only water.
  5. Sunday until 10:00 - water only.
  6. Sunday 10:00 – end of fasting (light meal).

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose the nearest weekend.Try a 36-hour fast.
  2. Drink water.A lot, constantly.
  3. Write down your feelings.What do you feel? When are you hungry? When does it go away?
  4. Exit correctly.Don't overeat. Start with light foods (vegetables, soup).
  5. If it's hard, don't suffer.Stop it, try next time.

Who shouldn't fast?Pregnant women. People with diabetes (without doctor's supervision). People with stomach ulcers. People with exhaustion.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Fearing hunger. Thinking you'll faint. You won't. The body can go without food for several days.

Mistake 2: Breaking the fast incorrectly. Pounce on food, binge eating. This is harmful.

Mistake 3: Not drinking water. Fasting isn't dry fasting. You absolutely need to drink.

Example.
One woman decided to try 24-hour fasting for the first time. She read about autophagy, watched interviews with doctors, and understood why it was necessary. She drank water, and at the first sign of hunger, she remembered, "My body is cleansing itself; that's normal." By evening, she felt lighter, her head clearer, and the morning heaviness had disappeared.

Her neighbor decided to join in. She hadn't prepared and didn't understand the process. By lunchtime, she had a headache, her mood was low, she ate a bun, and said, "Fasting isn't my thing."

They both did the same thing. The difference was in their understanding.

Question:
Have you tried fasting?
What does it feel like?

Practical task.
Choose the nearest weekend.
Try a 36-hour fast.
Write down your feelings.

MAIN:
Fasting works when there is an understanding of the process, rather than mechanical repetition.

Chapter 12.5 Morning Exercises

The essence of the phenomenon.
Don't confuse it with a workout. A warm-up is 2-3 minutes to wake up your body, get your blood pumping, and turn your brain on. It's not about muscles, it's about waking up.

Operating principle.
In the morning, the body is sluggish after sleep, blood circulation is slow, and the brain is still asleep. Exercises help get the blood flowing, wake up the joints, and activate the nervous system. The most important thing is to stand upside down or at least with your legs elevated to get the blood flowing to the brain.

What does it consist of?
My complex (2-3 minutes).

Stage 1. Warm-up (1 minute).Rotational and bending movements from top to bottom: neck - turns, bends; shoulders - rotations; arms - swings; body - bends, twists; pelvis - rotations; legs - swings, squats, rotations of the feet.

Stage 2. Handstand, headstand, or just legs up (1-3 minutes).This is the "most important thing." Blood rushes to the brain, it wakes up. If you're not prepared, just lie on your back and lift your legs up toward the wall or straight up.

*Important.You shouldn't do handstands or headstands more than twice a day. And only if you can do them. Don't risk it.*

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Tomorrow morningDo 1 minute of warm-up.
  2. Then 1-2 minutes with legs up.Lying on your back, lift your legs up toward the wall or just straight up. Do not do this more than twice a day.
  3. Compare your conditionWith a typical morning. More cheerful? Clearer?
  4. If there is possibility and preparation— Try a headstand or handstand. But only if you know how. Don't take any risks.
  5. Make it a habit.Every morning (if you did a Tabata workout 2-3 days ago, you don’t need to do morning exercises).

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not exercising at all. Wake up, drink coffee, and rush to work. Your body doesn't wake up until lunch.

Mistake 2: Doing a full workout in the morning and getting exhausted.
Exercise isn't a workout. It's a wake-up call.

Mistake 3: Ignoring inversions. Not paying attention to blood flow to the brain.

Example.
For years, the man woke up feeling groggy. Coffee didn't help, his brain only kicked in around lunchtime, and his productivity plummeted. He tried everything: contrast showers, energy drinks, going to bed early. Nothing worked.

A colleague advised: "Do three minutes of exercise. Not a workout—just wake up your body. And lie down for two minutes with your feet up." He tried it. The first few days, he was lazy. After a week, he noticed: he woke up easier. After a month, he stopped drinking coffee in the morning. His mind worked from the first hour.

Question:
What do you do in the morning to wake up?

Practical task.
Do a 1 minute warm-up tomorrow morning.
Then 1 minute with legs up.
Compare your condition with a normal morning.

MAIN:
In the morning, a short exercise and an inverted position (legs up) start the body better than coffee.

Chapter 12.6 Vitamins and Supplements

The essence of the phenomenon.
Our bodies don't get the vitamins they need in everyday life. The soil is depleted, the food isn't right, and stress is high. Supplements can help, but they need to be used wisely. They're not candy, they're medicine.

Operating principle.
Vitamins and supplements make up for what we don't get from food. But chugging them indiscriminately is a waste of money and a risk to your health. You should first get tested (in two or three places) to objectively determine what you're missing, and only then take them.

What does it consist of?
My approach (cautious): Use vitamins, but MEGA-CONSCIOUSLY.
Double-check everything, test it, don’t take anyone’s word for it!

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Get a blood testVitamins. Find out what you're really missing.
  2. Read reviews, research.Don't believe the advertising.
  3. Try.Take it for a month and see how you feel.
  4. Take a break.Don't drink constantly. Your body needs breaks.
  5. Repeat the test after 3 months.Check what has changed.

*Warning.An overdose of vitamins is just as dangerous as a deficiency.*
More doesn't mean better. Some vitamins can accumulate in the body and cause toxicity.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Taking vitamins without testing. "Everyone else takes them, so I'll take them too."

Mistake 2: Drinking everything in sight. By the handful, "just in case." Overdosing is dangerous.

Mistake 3: Thinking that vitamins replace normal food. No. They are a supplement, not a replacement.

Example.
One man was taking vitamins by the handful, "for immunity." He got tested and found he had too much vitamin D and not enough magnesium.

He stopped drinking everything and started drinking only what was necessary. His health improved.

But his friend continued to drink everything and after a year he developed kidney problems.

Question:
What vitamins do you take?
Based on analysis or just like that?

Practical task.
Get your blood tested for vitamins.
Based on the results, select additives.
Repeat the test after 3 months.

MAIN:
Vitamins are medicine, not candy. Take them seriously.

Chapter 12.7 On Habits and Retraining

The essence of the phenomenon.
A person can truly learn something only after 21 days. This isn't magic; it's the brain's physiology. It takes time to create new neural pathways. A habit is formed in about three weeks of regular repetition.

Operating principle.
The first seven days are a struggle; you have to force yourself. From days seven to 21, it gets easier. After 21 days, the habit is ingrained and requires no willpower. Formula: new habit = minimum 21 days.
Correcting an old habit = also at least 21 days.

What does it consist of?
Habit formation goes through three stages.

  1. 1-7 days. Resistance must be forced.
  2. Days 7-21. It gets easier.
  3. 21+ days. The habit is ingrained.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Choose one habit,that you want to implement. No more than one at a time.
  2. Do it for 21 days in a row.Don't miss it.
  3. Celebrate every dayin the calendar. Visualizing progress motivates.
  4. If you break down, don't quit.If you miss one day, continue the next. Don't miss two days in a row.
  5. Rate it in 21 days.Has it gotten easier? Are you used to it? Add the next one.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Trying to change overnight. "Starting Monday, I'll be a new person." It won't work.

Mistake 2: quitting after the first slip. "I missed a day, everything's lost." Nope. One slip is no big deal. Two in a row breaks the system.

Mistake 3: Not keeping track. Doing it "by eye" without marking it down. Visualization helps.

Example.
The woman wanted to learn English. She bought courses, downloaded apps, and enthusiastically started on Mondays. She studied for three hours, then gave up for a month. Three years later, she still made mistakes.

Her colleague learned the language differently: 20 minutes every day without fail. Even when he was sick, even when he was tired. The first week was difficult. By the third week, he got used to it. After three months, he was reading the news without any problems.

The difference isn't in ability. The difference is that one did it in spurts, while the other did it every day.

Question:
What habit do you want to implement?
How many times have you started already?

Practical task.
Choose one habit you want to implement.
Do it for 21 days in a row.
Mark every day on your calendar.

MAIN:
15-20 minutes daily works better than storming once a month.
A habit is formed in 21 days. Be patient. 

Habits are a secret weaponThey replace motivation and make achieving goals natural rather than heroic.

Chapter 12.8 Productivity Tools
(Evening Planning, 2-Minute Method)

The essence of the phenomenon.
There are simple tools that help you avoid spreading yourself too thin and get more done. I use two main ones: evening planning and the 2-Minute Method. They don't require any special skills, but they have a huge impact.

Operating principle.
Evening planning is when you know in advance what to do tomorrow. You don't waste the morning thinking about it.
The 2-Minute Method: When you're having trouble getting started, you tell yourself, "I'll do this in just 2 minutes." After 2 minutes, you're already hooked.

What does it consist of?

TOOL 1. Evening planning.Every night before bed, I write out my tasks for tomorrow. I prioritize them. I don't move on to the next task until I've completed it completely.

Plan format:

  • 9:00-11:00 — [Important and difficult] — DO IT FIRST
  • 11:00-13:00 — [Important, but simpler]
  • 14:00-15:00 — [Ongoing]
  • 15:00-16:00 — [Calls/Correspondence]
  • If there is time left - [Unimportant]

TOOL 2. The “2-minute” method.

  1. Set the timer for 2 minutes.
  2. Start doing what needs to be done.
  3. When the timer rings, ask yourself: “Should I stop or should I continue?”

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Every night before bedWrite a plan for tomorrow. Not in your head, on paper.
  2. Set priorities.The most important and difficult things come first.
  3. In the morning, start with the first point.Don't look at social media, don't drink coffee. Business first.
  4. If it's hard to start— set the timer for 2 minutes. Just start.
  5. Repeat every day.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not planning at all. Living as you please, grabbing whatever comes your way.

Mistake 2: Planning is too difficult. Schedule every hour, and then you run out of time and get frustrated.

Mistake 3: Not using the 2-Minute Method for complex tasks. Waiting for "inspiration" that never comes.

Example.
The manager was constantly working late. He was overwhelmed by tasks, urgent emails, and meetings—he'd be tackling one thing after another, and by evening he had no idea what he'd done at all.

A friend advised me to write out a plan for tomorrow in five minutes each evening: three main tasks, and the rest on a waiting list. And to start the morning not with email, but with the first task on the list.

The manager tried it. After a week, he started leaving an hour early. And for tasks he didn't want to start, he set a timer: "I'll do it in 2 minutes; if I don't get the hang of it, I'll quit." He almost always got the hang of it.

Question:
What are you putting off right now?

Practical task.
Find the task you've been putting off.
Set the timer for 2 minutes.
Do it.

MAIN:
Evening planning eliminates morning chaos.
The 2-minute method breaks resistance before the start.

Chapter 12.9 Water and Breathing

The essence of the phenomenon.
The two most basic things without which the body doesn't function. We often do both incorrectly. Water and breathing are the foundations of life, but we forget about them until we feel ill.

Operating principle.
The body is 60-70% water. Even mild dehydration reduces performance, worsens mood, and slows thinking.
Breathing is the only autonomic function that can be consciously controlled and through which one can influence one's state.

What does it consist of?

Water.Water requirement = weight (kg) x 0.03 = liters per day.
Example: 70 kg × 0.03 = 2.1 liters per day.

Rules for water intake:

  • Drink evenly throughout the day to avoid sudden changes.
  • Not in one gulp, so that it can be absorbed.
  • A glass of water immediately after waking up will get your body going.
  • A glass of water 30 minutes before a meal – prepare your stomach.

Breath.Techniques:

  • Square breathing. Inhale 4 times – pause 4 times – exhale 4 times – pause 4 times. Calming down.
  • Take a deep breath, exhale slowly. Inhale for 3 seconds, exhale for 6 seconds. Relax.
  • Hold your breath. Hold it on the exhale. Lung training.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Drink water according to the formulaAt least a week. Count how much you drank.
  2. Put down a bottle of wateron the desktop. So that you can see it.
  3. When you're stressed, do10 cycles of square breathing. Inhale 4 times, pause 4 times, exhale 4 times, pause 4 times.
  4. In the morning - a glass of water.In the evening, don’t drink a lot so you don’t have to get up at night.
  5. Monitor the color of your urine.Light - enough water. Dark - drink more.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Not drinking enough. Waiting until you're thirsty. Thirst is already a sign of dehydration.

Mistake 2: Drinking soda instead of water. Soda is not water.

Mistake 3: Breathing shallowly. Using your chest, not your stomach. Not saturating your body with oxygen.

Example.
The man felt constantly tired. He drank liters of coffee, and by evening his head ached, and his mood plummeted. He thought it was stress and lack of sleep.

The doctor asked at the appointment, "How much water do you drink a day?" "Well... tea, coffee, soup." "I mean pure water." It turned out he was drinking a glass a day, but he needed at least two liters.

He started carrying a bottle with him, sipping it throughout the day. Within a week, his headaches were gone, he no longer needed coffee, and his sleep became deeper.

Question:
How much water do you drink a day? Have you counted?

Practical task.
Drink water according to the formula for at least a week.
When stressed, do 10 cycles of square breathing.

MAIN:
Water and air are the basic, free fuel for the body.
Without them, your well-being suffers; with them, life is normal.

Chapter 12.10 Stress and Recovery

The essence of the phenomenon.
Stress isn't always bad. What's bad is the lack of recovery. Short-term stress (training, a deadline, a performance) makes us stronger. Chronic stress (constant pressure, fear) is destructive. The difference is the recovery period.

Operating principle.
Burnout is stress over time. Without recovery, burnout is inevitable. You're not a battery. You can't function without recharging. Rest is not a luxury, but a necessity.

What does it consist of?

Two types of stress.

  1. Useful (short). Training, deadline, performance. You become stronger after it.
  2. Harmful (chronic). Constant pressure, fear. The source needs to be removed.

Recovery methods.

  • Sleep. The main restorer.
  • Change of activity. Rest doesn't mean sitting on the couch, but rather changing your activity.
  • Nature. Reduces cortisol (the stress hormone).
  • Connecting with loved ones. Oxytocin (the hormone of trust and calm, which reduces anxiety) is produced.
  • Massage, sauna. Muscle relaxation.
  • Meditation. Relaxation for the brain.
  • Doing nothing. Just being, not doing.

Step-by-step diagram:

  1. Assess your current stress level(from 1 to 10).
  2. Estimate your recovery time(hours per week).
  3. If stress is high and recovery is low— Change your regime immediately.
  4. Take time to recoveron the calendar. Like a meeting with an important client.
  5. If you can't relax— Start small. 10 minutes of silence a day. Then 30. Then an hour.

Possible errors.

Mistake 1: Living in chronic stress without rest. "Once I finish the project, then I'll rest." Project after project, and there's no rest.

Mistake 2: Viewing rest as a waste of time. "I could be working during this time." No, I couldn't. Rest is an investment in productivity.

Mistake 3: Not noticing your body's signals. Irritability, insomnia, and a feeling of being out of control—these are signals. Don't ignore them.

Example.
The manager worked without days off for three months. At first, he felt a surge of energy—he managed to get everything done, and it seemed like that was how it should be.

In the fourth month, problems began: insomnia, irritability, mistakes in simple tasks, apathy.

He decided to "hang in there a little longer." Two weeks later, he was bedridden with fever, high blood pressure, and a complete loss of strength. The doctor said, "Chronic stress. My body couldn't handle it."

He took a vacation, turned off work chats, and got some sleep. He returned a week later, his productivity restored.

Question:
How much time do you devote to recovery?

Practical task.
Rate your current stress level (from 1 to 10).
Estimate your recovery time (hours per week).
If stress is high and recovery is low, change your regime immediately.

MAIN:
Stress is useful in short bursts.
Chronic stress without rest leads to failure.
Recovery is a must.


SECTION 12 SUMMARY: HEALTH AND TOOLS
Self-assessment checklist.

  • Discipline.Without violence, through habits, listening to the body.
  • Dream.Landmark: 10:00 PM, no alarm, afternoon nap. But listen to your body.
  • Physical education.Tabata - 10-20 minutes, but you can start with 3 sets.
  • Starvation.The benchmark is 36 hours, but you can start from 16.
  • Charger.2 minutes in the morning + legs up (or just stretch).
  • Vitamins.According to the analysis, consciously, 3 months for the result.
  • Habits.21 days to implement, but if it’s not your thing, look for another way.
  • Planning.Make a list in the evening, do it in the morning. Or in the morning – the main thing is to have a plan.
  • 2 minute method.Start small. If you're not into it, don't force it.
  • Water and breathing.Drink the norm (weight x 0.03), breathe consciously.
  • Stress.It takes as long to recover as it takes to exert yourself.

MAIN:
Health is a fundamental asset. Without it, everything else is meaningless. Take care of your body—you only have one. But take care of it wisely: listen to it, don't force it, find your own ways. All the tools in this section—try them on, adapt them to your needs, and keep what works.

BOOK TWO
(FOR CHILDREN AND THE SOUL)

(Only stories, parables, fairy tales)

Author: FreemanAV, a resident of planet Earth

Introduction to Book 2

A story about a father and son

The father was building a house. His seven-year-old son was hanging around, handing him nails, and watching.

The father said, "Bring the board," "Give me the hammer," "Move away, don't bother me." The son obeyed, but he didn't understand why some boards were laid lengthwise and others crosswise, why the nail had to be driven precisely here and not two centimeters to the left.

In the evening the father sat down next to his son and said:

"You didn't understand how to build a house. Because I gave you commands without explaining the process. Let's do it differently. I'll tell you why we're doing this, why the roof needs to be pitched and the foundation deep. And you'll remember the commands later."

The son fell asleep. And the father realized: children and those tired of complex words don't need instructions—they need stories.

Why this book?

The first book is a manual. For those who love diagrams, numbers, and algorithms.

The second book is about stories. For those tired of instructions. For children who haven't yet mastered formulas. For evenings when you want to listen rather than think.

There are no tables. No checklists. No step-by-step algorithms. Only parables, fairy tales, and real-life stories.

But don't be fooled by their simplicity. Each contains the same wisdom as Book One, just packaged in an easier-to-swallow form.

MAIN:
Some need blueprints. Others need stories. Both are true.

VOLUME 1. BUSINESS

SECTION 1. THINKING
(Stories and parables about right thinking)

Introduction to the section

The story of the tree and the gardener

In one garden there grew a tree. It was old and spreading, but the apples hanging on it were small and sour.

The gardener pondered for a long time what to do. He watered the leaves. He tinted the apples. He even tied large fake apples to the branches so the neighbors would think the harvest was good.

But the apples still remained sour.

One day a wise old man came, looked at the tree and said:

"You're treating the leaves. The problem is in the roots."

The gardener was surprised, but he obeyed. He began fertilizing the soil, watering the roots, and removing the weeds.

A year later, the tree produced such apples that the whole village came to try them.

MAIN:
If you want to change the fruits, change the roots. 

Chapter 1.1 The Tale of Two Ice Cream Sellers
(Entrepreneurial Thinking)

There were two ice cream vendors in the park. They sold the same product from identical stands.

The first salesman, when no one bought from him, would get upset: "People are greedy," "the weather is bad," "it's a bad day." He would be offended by everyone who passed by. By evening, he was angry at the whole world.

The second salesperson kept a notebook. He recorded the number of people who passed by, how many stopped, and how many bought. He calculated a 5% conversion rate. That meant that out of a hundred passersby, five would buy, and ninety-five would leave empty-handed.

He wasn't offended by the ninety-five. He was thinking, "How can I increase my conversion rate to 7%?" He changed the sign, added samples, and started smiling.

Within a month he was earning twice as much as the first.

Question:
Which of the sellers thought like an entrepreneur, and which like a disgruntled employee?

MAIN:
The entrepreneur sees rejections not as a personal drama, but as data for analysis.

Chapter 1.2 The Story of Grandma and the Pies
(Projects = Hypotheses)

One grandmother had the best pies in town. Juicy, golden-brown, with a variety of fillings. She baked them her whole life using her mother's recipe, and all the neighbors knew: if you want delicious pies, you should go to Grandma Masha.

One day her grandson asked her:

"Grandma, why don't you sell your pies? You could make money."

Grandma was offended:

— What do you mean, sell it? It's a family recipe. It's a tradition. It's the soul.

The grandson did not argue.

A year later, a bakery opened in their town. They made mediocre pies, but they sold them beautifully, with packaging and a sign. And for some reason, everyone went there.

Grandma looked at this and sighed:

— You see, people don’t understand what real pies are.

The grandson tried again:

- Grandma, let's try it. What do we have to lose?

Grandma waved her hand:

— Ah, youth. All you care about is business and money. But what about your soul?

Grandma's pies remained the best in town. Only the city forgot about it.

Question:
Why do you think grandma's pies were forgotten, even though they were the most delicious?

MAIN:
A family recipe is great. But if no one knows about it, you're just eating pies alone. 

Chapter 1.3 A Story About Chess and Checkers
(Business is a game with rules)

A grandfather taught his grandson to play chess. The grandson was smart and a quick learner, but he had one flaw: he constantly tried to win at any cost, even breaking the rules.

"Grandpa, why can't I walk in a straight line with my horse? What nonsense is this? I want to walk exactly like this right now."

Grandfather patiently explained:

"Because it's a game. Every game has rules. If you play by your rules and your opponent plays by theirs, it won't be a game, it'll be chaos."

The grandson didn't understand. He thought rules were boring, that they restricted freedom.

One day he went to play with a neighbor boy. He sat down at the board and started making up moves as he went along. The neighbor was surprised:

- You can't do that.

- Why?

- Because of the rules.

- Who invented them?

— I don’t know. But everyone plays like that.

The grandson was offended and left. He said to his grandfather:

— I don't want to play by other people's rules. I'll play my own game.

Grandfather sighed:

— Play, of course. Just remember: no one will play your game except you.

Question:
Do you like to play by the rules or do you make up your own?

MAIN:
You can invent your own game. But then don't be surprised that no one plays it.

Chapter 1.4: The Tale of Five Hats
(Projects = Temporary money chains)

Once upon a time there lived a master hatter. He made hats—beautiful, comfortable, and durable. Everyone in town wore his hats.

But hats don't last forever. Time passes, fashion changes, and people stop wearing hats.

The master became sad. He thought his business was dead.

And then he looked at his hands and realized: they were still golden. And he began making not hats, but caps. Then Panama hats. Then berets.

Every time one fashion trend went away, he switched to another. And he was always busy.

Question:
What is more important - the work or the hands that do it?

MAIN:
The case may end. But the master remains. Find a new case.

Chapter 1.5 The Story of the Gardener and His Garden
(Build the system - forget about the goals)

A gardener dreamed of growing the most beautiful rose in the world. He planted it, watered it, fertilized it, and talked to it. But the rose grew slowly, and the gardener was frustrated.

Another gardener simply tended the entire garden: watering all the flowers, loosening the soil, and removing weeds. He wasn't focusing on any one rose—he simply did his job every day.

A year later, the first gardener's rose still hadn't bloomed—he'd pressed it too hard. But the second gardener's entire garden was fragrant.

Question:
Which of the gardeners acted wiser?

MAIN:
Don't chase one goal. Build a system, and the results will come. 

Chapter 1.6 The Story of an Old Actor and His Roles
(The Master and His Workpieces: A Lesson from Andrei Mironov)

In a certain city there lived an old actor. Everyone knew him. When he walked on stage, the audience would freeze. He seemed to play each role effortlessly, as if reborn.

One day a young actor asked him:

"Master, what's your secret? Do you have any presets you use for your roles?"

The old actor smiled and replied:

- Look. If the actor5 blanks— he justamateurHe'll go on stage, something unexpected will happen, and he'll be at a loss.

If the actor50 blanks - He a good specialistReliable, but still has trouble remembering what to do.

And if the actor500 blanks— like mine — then it can be consideredMasterThere's no need to remember the ingredients anymore. They live within him. They come out on their own when needed.

The young actor was surprised:

— Five hundred pieces? That's a lot to learn, a lot to rehearse!

"Yes," the old actor nodded. "I rehearsed every day. I played hundreds of roles, even ones I didn't like. I failed, I made mistakes, but I never gave up. And now these preparations have become my reflexes."

“And the audience thinks you’re a genius,” said the young actor.

"Genius isn't being born with talent," the old man replied. "Genius is having developed five hundred or more ideas. Don't wait for inspiration. Build an arsenal."

And the old actor also said at the end:«And never be arrogant.There is always a Master who is better prepared than you."

Question:
How many "blanks" are in your business? 5, 50, or are you already aiming for 500?

Main:
Genius isn't spontaneity. It's 500 well-developed patterns and 10,000 hours of practice that have become your reflexes.

Chapter 1.7 The Story of Three Doors
(Three types of scaling)

Once upon a time there lived a merchant. He sold apples. At first, he sold 10 apples a day from a stand. Then he opened a shop and sold 100 apples a day. Then he wanted more.

He approached three doors.

The first door led to the market square. There, even more stalls could be set up and even more apples sold.

The second door led to a town where no one had ever seen apples. A new shop could be opened there.

The third door led to a garden where pears grew. It was possible to start selling more than just apples.

The merchant thought for a moment and chose the first door. When the square became crowded, he went to the second. And then to the third.

Question:
Which door would you open first?

MAIN:
First, grow where you're already growing. Then go to new places. And then try something new.

Chapter 1.8 The Story of the Archer and the Target
(Focus ONLY on the result)

The archery teacher placed a target in front of the students.

“Shoot,” he said.

The first student took a long time to aim, adjust his aim, check the wind, and look at the bowstring. But he still didn't release the arrow.

The second student fired the arrow quickly, without even aiming. The arrow flew into the bushes.

The third student looked at the target, took aim, and released the arrow. He hit the bull's eye.

“How did you do it?” they asked him.

"I just wanted to hit the target," he replied. "Not aim beautifully or shoot quickly. I wanted results."

Question:
Which of the students did the right thing?

MAIN:
The main thing is to hit the target. Everything else is unimportant.

Chapter 1.9 The Story of the Water Wheel
(Closed loop of the money chain)

A miller built a water wheel. The water flows, the wheel turns, and the millstones grind flour. The flour is sold, and the proceeds are used to repair the wheel so it keeps turning.

The neighbor built a wheel, ground some flour, sold it, and drank the money away. The wheel broke, and there was no money to fix it.

The wheel stands still, not turning. And there is no flour. And no money.

Question:
Why does one miller's wheel always turn, while another's is broken?

MAIN:
Always use part of what you earn to keep the wheel turning.

Chapter 1.10 The Story of the Smart Tailor
(Minimalism of knowledge)

A tailor needed a suit made. A customer came to him and said:

— I want you to study the history of costume over the last 200 years, the theory of fabrics, the chemistry of dyes, the psychology of color, and only then sew.

The tailor was surprised:

— Why? I already know how to sew. I’ll just make you a nice suit.

Sewed it. The customer was satisfied.

And the neighboring tailor began to study everything, but never started sewing.

Question:
Which tailor turned out to be smarter?

MAIN:
You don't need to know rocket science to bake a pie. You just need to know how to knead dough. That's the quickest way to success.

Chapter 1.11 The Story of the Apple Tree and the Apples
(Low-hanging fruits)

There stood an apple tree. Ripe, juicy apples hung on the lower branches, within easy reach. On the upper branches hung one huge, beautiful apple, but high up.

Vasya arrived. He saw the top apple and climbed for it. He climbed for a long time, got scratched, and almost fell. He plucked the apple and climbed down, leaving the lower ones hanging.

Petya arrived. He collected all the bottom apples, a full basket. Then he looked at the top one, thought about it, and climbed on. If it fell, the basket was already full, no big deal.

Question:
Who is smarter - Vasya or Petya?

MAIN:
First, gather what's at hand. Then climb for what's high up. 

Chapter 1.12 The Story of the Hedgehog and the Hare
(Know your Archetype and Psychotype)

The animals gathered to run a race. The hare is fast, the hedgehog is slow.

The hare says:

- Come on, hedgehog, let's run!

And the hedgehog replies:

"I can't run. I'd rather dig a hole, pick mushrooms, or curl up into a ball if there's danger."

The hare laughed and ran off. And the hedgehog went about his business.

In the evening the hare came running tired, and the hedgehog happy, with a full basket of mushrooms.

Question:
Which of the animals acted wiser?

MAIN:
Don't try to be a hare if you're a hedgehog. Do what you do best.

Chapter 1.13 The Story of the Foreman and the Spare Drills
(Human factor)

The foreman at the construction site used a single, reliable drill bit for his hammer drill. He drilled all day long without any problems.
At the most crucial moment, the drill broke. The foreman was furious: "How could this happen? I was keeping an eye on it! Why such a bad time?" He threw it away and went to get a new one. Within an hour, the entire construction site was at a standstill.

The old master asked, "How many drills do you have?" "One. The best!" "There should be at least three. One in use, one in reserve, and one being sharpened. Tools breaking is not a disaster, it's a normal occurrence."

The foreman bought two spare drills. A month later, the main one broke—he didn't even notice, just grabbed the next one. Work didn't stop for a minute.

Question:
Why does a professional always carry a spare tool?

MAIN:
People, tools and processes sometimes break down.
The pro doesn't get angry - he prepares a replacement in advance.

Chapter 1.14 The Story of the Coach and the Three Rules
(Any system is working)

The coach opened a Sambo club. Ten people came in the first year. He didn't give up. He held training sessions every day, taught, and explained.

Fifteen came in the second year. Some left, some stayed. The coach didn't change the schedule, didn't look for magic methods. He simply did his job every day.

By the third year, 30 had arrived. By this point, the first students were already helping the newcomers themselves. The system began to function automatically.

Another trainer opened a section nearby. If he didn't fill a group within a month, he'd close it. Then he'd open a new section, but with a different name. Then another. He never got around to getting the system up and running.

“You were just lucky,” he said.

"No," the first coach replied. "I just gave the system time." "I understand, I need: an influx (students), time (three years), a critical mass (the first students start helping themselves). No magic or abstract 'magic words.'"

Question:
Why did the first coach's section grow, but the second's didn't?

MAIN:
Any system works if it has a flow of people, time and patience to reach critical mass.

Chapter 1.15 The Story of the Seed and the Tree
(Timeframe: 2-3 years maximum)

A man plants a seed. He waits a day – it doesn't grow. He waits a week – the sprout is tiny. He waits a month – the stem is thin.

The neighbor came and said:

"Why are you messing around with it? Plant a big tree right away so it will bear fruit."

"Where am I supposed to get a big tree?" the man asked in surprise.

- Well, I don't know. Steal it, perhaps.

The man didn't steal. He watered his tree for three years. And in the fourth year, he harvested his first apple crop.

The neighbor continued to walk around without apples.

Question:
How many years does it take for a tree to bear fruit?

MAIN:
A large tree doesn't grow in a month. Wait and care for it for three years, and there will be a harvest.

Chapter 1.16 The Tale of Three Whales and One Ship
(Three questions that define everything)

A beautiful ship sailed on a certain sea. It had white sails, tall masts, and a brave crew. But one day, the ship lost its course and was caught in a storm.

"Captain, we're going to die!" the sailors shouted. "We don't know where to go!"

The captain came out on deck and said:

"Don't be afraid. I know what will save us. Three enormous whales live in this sea. They hold the whole world on their backs. We need to find them and ask for directions."

The crew didn't believe it, but there was nothing they could do. They sailed off to look for whales.

Firstthey metA whale named Who-Am-IHe was the biggest and the oldest. He asked the captain:

"Captain, who are you? Do you believe you can swim home? Or do you consider yourself a lousy sailor?"

The captain thought for a moment. It was true, there was a fear inside him that he was a bad captain. Keith said:

— Remember: you are who you think you are. If there is fear inside you, you will drown. If there is faith inside you, you will swim.

Secondthey metA whale named Where-I-SailHe was quick and sharp-sighted.

"Captain, where are you even going?" he asked. "If you're just sailing 'somewhere,' you'll never get anywhere. You need to know the exact address. Where's your home?"

The captain looked at the map and said firmly: “My home is behind that mountain, in a quiet bay.”

Thirdthey metA whale named What-I-Will-GiveHe was the harshest.

"Captain, what are you willing to give for your salvation?" he asked. "Are you willing to stay up all night, standing at the helm? Are you willing to give up some of your cargo to mend the sails? Or do you just want to be washed ashore for free?"

The captain understands. He is ready. He is ready to fight.

Three whales smiled and showed him the way. The ship sailed home.

Question:
Do you know your three pillars?
Who are you? Where are you sailing? And what are you willing to give for your dream?

MAIN:
Three pillars hold the world together: Who I am, Where I'm going, and What I'm willing to give.

Chapter 1.17 A Tale of Two Builders
(Ability to look into the future)

Two builders were building a house.

The first builder thought only of today. In the morning, he asked himself, "What do I need to do right now to get paid by evening?" He laid bricks, mixed mortar, and by evening, he was so tired he could barely stand.

The second builder thought differently. In the morning, he asked himself, "What can I do today to ensure this house will still be standing in 10 years and that people will live happily in it?" He selected the best materials, checked the foundation, and thought about how the water would flow and how the sun would warm it.

Ten years passed. The first builder's house began to fall apart. The roof leaked, and the walls cracked. But the second builder's house stood as good as new.

People were surprised: "How did you manage to build such a strong house?" And the builder replied: "I simply asked myself the right questions. Not about today, but about the future."

Question:
What do you think about more often - what will happen tonight, or what will happen in many years?

MAIN:
The quality of your life is equal to the quality of the questions you ask yourself. Ask questions about the future, and the future will be wonderful.

Chapter 1.18 The Story of the Magic Wallet
(The main goal of the first stage)

Once upon a time there was a boy. An ordinary boy who really wanted to become an adult and independent.

Every morning he woke up and thought: "When I grow up, I'll buy a car! When I grow up, I'll build a house! When I grow up, I'll travel the world!"

But for now he was only dreaming. And life was passing him by.

One day he met an old man sitting on a bench, calmly watching the sunset. The boy approached and asked:

- Grandpa, what do you dream about?

The old man smiled:

"I don't dream anymore, grandson. I just live."

"What do you mean, just living?" the boy asked in surprise. "Is it really possible to just live when I want so much?"

The old man looked at him carefully and said:

- Come on, I'll show you my secret.

They came to a small house. The old man opened the box and took out... an ordinary wallet.

"Look," he said. "Every month, money comes into this wallet. On its own. Without my participation. Not much, but just enough so that I don't have to worry about my daily bread."

The boy looked into the wallet. Sure enough, there were coins in there.

"So what?" he didn't understand. "It's just money."

“Look at me,” said the old man. “I’m calm. I’m not nervous. I’m not afraid of tomorrow. I can sit at sunset and just look at the sky. Do you know why?”

- Why?

"Because I have this wallet. Once, many years ago, I planted a small tree. I watered it, looked after it, never abandoned it. And now it's grown and bears fruit for me every month. Not much, but steadily."

The boy thought.

- And I thought we had to go straight for millions...

"Millions—that's later," the old man replied. "First, make sure you have a wallet like this. 500-1000 dollars a month—automatically. An amount that will cover your basic needs of 'I want to eat,' 'I want to live,' 'I want to breathe easily.'"

- How to do this?

— There are four steps. And they are suitable for anyone who is ready to stay in one place for two to three years.

Step one: Learn to create content.
Text, video, images. Anyone can do it now—there are tools to help.

Step two. Learn to drive traffic.
Content + consistency + platforms = people who see you.

Step three: Enter a self-replicating business.
One where your income is calculated not just from your sales, but from the sales of everyone you brought in. And everyone they brought in. And so on ad infinitum.

Step four. Reach critical mass.
Enough people for the system to start working on its own. Without you.

"And then what?" the boy asked.

— And then you choose. If you want, you can grow further. If you want, you can build an empire. If you want, you can just live and watch the sunsets. The main thing is that you will havebaseAnd without a base, any choice is an illusion.

The boy looked at the setting sun, at the calm old man, at his magic wallet...

And suddenly he realized: he had dreamed of great victories all his life, but had not even thought about the most important thing - about a quiet harbor from which to set out for these victories.

Question:
Do you have such a “magic wallet”?
Or are you still chasing millions without basic protection?

MAIN:
First, create a magic wallet. Then conquer the worlds.
Without a base, any hike is simply an escape from fear.

Chapter 1.19 The Story of the Sculptor and the Block of Marble
(Formula TO BE→ACT→ HAVE)

Once upon a time there lived a sculptor. One day he saw a huge block of marble and said, "A beautiful angel is hidden here. I will free him."

People laughed at him: "Are you crazy? It's just a stone. There's no angel there."

But the sculptor saw it differently. He already knew that there was an angel inside the stone. He already saw his face, his wings, his smile. He alreadywasto those who create angels.

And he began to work. Day after day he cut away everything unnecessary. Heactedjust as one acts who sees an angel in a stone.

A year later, a beautiful statue stood in the square. People came to look at it and admire it. The sculptorhadwhat I saw in my imagination.

"How did you do it?" they asked him.

"I simply removed everything unnecessary," the sculptor replied. "The angel was there from the very beginning. I simply helped him appear."

Question:
What do you want to create in your life?
Do you see it now, as the sculptor saw the angel?

MAIN:
First BE. Then DO. HAVING is simply a result.
Don't confuse the order.

Chapter 1.20 The Story of Three Brothers and the Family Castle
(Three steps to a new identity: Awareness, Acceptance, Attitude)

An old family owned a castle. A beautiful stone one, with towers and oak gates. But over time, it fell into disrepair. The walls were cracked, the roof was leaking, the gates were crooked.

An old gentleman had three sons. Before he died, he called them and said:

"The castle is ours. Whoever restores it to its former glory will become its master."

The brothers went their separate ways.

---

First brother, Ivan, got right down to business. He bought new paint, boards, and nails. He didn't sleep at night, working from dawn to dusk.

But as he looked at the castle, a voice inside him said, "This isn't mine. I'm just a worker. Why am I doing this? Who needs this?"

He painted the walls, but felt tired. He repaired the roof, but felt angry. After a month, he gave up and left.

“It’s too hard,” he said to his brothers.

---

The second brother, Peter, was smart. He sat on the hill, looked at the castle for a long time, and honestly said to himself:

"I see what needs to be done. Here are the walls—they need to be reinforced. Here is the roof—it needs to be re-roofed. Here are the gates—they need to be replaced. It will take three years. For three years, I won't go for walks, I won't vacation, I won't spend money on myself. I'm ready."

He accepted the price. He didn't whine. He simply said, "It's unavoidable. I'm paying."

But the cold still lingered within him. He did what he had to, but his heart was silent. The lock grew stronger, but Peter didn't feel like he was "master" of it.

---

The third brother, Semyon, acted differently.

At first he did the same as Peter. He sat down on the hill, looked at the castle, and honestly said to himself:

"It's going to be tough. Three years without rest. I'm ready."

And then he took another step. He entered the castle and said:

"This isn't just stone and wood. This is my father's house. This is my children's house. This is mine. I'm not a 'worker' here. I'm the owner."

From that day on, everything changed.

He didn't "paint the walls" - hedecorated his houseHe didn't "fix the roof" - heprotected his family from the rainHe didn't "change the goal" - hebuilt an entrance for his grandchildren.

Work is no longer work.

---

Three years later, the castle stood as good as new. The walls shone, the roof gleamed, the gates were strong and beautiful.

Ivan came to see and said:

- How did you manage? I tried too, but it didn't work.

Semyon replied:

- You triedwork. And I firstUnderstood, what will it cost me (Realization). Thenaccepted this pricewithout bargaining and grievances (Acceptance). And then he stopped being an employee andbecame the Master.I fell in love with the castle. Not because I "had to," but because it was mine (Attitude).

Peter stood nearby, listened, and suddenly realized:

"I took the first two steps. I realized the price. I accepted it. But I still didn't love the castle. I was building something that wasn't mine. That's why it was cold inside."
