Semyon put his hand on his brother’s shoulder:

— You can start now. Every day is a new choice.

---

Question:
Have you ever done something that was necessary, but inside you felt heavy and empty?
Which of the three steps did you miss?
Awareness? Acceptance? Or Attitude?

MAIN:
You can't love a business until you realize and accept its value.
First, self-honesty. Then, acceptance of the facts. Then, true love and the Master's attitude.

Chapter 1.21 The Story of the Map and the Legs (Mathematics and Miracles)

One traveler bought the most detailed map of the world. He studied it inside and out, knowing every pass, every village.

But he never went on the trip.

“Why?” he said. “I already know everything.”

The other traveler didn't have a map. He simply walked wherever his eyes led him. He often hit dead ends and wandered in circles.

One day they met. The first was sitting with a map, but he hadn't gone anywhere. The second, dirty and tired, was climbing out of yet another swamp.

"Let's do it together," said the second one. "You have the map, I have the legs."

We went together. And we got there.

Question:
Who was more important - the map or the legs?

MAIN:
A map without legs is dead. Legs without a map are blind.
Only together they lead to the goal.

Chapter 1.22 The Tale of Two Brothers and a Magic Mill
(The Complexity Barrier: Why Big Money Is Where Things Are Complex)

Once upon a time there were two brothers. Both wanted to build a mill to grind flour and sell it in town.

The eldest brother, Ivan, was simple and impatient. He said, "What's there to think about? Here's the river, here are the stones. I'll quickly build it and start raking in the cash." He built a wall of stones, placed the wheel directly in the water, poured in some grain, and waited. The wheel spun a bit and then stopped. The water seeped into the cracks, the grain got wet, and nothing came of it. Ivan spat and left. "It's not my thing," he said.

The younger brother, Pyotr, was diligent and loved to figure things out. First, he sat down and drew the entire mill on the ground. Where the dam would be so the water would flow evenly. Where the trough would be so the water would hit the blades at the right angle. Where the millstones would be, how they would turn, and where the flour would be collected.

Then he started building. First the dam—the water wouldn't obey, so he had to rearrange the stones three times. Then the trough—he had to adjust the angle so the wheel would turn quickly. Then the millstones—they would get stuck or grind too coarsely. Pyotr fiddled around for a month, two, three. The neighbors laughed: "Your brother built it in a day and left, and you're still digging."

But Peter didn't give up. He tweaked and tweaked his mill. And one day, it started working. The wheel spun smoothly, the millstones ground fine flour, and the sacks filled one by one.

Peter hired an assistant to look after the mill, while he began building a second one in a neighboring village. Three years later, he had five mills. He no longer carried the sacks himself, but simply walked around and checked that everything was working properly.

Ivan came to him and asked: "How did you do it? I also had a river, and stones, and grain. Why didn't it work for me?"

Peter replied, "Because you wanted it to be simple. But a mill is complicated. Everything needs to be fine-tuned. I wasn't afraid of this complexity, and that's why I succeeded."

"But you know what's most interesting?" Peter added. "Once I got everything set up, the mill became simple. The water flows, the wheel turns, the flour pours out. It's complex on the inside, but simple on the outside. And that's the whole point."

Question:
Why do you think Ivan failed to build a mill, but Peter succeeded?
Which of them is closer to you?

MAIN:
What brings much torment is never simple inside.
But on the outside, it must be simple. Don't be afraid of the complexities along the way—but build something simple in the end.


SECTION 1 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.

· I know 22 stories about how the mind of a successful person works?
· I understand that knowledge is not the main thing, the main thing is to apply it?
· What story do I remember the most?
· What lesson from this section can I apply today?

MAIN:
Thinking is the foundation. Everything else is built on it.
Strengthen the foundation and the house will not collapse.

SECTION 2. SYSTEMS
(Stories and parables about the laws of life and money)

Introduction to the section

A story about two brothers and boats

Once upon a time there were two brothers. Both wanted to build a boat to sail down a stream.

The first brother made a boat by eye. It was beautiful, with a sail and a flag. He launched it, but the boat capsized and sank.

The second brother sat on the shore for a long time. He watched the water flow, the wind blow, the driftwood. Then he took a plank, calculated the dimensions, and made a keel for stability.

I launched it into the water and the boat sailed smoothly and far.

"Why did you succeed and I didn't?" asked the first brother.

“Because I studied the laws first,” the second replied. “And then I built.”

MAIN:
The laws don't lie. You just need to know them.

Chapter 2.1 A Tale of Two Bakers on the Same Street
(Entrepreneurial Thinking)

Two bakers lived on the same street. Both baked equally delicious bread.

The first baker, when no one bought from him, would be sad: "People don't appreciate my work; everyone has bad taste." He would get offended, angry, and change nothing.

The second baker, when he saw that customers were few, said to himself, "It's not a problem—it's data." He counted how many people passed by each day, how many came in, and how many bought. He realized the conversion rate was low. He changed the sign, installed a sampler table, and extended the opening hours.

A month later, a line formed around him. The first one still had no one.

“You were just lucky,” said the first one.

"No," replied the second. "I was just thinking like a businessman, not like an offended baker."

Question:
Which bakers acted as entrepreneurs and which as artisans?

MAIN:
An entrepreneur sees a problem as data for analysis, not as a reason for resentment.

Chapter 2.2 A Tale of Two Apartments (Assets and Liabilities)

Two brothers each inherited an apartment from their grandmother.

The older brother, Nikolai, moved into his own apartment, renovated it, and bought furniture. He paid the utilities every month, and when something broke, he fixed it. The money just went away.

The younger brother, Mikhail, didn't move into the apartment. He said, "I'll stay with my wife for now and rent it out." He rented it out to good people. The tenants paid the utilities, and they also brought in rent. Every month, Mikhail had extra money.

Five years later, Nikolai was still working and barely making ends meet. Mikhail, meanwhile, used the rent to open a small bakery.

The same apartment. The same walls. But it made one poorer, the other richer.

Question:
Do you have things that bring you money? Or only things that take it away?

MAIN:
The active puts money in his pocket. The passive takes it out.

Chapter 2.3 The Story of Petrovich and His Vedomosti
(Cost of Living / Lifecost)

Once upon a time there lived a man named Petrovich. He worked at a factory and earned 50,000 rubles a month. Seemed normal by the standards of his town. But by the end of the month, he always ran out of money. Always.

"Petrovich, where do you spend your money?" the friends asked.

"How should I know?" Petrovich asked, surprised. "I don't really spend it on anything."

One day, his daughter, a student, came home for the holidays. She saw her father struggling and said:

- Dad, let me help you. I'll write down all your expenses for a month.

Petrovich initially refused, but his daughter insisted.

For a whole month, she followed him around with a notebook. Petrovich bought sunflower seeds—she wrote them down. Petrovich lent money to a neighbor—she wrote them down. Petrovich bought beer with dried roach—she wrote them down.

At the end of the month she sat down and counted.

"Dad, do you know how much you spend on little things?" she asked.

“Well, a thousand, probably,” Petrovich shrugged.

"Eight and a half thousand," said the daughter. "Sunflower seeds, beer, cigarettes, chips, coffee from the kiosk. Eight and a half thousand a month."

Petrovich was stunned. He hadn't even noticed these expenses.

Question:
Do you know how much money you spend on small joys?
Try to count.

MAIN:
You can't get where you're going if you don't know where you're starting from.

Chapter 2.4 The Story of the Five Brothers and Their Characters
(Know your Archetype and Psychotype)

Once upon a time there were five brothers. All from the same parents, all raised in the same house, but with different personalities.

First brother, IvanHe was like a king. He loved order, bossed his younger brothers around, and assigned who would wash the dishes and who would go buy bread. His brothers obeyed him because Ivan always thought of everyone at once.

The second brother, PeterHe was a warrior. He'd fight at the slightest provocation, argue until he was hoarse, and protect the younger brothers from bullies in the yard. Without him, his brothers would be scared, but with him, it was sometimes noisy.

The third brother, Stepan, was a keeper. He picked mushrooms, dried them for the winter, fixed broken toys, and saved money in a piggy bank. He always said, "Don't spend it all at once, save it for tomorrow."

The fourth brother, AndreyHe was a magician. He loved reading books, concocting clever plans, and building construction kits. If something broke, Andrei didn't fight or complain, but sat down and figured out how to fix it with his mind, not force.

Fifth brother, AlexeyHe was a clown. He was always laughing, making up games, teasing his elders, but he did it so cheerfully that no one took offense. When his brothers were arguing, Alexey would come out and say, "Let's play snowballs together!" and everyone would make up.

They also had friends with different temperaments.

KolyaHe was choleric—quick as lightning, fired up by any idea, but quickly cooled. If it was necessary to run for a doctor, Kolya was the first to arrive. If he had to sit and wait, Kolya couldn't handle it; he ran away.

MishaHe was sanguine—cheerful, sociable, friends with everyone, but he could be friends with one person today and another tomorrow. Not out of malice, just his character.

FedyaHe was phlegmatic—slow, calm, reliable. If Fedya promised to come, he did. If he had homework to do, he did it—not quickly, but thoroughly.

GrishaHe was a melancholic person - he experienced everything deeply, felt it subtly, but if he was offended, he could cry and withdraw into himself.

And the brothers realized: they were all different. Ivan couldn't be a jester, and Alyosha couldn't be a king. Kolya couldn't sit still, and Fedya couldn't run around like crazy. And that was okay. The main thing was to know who you were and do your own thing.

Question:
Which of your brothers are you like?
And which of your friends?

MAIN:
Each of us has all five of these within us. The main thing is to activate the right one at the right time.

Chapter 2.5 The Story of Uncle Vitya and the Four Roads
(Cashflow Quadrant)

Four neighbors lived in the same courtyard. All adults, all working, but they lived differently.

Uncle VityaEvery morning he got up at 6 a.m., drank tea, and went to the factory. He returned at 6 p.m., had dinner, and went to bed. His salary was fixed—40,000 rubles. No more, no less.

Aunt ZinaI was a hairdresser. I worked from home. Neighbors, friends, and friends of friends came to see me. I could cut five people's hair in a day, or none at all. In a good month, I earned 80,000, in a bad month, 20,000. But at least I was my own boss.

Uncle SeryozhaI opened a small shop. I hired two saleswomen and only brought in goods and checked the reports myself. Sometimes he'd go to the seaside for two weeks, and the shop would still be open.

Aunt MashaShe didn't work at all. About ten years ago, she bought a couple of apartments, rented them out, and lived off the proceeds. She said her money worked, and she rested.

Four neighbors. Four different ways to make money.

Question:
Who do you think is the freest of them?
And who rests the least?

MAIN:
There are four roads. Each leads to its own place.

Chapter 2.6 The Story of the Elephant Who Was Ate Piece by Piece
(Small Steps System)

Some animals got together and decided to eat an elephant. The elephant was big, and the animals were small. What should they do?

The lion, as the king of beasts, said:

"I'll eat the whole elephant. I'm the king, I can."

He took a bite, choked, and almost choked. He didn't eat any more.

The bear says:

"And I'll eat it piece by piece. Today a leg, tomorrow a trunk, the day after tomorrow ears."

He started with his leg. He chewed for a long time, got tired, and fell asleep. He woke up and his leg was still there. He spat and left.

And the ant crawled up to the elephant, pinched off a tiny piece, and ate it. Then another. Then another.

The animals laughed at him:

- Ant, do you seriously think you can eat an elephant with such crumbs?

The ant didn't respond. He simply continued to nibble off a piece every day.

A year later, nothing remained of the elephant.

Question:
What helped the ant eat the elephant – strength or patience?

MAIN:
An elephant is eaten one piece at a time. A big goal is achieved in small steps and over time. 

Chapter 2.7 A Tale of Two Runners (Discipline vs. Talent)

In a certain town there lived two runners. One was named Taras, the other Denis.

Taras was incredibly talented. He ran faster than anyone since childhood, without even training. He'd just get up and run—and outrun everyone.

Denis was an ordinary guy. He was an average runner. But he got up every morning at 6 a.m. and ran. In any weather. In rain, in snow, in heat.

Taras laughed at him:

"Why are you trying so hard? You can't run faster than me anyway. I have talent, what do you have?"

Denis remained silent and continued running.

A year later, they held a big race. Taras went to the start line, warmed up a bit, and then ran. Denis ran too.

Taras trudged to the finish line last, out of breath and tired from lack of training. Denis, however, was first.

"How did you get ahead of me?" Taras was surprised. "You have no talent!"

"I have discipline," Denis replied. "I ran every day while you were resting."

Question:
What is more important: talent or hard work?

MAIN:
Talent without effort is a sports car in the garage. Discipline without talent is a workhorse that comes first. 

Chapter 2.8 The Story of the Blacksmith and His Apprentices
(The Triangle of Successful Business)

In a certain town there lived a blacksmith. His name was Ilya. He was the best in his trade: his swords were worth their weight in gold, his knives remained blunt for years, and his horseshoes served faithfully.

But Ilya had one problem. He worked alone.

Every morning he rose at 4 a.m., stoked the furnace, forged metal, sharpened blades, and tempered steel. By evening, he was dead. He had enough money to live on, but not much else. And no free time either.

One day his nephew, a young man named Anton, came to him and said:

"Uncle Ilya, take me as an apprentice. I'll carry coal, blow the bellows, hold the blanks. And you'll show me how to forge."

Ilya chuckled:

"Why do I need an assistant? I can do everything myself."

- And you count - Anton did not retreat.

Ilya sat down, took the paper and began to count.

Alone, he forged 10 swords a week. The money was enough for food, workshop rent, and a little for a rainy day. But what if he hired Anton? He'd do all the grunt work, and Ilya could focus on the most difficult parts. Together, they could forge 25 swords a week. Even after paying Anton, Ilya would still have more left than he does now.

"What if we take two assistants?" Ilya wondered.

Anton smiled:

"What if there are three? What if you stop forging altogether and just teach us and check the quality?"

Ilya was afraid. Hand over his business to someone else? But Anton was persistent.

He hired three apprentices. The first carried coal and blew the bellows. The second held the blanks and carried the tools. The third polished the finished blades. And Ilya forged—only the most important things, only what he knew best.

Within a month they were forging 40 swords a week.

Six months later, Ilya was no longer standing at the forge. He was walking between the three blacksmiths he had trained, checking to make sure everything was being done correctly.

Now he did not forge with his hands - he forged with people.

At the end of the year, Ilya sat on the porch, drank mead and watched his workshop smoke through three chimneys.

"Uncle," asked another local boy, Volodya, "You used to work more, and now you're resting. How come?"

"I'm working," Ilya smiled. "I just work differently. Before, I forged alone. Now I've created a system where three work, and soon there will be five. The more people, the more swords. And the more time I have to live."

Question:
What is more important: being able to do everything yourself or being able to build a team that will get more done?

Main:
One blacksmith – 10 swords. Ten blacksmiths – 100 swords. And the blacksmith who has taught ten receives 100 swords without lifting a hammer.

Chapter 2.9 The Story of the Conveyor Belt and the Carrot
(Build the system - forget about the goals)

A man really wanted a carrot. He set a goal: "Get the carrot!" and ran. He ran across the entire field after one carrot. The carrot was far away, he got exhausted, fell, but still didn't get it.

Then he saw his neighbor. He wasn't running. He'd built a conveyor belt that dug a bed, planted seeds, watered, fertilized, and weeded. For the first two months, nothing but green stalks. The neighbor didn't move, didn't run, just did his job.

Three months later, the conveyor belt churned out 100 carrots. They "ran" to him on their own—he was simply harvesting the crop.

The first person asked, "How did you do it?" The neighbor replied, "I wasn't chasing results. I built a system. And the system itself produces results."

Question:
Who is smarter - the one who runs after one carrot, or the one who built a conveyor belt?

MAIN:
It's better to build a conveyor belt than to run after one carrot.

Chapter 2.10 A Tale of Two Merchants
(Choice of path: Yours or Someone else's)

Two merchants were selling at the market.

The first one sold other people's goods.He'd go to a merchant, buy toys from him, and sell them. The merchant would give him a percentage. The money was enough to live on, but he couldn't earn much—the percentage was small.

The second one made toys himself.It was difficult at first—few customers, almost no money. But after a year, word got out, his toys became popular, and he became rich.

The first merchant envied the second, but he didn't change anything. He was afraid to start his own business.

Question:
Which path would you choose - an easy one, but with a ceiling, or a difficult one, but with great freedom?

MAIN:
Alien - fast and easy, but at a short distance.
Yours - long and hard, but forever.

Chapter 2.11 The Story of the Climber Who Whined
(The Price of Freedom)

One climber dreamed of conquering Everest. He watched movies, read books, and collected magazine clippings. Every night, he went to bed imagining himself standing on top of the world.

But when it came to training, he started whining:

— My legs hurt. It's hard to breathe. The sleeping bag is uncomfortable. The food is bad. The weather is bad. The instructor is angry.

They told him: "Friend, this is Everest. Did you think there would be feather beds and orange juice there?"

The climber was offended and continued to whine. He abandoned training and didn't go to the mountains.

A year later, his neighbor, who was weaker physically but stronger in spirit, went to the mountains. He trained, endured pain, froze, and suffocated. And he made it.

And the first climber remained sitting at home, watching movies about mountains and complaining that life is unfair.

Question:
Which of them was actually stronger?

MAIN:
Everest isn't given away for the sake of pretty eyes. It's paid for with sweat, blood, and tears.

Chapter 2.12 A Tale of Two Kingdoms
(Two Choices of Freedom)

In a distant land there were two kingdoms. And the people in them lived differently.

In the first kingdomPeople were free. They didn't pay taxes, knew no king, and decided for themselves when to get up and what to do. But they lived modestly—each had a small house, a garden, and a couple of chickens. But no one told them what to do.

In the second kingdomPeople lived richly. They had huge castles, carriages, and expensive clothes. But the king was in charge of everything. He decided who got what, who got punished, who got rewarded. People depended on him and were afraid of angering him.

One day, two wise men from different kingdoms met and started arguing.

"There is freedom in our kingdom!" said one. "We are our own masters!"

"And in our kingdom there is wealth!" answered another. "We can afford everything!"

A boy passed by, heard their argument and asked:

— What is better: to be free but poor, or rich but not free?

The sages fell silent. And the boy moved on, thinking that everyone should choose for themselves.

Question:
What will you choose - freedom or wealth? Why?

MAIN:
Freedom comes in different forms. Choose wisely and pay your price.

Chapter 2.13 The Story of the Apprentice and the Three Masters
(About the Mentors)

Once upon a time there was a boy who wanted to become a great master. He came to the first sage and asked to become his apprentice.

"Very well," said the sage. "You will live with me, serve me, and do everything I say. In ten years, perhaps, I will reveal my secrets to you."

The boy thought and left.

He came to the second sage.

“Okay,” said the second one. “Pay me every month and I’ll teach you everything I know.”

The boy thought about it and left - he had no money.

He came to the third sage, the oldest and wisest.

“Grandfather, take me as an apprentice,” the boy asked.

"I can't teach you every day," the elder replied. "But I left my knowledge in books, in stories, in deeds. Go, read, listen, observe. Gather knowledge bit by bit from different masters. Learn patience from one, courage from another, kindness from a third. And then you will become great."

The boy did just that. He read books, listened to travelers' stories, and observed various masters. After a few years, he himself became a sage.

Question:
Who do you learn from? Who is your role model?

MAIN:
At first you are an empty vessel. The mentors fill you.
When you are filled, you yourself will become a source.

Chapter 2.14 The Story of the Three Builders (CON-TON-FON)

Three builders were building a house.

First,Ivan worked like an ox. From morning until night, he carried bricks, mixed mortar, and laid walls. He was so tired during the day that he'd drop dead. But the house grew slowly.

"Why are you alone?" they asked him. "Hire some people, it will go faster."

"I don't have time to look for people," Ivan waved his hand dismissively. "I have enough time as is."

After a year, he built one floor and ran out of steam.

Second,Pyotr started off differently. He spent a month searching for good assistants, negotiating, and assigning tasks. He himself barely did any work—he just gave orders. But once the process got going, the house grew before our eyes.

"Why aren't you working?" they asked him.

“I’m working,” Peter answered. “I’m working with my head.”

Six months later the house was ready.

Third,Nikolai didn't build anything at all. He first drew up the plans, then found a foreman, then negotiated with suppliers, then... And so on. He planned everything so well that he never started construction.

"And the house?" they asked him.

"The house will be built," Nikolai answered. "Now I'll polish the plan perfectly and get started."

A year later, he was still refining the plan.

Question:
Which of the builders did the right thing?

MAIN:
First, assemble a team. Then set up a process. Then reap the rewards.

Chapter 2.14.1 The Tale of Two Gardeners and Their Gardens
(BACKGROUND CARE: Why a System Dies Without an Owner)

In a certain village lived two gardeners. Both had inherited beautiful apple orchards from their father.

The first gardener, Nikolai, was hardworking and smart. He designed the irrigation system himself, installed pipes from the stream, and installed drippers for each tree. He hired helpers and taught them how to trim branches, weed, and harvest. When the garden began to generate a steady income, Nikolai said, "Well, now the system works automatically. It's time to relax." He left the garden in the hands of his helpers and went traveling.

The second gardener, Mikhail, did the same. But he did it differently. Every spring, he personally walked the garden and checked to see if the branches had frozen over after the winter. Every summer, he checked for pests and clogged irrigation pipes. Every fall, he checked the assistants' harvesting, ensuring that some apples were set aside for sale and some for soil amendment. Sometimes he went away on business, but he always returned and went straight to the garden.

Five years have passed.

Nikolai returned from his travels and didn't recognize his garden. The irrigation pipes were clogged, and half the trees had withered. His assistants, left without a supervisor, worked half-heartedly: they picked the apples carelessly, bruised them, and didn't sort them. The customers had gone to other gardeners. The garden was yielding ten times less than before. Nikolai clutched his head: "How can this be? I set everything up! The system worked!"

At this time, Mikhail was expanding his orchard. He planted another plot, hired new assistants, trained them, and appointed a supervisor. His apples were renowned throughout the region. Buyers came from far and wide. Mikhail also left occasionally, but he always knew what was happening in the orchard and always returned to check on the main events.

One day, Nikolai came to Mikhail and asked, "Why is your garden growing while mine is dying? We started out with the same thing. We had identical gardens."

Mikhail replied, "A garden is like a child. It's not enough to just give birth to it and raise it. It needs to be nurtured every day. Even when it's fully grown, it needs to be looked after. Pipes get clogged, helpers get lazy, the weather changes. I haven't taken a break from the garden. I've simply learned to care for it and tend to it differently. Not just hauling the apples myself, but making sure everything is running as it should."

Nikolai realized his mistake. He returned to his garden, rolled up his sleeves, and began to restore what had fallen into disrepair during his years of absence. It took another three years. But he never again left the garden unattended.

MAIN:
A garden that is not looked after turns into a forest.
A system that is not maintained turns into chaos.

Chapter 2.15 The Story of the Tree Under Which the Animals Gathered (The Power of Community)

In a forest there grew an old tree. It was so large and spreading that a hare, a fox, and even a wild boar could find shelter from the rain under its crown.

The animals noticed this and began coming under the tree not only in the rain. They simply gathered there in the evenings, sharing news, telling stories, and playing. Some brought tasty roots and treated others. Some told where the mushrooms were plentiful this year.

The tree stood silently. It didn't call to the animals, didn't scream, "Come to me!" It simply was. And the animals found their way to it.

One day a traveler was passing by, saw this company and was surprised:

"Tree, why are there always so many people under you? The other trees are empty, and you have a holiday every day?"

The tree thought and answered:

— Probably because I'm not just standing there. I'm sheltering from the rain. I'm providing shade in the heat. It's soft to lie on the grass beneath me. And also... I'm not pushing anyone away. There's room for everyone here.

The traveler thought for a moment and walked on. And the animals continued to gather under the old tree. Because it had become more than just a place for them, butin its place.

MAIN:
Don't invite people. Create a place where they feel good, and they will come.

Chapter 2.16: The Three Gears
(Any system is working)

The master assembled the clock and inserted three gears into it.

The first gear was the Input. It captured the time with its wheel and passed it on.

The second gear was Time. It spun slowly but surely, counting down the seconds.

The third gear is Critical Mass. When enough little wheels accumulated, they began to spin on their own, without the help of a master.

The master looked at his watch and said:

"Now they'll last forever. The main thing is not to break them prematurely."

Question:
What's more important in any undertaking: breaking everything down quickly or giving it time to get ready?

MAIN:
Any system needs time to work. Don't break it before its time. 

Chapter 2.17 The Story of the Blacksmith and the Magic Sword
(Timeframe: 2-3 years maximum)

In a certain village lived a young blacksmith. His name was Myron. One day, in a dream, he saw a beautiful sword—light as a feather and strong as a rock. This sword could cut through any stone without dulling.

Myron woke up and decided: "I'll forge such a sword! I'll become the greatest blacksmith!"

He grabbed the biggest hammer and a piece of iron and began to forge. He hammered for an hour, two, and only ruined the iron.

“It’s not working,” he said, upset.

The old master came to him and asked:
- What are you doing, boy?
"I'm forging a magic sword," Myron replies. "But the iron won't obey."
The master chuckled:
"You can't forge a sword from just any iron. First, you have to find the ore. Then smelt the ore into cast iron. Then weld the cast iron into steel. Then reforge that steel ten times, layer it, anneal it, temper it... It'll take at least three years."
"Three years?" Myron was taken aback. "I wanted it in three days."

But there was nothing to be done. Miron went looking for ore. He found it. Then he built a smelting furnace. Then he smelted steel, burned coal, and trained hammermen. He wanted to give up many times; it seemed like a lot of work, and still no sword.

But three years later, when he dipped the blade into the water for the last time, a blue flame ran across the blade. Myron picked up the sword—it sang in the wind. It wasn't just a piece of metal. It was the embodiment of three years of his labor, patience, and faith.

Question:Why didn’t Myron make the sword on the very first day?

MAIN:
The sharpest sword is forged over years.
The strongest business is built over years.
If you want not a craft for an hour, but a work for a lifetime, give yourself time to forge it.

Chapter 2.18 The Story of the Baker and the Three Ovens
(Scaling)

Once upon a time there lived a baker. He baked bread in a small bakery. He got up at 3 a.m., kneaded the dough, stoked the oven, and baked the loaves. By 8 a.m. the bread was ready, and the baker was already legless.

He baked 20 loaves a day and sold them to neighbors. The money was enough to live on, but not much more.

One day his son, who was studying in the city, came to him and said:

— Dad, why do you only bake 20 loaves? You can bake more!

"Son," the baker replies, "I'm tired as it is. I just can't handle it anymore."

The son thought and said:

"Let's install three ovens instead of one. And hire two helpers. You'll knead the dough, they'll bake and sell."

The baker was scared:

— It's expensive! Buying stoves, paying people...

- And you count - the son did not lag behind.

We did the math. It turned out that if we bake 60 loaves a day, there'll be enough money for the ovens, the helpers, and even some left over.

They installed ovens and hired help. Within a month, the baker was baking 60 loaves, and he felt less tired—others were helping!

A year later, the baker already had five bakeries in different districts. He visited them, checking on them, and the money was flowing in like a river.

Question:
What do you think was more important for the baker: installing new ovens or learning to trust his assistants?

MAIN:
You can't do much with just your hands. To grow, you need other hands.

Chapter 2.19 The Story of Uncle Petya and His Dream
(The Myth of Passive Income)

Uncle Petya dreamed of passive income his whole life. So he could do nothing and still have the money rolling in.

He bought a book called "How to Make a Million While Lying on the Couch." He read it and was delighted: "That's it, I'm quitting tomorrow!"

He quit his job, lay down on the couch, and waited for the money to come. It didn't come for a day, it didn't come for two, it didn't come for a week.

Uncle Petya went to the book's author to sort it out. And the author said to him:

— Have you built a system? Assembled a team? Set up processes?

“No,” says Uncle Petya. “I was lying on the couch, just like you taught me.”

"That's not what I'm talking about," the author laughs. "Passive income is when you work like a horse for five years, and then the system does the work for you. But if you immediately lay down on the couch, that's why you're broke."

Question:
What should you do first so that you don't have to do anything later?

MAIN:
Passive income isn't a freebie. It's deferred payment for your past work.

Chapter 2.20 A Tale of Two Firefighters (Systems Approach)

A fire broke out in one city. Two fire crews rushed to the scene.

The first team immediately rushed to put out the fire. They ran, shouted, doused it.
water - we were tired, but we put out the fire.

The second team first inspected the building, found the cause of the fire, shut off the gas, turned off the electricity, and only then began to extinguish the fire.

A week later, there was another fire in the same building. The first team rushed to the scene again, putting it out again. The second team arrived, looked around, and said:

"We eliminated the cause last time. There's nothing left to burn here."

And they left.

Question:
Which team worked smarter?

MAIN:
It's better to build a system once than to run around with a bucket of water all your life.

Chapter 2.21: The Story of the Salesman and the Hundred Calls
(Law of large numbers)

A young salesman got a job at the company.
On his first day, he made 10 calls—no one bought anything. He was upset: "I have no talent, I have to quit."

The mentor said, "Ten calls aren't statistics. Make a hundred. Then you'll see the picture."

The salesman made a hundred calls. Of these, 10 people were interested, and three bought. He realized his conversion rate was 3%. Now he knew: to get one sale, he needed 30-35 calls.

He stopped worrying about every rejection. Rejection became not a tragedy, but simply a step toward his goal. He planned: "I want 10 sales a week—that means I'll make 300 calls."

After a month, his results became predictable. He stopped guessing—he started counting.

Question:
Why did the salesperson stop being afraid of rejections after a hundred calls?

MAIN:
One attempt is a coincidence. A hundred attempts is statistics. A thousand is a prediction.

Chapter 2.22 The Story of Pareto and His Peas
(Pareto and Double Pareto)

There lived in Italy a scientist named Vilfredo Pareto. He had a garden where peas grew.

One day, Pareto was sitting on the veranda, drinking wine and watching his wife harvest peas. He noticed something strange: 20% of the pods produced 80% of the total peas. The remaining 80% of the pods were small and produced only 20% of the harvest.

He thought about it. Maybe it was a coincidence? He started observing other things. It turned out to be the same everywhere.

20% of efforts give 80% of results.
20% of clients generate 80% of revenue.
20% of the books in the library are read by 80% of the people.

Question:
What do you think are the most important 20% of your tasks?

MAIN:
Find your 20% and you will get 80% of the result.

Chapter 2.23: The Story of a School Class
(Normal distribution / Gaussian)

There were 30 students in one class. The math teacher was strict and graded fairly.

At the end of the year she looked at the magazine and saw:
— Two students are excellent students, with straight A's.
— Three of them are C students, they barely managed to get Cs.
— The remaining 25 are good students, getting Bs and Cs, evenly distributed, no stars.

The teacher paused. Why was that? After all, she treated everyone equally.

Because it's a law of nature. In any group of people, there are 5-10% stars, 5-10% laggards, and 80% average.

Question:
Who is better to be: a star, an average student, or a loser?

MAIN:
Don't try to make everyone a star. It's impossible.
Find your stars and work with them.

Chapter 2.24: A Tale of Two Neighbors and Snow Clearing
(Goal Decomposition / Doubling Method)

There was a lot of snow this winter. Two neighbors needed to clear the paths to their houses—each 100 meters long.

The first neighbor came out with a shovel, looked at the snowdrifts, and sighed with displeasure: "100 meters—that's an eternity!" He dug for an hour, got tired, and gave up. The next day, another hour. A week later, the path was half finished. He froze and fell ill.

The second neighbor called his son and two nephews. They calculated that the 100 meters of path could be divided into four parts—25 meters each. In the first hour, they cleared the entire 100 meters. Then they helped their neighbor—within an hour, they cleared his path, too.

The first neighbor was surprised: "How did you do it so quickly?" They replied, "We didn't do it individually. We calculated everything, divided the work into quotas for each person, and pooled our efforts. And if there were 10 or 20 of us, we would have done this job even faster."

Question:
Why did the second neighbor do it faster?

MAIN:
Don't tackle a big task alone. Break it down into parts and call in helpers. 

Chapter 2.25 The Story of the Four Blind Wise Men
(Four main elements for decomposition)

Four blind wise men decided to study what an elephant was. They were brought to an elephant and allowed to touch it.

The first one touched the leg and said:
— An elephant is a column. Thick and round.

The second one touched the trunk and objected:
- No, an elephant is a snake. Flexible and long.

The third one touched his ear:
- What are you talking about? An elephant is a fan. Big and flat.

The fourth touched the tail:
— An elephant is a rope. Thin and with a tassel at the end.

And they began to argue. Each argued their point, and neither could convince the other. This was because each only sensed, understood, and imagined a part, but could not see the whole.

Question:
How to understand what an elephant really is?

MAIN:
An elephant is not a leg or a trunk. An elephant is everything together.

Chapter 2.26 The Story of the Gardener and the First Sprout
(Critical Mass of the Asset)

The gardener planted a small seed. At first, he watered it himself, loosened the soil, and protected it from the cold. The seedling was weak, and without care it would have died.

But the gardener didn't give up. He watered it day after day, month after month.

Then the sprout grew, strengthened, and sent roots deep into the ground. It could now obtain water and nutrients on its own.

Within a few years, it was a large tree. It provided shade, attracted birds, and shed its leaves, which fertilized the surrounding soil.

"How did you manage to grow such a tree?" the neighbors asked.

“First I worked for the tree,” the gardener replied. “And now the tree works for itself (and for me).

Question:
How long do you think it takes for a small sprout to grow into a large tree that lives on its own?

Main:
First you work for the system. Then the system works for you.

Chapter 2.27 The Story of the Drop and the Stone
(Cumulative effect)

A drop dripped onto a stone. Drip, drip, drip. The stone was big and hard, but the drop was small and weak.

"What are you doing?" the other drops laughed. "You'll never break through this stone. It's so huge!"

"I'm in no hurry," the drop replied. "I'm just doing my job."

A month passed. A small hole appeared in the stone.
A year passed. The hole became a depression.
Ten years passed. The drop pierced the stone.

"How did you do that?" the other drops asked in surprise.
"It's very simple. I fell every day. Strength isn't in the number of hits, it's in the frequency and quantity."

Question:
What helped the drop break through the stone – strength or persistence?

MAIN:
A drop wears away a stone not by force, but by the frequency and number of falls, accumulating the overall effect over time.

Chapter 2.28 The Tale of Two Gardeners and an Apple Tree
(Average value)

Two gardeners grew apple trees. The first gardener picked apples every day and rejoiced when he found a large one. "Today, 10 big apples—good job!" he said. Tomorrow, two small ones—"bad day." He didn't realize how many apples his trees were actually producing.

The second gardener kept track. He wrote down how many kilograms of apples he picked per day, then per week, then per month. At the end of the month, he calculated the average: 30 kg per day.

When buyers arrived, the first gardener quoted a price based on the best day. The buyers left—the price was too high. The second gardener quoted a price based on the average harvest. The buyers were satisfied, and he sold apples reliably every month.

Question:
Why did the second gardener manage to sell apples reliably, while the first one did not?

MAIN:
Don't judge by your best day. Calculate the average—it will show the truth.

Chapter 2.29: A Tale of Two Fishermen and Their Catch (KPI and ROI)

Two fishermen were fishing in the same lake.

The first fisherman only counted the number of fish he caught every day.
50 fish—good job, 20—bad job. He was chasing numbers: he hauled in small fish, not paying attention to size, not thinking about demand. By the end of the week, he had 300 fish, but half of them were small—nobody bought them. The other half went bad because he couldn't sell them in time.

The second fisherman counted differently. He kept track of how many big fish he'd caught, how many kilograms he'd hauled in a day, how much money he'd made at the market, and how much he'd spent on gear. He'd release the smaller fish back into the lake, catching fewer, but larger ones.

By the end of the year, the first fisherman had caught 10,000 fish, but made little profit—half of them rotted, half were given to the cats. The second caught 3,000 fish, but sold them all at a good price, earning three times as much.

Question:
Which fisherman turned out to be smarter: the one who counted the number of fish, or the one who counted the profit?

MAIN:
It's not enough to just do things. You have to do things that bring results. Count your money, not your fish.

Chapter 2.30: The Story of Aunt Lyuba and Her Cheesecakes
(MVP - Small Productive Model)

Aunt Lyuba baked the most delicious vatrushki in the area. With cottage cheese, with jam, with potatoes—finger-licking good.

She dreamed of opening her own bakery. A real one, a big one, with a display case, tiled walls, and a sign.

"I've even come up with a name!" she boasted to her friends. "'Aunt Lyuba's'! I'll order uniforms, aprons with the logo, and..."

“Aunt Lyuba,” her nephew Seryozha interrupted, “first try selling at least one cheesecake to strangers.”

"Who would I sell it to?" Aunt Lyuba said, offended. "I'll sell it when I open the bakery."

But Seryozha didn't lag behind. On the weekend, he helped Aunt Lyuba bake 20 vatrushki (cheesecakes), put them in a large basket, laid out a clean napkin, and took Aunt Lyuba to the market.

"Sit and sell," he said. "And I'll stand nearby."

Aunt Lyuba was embarrassed, but she sat down. By lunchtime, she had sold all 20 cheesecakes.

"Oh, and what money!" she was surprised. "And I only sat there for half a day."

A week later, she baked 50 vatrushki (cheesecakes). She sold them in three hours.

Within a month, she had a permanent spot at the market, and a year later, her own small bakery. No expensive renovations, no marble, no sign, but a line of customers.

"Do you remember," laughed Seryozha, "you wanted to open a bakery right away, without even trying to sell it?"

"Oh, shut up," Aunt Lyuba waved her hand. "It's good you talked me into it. Test it first, then build it."

Question:
Why did Aunt Lyuba first want to open a bakery, but Seryozha suggested starting with a market?

MAIN:
Before you build a bakery, sell at least one cheesecake from the stand.

Chapter 2.31 A story about two photographers and a rare bird
(Synchronization of events / Luck)

Two photographers went into the forest to photograph a rare bird.

The first photographer took his phone, showed up whenever he felt like it, sat down on a tree stump, and started scrolling through his feed. That same bird flew past—he didn't even notice because he was staring at the screen. The bird flew away. He returned empty-handed and said, "Bad luck."

The second photographer prepared for a week: he studied the bird's habits, learned where it nested, arrived an hour before dawn, loaded his camera, and adjusted the lens. The bird flew in—he snapped the shot, and it was perfect.

"You're lucky!" said the first one.

"No luck," replied the second. "I was just ready."

Question:
Who is luckier more often: those who prepare or those who hope for luck?

MAIN:
Luck doesn't come to those born under a lucky star. It comes to those who prepare.

Chapter 2.32: A Tale of Two Loaders (Levers)

There were two loaders working at a construction site. One was young and strong. The other was older and more experienced.

One day, a heavy beam needed to be lifted to the second floor. A young loader approached, leaned his shoulder against it, and braced himself—the beam didn't even budge.

"It's no use," he said, wiping away the sweat. "It's too heavy. We need to call ten more people."

The experienced loader silently walked up to the corner, picked up a long metal crowbar, slid it under the beam, pressed down from the other end – and the beam lifted up.

“Give me some support,” he said to the young man.

A minute later the beam was lying on the second floor.

"How did you do that?" the young man asked in surprise. "You're weaker than me!"

"I'm not stronger," the experienced man replied. "I just know where to find a fulcrum. A crowbar is a lever. It multiplies force. You don't have to strain yourself, you just need to find the right lever."

Question:
In what situation do you try to lift a "beam" with your bare hands, even though there is a "crowbar" lying nearby?

Main:
You don't have to be the strongest. You just have to know where and how to apply your strength.

Chapter 2.33 The Story of the Gardener with Scissors
(Occam's Razor)

The gardener had a bush. It was beautiful, but it was too overgrown. Branches stuck out in all directions, leaves blocked out the light, and flowers couldn't get through.

The gardener took a pair of huge shears and began trimming the bush. He cut off all the excess, leaving only the strongest branches.

The bush immediately improved. The flowers bloomed, the sun hit the leaves, and the branches stopped breaking.

The neighbor asked:
— Why did you trim it? It was such a beautiful bush!

"It was shaggy," the gardener replied. "And I left only what was needed. Anything extra just gets in the way."

Question:
What's better: a lot of everything or just the essentials?

MAIN:
Simplicity is the highest form of complexity. The simpler a system, the more reliable it is.

Chapter 2.34 The Story of the Gardener and Two Beds
(Low-hanging fruit concept)

The gardener had two beds.

The first bed contained vegetables that required long and difficult cultivation: artichokes, asparagus, and cauliflower. The gardener spent countless hours watering, fertilizing, and weeding them, but the harvest was meager.

The second bed contained vegetables that sprouted from the ground on their own: radishes, lettuce, and dill. The gardener barely tended them, but harvested the crops every day.

One day the son asked:

"Dad, why don't you plant more radishes and lettuce? Why are you bothering with the artichokes?"

The gardener paused. His son was right. He could first gather what grows easily, eat his fill, stock up, and then deal with the finicky vegetables.

Question:
Why do you think the gardener struggled with difficult vegetables at first, even though the easy ones grew on their own?

MAIN:
First, collect what comes naturally to you. Then tackle the difficult ones.

Chapter 2.35 The Story of the Debtor and the Debtor (NNNN and NNN)

Two people were sitting in line.

The first one complained:

"Everyone owes me something. My parents should have raised me right, but they didn't. The state should provide me with a pension, but it doesn't. My friends should understand, but they don't."

The second one said:

— I don't owe anyone anything. And no one owes me anything. If I need something, I do it myself. If someone helps, I'm grateful. If not, well, that's life. I don't expect anything from anyone.

The first one left with a prescription. The second one left and went on to open his own business.

A year later, the first one was still taking medication and was resentful of the world.
The second one had already become financially independent and was traveling.

Question:
Which of them is happier?

MAIN:
No one owes anything to anyone. Accept this, and it will become easier to breathe.

Chapter 2.36 The Story of the Builder and His Assistants
(Human factor)

A builder was building a house. He hired three brothers to help him.

The brothers worked well, but sometimes they made mistakes. They'd lay a brick in the wrong place, or mix the mortar too thin.

The builder was angry, shouted at them, and forced them to redo it.

One day, the older brother didn't show up for work. It turned out his mother was sick. The builder became even more angry—the deadline was looming!

The old master came to him and said:

"Why are you angry? People aren't robots. They get sick, they make mistakes, they get tired. Did you take that into account in your plans?"

“No,” the builder was taken aback.

"That's why everything falls apart for you. You always have to leave some cushion—for human error."

Question:
Why didn't the builder take into account that the assistant's mother might get sick?

MAIN:
There are no perfect people. There are plans that take into account that people can make mistakes.

Chapter 2.37 A Tale of Two Diggers
(Why don't most people succeed?)

Two diggers were digging holes. One was diligent, the other was lazy.

The diligent one would get up at 5 a.m. and dig until evening, with calluses on his hands and sweat pouring down. The lazy one would get up at 9 a.m., dig for an hour, rest for two, dig for an hour, rest for two.

A month later, we looked at the results. The diligent one dug 10 holes. The lazy one dug 3 holes.

Everyone thought that the diligent one was a good guy, and the lazy one was a backward one.

But a year later, it turned out that the lazy man had stopped digging holes. He invented the excavator. Now he sits in the cab and digs 100 holes in an hour. And the diligent man still digs with a shovel.

Question:
Who was actually smarter?

MAIN:
Don't confuse sweat on your forehead with forward movement.
Sometimes it's better to sit down and think than to get up and run.

Chapter 2.38 The Chicken and Egg Story (Diversification)

There was a man who had one hen. A good hen, she laid an egg every day.

The man was happy, sold eggs, and lived happily.

But one day the hen got sick and stopped laying eggs. The man was left penniless.

He went to his neighbor to ask for advice. The neighbor had three hens. One was laying eggs, the second was brooding, and the third was just wandering around.

"Why do you have three?" the man asked. "Are you missing some balls?"

"Enough," the neighbor replied. "But if one gets sick, I have two more. I won't be left without eggs."

The man thought about it. He returned home and bought two more chickens. And never again was he left without eggs.

Question:
Why did the neighbor turn out to be smarter?

MAIN:
One chicken is risk. Three chickens are stability.
Don't put all your eggs in one basket. 

Chapter 2.39 The Black Swan That Changed Everything
(Black swans)

White swans lived in a pond. They swam, dived, caught fish, and thought it would always be like this. The sun was shining, the water was warm, and there was plenty of food. They couldn't even imagine that things could be any other way.

"There are only white swans in the world," they said. "It's the law of nature."

But one day, a black swan swam onto the pond. He was huge, beautiful, and completely different from all the others.

The white swans were frightened:
— Who are you? Where did you come from? We've never seen anyone like you!

The Black Swan smiled:
"I lived somewhere else, far away from here. We're all like that there."

And suddenly everything changed. The water grew colder, the fish disappeared, the wind blew stronger. The white swans didn't know what to do. They were used to everything being fine and weren't prepared for difficulties.

And the black swan said:
"I didn't come to scare you. I came so you know: the world isn't always as you're used to seeing it. Sometimes something new, unexpected, comes along, and everything changes. You have to be prepared for that."

Question:
Are you prepared for the fact that one day a “black swan” might appear in your life – something unexpected, new and not always positive?

MAIN:
There are more than just white swans in the world. Sometimes black ones come too. Be prepared.

Chapter 2.40: A Tale of Three Athletes (Antifragility)

Three athletes trained in one section.

The first one was fragile. When the coach gave him a difficult exercise, he would lose heart, whine, and complain of pain. And after his first injury, he quit training altogether. His career was over before it even began.

The second one was strong. He endured, didn't whine, and did everything he was told. But he didn't get stronger—he simply remained the same. Neither better nor worse. Strong, but not growing.

The third was flexible. He fell, too. He got injured, too. But after each fall, he got up, analyzed what he had done wrong, and trained even harder. Every mistake made him stronger. Every failure, wiser. Every injury, more careful, but not weaker.

Ten years later, the first was working as a salesman in a sporting goods store, telling customers how hard it was to be an athlete. The second became a coach in the same sporting goods store—not bad, but average. And the third won gold at the Olympics.

“How did you manage to do that?” they asked him.

"I wasn't afraid of falling," he replied. "I was afraid of not getting up. Every blow made me stronger because I learned from it."

Question:
Which of the three athletes do you think was successful and why?

Main:
It doesn't matter how many times you fall.
What matters is how many times you got up and became better than you were before you fell.

Chapter 2.41 The story of Uncle Borya, who got rich
(Big money = big problems)

Uncle Borya lived in a certain town. He was a simple mechanic, fixing faucets and pipes. He had enough money for food, an apartment, a small car, and fishing on weekends. And Uncle Borya was happy.

One day he bought a lottery ticket and won a million rubles!

At first, Uncle Borya jumped for joy. Then he ran to his wife:

— Get ready, let's go to Turkey! I'll buy you a fur coat! A new car!

And a week later the problems started.

First came some "old friends" Uncle Borya hadn't seen in ages. One asked for a loan for an operation, another for a business venture, and a third just for fun, "until payday." Uncle Borya wasn't one to refuse—he handed over half a million.

Then the bandits came. They said, "We have to share." Uncle Borya got scared and gave another two hundred thousand.

Then the tax office came: "Where's the declaration? Where are the taxes?" I had to pay a fine.

A month later, distant relatives from another city showed up. An auntie Uncle Borya had never even seen demanded, "You're rich now, help me!"

By the end of the month, a million was down to a hundred thousand. Uncle Borya was gloomy. His wife was nagging him. The friends who had received the money had disappeared. The bandits promised to come again.

Uncle Borya sat in the kitchen and sighed:
— I wish I hadn't bought that ticket. We were living peacefully...

Question:
What do you think Uncle Borya did wrong?
What should have been done with the money?

MAIN:
Big money does not mean freedom, but new challenges and risks.
They destroy without preparation.

Chapter 2.41.1 The Tale of Two Kings and Their Treasures
(The Public Billionaire Trap)

In one kingdom there lived two kings. Both received enormous treasures.

The first king, named Severus, hid all his gold in a deep cave behind seven locks. He dressed simply, lived in a small house, and told no one about his riches. When someone asked why he lived so modestly, Severus replied, "Why should I show off my gold? I already know I have it. And as long as no one knows about it, no one will come to take it away."

The second king, named South, took a different approach. He built a vast palace with golden domes. Every day, he rode out to the square in a carriage adorned with precious stones. The people admired him; guests came from all over the world to see South's wealth. But along with this admiration came other guests. Tax collectors came and said, "Since you're so rich, pay ten times as much into the treasury." Bandits came and began plotting how to break into the palace. Envious neighbors came and began plotting intrigues.

One day, a messenger came to the North and told him that the King of the South had been captured and imprisoned. The North sighed and said, "He chose to show off. I chose to rule. These are two different paths."

Question:
Why do you think King North hid his wealth, while King South put it on display?
Which of them ended up safer?

MAIN:
You can own a treasure. Or you can show it off.
But you can almost never do both at the same time.

Chapter 2.42: The Tale of the Echo in the Mountains (The "Eye for an Eye" Strategy)

A boy went with his grandfather to the mountains. They walked for a long time, and the boy got tired. He got bored and decided to make some noise.

He took a deep breath and shouted into the void:

- Hey-hey!

And the mountains answered him in kind: "Hey-hey!"

The boy liked it. He shouted:

- You are good!

And the mountains answered: "You are good!"

Then the boy decided to test what would happen if he shouted something angry. He frowned and shouted:

- You're a fool!

And the mountains answered: "You are a fool!"

The boy was offended. He tugged his grandfather's sleeve:

- Grandpa, someone is calling you names!

Grandfather smiled and said:

"It's not someone. It's your own voice. Mountains always return exactly what you send them. Shout out something good, and you receive something good. Shout out something bad, and don't be offended; you sent it there yourself."

The boy thought. Then he took another breath and shouted:

- I'm sorry, I was wrong!

And the mountains answered: “Forgive me, I was wrong!”

From then on, the boy always remembered: mountains don't get angry, mountains don't take revenge. They simply return what you gave them.

Question:
Why do you think the mountains responded to the boy with the same things he shouted at them?

MAIN:
Whatever you put out into the world, it will return to you. Don't be angry with the echo.

Chapter 2.43 A Tale of Two Aquariums
(Two types of people and transparency of relationships)

There were two aquariums in one house.

In the first aquarium, the water was murky and dirty. The fish swam erratically, colliding with each other, and not knowing where they belonged. They often fought.

In the second aquarium, the water was crystal clear. All the fish were visible, each knew its place, swam in orderly rows, and no one disturbed anyone else.

The aquarium owner explained:

"In murky water, no one can see anyone else; everyone is afraid of everything, that's why they quarrel. But in clear water, everything is visible, everyone is calm."

Question:
What kind of aquarium would you like to live in?

MAIN:
Transparency in relationships and management removes fear and chaos.
Muddy waters are conflicts.

Chapter 2.44 The Story of the Pumpkin Field (The Pumpkin Method)

A farmer had a field with pumpkins growing on it.

The farmer watered every pumpkin in a row, fertilized every one, and tended them all equally. The pumpkins grew, but they were all small and unsightly. No one bought them.

Another farmer also had a field. But he did it differently.

He chose the most promising pumpkin and devoted all his energy to it. He watered it exclusively, fertilized it exclusively, and protected it from weeds. He barely touched the other pumpkins.

By autumn, he had grown a giant pumpkin. So huge that all the neighbors came to see it. It was purchased for a large sum of money for an exhibition.

“And the other pumpkins?” they asked him.

"And the rest are for cattle feed," the farmer replied. "But one of them made me famous throughout the entire region."

Question:
Which farmer turned out to be smarter?

MAIN:
You can't grow a giant pumpkin if you water the entire field.

Chapter 2.45 The Tale of Three Masters and One Cart
(The Power of Teamwork)

Three craftsmen worked in one village workshop.

The first one made wheels. He selected the strongest wood, turned the spokes, and covered the rims with iron. His wheels lasted for years and didn't fall apart over bumps.

The second one made axles. His work was unnoticeable, but without it, the wheels couldn't turn. He selected the perfect size so there was no play, but also no jamming. His axles didn't creak or break.

The third was an assembler. He took the finished wheels and axles, connected them to the body, and checked that the cart ran smoothly, didn't lean, and didn't fall apart at the first turn.

One day they argued about which of them was the most important.

"Without my wheels the cart won't move!" said the first one.

"Without my axles, the wheels will simply fall off as soon as you load them!" the second one objected.

"And without me, you're both just a pile of wood," the third one chuckled. "I'm bringing your work together into one whole."

They were offended and went their separate ways. Each decided to make a cart themselves.

The first one turned out some beautiful wheels. But when he tried to attach them to the body, he realized he didn't know how to make an axle. The cart stood on its wheels, but it wouldn't move.

The second one made perfect axles. But without wheels, they were just iron bars. There was nothing to attach to them.

The third assembled the body, but without wheels and axles it remained just a box on the ground.

A month later they met, tired and having achieved nothing.

"We behaved like children," said the first. "Everyone was pulling for themselves."

"Let's work together," suggested the second one. "You make the wheels, I'll make the axles, you assemble them."

They got back to work. The carts started moving. The money started flowing.

Question:
Why do you think the craftsmen couldn't make the cart alone, but together they could?

Main:
On a team, everyone is important. Even the strongest one is a true warrior alone. Together, they are masters.

Chapter 2.46 The Balloon Story (Five Faiths)

A hot air balloon was flying. Beautiful, big, bright. The pilot sat in the basket, enjoying life.

Suddenly the wind picked up and the ball began to be thrown from side to side.

The pilot was thinking only about this:
"I believe the ball will hold up! It's strong!" That was self-confidence.

"I believe in the company that made it! They're professionals!"—faith in the manufacturer.

"I believe in the laws of physics! The ball can't fall if the burner is working!" — faith in the system.

"I believe in my passengers! They're not panicking, they're not twitching!"—faith in the crew.

"I believe this flight is necessary! That I'm not flying in vain!"—faith in the mission.

And the pilot calmed down. And the balloon held up. And they all, along with the passengers, reached their destination.

But what if even one faith had failed? What if the pilot had doubted himself? Or the balloon? Or why he was flying at all? His hands would have dropped, and the balloon would have crashed.

Question:
How much faith does it take to move calmly and steadily in any project?

MAIN:
All Five Faiths are needed for stable forward movement.
Without any of them, nothing will work.

Chapter 2.47 The Story of Two Merchant Brothers
(Two types of promotion)

Two brothers were trading at the market. They sold the same goods, in the same row. But they sold them differently.

The older brother, Ivan, would come out to the counter every morning and start shouting:
— Come on in, grab some! The best merchandise! The lowest prices!

He'd grab passersby by the sleeves, drag them in, and practically force them to buy. By evening, he'd be exhausted, his voice hoarse, but he still sold a lot.

The younger brother, Pyotr, sat quietly. He simply arranged his wares beautifully, smiling at passersby, but never grabbed anyone. Sometimes people approached him with questions, and he calmly answered, telling stories, demonstrating.

At the end of the month, we compared revenue. Ivan's was 100,000. Petr's was 80,000.

"You see," said Ivan. "I sold more."

"Wait," Peter replied. "How much did you spend on screaming? Your voice trembled, you bought some candy. You ate more because of the stress. You tore your shirt. Your net profit is 90,000. And mine is 80,000, and I was sitting quietly, drinking tea."

"So what?" Ivan persisted. "I still earned more."

"Just wait," Peter smiled. "Those you grabbed by the sleeves won't come back to you. But they come to me again and again. And they bring friends. In a year, I'll have 200 thousand, and you'll have the same 90."

Question:
Which brother will be the winner in a year?

MAIN:
A scream sells once. A smile sells a lifetime.

Chapter 2.48 The Story of Vovochka and Three Letters
(The "3-message" method)

Vovochka fell in love with a girl from a parallel class. She was beautiful, smart, and an excellent student. But Vovochka was a poor student and a troublemaker.

Friends advised:
— Go up to her and say directly: "Let's be friends." What's the point of dragging it out?

Vovochka did just that. He came up to me during recess and said:
- Masha, let's be friends!

Masha twirled her finger at her temple and left.

Vovochka was upset. He went to his older brother for advice. His brother was smart and went to college.

"Are you stupid or something?" said the brother. "Who does that? It should be done differently."

And he taught Vovochka the three-letter method.

Letter One:
Vovochka wrote Masha a note: "Masha, you answered so well on the test today. I didn't even understand how you did it. Respect."

Masha was surprised, but answered: “Thank you.”

Letter two (three days later):
"Masha, I'm trying to solve a math problem here, and I'm having trouble. Can you help me? You're smart."

Masha helped. Vovochka thanked her.

Letter three (a couple of days later):
"Masha, thank you so much for your help. I couldn't have done it without you. Listen, maybe we should go to the movies on Saturday? I'm treating you."

Masha agreed.

Question:
Why did Masha agree after the third letter, but refuse after the first?

MAIN:
Make friends first, then propose.

Chapter 2.49 The Story of the Silent Salesman
(Six Priorities + Five Golden Questions)

A man walked into the store. He looked at the display case, sighed, and got ready to leave.

The salesman, an experienced guy, came up to me and instead of the usual “what do you want?” asked:

— Tell me, what is most important to you right now?

The man was surprised, but answered:

— I don’t know... Probably so that the children don’t need anything.

"Why is this so important to you?" the salesman asked.

— Because I didn't have a lot of things as a child. I don't want mine to have the same.

"I understand," the salesman nodded. "But what if you can't give it to them?"

The man's face darkened:
— I'll feel like a worthless father. Like I failed.

- And how long has this been bothering you?

— All my life, probably.

The seller was silent. Then he said:

"I have something that will help you earn more. Not today, but in six months. If you're willing to work, of course. Should I tell you?"

The man nodded. And after six months, he really did start earning more.

Question:
Why did the man agree to buy even though the seller wasn't selling anything?

MAIN:
Don't sell. Help the person want to buy.

Chapter 2.50 The Story of the Persistent Salesman
(Six touches)

A man came into a store to look at televisions. Just to look. Maybe he'll buy one, maybe not.

The seller jumped up to him:
— What do you want? This one is good, this one is even better, this one is the best. Buy it!

The man politely refused and left.

The seller was upset:
— It didn't work.

A week later the man came again. He looked, walked around, and left again.

The seller didn’t come back anymore – he was offended.

A month later, the man came back for the third time. He walked around, looked, and bought it.

The seller was surprised:
— Why did you buy it today and not last time?

"Because last time I was just looking around. I had to get used to the idea that I even wanted a TV. Then choose a model. Then compare prices. Then consult with my wife. This doesn't happen overnight."

Question:
How many times do you have to show a product to someone before they buy it?

MAIN:
For a person to buy, at least six touches with the product are needed.
Don't give up after the first rejection.

Chapter 2.51 The Story of a Student and a Professor
(Six degrees of separation)

A student dreamed of getting into a graduate program with a renowned professor. But the professor was in another city, at another university, and the student had no idea how to approach him.

He went to his supervisor:
"Ivan Petrovich, help me. I want to see Professor Sokolov, but I don't know how to contact him."

"Oh, I studied under Sokolov!" said Ivan Petrovich. "I can give you the phone number of his former graduate student; he's in Moscow now."

The student called his former graduate student. He said:
"Sokolov rarely answers his calls these days. But I know his wife; we were at a conference together. I can give her email."

The student wrote to the professor's wife. She replied:
- My husband is very busy now, but if you want, I will give him your letter.

A week later, Professor Sokolov himself called the student:
— Hello, my wife gave me your letter. Tell me about the work.

The student told the story. The professor liked it. A year later, the student was already in graduate school.

Question:
How many people helped the student get to the professor?

MAIN:
Any person on the planet is no more than six handshakes away.
Just start looking.

Chapter 2.52: The Story of the Road with a Blockage
(Quick adaptation to changes)

People were walking along the road. Suddenly, there was a blockage ahead. Rocks, trees, no way through.

Some people stopped and cried:
— Everything is lost! There is no road! We can’t go any further!

Other people sat down and waited for the rubble to be cleared. They waited a day, two, a week—no one did.

The third group looked around, found a detour, and followed it. It was a difficult path, but they continued on.

A month later, they reached their destination. Those who had cried and waited remained at the rubble.

Question:
Which people turned out to be the smartest?

MAIN:
A crisis is not a time to retreat. It's a time to find a way around it. 

Chapter 2.53: The Story of Ashot and His Shawarma
(Personal brand and target audience)

Ten shawarma places opened in one city. All the same: lavash, chicken, vegetables, sauce. Prices were roughly the same. And they all stood around waiting for customers.

But there was one spot where there was always a line. Ashot was standing there.

Ashot didn't just wrap shawarma. He smiled at every customer. He remembered each customer's favorite sauce. He asked about their health, their children, their work. He even let them try new sauces for free.

People weren't going for shawarma. People were going to see Ashot.

When the 11th location opened, the owners were surprised:
"Why is there a line at Ashot's and ours is empty? We make the same shawarma!"

They sent a secret shopper. He took the shawarma from Ashot and brought it to them. They tried it—it was exactly the same. Even the sauce was the same.

Then they approached Ashot and asked:
"Listen, what's the secret? You have the same product as everyone else."

Ashot smiled:
"You have the product. I have me. People don't come to eat shawarma. People come to be noticed."

Question:
What did people buy from Ashot – shawarma or attention?

MAIN:
People choose people, not services. You can be copied, but your attitude cannot.
Everyone's shawarma is the same. Attentive and good-natured Ashot is the only one.
Become Ashot.

Chapter 2.54 The Story of the Three-Legged Horse
(Competencies + Resources + Money)

The blacksmith had a horse. Beautiful, strong, fast. But it limped on one leg.

The blacksmith shod her three healthy legs. The horse ran faster. But the fourth leg still hurt, and the horse couldn't gallop for long.

A sage came and said:

"You're treating the healthy legs. But the bad one remains bad. The horse won't gallop until you heal all four."

The blacksmith cured the sore leg. The horse galloped.

"Why didn't I realize this before?" the blacksmith asked in surprise. "All legs are important."

Question:
Why couldn't the horse gallop fast even when three legs were healthy?

MAIN:
A horse gallops on four legs. If one hurts, you won't get far.

Chapter 2.55: The Story of a Photographer and a Neural Network
(Tools: Content, Neural Networks and Automation)

Once upon a time, there was a photographer. He shot weddings, corporate events, and children's parties. He worked a lot, but somehow, there was no passion. He had enough money, but there wasn't enough left for much else.

One day his nephew, a student programmer, came to him and said:
— Dude, why do you only have 500 subscribers online? You're such a great videographer!

"And when do I have time to do this?" the photographer asked, surprised. "I spend all day shooting weddings, then I edit them, and then I sleep."

The nephew sighed, took out his laptop and set up neural networks for him in an hour.

"Look," he said. "Now you don't have to write your own posts. The neural network writes them for you. No need to come up with ideas—the neural network generates 100 ideas in a minute. No need to spend hours processing each photo—the neural network does it in seconds."

The photographer didn't believe it, but decided to try.

Within six months, he had 50,000 subscribers. People were lining up to see him six months in advance. He tripled his prices, but his clientele remained the same.

"What's the secret?" my colleagues asked. "You used to shoot so-so stuff."

"I still shoot the same way," the photographer smiled. "It's just that no one knew about me before. But now they do. Because I talk about my work every day. Not with my hands, but with neural networks. But I do talk about it."

Question:
What has changed in the photographer's life—his ability to take pictures or his ability to talk about himself?

MAIN:
You don't have to do it yourself. Find a way to have it done for you. 

Chapter 2.56: The Story of a Boss Who Did Everything Himself
(Delegation)

There was a boss. A good, responsible one. But he had one flaw: he didn't trust anyone.

I wrote the reports myself, answered the letters myself, called the clients myself, checked the quality myself, and did the accounting myself.

I worked from 8 a.m. until midnight. I never took a day off. My wife complained, my children were upset, and my health was deteriorating.

One day, he collapsed from exhaustion. While he was in the hospital, his deputy, whom he had never trusted, took over.

When the boss returned a month later, he expected to find ruins. But everything worked. Even better than when he'd been there.

“How did you do it?” he asked.

"I just did what you taught me," the deputy replied. "I only got more done because I didn't sweat the small stuff."

And then the boss realized that all these years he'd simply been afraid to let go of control. But as soon as he did, the system began to breathe.

Question:
Why did the boss work more and achieve less than his deputy?

MAIN:
If you do everything yourself, you're simply the busiest person in the cemetery. Delegate. 

Chapter 2.57 A Tale of Two Builders
(Speed ​​is more important than quality at the start)

Two builders were building a house. One was a perfectionist, the other a pragmatist.

The perfectionist checked every angle, adjusted every plank, sanded every surface. The house was turning out perfect, but he'd been building it for three years.

The pragmatist worked quickly. Straight where necessary, a little crooked where possible, but quickly. A year later, the house was ready, and he moved in.

The perfectionist looked at his house and grimaced:
— The angles aren't 90 degrees, the floor creaks, the roof is slightly uneven. Is it possible to live like this?

"Yes, yes," the pragmatist replied. "I'm already living there. And by the time you finish your perfect house, I'll have lived in this one for five years. And in that time, I'll have earned enough money for another house."

Question:
Which builder is smarter?

MAIN:
Better (at the start) quickly and crookedly than perfectly, but never. 

Chapter 2.58 The Story of the Bridge Builder (Feedback)

In a certain town there lived a builder. He built bridges. Good, reliable, beautiful ones. Everyone praised him.

One day a customer came to him and said:
— Build me a bridge across the river. Here are the plans, here is the money.

The builder built it. The bridge turned out great. But for some reason, the client was dissatisfied.

"What's wrong?" the builder asked in surprise. "I did everything according to the drawings!"

"Did you ask me what I really wanted?" the customer replied. "I didn't need a bridge. I needed a cattle crossing. And you built a car crossing. The cattle are afraid to cross it."

The builder thought about it. On the next project, he did it differently.

I came to the customer and asked:
— Why do you need a bridge? What will you transport across it? Who will use it? What time of year?

The client was surprised: no one had asked him about it before. He explained everything in detail.

The builder built a bridge that was perfect for the job. The client was delighted and recommended the builder to everyone he knew.

Ten years later, the builder became the most famous in the region. People lined up to see him.

"What's your secret?" my colleagues asked. "You're no better at building than anyone else."

“I build the same way,” the builder answered. “But before I build, I listen.”

Question:
