A man built a house. Beautiful, large, with carved shutters. All the neighbors admired it.

But when the storm came, the house collapsed. Because he built it on sand.

Another man was also building a house. But before he began, he sat down and asked himself four questions:

—Who am I? Why am I here? Why did I choose this place? And what will I do if the ground beneath the house starts to give way?

He searched for answers for a long time. Not from his neighbors. Not from his parents. But within himself.

When he found the answers, he began to build. Not on sand, but on rock.

The storm came to him too. The house stood firm.

“Why did your house survive?” they asked him.

— Because I answered four questions first. And only then did I start building.

Question:
What four questions do you think the builder asked himself? Have you ever asked yourself these questions?

 MAIN:
Before building a house, check the foundation. Before starting a family, answer the most important questions.

10.8.2 The story of ancient people and the first fire
(The roots of the phenomenon. Why did the family appear?)

Long ago, people lived alone. Each person found their own food, kept themselves warm, and protected themselves from animals.

But one day, a cold winter arrived. One man was so cold that he almost froze. And then he saw another man who was also shivering from the cold.

They sat down next to each other, pressed their backs against each other, and it became warmer.

Then they lit a fire. One collected brushwood, the other tended the flames. It was easier with two people.

Then they had children. They needed protection, food, warmth. And the parents did all this together.

That's how family came to be. Not because someone made up the rules. But because it's easier to survive together.

Question:
Why do you think people started living together?
What was more beneficial for them: being alone or sticking together?

MAIN:
Family wasn't born out of rules. Family was born out of a desire to be together.

10.8.3 The story of the king and his decrees
(Why is the institution of marriage overrated?)

One king really loved issuing decrees. He wrote a decree that everyone should eat only with spoons. Then a decree that everyone should wear only blue pants. Then a decree that everyone should marry only with his permission.

People obeyed because they feared the king. But they didn't become happier.

One day a wise man said to the king:

"Your Majesty, you can issue any decree. But you can't force people to love each other. A marriage by decree isn't a family. It's just a piece of paper."

The king thought for a moment and rescinded all the decrees regarding the family.

People breathed a sigh of relief. Those who wanted to live together, did so. Those who didn't, separated. And everyone became happier.

Question:
Do you think it's possible to force people to love each other through decree? What's more important—a piece of paper or what people actually feel?

MAIN:
A piece of paper doesn't make a family. Love makes a family.

10.8.4 The story of the bird that flies alone
(A look through the "Concept of a Free Man")

A bird flew in the sky. It flew alone, but it wasn't lonely. It knew it could join the flock at any moment, or it could fly on its own.

One day another bird flew up to her and asked:

— Can I fly with you?

"You can," the first one answered. "But know this: I fly where I want. If it's your way, go ahead. If not, you can always turn around."

So they flew together. Not because they had to. But because they wanted to.

Question:
Why do you think the birds flew together, even though each was free? What kept them together—obligation or desire?

MAIN:
A free person is in a union not out of obligation, but out of sincere desire.

10.8.5 A Tale of Two Travelers
(How to build relationships for a Free Man?)

Two travelers were walking through the desert. Each had their own path, but for a while they were on the same path.

They could walk side by side. They could help each other. They could share water.

But they knew that at any moment their paths could diverge. And that was okay.

They didn't demand that each other stay close. They weren't offended if one wanted to rest while the other wanted to move on. They simply stayed together as long as they were happy.

And when their paths diverged, they thanked each other for the journey and went on their way. Each on their own.

Question:
What do you think helped the travelers to part ways peacefully when their paths diverged?
Why didn't they quarrel?

MAIN:
The union of two free people is not a cage.
This path is nearby, as long as we are on the same path.

10.8.6 The Story of the Nest and the Chicks (About Children)

The eagle built a nest on a high cliff. She hatched her chicks. She fed them, kept them warm, and protected them.

The chicks grew, became covered with feathers, and learned to spread their wings.

And then one day the eagle pushed the chicks out of the nest.

"Mom, what are you doing?" they screamed as they fell down.

"Fly!" the eagle cried. "You can do it!"

The chicks flapped their wings in fear. And flew off. At first hesitantly, then more and more boldly.

They returned to the nest, but the eagle pushed them out again.

“Fly on!” she said. “Find your rocks, your nests. I gave you life. Now live yourselves.”

The chicks flew away. And the eagle was left alone. But she was happy: her children were flying.

Question:
Why do you think the eagle pushed her chicks out of the nest? Did she fall out of love with them, or was this just her way of showing her love?

MAIN:
You gave birth – it’s your choice. They don’t owe you anything.
Your task is to prepare them for flight.

Chapter 10.9 The Story of Vova and His Parents (About Parents)

Vova's parents loved him very much. They wanted him to grow up to be a good person. They told him what to do, how to think, who to be friends with, where to go.

Vova obeyed. He thought his parents knew best.

But when he grew up, he realized he wasn't living his own life. He became an engineer because his dad was an engineer. He married the girl his mom chose. He lived in the city where his parents grew up.

But there was no happiness.

One day he met an old sage and asked:

— Why am I unhappy? I did everything right, just as my parents taught me.

The sage replied:

"Your parents gave you life. It's a priceless gift. But they can't give you your life. You must create it yourself. Respect your parents, thank them, but live by your own wits."

Vova thought for a moment. Then he went to his parents and said:

"Mom, Dad, I love you very much. Thank you for everything. But now I will live on my own. Forgive me if I upset you."

The parents were offended at first. But then they realized: their child had grown up.

Question:
Do you listen to your parents or are you starting to think for yourself?

MAIN:
Your parents gave you life. You must live it yourself.

Chapter 10.10 A Story About Three Friends (About Friends)

Petya had three friends.

His first friend, Kolya, was always there when Petya was happy. When Petya got straight A's, when he received gifts, when he was in a good mood—Kolya was right there. But when Petya was down, Kolya disappeared.

The second friend, Misha, was there when Petya was down. He comforted, helped, and supported him. And when Petya was feeling better, Misha would say, "Well, you can manage without me now," and leave.

The third friend, Sasha, was always there. Through good times and bad. He didn't disappear when things were good, and he didn't leave when things were bad. He was simply a friend.

One day Petya asked his grandfather:

- Grandpa, which of them is a real friend?

The grandfather replied:

— The one who never disappears. Never.

Question:
Which of your friends never disappears?

MAIN:
A friend isn't someone you have fun with. A friend is someone who doesn't disappear when things are tough.

Chapter 10.11 The Cactus Story
(About enemies and toxic people)

In one pot lived different flowers: a rose, a daisy, a bellflower. And one more strange flower – a cactus.

The cactus was prickly. Whenever anyone came close to it, it would prick everyone with its needles and cause pain.

"Why are you prickly?" the flowers asked.

"I didn't do it on purpose," answered Cactus. "I was born this way."

The flowers endured. They thought Cactus would improve. But he didn't improve—he was a cactus.

One day, a new flower was planted in a pot—a violet. It was delicate and beautiful. As soon as it grew, a cactus pricked it with its needles. The violet became ill and nearly died.

Then the gardener took the Cactus and transplanted it into a separate pot.

"Why did you remove it?" the flowers asked.

"Because he was in your way. He's not bad, he's just like that. He's fine alone, and you're fine without him."

Question:
Are there people in your life who hurt you? Maybe they just need to live separately?

MAIN:
Prickly people are like cacti. They're happy alone, but you're happy without them.

Chapter 10.12 The Story of the New Girl
(About dating and new people)

A new girl came to class. Her name was Anya. She sat at the last desk and sat quietly, not talking to anyone.

The guys looked at her and turned away - their own business, their own friends, why do they need a new girl?

And Petya came up.

“Hello,” he said. “Why are you sitting alone?”

“I don’t know anyone,” Anya answered.

"Would you like me to show you around school?" Petya asked.

Anya nodded.

They walked together. Petya showed her the cafeteria, the gym, and the library. Anya got her bearings a bit.

The next day she was already smiling. And a week later she had new friends.

The teacher asked Petya:

- Petya, why did you approach her and the others didn’t?

"I don't know," Petya shrugged. "I just came up to her. She's new."

Question:
Do you approach new people or wait for them to approach you?

MAIN:
Every new person is a new universe. Discover them.

Chapter 10.13 The Story of a Boy Who Was Afraid of Being Alone (Loneliness as a Choice)

Once upon a time there was a boy. He was very afraid to be alone. When everyone left, he became scared and sad.

He called his friends, ran to his neighbors, just so as not to be alone.

One day he went to visit his grandmother in the village. She lived alone. She had no neighbors nearby, only forest and fields.

The boy arrived and asked:

- Grandma, aren’t you afraid to be alone?

“No,” Grandma smiled. “I’m fine.”

- What are you doing alone?

— I talk to myself. I play with myself. I make friends with myself. When you learn to be friends with yourself, you will never be lonely.

The boy tried it. At first it felt strange. But then he suddenly realized he was interested in himself.

He returned to the city and was no longer afraid of loneliness. He knew: when he was alone, he was not alone. He was with himself.

Question:
Can you be alone? Or do you always need others?

MAIN:
Those who can't be alone will never learn to be with others. Start with yourself.


SECTION 10 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.

· I remember the rule NNNN: no one owes anything to anyone?
· Do I follow the Sun's principle: shine equally on everyone?
· I understand that happiness is within – I don’t expect it from others, but create it myself?
· Do I apply the "Eye for an Eye" rule: do I treat a person the way he treats me?
· Am I building a family as a union of love, and not as an institution of duty?
· Am I filtering my environment and removing toxic people?
· I can be alone - and then I feel good with others?

MAIN:
People are the most valuable and the most dangerous thing in your life.
Choose them carefully.

SECTION 11. THE SUPERMAN
(Stories about the power that lies within)

Introduction to the section

A story about a man who didn't know his own strength

Once upon a time there lived a man. An ordinary man, unremarkable in no way. He worked, raised children, and grew old.

One day, he was walking through the forest and saw a huge boulder blocking the path. People avoided it, making a three-kilometer detour.

The man approached the stone and pushed it—it didn't even budge. He called for help, but no one answered.

Then he sat down next to her and thought. He recalled his childhood, his youth, his adulthood. He recalled all the difficulties he had overcome. He recalled how once, when his son was in trouble, he ran faster than he had ever run in his life.

And suddenly he realized: the power had always been within him. He just didn't know how to activate it. It only awakened at critical moments.

He stood up, walked up to the stone, leaned his hands on it... and moved it.

There was no longer a stone on the path.

People were surprised: "How did you do that? You're just an ordinary person!"

And he replied: "I am ordinary. I just finally got to know the real me."

MAIN:
There's a power slumbering within you. Get to know it.

Chapter 11.1 The Tale of the Sleeping Giant
(We are all superhumans)

In a certain cave, a giant slept. He slept for a thousand years. People passed by and didn't know there was a giant inside.

Sometimes the giant would wake up. He would stretch, the mountain would shake, and the people would be frightened. But then the giant would fall asleep again.

One wise man said:

"There's a giant like that inside each of us. It's asleep. But when we really need it, it wakes up. Then we can lift a car, save a child, do the impossible."

"How do you wake up a giant?" the people asked.

“It’s very necessary,” the sage replied. “When it’s truly necessary.”

Question:
Has your giant woken up yet? Or is he still sleeping?

MAIN:
There is a power dormant within you. You can awaken it.

Chapter 11.2 The Story of Mom and the Car
(Testimonies of the Awakened Force)

A woman was walking with her young son when a car parked nearby rolled and pinned the boy against the wall.

The woman ran up, braced herself against the car, and... lifted it. A heavy machine, weighing over a ton. Lifted it and tossed it aside.

The boy was saved.

Then the men came up to her and said:

— How did you do that? You're so small and weak!

“I don’t know,” the woman replied. “I just saw my son under the car. I wasn’t thinking. I just did it.”

The neighbors tried to move the car later, but ten people couldn't. But one mother managed it.

Because a giant has awakened inside.

Question:
For whom or what would you lift a car?

MAIN:
When you have to save those you love, the giant within you awakens.

Chapter 11.3 The Story of the Toy and the Remote Control
(Man as a bio-suit)

Little Seryozha had a favorite radio-controlled car. It was beautiful, red, and had shiny wheels. He could control it with the remote control: it went forward, backward, turned, and flashed its headlights.

One day the car broke down. A wheel fell off, the paint peeled off. Seryozha was upset and was about to throw it away. But Dad said:

— Wait. The car is just a piece of metal. And the real you is the one holding the remote control. You're not broken, are you? So go fix the car.

Seryozha thought for a moment. He realized that he wasn't a machine. He was the one who controlled it.

Our body is like that little car. Beautiful, fast, capable of so many things. But if the body ages or gets sick, the real me doesn't go away. The real me is the one inside, the one who sees the world through my eyes and makes decisions.

Question:
Who do you think is more important - the car or the one holding the remote control?

MAIN:
You are not a machine. You are the one who holds the remote control and controls it.

Chapter 11.4 The Story of the Stairs in the House
(Ladder of Evolution)

Imagine a multi-story building. On the first floor live microbes and bugs. On the second, fish. On the third, mice and rabbits. On the fourth, monkeys. On the fifth floor, we—humans—live.

A man stands on his fifth floor, looks down and thinks: "How low those on the first floor were! And I am almost at the very top!"

But if you look up, you'll see the house doesn't end. There's a sixth floor, a seventh, an eighth. Those who live there are smarter, stronger, and kinder than us. We don't see them, just as an ant doesn't see a person, but they're there.

This means there's room for growth. It's not about jumping to the next floor—that would take millions of years. It's about growing within yourself: learning, training, becoming better. This is something everyone can do. And that, too, is a step up. Not up the ladder of species—up the ladder of self.

Question:
What do you think, if there is a sixth floor above the fifth, who could live there?
Or maybe “psychics” and others are the “next” level (step) of evolution?

MAIN:
You are not the pinnacle. You are simply on your step.

Chapter 11.5 The Story of the Flashlight and the Treasure
(The power of thought / quantum view)

A pirate buried treasure on an island. He buried a chest of gold and drew a map. But the map was strange: it depicted not an island, but the treasure itself. The pirate thought that if he stared long and hard at the map, the treasure would be found.

Of course, nothing was found.

Another pirate did something different. He took a flashlight, drew a real map of the island, and went searching. Where the flashlight shone, he saw a road, trees, and rocks. And eventually, he found the chest.

Our thoughts are like a flashlight. Where we shine it, we see it. If you shine it on your fears, you'll see only fears. If you shine it on your dreams, you'll see the path to your dreams.

Question:
Where are you shining your flashlight today?

MAIN:
You see only what you look at. Choose what you look at.

Chapter 11.6 The Story of a Book That Changes Lives
(The Power of Thought by William Atkinson)

One man read many books. But his life did not change. He read and forgot.

Another man read one book, "The Power of Thought." He read it slowly, a page a day. And he did what it said.

A year later, his life changed beyond recognition.

"What's the secret?" they asked him.

"I didn't just read. I did," he replied.

Question:
Do you read books to know or to do?

MAIN:
Read not to know. Read to change.

Chapter 11.7 The Story of the Map and the Legs
(Mathematics and miracles: how do they go together?)

One traveler bought the most detailed map of the world. He studied it inside and out, knowing every pass, every village.

But he never went on the trip.

“Why?” he said. “I already know everything.”

The other traveler didn't have a map. He simply walked wherever his eyes led him. He often hit dead ends and wandered in circles.

One day they met. The first was sitting with a map, but he hadn't gone anywhere. The second, dirty and tired, was climbing out of yet another swamp.

"Let's do it together," said the second one. "You have the map, I have the legs."

We went together. And we got there.

Question:
Who was more important - the map or the legs?

MAIN:
A map without legs is dead. Legs without a map are blind.
Only together they lead to the goal.

Chapter 11.8 The Story of a Shaman and an Ordinary Boy
(Psychics, shamans, witches - who are they?)

In one village there lived a shaman. He knew how to heal people, communicate with spirits, and foretell the future. People came to him for advice.

In the same village lived an ordinary boy, Petya. He didn't heal anyone, and he couldn't see the future. But sometimes he had dreams that came true. Sometimes he knew who was calling on the phone without even looking. Sometimes he sensed it was going to rain.

He asked the shaman:

- Why can you do it and I can’t?

"You can do it too," the shaman replied. "You just don't practice. I listen to myself every day, I train my gift. But you live like everyone else, and your gift sleeps."

Petya began training. And within a year, he, too, began to see and feel things that others couldn't.

Question:
Do you listen to your intuition? Or do you live like everyone else?

MAIN:
Shamans and psychics aren't the chosen ones. They simply weren't afraid to look within themselves. Take a look too.

Chapter 11.9 The Story of the Fire and the Ladder
(Critical situations as an awakening)

A fire broke out in a house. People ran out into the street, screaming and crying.

A little boy remained on the third floor. He stood by the window, unsure what to do. The firefighters had no ladder.

Suddenly, a man ran out of the crowd. He ran to the wall, grabbed a drainpipe, and climbed up. Quick as a cat. A minute later, he was at the window, grabbed the boy, and climbed down.

Then everyone was surprised:

— How did you manage to climb up to the third floor through the pipe? It's impossible!

“I don’t know,” the man replied. “I just saw a boy in the window. I wasn’t thinking. I was just climbing.”

Then he tried again, but couldn't. The strength came only when it was needed.

Question:
Have you ever done the impossible because there was no other way?

MAIN:
Don't wait for life to kick your ass. Learn and develop your skills yourself.

Chapter 11.10 The Story of the Wall and the Door
(The Limits of Superman)

One man decided he could do anything. He walked up to a stone wall and tried to walk through it. He hit his forehead—it hurt.

He approached the river and tried to cross the water; he would have drowned if he hadn’t been saved.

He came to the sage and said:

— Why can't I do it? I'm superhuman!

"Superhuman doesn't mean omnipotent," the sage replied. "You can't overturn the laws of nature. But you can become stronger, smarter, kinder. That's your superpower."

The man thought for a moment and went to study, train, and grow. And after a few years, he truly became stronger and smarter. But he didn't smash his head through the wall—why? He found the door.

Question:
Are you trying to break down walls with your head or looking for doors?

MAIN:
Know your boundaries so you can expand them. But don't confuse boundaries with illusions.

Chapter 11.11 The Story of Three Brothers and a Fire
(Your power is in your hands)

Once upon a time there were three brothers. The first was called Knowledge, the second - Faith, and the third - Action.

One day they went hiking. It got cold in the forest at night. They needed to light a fire.

Knowledge said, "I know how to light a fire. You need dry branches, tinder, and flint. I've read about it in books." But he didn't light the fire himself—he lacked the strength and confidence.

Vera said, "I believe we'll get warm! I can feel the warmth!" But he didn't know which side to approach the branches from, so he just sat and waited for a miracle.

Action grabbed the matches and began lighting the damp branches. They smoked but didn't ignite. Action grew angry, threw the matches down, stamped his feet, but no fire appeared.

The brothers had a fight. Knowledge shouted, "Without me, you're savages!" Vera shouted, "Without me, you're soulless robots!" Action shouted, "Without me, you're useless chatterboxes!"

An old forester passed by. He looked at the brothers, sighed, and said:

"Knowledge, find some dry branches under the spruce. Faith, don't worry about the fire going out—it will flare up again. Action, wait until the branches dry, and then add them one by one."

The brothers did as he said. The fire blazed.

The forester smiled:
— Remember: Knowledge without Action is dead. Action without Knowledge is blind. And Faith without both is just a dream. Only together will you find warmth.

Question:
Which brother is closer to you? Knowledge? Faith? Action? And which one do you lack?

MAIN:
Strength doesn't come from knowledge. It doesn't come from faith. It doesn't come from actions. Strength comes from the combination of all three.


SECTION 11 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.

· I know we're all superhumans? We all have powers, they're just dormant?
· I believe that the mother lifting the car is not a fiction, but a fact?
· I understand that my body is an instrument (bio-suit), and the soul is the operator?
· I remember that man is not the pinnacle of evolution, but just a step?
· Do I believe that my attention changes reality (the power of thought)?
· I understand that mathematics and miracle are two levels of the same reality?
· I know that psychics are not a chosen few, they just developed what everyone has?
· Am I able to enter a resourceful state consciously, without waiting for a critical situation?
· Do I know my real boundaries and not confuse them with imaginary fears?
· Do I remember the formula of my strength: knowledge multiplied by faith multiplied by actions?

MAIN:
You are superhuman. You are just sleeping. Wake up.

SECTION 12. HEALTH AND TOOLS
(Stories about how to take care of yourself)

Introduction to the section

A story about an astronaut and a spacesuit

An astronaut has a spacesuit. Without it, outer space means death. The spacesuit provides air, warmth, and protection.

You have a spacesuit too. This is your body.

You can run, jump, hug your mom, eat ice cream, and laugh until you drop.

But if the spacesuit breaks, everything will stop.

Therefore, it needs to be looked after. Fueled (fed) on time. Ventilated (breathes). Rested (slept). Cleaned (washed). Trained so it doesn't rust.

MAIN:
Your body is your spaceship. Take care of it.

Chapter 12.0: The Blacksmith and His Bellows (The Main Rule of Health: Habits are More Important than Motivation)

Once upon a time there lived a blacksmith. He was strong and hale. But as he aged, he noticed: his hands weren't what they used to be, his back ached, and he had no energy.

He went to the old master.

"I want to be healthy like before," he said. "Give me motivation."

The master chuckled:

"Motivation is like a spark from a flint. It flares up and then goes out. You don't need sparks. You need bellows."

"Bellows?" the blacksmith asked in surprise.

— Yes. The fire in the forge doesn't burn from sparks. It burns from the bellows. The bellows pump air constantly, without stopping. The fire burns evenly and hotly.

— And what are “furs” for health?

— It's habits. Every day, at the same time, you do the same thing. Exercise. Water. Sleep. Eat. Workout. You don't think—you just do it. And after a month, it becomes as natural as breathing.

The blacksmith returned home and began to develop habits. Not heroic feats, but simple daily routines.

Six months later, he was strong and vigorous again. And the fire in his forge burned steadily—without motivation, simply on the bellows.

Question:
What do you think is better: waiting for motivation or creating a habit?

MAIN:
Habits are the bellows of your health. They don't fade away like motivation.
They work every day.

Chapter 12.1 The Story of the Gardener and the Roses (On Discipline)

The gardener had a beautiful garden. Roses, lilies, and tulips grew there. Everyone admired this garden.

One day a neighbor came and asked:

"How do you manage to grow such beautiful flowers? You're always watering them, pruning them, fertilizing them. It's so boring!"

"It's not boring," the gardener replied. "It's love. I don't force myself to tend the garden. I just love it and do what needs to be done."

It's the same with the body. Discipline isn't about forcing yourself to suffer. It's about loving yourself and eliminating everything unnecessary that prevents you from being healthy.

Question:
Do you take care of yourself with love or through force?

MAIN:
Discipline isn't about beating yourself up. It's about caring.

Chapter 12.2 The Battery Story (Sleep and Mode)

The boy had a battery-powered toy. He played with it all day, but the batteries died and the toy stopped working.

The boy put in new batteries and the toy started working again.

A person is like a battery-powered toy. But our batteries are sleep. If we don't get enough sleep, the batteries don't have time to recharge, and we can't do anything: play, learn, or enjoy ourselves.

One boy decided that sleeping was boring. He went to bed late and woke up early. After a week, he became grumpy, tired, and didn't want to do anything.

Another boy went to bed at 10 p.m. and woke up on his own, without an alarm clock. He always had the energy to play, study, and have fun.

Question:
Are your batteries charged? Are you getting enough sleep?

MAIN:
Sleep isn't a waste of time. It's recharging the batteries of your bio-suit.

Chapter 12.3 The Story of the Hare and the Tortoise
(Physical education: Tabata)

The hare ran quickly, but not far. The tortoise crawled slowly, but for a long time.

One day, they argued about who was better. The hare ran the hundred meters in five seconds and collapsed. The tortoise crawled for an hour but never reached the finish line.

The coach came and said:

"In sports, the most important thing is not to do it quickly or for long. The most important thing is to do it correctly. Do the exercises wisely."

And he showed them Tabata—20 seconds of work, 10 seconds of rest. The hare managed to run, the tortoise to crawl. Everyone was happy.

Question:
Do you exercise? Or just run around aimlessly?

MAIN:
Better 20 minutes wisely than 2 hours uselessly.

Chapter 12.4 The Story of Spring Cleaning (Fasting)

The girl had a large closet at home. She stored toys, books, and clothes in it. The closet quickly filled up, and the girl stopped cleaning it.

One day my mother said:

"Let's do a spring cleaning. Let's throw out everything unnecessary and keep only what's necessary."

They opened the closet and began sorting through it. There was so much junk in there! Old candy wrappers, broken toys, useless pieces of paper. They threw it all out. The closet felt clean and free.

Our bodies also sometimes need a thorough cleaning, once every 3-4 weeks. When we fast for a day, our bodies begin to eliminate all the accumulated waste.

Question:
Have you ever tried not eating for one day?
Your body will thank you.

MAIN:
Sometimes the best thing you can do for your body is to not put anything in it.

Chapter 12.5 The Story of the Morning Dew (Morning Exercises)

In the morning, the sun rose, dew glistened on the leaves, birds sang. The whole world woke up.

But one boy wouldn't wake up. He lay in bed, unable to open his eyes. His body was asleep, his head was asleep, even his toes were asleep.

One day, he saw a kitten waking up. First, the kitten stretched, arched its back, then sharpened its claws, then washed itself with its paw. And only then did it go to eat.

The boy decided to try the same. In the morning, he got up and did simple movements: stretching, bending, squatting. Just two minutes. And his body woke up, his mind started working.

Question:
Do you wake up on your own or do you need to be woken up?

MAIN:
Morning sets the tone for the whole day. Wake yourself up right.

Chapter 12.6: A Story About Vitamins and Grandma's Garden Beds
(Vitamins and Supplements)

Grandma grew vegetables and fruits in the village: carrots, apples, currants. Grandma fed her grandson only her own produce.

The grandson grew up healthy and strong.

One day, he went to the city and started eating everything in sight—chips, candy, soda. A year later, he began getting sick often and getting tired quickly.

The doctor said:

- You lack vitamins.

"Which ones?" the boy asked.

— The ones in grandma's vegetables.

The boy returned to his grandmother and became healthy again.

Question:
Do you eat healthy food or only tasty food?

MAIN:
Vitamins are the fuel for your bio-suit. Fuel up properly.

Chapter 12.7 The Story of 21 Days
(On habits and retraining)

One boy wanted to learn to play the guitar. He bought a guitar, practiced for a day, and then gave up.

A month later I picked up the guitar again, practiced for one day, and then gave it up again.

This went on for a whole year. He still hadn't learned.

Another boy practiced every day for 15 minutes. The first few days were difficult; his fingers ached. After a week, it got easier. After three weeks, he was already playing simple melodies. And a year later, he was performing in concerts.

“How did you learn?” they asked him.

— I simply didn't miss a day. 21 days—and the habit stuck.

Question:
What do you want to be able to do? Are you ready to practice for 21 days straight?

MAIN:
21 days of patience - 21 years of ease. Be patient.

Chapter 12.8 The Story of the Plan and the Magic Timer
(Productivity Tools)

Petya had a lot to do: homework, practice, cleaning, helping his mother. He didn't know what to do, and as a result, he couldn't keep up with anything.

He went to his older brother.

“Teach me how to do everything,” he asked.

His brother gave him a notebook and said:

— Every evening, write down what needs to be done tomorrow. And write it in order: first the most important, then the rest.

Petya tried. By the morning, he already knew what to do. He didn't waste time thinking. He managed to do everything.

His brother also taught him a magic timer.

If you really don't want to start, set a timer for two minutes and do it. After two minutes, you'll be ready to keep going.

Petya tried it. And it's true—he always wanted to continue.

Question:
Do you plan your day or do you live as you please?

MAIN:
Every big thing starts with a small “I’ll just try.”

Chapter 12.9 The Story of the Flower and the Rain (Water and Breath)

A flower grew in the desert. The sun was blazing, the wind was drying, there was no rain. The flower was drying up.

The wind flew by and asked:

— What's wrong with you?

“I’m thirsty,” the flower whispered.

“And you drink,” said the wind.

- There is no water.

The wind flew away. And the flower withered.

Another flower grew by the stream. It drank water every day, blossomed, and enjoyed life.

It's the same with humans. Without water, they wither. They need to drink every day, a little at a time, throughout the day. Like a flower by the stream.

And you also need to breathe. Deeply, calmly. When you're scared, breathe in a square pattern: inhale, pause, exhale, pause. And the fear goes away.

Question:
Do you drink water every day? Can you breathe when you're scared?

MAIN:
Water and air are free. But without them, there is no life. Don't forget about the base.

Chapter 12.10 The Story of the Bow and Arrow
(Stress and Recovery)

The boy had a bow and arrows. He drew the string back and shot. Then he drew it back again and again.

If he drew his bow and held it drawn all day, the bow would break. The bowstring would snap.

It's the same with humans. Stress is like a taut bowstring. A little tension is good, the arrow flies. But if you tense up all the time, you'll break.

You need to relax. Sleep, walk, play, chat with friends. Then the bowstring will be ready to fire again.

Question:
Do you know how to relax?
Or are you always tense, like a drawn bow?

MAIN:
You are not a battery.
You can't work without recharging.
Rest.


SECTION 12 SUMMARY
Self-assessment checklist.

· Do I maintain discipline without forcing myself, through habits?
· I go to bed at 10:00 pm, wake up without an alarm clock and practice an afternoon nap?
· I do Tabata for 20 minutes 2-3 times a week?
· I fast for 36 hours once every two weeks?
· I do morning exercises for 2 minutes and raise my legs up?
· Am I taking vitamins based on tests and not “just in case”?
· I know that a new habit is formed in 21 days?
· I plan my to-do list in the evening and do it in the morning?
· Do I use the 2-minute method to start a difficult task?
· Am I drinking enough water and breathing consciously?
· Do I recover as much as I put in the effort?

MAIN:
Health is a basic asset.
Without it, everything else is meaningless.
Take care of your body - you only have one.

GLOSSARY OF TERMS

Introduction

You've just read two books. Perhaps some terms seemed complex or unfamiliar. Somewhere along the way, you encountered words that required further explanation.

This dictionary is your guide. There are no complex scientific definitions here. Just simple explanations—understandable for mathematicians, humanities scholars, and even children.

PART 1. BUSINESS AND MONEY

1. BM — Business Model
Simply put, it's how a business makes money. It's the way a company converts its efforts into revenue. "A bakery makes bread and sells it" is one business model. "A website that helps people rent out apartments" is a completely different business model. Understanding a business model is the answer to the question: "How exactly does your business make money?"

2. MVP (Minimum Viable Product)
This is when you want to do something big, but first you build a very simple and cheap version. Your goal isn't to build the perfect product, but to quickly test whether people even need it. Before building a restaurant, try selling food from a stand. If people buy it, you can think about what's next.
If not, you saved millions.

3. KPI — Key Performance Indicator
This is your personal KPI. If your goal is to get stronger, your KPI might be the number of push-ups you do today. If you want to earn more, your KPI might be the number of calls you make to clients. A KPI helps you understand: were you really working today or just pretending to? This is your success diary.

4. ROI (Return on Investment)
A metric that helps you understand whether your investment has paid off. Invested 100 rubles and got back 200? Excellent ROI! Invested 100 and got back 50? Bad ROI; the idea didn't work. The formula is simple: (Revenue minus Costs) divided by Costs and multiplied by 100%. If the number is greater than zero, you're in the black; if less, you're broke.

5. Efficiency – Coefficient of Performance
This shows how effectively you use your resources. You can sit at work for 12 hours and get nothing done (low efficiency), or you can do everything in 2 hours (high efficiency). It's about results, not time spent. High efficiency means you do what needs to be done quickly and efficiently.

6. CA — Target Audience
This is the group of people your product or idea is intended for. For a toy store, the target audience is children and their parents. For a section
Boxing's target audience is teenagers and adult men. The more accurately you know your target audience, the easier it is to sell. You wouldn't sell boxing gloves to grandmothers, would you?

7. AI – Artificial Intelligence
These are computer programs that can learn and do things similar to humans: draw pictures, write texts, talk, and find errors. AI is everywhere now: in phones, in search engines, in various programs. AI won't completely replace humans, but it can do boring work for you 100 times faster.

8. P2P (P2P) - from person to person
This is when people negotiate directly, without intermediaries. For example, you sell a bicycle to a friend—that's P2P. Or when people lend money to each other through a special app, bypassing a bank. No intermediaries means no unnecessary fees or bureaucracy.

9. n8n
This is the name of a useful computer robot. It can connect various other programs and configure them to work autonomously, without human intervention. For example, when someone submits a request on the website, n8n can automatically send a thank-you email, save the data in a spreadsheet, and notify you via Telegram. And all without your intervention.

10. MLM – Multi-Level Marketing (or Network Marketing)
This is a sales method where a company attracts not just salespeople, but partners. These partners can not only sell their own products but also invite other partners, receiving a percentage of their sales. This is what a "network" is. This model has both advantages (you can start without investment and earn your first profits within the first few months; the company handles all organizational matters) and disadvantages (everything you do is by someone else's rules and you don't own anything).

11. GTO (Group Trade Turnover)
A key term for MLM. It's the total turnover of your entire structure (all the people you've recruited and all the people they've recruited). It's the GTO that distinguishes a truly self-replicating system from a simple referral program that only pays for the first line. GTO is the mathematical basis of recursion, the fuel that makes self-replication infinite and economically viable.

12. Media — Mass Media
These are all the channels through which people get news and information: television, newspapers, radio, news websites, and online channels. The media can lie, they can tell the truth, or they can simply entertain. Your job is not to believe everything, but to verify and analyze.

13. Entrepreneur
It's not just a profession or a job description. Being an entrepreneur is a way of life and a way of thinking. They see opportunities where others see problems. They take responsibility, take risks (consciously), and create systems that benefit people and bring them money. An entrepreneur doesn't wait for someone else to give them a job—they create jobs themselves. Their main tool isn't a machine or money, but a mind capable of calculation, seeing connections, and unafraid of mistakes.

14. Entrepreneurial thinking
This is a unique perspective on the world. A person with this mindset doesn't wait for someone else to give them a job. They seek out opportunities to solve people's problems and make money doing so. They're not afraid to try, make mistakes, and try again. For them, a mistake isn't a failure, but an experience. This mindset can be developed at any age.

15. Systems thinking
It's the ability to see not just individual facts, but the whole picture. To understand how one thing connects to another. To see not just "the bus broke down," but to understand how it will impact traffic jams, delays, and sales at roadside stores. In business, this helps you avoid putting out fires every day, but rather build a system that prevents them from happening.

16. Cash flows
This is the movement of money. Money comes to you (salary, profit, gifts) - this is the "incoming flow". Money leaves you (purchases, paying bills,
Taxes are an "outflow." A successful business always ensures that inflows outweigh outflows. If the opposite is true, something needs to change immediately.

17. Business Mathematics
This isn't about complex formulas. It's about understanding that business is governed by simple laws of numbers. You can't get 100 sales if you have zero contacts. You can't earn a million if you only made 10 calls. This is the simple mathematics of success: the number of correct actions translates into the quality of the result.

18. Statistical units
In business, this is what you call every person you interact with (call, write, meet). You don't think, "Oh, he offended me by turning me down." You think, "Another one said 'no.' I'll move on." This helps you avoid getting upset by rejections. Rejection is just a statistic, not a personal tragedy.

19. Hypothesis
This is your assumption, a hunch that needs to be tested. "I think if we make cucumber-flavored lemonade, it will sell well." Until you've tested it, it's just a hypothesis. Business is a constant testing of hypotheses. Some are confirmed, others are not. The key is to test quickly and not fall in love with your ideas.

20. Scaling
This is when you've found a working way to make money and now you want to make it bigger. You started with three clients, now you have 300. You started with one store, now you've opened ten more. You're increasing ("scaling") your success. But you can only scale something that's already working well. Scaling a failure means going broke even faster.

21. Money chains
This is a sequence of actions that inevitably leads to money. For example: Made an advertisement→An interested client has arrived→ Bought
product →Received the money. That's the chain. If even one link is missing (there's advertising, but the client doesn't come), the chain breaks. The businessman's job is to build strong chains that function without fail.

22. Life cycle (of the project)
Every business or idea goes through its own stages: birth (launch), rapid growth (when everything takes off), calm maturity (stability), and then decline and decline, when it loses its appeal. It's important to recognize this early and come up with something new, so as not to cling to a project that's already dead.

23. Passive income
This is money that comes to you without your daily work. For example, you rent out an apartment and collect rent. Or you write a book and receive a percentage of sales. Or you invest in stocks and receive dividends. But to earn passive income, you first need to work hard: earn money for an apartment, write a book, and accumulate capital.

24. Assets
This is what puts money in your pocket. The apartment you rent out. The car you drive as a taxi driver. Money in a bank account that earns interest. Your knowledge that you sell. The more assets you have, the richer you are. Poor people usually buy liabilities, and rich people buy assets.

25. Liabilities
These are the things that take money out of your pocket. The apartment you live in (you pay for electricity, water, and heating). The car that's just sitting in the garage (you pay taxes, insurance, and gas, and it only loses value). The loan you have to pay off. The things you bought and don't use. Liabilities make you poorer.

26. Lifecost and Basic Income
These are two related concepts, your financial foundation. Lifecost is the cost of your monthly living. Calculate everything: food, clothing, internet, transportation, utilities, entertainment. This is your starting point, your financial "zero." Basic income is that minimum amount (your Lifecost) that you can't go below. Anything you earn above this amount can be saved, invested, or spent on development. Anything below this amount puts you in the red and into debt. If you don't know your Lifecost, you're living blind.

27. Cashflow Quadrant
This is Robert Kiyosaki's theory, which divides all people into 4 types based on the way they earn money:
• R(Employee) - works for hire, sells his time.
• With(Specialist) - works for himself, is his own boss.
• B(Businessman) - he has other people working for him, his business runs without him.
• AND(Investor) - his money works for him.
The path to true freedom is the transition from the left quadrants (R, C) to the right (B, I).

28. Investors
These are people who have money and invest it in other people's projects to make even more money. They "plant seeds" with money and wait for a profitable "tree" to grow. Investors come in all shapes and sizes: some fund startups, some buy stocks, and some invest in real estate. The key is that they don't work with their hands; they work with capital.

29. Decomposition
This is the ability to break a large and complex goal into small, simple steps. The goal of "Learn English" is complex and unclear, but "Learn 3 new words today" is simple and clear. Decomposition turns "unclear how" into "clear what to do right now." You can only eat an elephant one bite at a time.

30. Doubles method
A simple planning method. You look at your results from last month and set a goal to double that this month. If you made 10 sales today, your goal is 20 next month, and 40 the month after that. It's a growth ladder that's easy to follow. It's achievable, unlike the abstract idea of ​​"I want a million tomorrow."

31. Unit of cost
This is your smallest "coin" in business that you work with. For example, if your product costs 100 rubles, then you base all your plans on multiplying those 100 rubles by the number of sales. If your average bill is 5,000 rubles, then you calculate everything in terms of those 5,000 rubles. This helps you think big, rather than worry about pennies.

32. Unit of time
Your planning and reporting cycle. Businesses typically calculate money by the month. If you plan daily, you're thinking about routine matters. If you plan monthly, you're thinking about tactics. If you plan annually, you're thinking about strategy. If you plan for 100+ years, you're thinking about legacy. Choose your timeframe and plan within it. This helps you avoid stressing out every day.

33. Law of large numbers
A simple and powerful law: the more attempts you make, the closer your result will be to the expected one. If you call 10 times and no one buys, it means nothing. But if you call 1,000 times, you can draw precise conclusions. Quantity turns into quality.

34. Pareto Law (20/80)
The golden rule of effectiveness: 20% of your efforts produce 80% of your results. And the remaining 80% of your efforts produce only 20% of your results. Therefore, it's important to find those crucial 20% of tasks and focus on them first. For example, 20% of your clients generate 80% of your profits. Find them and work with them.

35. Double Pareto
This law works with people. If you invite 100 people, only 20 of them (20%) will become your active assistants. And of those 20, only 4 people (20% of 20%) will do 80% of the work. It's math, and it's not offensive. Just don't expect everyone around you to be a star. Find your 4% and work with them.

36. Normal distribution (Gaussian)
This is a "bell" that describes any large group of people. In any class, there are 10% straight-A students, 10% failing students, and 80% good students. In any business, there are 10% super salespeople, 10% unsuccessful ones, and the rest are ordinary people. Don't waste your energy trying to make everyone a straight-A student—it's impossible. Work with those who deliver results.

37. Critical Mass
This is the minimum number of people, money, or resources you need to get things going, like a snowball. While you have a small following, you have to entertain them yourself. But once you've reached "critical mass," they'll start telling each other about you, and the process becomes unstoppable.

38. Cumulative effect
Small, almost imperceptible actions every day lead to enormous results many days later. A drop wears away a stone. If you read 10 pages every day, in a year you'll have read 15 books. If you save 100 rubles every day, in a year you'll have 36,500 rubles. Small steps work wonders.

39. Average value
A simple analysis tool. If you divide your total revenue by the number of customers, you'll find out how much each customer brings in on average (the average order value). This helps you make realistic plans. For example, if the average order value is 1,000 rubles, and you want to earn 100,000, you'll need 100 customers.

40. Synchronization of events (luck)
It's the scientific term for "luck." When you prepare for a long time (train, study, build connections), and then the right moment or person comes along. It seems like you just got lucky. But in reality, it was your preparation that met opportunity. Luck comes to those who are prepared.

41. Levers
It's a way to influence big things with small forces. In business, leverage is the only way to escape the "selling your time" trap. One person with leverage is more powerful than a thousand without it. There are five types of leverage: people, money, systems, AI, and content.

42. Occam's Razor
An old philosophical principle: "Don't invent unnecessary things." If something can be explained simply, don't look for complicated explanations. If business can be simplified, don't create unnecessary complications. The simpler the system, the more reliable it is. The fewer parts it has, the less likely it is to break. Cut away all unnecessary details.

42.1 Complication Barrier(Chapter 1.22). The concept that big money lies beyond the willingness to go through complex calculations and systems, while the brain's natural desire to conserve energy pushes people toward simple but low-paying models. The opposite of Occam's Razor.

43. Low-hanging fruit
These are the easiest and quickest victories. Don't climb to the very top of the tree for just one apple. First, gather all the low-hanging, ripe ones. In business, this means: do the easiest and most obvious first. Sell to those who are ready to buy. Do what will give you the quickest results, and then tackle the difficult ones.

44. Pumpkin Method
This is a strategy of focusing on your best clients. The metaphor is simple: you can't grow a giant pumpkin by watering the entire field. In business, this means finding your most profitable and enjoyable clients ("champion pumpkins"), focusing all your resources on them, and weeding out problematic and low-value clients ("weeds"). The key idea: the customer isn't always right. Only the right customer is right.

45. The Smart Copywriter Strategy
This is a simple and powerful strategy for a quick start and growth. Instead of reinventing the wheel, you study the best in your niche, take their best tricks, and assemble your own unique system. The strategy's three steps are: 1) Study, 2) Build, 3) Launch. The main law of the market:
There's no shame in copying. It's shameful to copy without improving.

46. ​​Human factor
A reminder that people aren't perfect robots. They can get tired, make mistakes, get offended, get lazy, get sick, have bad days, and experience personal drama. A smart businessman always takes this into account in their plans. They build systems that are resilient to human error and build in a safety margin.

47. TO BE→ACT→ HAVE
The formula for achieving any goal. First, you make a decision and become the person who has already achieved the goal (BE). Your actions automatically follow from this new state (ACT). And the result (HAVE) becomes the inevitable consequence. Don't confuse the order.

48. Personal brand
It's your reputation and how others perceive you. If people think of you as "reliable, honest, and a professional," you have a strong personal brand. People will want to work with you and buy from you, even if your competitors offer lower prices. A personal brand takes years to build, but it always works for you.

49. Neural networks
These are intelligent computer programs that learn from a huge number of examples. Show a neural network a million cat photos, and it will learn to recognize cats in any new photo. Neural networks can now write text, draw pictures, compose music, translate, and even program. They won't replace you, but they can make boring work hundreds of times faster.

50. AI (general, text)
These are large language models that can process text: answer questions, write articles, translate, and generate ideas. The best examples are ChatGPT (OpenAI), YandexGPT, and GigaChat (Sber). They are trained on huge text arrays and can support dialogue, write code, compose emails, and much more. It's your personal assistant, always at your fingertips.

51. AI for pictures
These are neural networks that generate images based on text descriptions. You type in "cyberpunk red dragon" and get an image in a minute. Examples: Midjourney (the most popular for artistic images), Kandinsky (from Sber, good for Russian-language queries), and Shedevroom (from Yandex). They allow you to create unique illustrations, designs, and logos without an artist.

52. AI for music/sound
These are generative neural networks that compose music and sounds. You specify the style, mood, and instruments, and get a finished composition. Examples: Suno (a leader in generating songs with vocals), Udio (very high-quality sound). They allow you to create soundtracks, background music for videos, and jingles without a composer.

53. AI for video
These are neural networks that create videos based on text descriptions or images. This is the technology of the future, and it's already here. Examples include Sora (by OpenAI, not yet open to everyone, but boasting incredible quality) and Runway (already available, allowing for video editing and generation). They open up opportunities for one-person production of films, advertising, and animation.

54. AI Autobots / AI Assistants
These are AI-powered programs that automate communication and assistance. They can respond to clients in chats, provide consultations, schedule appointments, and fill out documents. Unlike traditional bots, they understand context and can engage in meaningful dialogue. They save hours of work every day.

55. AI virtual avatars
These are digital copies of real or fictional people, created using AI. They can move, speak, smile, and answer questions. They are used for video presentations, as presenters, and for narrating courses. Soon, anyone will be able to create their own digital double to work in their place.

56. Automation
This is when you make technology and programs do boring, repetitive work for humans. Instead of sitting and sending 100 emails manually, you can set up a program once, and it will do it in 5 minutes. Automation frees up your time for what really matters—creation, strategy, and relaxation.

57. Traffic
In business, this is the flow of visitors, clients, or people. "Lots of traffic" means lots of people visiting your website or store. Traffic can be free (from search engines, social media) or paid (from paid advertising). Without traffic, there are no sales. It's like a flow of water: without it, the mill doesn't work.

58. Content
This is any information you create and distribute to attract people's attention. Content is king in the modern world. Without content, there is no traffic. Without traffic, there is no money. There are four main types of content: text, photos, audio, and video.

59. Content plan
This is your daily content release schedule. It's like a meal plan: you know in advance what you'll eat for breakfast, lunch, and dinner. A content plan makes your promotion systematic, not chaotic. You don't have to think every morning, "What should I write about today?"; you just open your plan and get going. The simple formula for success is: Growth = Content × Time × Regularity. A content plan is what ensures regularity.

60. Promotion platforms
These are online platforms where your target audience gathers and where you can post your content. Each platform is its own "fishbowl" with its own rules and audience. In the book, we discuss four main ones: Telegram, YouTube, VKontakte, and Zen. Your task is to choose one or two platforms and work on them regularly, rather than spreading yourself too thin on everything at once.

61. Marketplaces
These are huge online platforms where thousands of different sellers sell their products, and buyers can find everything in one place. Examples: Wildberries, Ozon, Amazon, AliExpress. The marketplace doesn't produce the products itself; it simply provides a space for sellers and convenience for buyers. And it takes a commission for this.

62. Aggregators
These are websites that aggregate offers from multiple companies to make it easy for you to compare and choose. Aviasales aggregates all airline tickets, Bestchange aggregates all currency exchange services, and Sravni.ru aggregates bank and insurance companies. You don't sell tickets; you simply help people choose the best deals and get a commission.

63. Affiliate programs
This is when a company says, "Bring us customers, and we'll pay you a percentage of their purchases." You become a sort of agent for that company. Tell a friend about a good bank, and they sign up for a card—you get a bonus. Sell someone else's online course through your link—you get a commission. It works without its own inventory or warehouse.

64. Affiliate centers
These are websites that bring together many different affiliate programs. You don't have to search the internet for each program separately; everything is already in one place. You go there, choose, grab a link, and start promoting. It's very convenient for those looking to make money through affiliate programs.

65. Offline / Online
• Offline— this is everything that happens in the real, physical world, not on the Internet (a store near your home, meeting with friends in the park, a concert in a hall).
• Online— this is everything that happens on a phone or computer via the Internet (websites, social networks, online courses, video calls).

66. Freelance exchanges
These are websites where people look for temporary project work, and clients look for contractors. Need to design a logo? Go to a freelance marketplace; there are designers there. Need to write text? There are copywriters there. Freelancers sell their skills, clients buy them, and the marketplace takes a small commission for its services.

67. Financial exchanges
It's a place (mostly virtual now) where people trade money, currencies, and securities. It's like a huge market, but instead of potatoes and tomatoes, it's dollars, euros, and company stocks. Buyers and sellers meet on the exchange, and the exchange charges a commission for every transaction. The turnover there is enormous, so even a small commission can bring in billions.

68. Cryptocurrency
This is digital money that no government prints. It exists only on the internet, as records on the blockchain. The most famous cryptocurrency is Bitcoin. No one controls crypto (except the market), and its price can rise and fall wildly. You can get fabulously rich with crypto, or you can lose everything in a single day.

69. Stock exchanges
This is a place where people trade parts of companies—stocks. You can buy a small piece of Apple or Gazprom and become a co-owner. If the company grows and makes money, your share price rises, and you can receive a share of the profits (dividends). The stock market is one of the main ways to make your money work for you.

70. Forex
This is an international market where people trade various currencies (dollars, euros, and rubles). Traders try to predict which currency will appreciate and which will depreciate, and profit from the exchange rate differences. This is a very risky endeavor, especially for beginners. Professionals sometimes make money, but beginners lose their money 99% of the time.

71. Crowdfunding
This is when the creator of an idea (a film, a book, a game, an invention) asks ordinary people (the "crowd") to chip in to bring it to life. People donate, and the creator promises them a gift or a finished product. If many people like the idea, they chip in, and the project gets off the ground. Many great things have come into being this way.

72. Escrow services (Transaction guarantors)
This is a reliable intermediary that holds the buyer's money until both the seller and the buyer fulfill their promises. The buyer transfers the money to the escrow account, the seller ships the goods, the buyer receives them and confirms them—only then does the money go to the seller. It's safe for everyone; no one can cheat anyone.

73. Spread
This is the difference between the purchase price and the sale price. For example, an exchanger is willing to buy a dollar from you for 80 rubles and sell it to you for 82 rubles. The 2-ruble difference is the spread, the exchanger's profit. The wider the spread, the more the intermediary earns. The smaller the spread, the more profitable the deal is for you.

74. Conversion
This is an indicator of how many people did what you wanted them to do. For example, out of 100 people who saw your ad, 5 bought the product. Conversion = 5%. Out of 1,000 people who visited your website, 50 left a request—a conversion of 5%. The higher the conversion, the better and more effective your website, your ad, and your salesperson are.

75. Deadlines
These are the deadlines by which the work must be completed.
"Submit the report by Friday"—Friday is the deadline. Deadlines mobilize, preventing slacking off and endlessly procrastinating. Without deadlines, projects can drag on for years. With deadlines, you clearly understand: time is short, you have to get things done.

76. Checkpoints
These are intermediate goals on the way to the big one. Like stations on a train. They help us understand in time that we're on track and on schedule. For example, the goal is to earn 1,000,000 in a year. The milestones are: March 31st – 250,000, June 30th – 500,000, September 30th – 750,000. If you only have 100,000 in March, you'll see the problem right away, not in December.

77. Funnel (sales)
This is the journey a person takes from the moment they first learn about you to the moment they buy. Initially, many people enter (they saw the ad), then some drop out (they weren't interested), then some more (they didn't finish reading), and only a few make it to the purchase stage. If you draw a diagram, it looks like a funnel: a wide entrance and a narrow exit. The goal of a business is to make the funnel wider and lose fewer people at each stage.

78. Man-hours
This is a unit of measurement for work volume. If 1 person works 1 hour, that's 1 man-hour. If 100 people work 1 hour, that's 100 man-hours. This helps you understand how much effort needs to be put into a project. For example, if a project requires 1,000 man-hours, and you have a team of 10 people, they will complete it in 100 hours (4 days). This is useful for planning.

79. Trade turnover
This is the total amount of money a company received from selling goods or services over a given period (day, month, year). Don't confuse it with profit. Turnover is all the money that comes into the cash register. Profit is what's left after all expenses are subtracted from that money. You can have a huge turnover but a meager profit.

80. Commissions
This is the percentage charged by intermediaries for their work. A bank charges a commission for transferring money, a marketplace charges a commission for selling on its platform, and an aggregator charges a commission for attracting a client. Commissions can quietly eat into a significant portion of your income, so they should always be factored into your calculations.

81. Duties
This is a tax the government charges on the import or export of goods. If you bring a batch of goods from China, you'll have to pay duty at the border. The same applies if you ship goods abroad. Duties protect local producers (to prevent foreign goods from being too cheap) and contribute to the state budget.

82. Rate
The price of a unit of your time, skill, or resource. If you know your rate, you understand what's profitable to do yourself and what should be delegated. You set your rate yourself.

83. Margin
The difference between the selling price and the cost price. This shows how much you earn from a single sale. A high margin means a large safety margin. A low margin means you're working for volume.

84. Closed loop of the money chain
When the money you earn is used again to attract new clients. Once you make a profit, part goes straight into advertising, and part goes into development. The cycle never ends. If you break it, you start from scratch every time. This is the foundation for a "perpetual" business.

85. Compound interest
A mechanism whereby interest is calculated not only on the initial amount but also on the interest already accrued. Capital "generates" new capital. Formula: Future Value = Current Capital × (1 + Rate) ^ (Time).
This is one of three types of self-replication (along with people and the system). The main limitation: it requires start-up capital and time.

86. Reinvestment
Reinvesting a portion of earned profits back into the business to increase future income. Unlike simply "spending it all," reinvesting triggers compound interest. In business, this could be investing in new equipment, advertising, or hiring employees. In investments, it could be buying new shares with dividends received. The golden rule: at least 30% of income should be reinvested.

87. The 5 Units Rule (MVP)
A method for testing a business idea before making large-scale investments. Find 5 people interested in the idea. Sell 5 units. Get 5 reviews. Make 5 improvements. Only then scale. This is a minimum viable model (MVP), which allows you to test a hypothesis with minimal investment. If even 5 people don't buy the product, the idea doesn't work, and there's no need to invest millions.

88. Technotronic Age
A modern era in which technology (especially digital and information technologies) shapes the development of society, the economy, and everyday life. Characteristic features include automation, artificial intelligence, total connectivity, digital footprints, and accelerating change. In the technocratic age, those who quickly adapt and leverage technology win. Those who ignore it or fear it lose.

89. Luddite
A person who is negative about new technologies, fears them, or actively resists their implementation. The term originates from 19th-century English workers (Luddites) who destroyed machine tools, fearing that machines would put them out of work. In a modern context, a Luddite is someone who says, "Things were better before," rejects neural networks, automation, and digital tools, and thus condemns themselves to falling behind.

90. Critical thinking
The ability to analyze information, verify facts, distinguish opinion from evidence, and recognize logical fallacies and manipulation. A critical thinker doesn't take advertising, the media, or "gurus" at their word. They ask questions: "How do we know this?", "Who benefits?", "What evidence is there?", "What if it were the other way around?" This is a basic skill for a free person in a world of information overload.

91. Responsibility
The flip side of freedom. The more freedom of choice you have, the more responsible you are for the consequences of your decisions. A child has little freedom—their parents are responsible for them. A slave isn't free—their master is responsible for them. A free person is responsible for everything themselves. The formula for resentment is: resentment = expectation minus reality. If expectation is zero, there is no resentment. Responsibility begins with the honest admission: "I chose this path myself, I am responsible for the outcome."

92. Internal sabotage
This is when you're holding yourself back. You seem to want to get something important done, but you start getting lazy, getting distracted by trivial things, making excuses, and endlessly watching YouTube instead of working. This is your inner enemy. You can and should fight it by recognizing its tricks and using simple techniques like the "2-minute method."

93. Burnout
It's a state of complete exhaustion—physical and emotional. When you no longer have the strength or desire to do anything. It occurs when you work for a long time without proper rest, without joy, and without visible results. It's as if your body is saying, "That's it, stop, I can't do this anymore." Burnout can only be cured by real rest, a change of activity, and a reassessment of your goals.

94. Competencies
These are your knowledge, skills, and abilities. What you can do well. A competency is being able to drive a car. A competency is speaking English. A competency is sales or programming. The more valuable competencies you have, the more valuable you are to the market. Competencies can and should be constantly developed.

95. Resources
This is all you have to achieve your goal: money, time, connections, your health, your knowledge, your assistants, your technology. Resources are always limited. The ability to wisely allocate your limited resources is one of the most important skills in life.

96. Double-sided platforms
It's a business that brings together buyers and sellers without producing anything itself. Like Avito: sellers list their products, buyers search for them, and the platform simply helps them meet and takes a small commission. The more sellers and buyers there are, the more valuable the platform becomes.

97. Tool bases
This is a business that doesn't search for gold, but sells shovels to prospectors. It doesn't build houses, but sells hammers and nails. During the gold rush, the biggest earners weren't those who prospected for gold (they took risks and often ended up with nothing), but those who sold them shovels, tents, and jeans. Create tools that everyone needs.

98. One-sided businesses
These are typical businesses that sell a single, specific product or service. For example, a shoe store. If shoe sales stop (due to a recession or a fashion fad), the business will close. Such businesses are vulnerable because they rely on only one product, one niche, one market. But they're easy to start.

99. Community funding
This is when people pool money to buy something expensive for everyone, like a paid course or a computer game. Everyone pays a small amount, gains access, and the seller loses profit because they only make one sale instead of 100. From the author's perspective, this is piracy.

100. Cryptomixers
These are special services that "mix" cryptocurrency from different people to obscure the trail and make it impossible to determine who transferred what to whom. They are used for anonymity. They are often associated with illegal activities, because honest people usually don't hide their financial traces.

101. Concept
It's simply a way of thinking, your personal perspective on a particular issue. "My concept of raising children" means this is how I see it and these are the rules I follow. "The concept of a free person" is a set of your ideas about what it means to be free. This concept can be your own or borrowed from others.

102. Archetypes (Emperor, Warrior, Guardian, Magician, Jester)

These are deep, innate behavior patterns that determine your role in a team, what kind of business is right for you, how you make decisions, and how you respond to crises. These aren't "fairytale characters," but rather stable patterns in your psyche.

Five basic archetypes:

  1. Emperor— a manager. Takes responsibility, assigns roles, and establishes order. Strength lies in structure and discipline. Shadow—authoritarianism and hypercontrol. Management, strategy, and system creation are suited to this type.
  2. Warrior— provider and protector. Breaks down walls, takes risks, achieves goals. Strength lies in determination and courage. Shadow — pride and revanchism. Negotiations, new launches, and active sales are suitable for this person.
  3. Guardian— support and rear. Creates reserves, ensures stability, and cares for resources. Strength lies in reliability and frugality. Shadow—greed and the deadening of resources. Finance, logistics, and asset management are suited to this.
  4. Magician— a strategist and analyst. Sees patterns, calculates risks, and finds innovative solutions. Strength lies in wisdom and foresight. Shadow: analysis paralysis and complication. Analytics, strategy, and complex systems development are suited to him.
  5. Shot— a gambler and trickster. Sees opportunities, takes risks easily, and finds a way out where there isn't one. Strength lies in flexibility and creativity. Shadow—infantilism and a loss of reality. Creative projects, startups, and communications are suited to this person.

Why this is important:If you choose a business model that doesn't align with your dominant archetype, you'll burn out even if it makes money. If it does, your work becomes an extension of you, not a struggle.

How to identify your archetype:See Chapter 1.12 and Chapter 2.4 in Book 1.

103. Psychotypes
These are innate characteristics of a person's psyche, their temperament. One person is loud and active (choleric), another is calm and slow (phlegmatic), a third quickly changes mood (sanguine), and a fourth experiences everything deeply within themselves (melancholic). Understanding personality types helps us better understand people and avoid expecting things from them that are not typical.

104. Patterns (behavior)
These are habitual, repetitive ways of reacting and acting. If you start yelling during every argument, that's your behavioral pattern. If, when stressed, you run to the fridge to eat sweets, that's a pattern. Patterns can be noticed and changed if desired, but this requires awareness and effort.

105. Paradox
This is a situation or statement that seems counterintuitive or impossible, yet is actually true. For example, the paradox of choice: the more options you have, the harder it is to choose, and the less satisfied you are with your choice. The paradox of freedom: absolute, unfettered freedom can lead to complete chaos.

106. 100+
A universal designation for "100 and more" (≥100). It's used where precise counting isn't important, but rather the principle of "sufficient number" or "indefinitely long." "100+ years" means "a very long time, an eternity within the bounds of a human lifetime." "100+ people" is any number greater than a hundred, which is already covered by the mathematics of large numbers.

107. NNNN - Nobody Owes Anything to Anyone
The most important rule in life. It's not about everyone being mean. It's about helping others being a good choice, not an obligation. And if someone doesn't help you, it's not because they're bad, but because they might have their own reasons. Apply this rule, and you'll stop expecting things from people that they didn't promise. Resentment will go away.

108. NNN - Nothing is Impossible
It's about believing in yourself. Of course, you can't jump over a skyscraper. But if you train, study, and try for a long time, you can achieve results that seem miraculous to others. NNN isn't about denying physics, but rather that most limitations are in your head. Impossible? Try it. Can't do it? Prove it.

109. The Principle of the Sun
This is a wise rule for treating people. The sun shines equally on everyone: good and bad, rich and poor, yours and strangers. Your attitude toward everyone should be even, kind, and calm. You simply give your light. And how people perceive it, whether they respond in kind or retreat into the shadows, is their own business.

110. Tit for tat strategy
A healthy rule for relationships: Always start a conversation with someone on a clean slate, with a smile and kindness, and don't dredge up past grudges ("an eye for an eye"). But if someone treated you badly last time, this time treat them the same way they treated you last time ("an eye for an eye"). This isn't revenge, but a way to protect yourself and give the other person honest feedback.

111. Community
A community is a group of people united by something: common interests, a common goal, a common faith, a common problem, a common place of residence. Fans of a musical band are a community. Residents of a single apartment building are a community. Loyal followers of a blogger who communicate with each other in chat rooms are a community. In modern business, community is the most important and valuable asset.

112. Online community
These are people who communicate and make friends online, on social media, on forums, and in themed chat rooms. They don't have a common physical meeting place, but they share common interests and online platforms. They may never meet in person, but they feel part of a close-knit group.

113. Audience
A group of people who consume your content (read, watch, listen), but aren't necessarily connected to each other. It's different from a community: in an audience, the connections are only with you (one-way), while in a community, people are also connected to each other. An audience may be loyal, but it doesn't "live" on its own. The entrepreneur's job is to transform an audience into a community.

114. Ambition
This is the true extent of your desires. Not superficial "wants," but a deep, inner aspiration. This is the single most important question on which everything else in your life depends. "What is the extent of your ambitions?" and immediately followed by "What are you willing to give and do to achieve them?"

115. Planning horizon
This is the timeframe in which you typically think and plan. A week, a month, a year, 10 years, 100 years. Your planning horizon determines your strategy. If you think a month ahead, you'll live in the present. If you think 100 years ahead, you'll be building systems for your descendants.

116. The price of the issue
