This is what you're willing to give up to realize your ambitions. It's the most important filter that separates empty dreams from real goals. A dream ("wish") is worth nothing. Ambition demands a specific price: time, effort, comfort, money, relationships. Ready to pay? Then act.

117. Freedom of Independence and Freedom of Opportunity
Two fundamentally different types of freedom.
Freedom of independenceYou don't report to anyone, you set your own schedule, and you're independent of the government and your employer. The price: low demands and high discipline.
Freedom of opportunityYou have capital, assets, and opportunities. You can build, create, and influence. The price: you're tied to your system, which brings in big money. You're the boss, but your empire requires your presence.

118. Ego-approach
The desire to do everything your own way, uniquely, anything but like everyone else. Often leads to reinventing the wheel, wasting time and energy on what's already worked for others. An ego-driven approach hinders growth. "I won't be like everyone else" is a path to ruin.

119. Intelligence approach
The ability to copy working models and successful strategies of others. Understanding that there's no need to reinvent the wheel; simply take the best practices, adapt them, and make them even better. An intelligent approach is the path to quick and unique results.

120. Teacher
This is a 10x acceleration tool. It's the person you learn from, whose experience and, most importantly, mindset (the state of "BEING") you adopt. A mentor doesn't do things for you; they show you the map, where the holes are and where the shortcuts are, shortening your path to your goal exponentially.

121. Self-reproduction
The only mechanism that allows you to build an asset that works without your constant participation and gives you true freedom.
There are three types of self-replication: people (affiliates/MLM), the system (network effect, platforms), and capital (compound interest). If your model lacks any of these, you've created a job, not an asset.

122. 100+ years
A horizon of thought for building a true, lasting asset. "Background" mode, when the system operates without your participation and will continue to operate after you leave. The idea that you're building not for yourself, but for your children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren.

123. Portfolio (hypotheses, projects)
It's a collection of different projects or ideas that you manage simultaneously. Like an investor with a stock portfolio to mitigate risk, if one project fails, the others will pull you through. You don't invest everything in one idea, but diversify.

124. Pool (of mentors, partners)
It's a group of people gathered for a common goal. For example, a pool of 3-5 mentors is your personal "board of directors," each of whom teaches you something different. Or a pool of partners is your team, working together toward a common goal.

125. Case
This is a real-life example, a concrete story that illustrates a law or principle. The book is full of case studies—stories about Marina, Vovochka, and Aunt Lyuba—that help us understand the dry theory through real-life examples.

126. The Path of the Warrior
A life and business strategy based on discipline. You choose a ready-made system (affiliate program, MLM), set a daily quota of actions, and fulfill them without fail. You don't build an empire, you don't manage others—only yourself. Result = Contacts × Time × Discipline.

127. The Path of the Architect
A life and business strategy based on creation. You build your own system, business, platform. You hire people and are responsible for everything. The reward is an asset that operates without you. The price is high risk and constant presence.

128. CON—TON—BACKGROUND
This is the author’s formula for a stable and “eternal” business:
• HORSE— assemble a working "constructor" (business system, product, team). Investment period.
• TONE— set up this builder, add advertising and promotion to get it working at full capacity. Growth period.
• BACKGROUND— keep it running so that money keeps flowing, even when you're not actively working. A period of stability and freedom.

129. The Triangle of Successful Business
Simple and powerful formula:Big team→Lots of money + Lots of free timeWant both money and time to live? Build and develop your team; don't try to do everything alone. A lone wolf always runs up against his own time constraints. A team provides leverage and scale.

130. Five scenarios for a minimalist life
These are 5 specific ways to live freely and with a minimum of things, described in the book:
• Scenario A (Estate):Own house in the village, autonomy, subsistence farming.
• Scenario B (City):Living in a city apartment, but according to the principles of minimalism, without unnecessary things.
• Scenario B (Nomadism):Living in a camper van, constantly traveling.
• Scenario G (Hybrid):A plot of land + a motorhome on it.
• Scenario D (Six Flags):Several bases (houses) in different countries of the world.

131. The "3-message" method
A sustainable and effective way to meet and communicate online (on social media and messengers). You send the person three short messages, spaced 2-3 days apart:

  1. A compliment, gratitude for his content (without selling!).
  2. General topic, useful proposition or interesting information.
  3. If the person responded, it’s a business proposal, a sale.
    This works much better than a long "Hi, buy from me..."

132. Five Faiths
Five fundamental pillars on which any great undertaking rests:

  1. Belief in yourself.
  2. Faith in the Company (the organization you work with).
  3. Faith in the System (in the rules and methods of work that you use).
  4. Faith in the Team (in the people who are next to you).
  5. Faith in the Product (what you make or sell).
    If even one of these supports is loose or missing, the entire structure could collapse.

133. Five prohibitions
Five topics you should never discuss with anyone to avoid ruining your relationships and reputation.

  1. Someone else's personal life— Gossip kills trust.
  2. Other people's finances— how much each person earns and spends.
  3. Religion— Disputes about faith lead to nothing but quarrels.
  4. Politics— divides people into “us” and “them”.
  5. Negativity about your group, company, mentor— If you don’t like it, leave, but don’t destroy it from the inside.

134. Network effect
This is a characteristic of two-sided business models, where a product or service becomes more valuable to each user as the total number of users grows. The more people use the platform, the more useful it is. The more sellers there are on Avito, the more buyers there are, and vice versa. This is the self-replication of the system, not the people.

135. Slave of his Lamp
A term coined by the author to describe the flip side of the role of Architect (the creator of your own business/platform). You are the King, you are the Master, you set the rules. But that's precisely why all responsibility rests with you. You can't relax, you can't let go of control, because this is your brainchild. You are rich and powerful, but you are eternally chained to your "lamp"—your creation, which requires your constant presence and attention.

136. Operating room (operating activities)
The day-to-day operations of a business: manufacturing goods, providing services, communicating with clients, shipping, and invoicing. This is what needs to be automated. The entrepreneur's goal is to eventually remove themselves from the day-to-day operations, delegating them to employees or automating them. While you're in charge of the day-to-day operations, you're not an entrepreneur, but a self-employed professional.

137. Income
Money or material assets that a person or company receives as a result of their activities. There are different types: active (you work and you earn), passive (money comes without your daily effort), residual (a one-time effort generates multiple returns), and portfolio (investment income). A smart person strives to have multiple sources of income so that the failure of one doesn't destroy everything.

138. Residual income
A type of income in which a single effort generates money repeatedly over time. Examples: royalties from a book or song, a percentage of affiliate sales (in MLM), commissions on repeat customer purchases, or income from a one-time course. The difference from passive income is that passive income requires no immediate input from you, while residual income is the result of your past efforts, which continues to generate income.

139. Financial freedom
A state in which your passive or residual income exceeds your basic expenses (lifestyle costs). This doesn't mean billions. It means you don't have to think about money every day. You can choose work you enjoy, not out of necessity. You can say "no" to an unfavorable offer. Financial freedom isn't about wealth, it's about not having to worry about tomorrow.

140. Self-realization
Unleashing your potential, realizing your abilities and talents. One of the six main human priorities (along with financial freedom, time freedom, helping others, owning a business, and providing for your children's future). In business, self-realization is when your work aligns with your personality, archetype, and inner values. When work is not a burden, but a joy.

141. Horizontal and vertical expansion of competencies
Two ways to develop your skills and knowledge.

  • Horizontal expansion— increasing breadth: exploring related fields, new tools, and mastering new skills. Example: a copywriter learning to work with neural networks and Photoshop.
  • Vertical expansion— Increasing depth: you delve deeper into your core area and become a top-level expert. Example: a copywriter learns to write copy that sells 5 times better.
    Both are important for freedom and sustainability.

142. The concept of "10,000" (steps, hours, people, mistakes)
A general concept for several regularities:

  • 10,000 hours(Gladwell's rule) - this is approximately how much practice is needed to become a world-class expert.
  • 10,000 steps— the recommended daily intake of physical activity.
  • 10,000 subscribers— a critical mass at which your community begins to grow on its own (in the book, the “golden rule”).
  • 10,000 joints— in some cultures it is believed that this is the number of mistakes one must make to master any craft.
    The key idea: significant results require a lot of attempts, time, and people. Don't expect a miracle after ten tries.

143. Timing is the distance in time
Understanding that any significant undertaking takes time. A tree doesn't grow in a month. A business isn't built in a year. A system doesn't automatically start working in a week. Accepting this fact relieves stress and panic. You stop fidgeting and start calmly taking steps, knowing that results will come in 2-3 years, not tomorrow.

PART 2. ENVIRONMENT AND LIFESTYLE

144. Jurisdiction
This is the country or territory under whose laws you reside or where your business is registered. "Changing jurisdiction" means moving to another country or re-registering a company where taxes are lower and the laws are more clear. Different countries have different rules, and smart people take advantage of this.

145. Tax
It's a government agency that ensures that all citizens and companies pay their taxes on time and in full. It collects money from the population for the country's needs: the army, roads, schools, hospitals. It's best not to quarrel with the tax office. You can legally optimize taxes, but go completely overboard.
"shadow" is dangerous.

146. Shadow sector / Shadow economy
This is a business and money that people hide from the state to avoid paying taxes. It's always risky and illegal. If you're in the shadows, you can't advertise openly, you can't complain if you've been defrauded, and you could be charged at any moment. It's not worth the effort.

147. Digital traces
This is all the information about you that remains online: your likes, comments, search queries, purchases, geolocation, and conversations. These traces make it easy to find and identify you. Your digital footprint remains forever—even if you deleted a post, it could still be in someone else's screenshots. Be careful what you post online.

148. Digital (electronic) wallets
Services and apps that allow you to store, send, and receive money online without visiting a bank. Examples: PayPal, WebMoney, Qiwi, YuMoney, and cryptocurrency wallets. They are convenient for online payments, transfers, and international settlements. Key risks: hacking, account blocking, and fraud. Security rule: don't keep all your money in one e-wallet.

149. Digital signatures
An electronic equivalent of a handwritten signature, confirming the authenticity of a document or transaction. It uses cryptography to protect against forgery. It is used in banking transactions, legally binding documents, and government services. It enables remote contracting without physical presence. It is a key tool in the digital economy.

150. Social networks
Online platforms for communication, content sharing, and building social connections. Examples: VKontakte, Telegram (channels and chats), YouTube (as a social network), Odnoklassniki. For entrepreneurs, social media is a tool for building a community, personal brand, and free traffic. The main rule: don't spread yourself too thin across all platforms; choose one or two and use them regularly.

151. Messengers
Instant messaging, calling, and file sharing apps. Examples: Telegram, WhatsApp, Viber, Signal. They offer greater privacy and speed than social media. For businesses, messaging apps are a channel for communicating with clients, providing support, and sending newsletters. Telegram is also a platform for creating communities (channels, chats, bots). Safety rule: don't say anything in messaging apps that you wouldn't say out loud in public.

152. Crypto wallet
A software or hardware device for storing cryptocurrency and access keys. They can be hot (connected to the internet, convenient for transactions but vulnerable to hacking) or cold (offline, extremely secure but inconvenient for frequent transactions). Security rule: For long-term storage, use a cold wallet. For daily transactions, use a hot wallet with a small amount.

153. Macroworld
This is the entire vast world around us. Galaxies, planets, countries, cities, nature, other people—everything that exists outside, outside our bodies. The macrocosm exists independently of us. We can influence it, but very weakly. But it influences us constantly.

154. Microworld
It's a small world within us. Our thoughts, feelings, memories, fears, dreams, beliefs, values. It can't be touched, but it's absolutely real to us. It's the microworld that determines how we perceive events in the macroworld. Two people in the same situation can feel completely different—it's all about their microworld.

155. Virtual levels
These are structures that exist not as physical buildings, but as shared agreements between people. For example, the state, the army, schools, religion, large corporations, money. They cannot be touched, but they greatly influence our lives. They exist as long as we all agree that they exist.

156. The Matryoshka Principle
This is when one system exists within another, which exists within a third, and so on. You exist within your family, your family exists within your city, your city exists within your country, your country exists within your planet, and your planet exists within your galaxy. Understanding this principle helps you avoid overestimating your influence and underestimating the influence of external forces.

157. Globalizers / Globalists
This is the book's description of the world's richest and most influential people and organizations, who have long-term plans for all of humanity. They are believed to control most of the world's resources and have influence over the governments of various countries. This isn't necessarily a conspiracy theory, but rather an observation that there are structures operating on a planet-wide scale.

158. World order
This is how relations between countries and major players across the planet are structured: who is in charge, what rules everyone lives by, what values ​​are considered important. The world order is not eternal; it changes. There was a period when Britain was dominant, then the United States, and now we are witnessing an era of change and a redistribution of influence.

159. Free Man / Freedom of Choice
This is a person who understands that they were born on planet Earth and consciously decides how to live their life. They choose who to be friends with, what to believe in, how to spend their time and money. They don't allow the government, advertising, bosses, or relatives to control them without their consent. They choose their own life, not just drifting along.

160. Inhabitant of planet Earth
This is the most important and true title of a person. You are not simply a "citizen of Russia" or an "American." You are, first and foremost, a human being, living on the same planet with all other people. This understanding removes many artificial boundaries and gives you the feeling that the whole world is your common home.

161. Self-proclaimed (states)
We've been taught since childhood that states have always existed. The book's author reminds us of a simple truth: borders and governments were invented by people themselves. They haven't existed forever, so they are "self-proclaimed." This doesn't mean they can be ignored, but it does mean they are not divinely given, but rather a human construct that can change.

162. Special services
These are government organizations that conduct intelligence, espionage, counterintelligence, and defend the country from external and internal enemies (FSB, CIA, MI6, etc.). They operate covertly, collect classified information, and conduct covert operations. The average person typically doesn't encounter them, but they exist and greatly influence global events.

163. Total control
This is a situation where someone (the government or a powerful corporation) is trying to monitor your every move, know everything about you, and control your life. Cameras on every corner, phone tapping, internet data collection, bans and restrictions—all these are elements of total control. This manifests itself differently in different countries.

164. Criminal structures
These are organized criminal groups, gangs, and mafias. They live by their own criminal laws, often feuding with the state and with each other over spheres of influence. The book mentions that the state and criminals sometimes act as "communicating vessels": the harsher and more unjust the state, the more the shadow economy and criminal activity flourish.

165. Global players
The same as globalizers: the largest and most influential forces on the planet. These could be transnational corporations (Google, Apple, Microsoft), powerful financial groups (the Rothschilds, the Rockefellers), or international organizations (the UN, WHO, WTO). They influence the lives of everyone, even if we don't notice.

166. Resources of the planet
This is everything the Earth provides us with for life and production: water, air, forests, oil, gas, gold, coal, rare metals, fertile soil. All these resources are limited, and countries and corporations constantly compete for them. Whoever controls the key resources, in many ways, controls the world.

167. Minimalism / Minimalist Life
It's a lifestyle in which you consciously surround yourself with only what's most necessary and loved. Fewer things, less clutter, less fuss, less debt. But plenty of free space in the house, time, and money for what's truly important: travel, spending time with loved ones, pursuing your favorite activities, relaxing, and self-improvement.

168. The concept of minimalist living
This is a collection of ideas and rules on how to live with a minimum of possessions and a maximum of freedom. The book offers five ready-made scenarios for such a life: Estate (your own house), City (apartment), Nomadism (motorhome), Hybrid (land + motorhome), and Six Flags (bases in different countries). Everyone can choose their own.

169. Autonomy
This is independence and the ability to provide for everything one needs. An autonomous home provides its own light (solar panels, generator), heat (stove, firewood), water (well, borehole), and food (garden, animals). An autonomous person is less dependent on the government, stores, and utilities.

170. Autonomous Fortress / Estate
This is a house in a village or rural area that is as self-sufficient as possible: its own garden, its own livestock, its own well water, its own winter supplies, its own electricity. Like a small fortress, where you are your own master and king. It requires land, labor, and time, but it offers real, tangible freedom and independence.

171. Food safety
It's your confidence that you and your family will always have food, even if all the stores suddenly close, a crisis, or a natural disaster occurs. Having your own vegetable and berry garden, a stockpile of grains, canned goods, and frozen foods gives you a deep sense of security and peace.

172. Straw
This is an ancient and very inexpensive building material, literally made from the earth—a mixture of clay, straw, and water. It can be used to build a warm and environmentally friendly home practically for free, provided you have access to clay and straw. Such houses last for decades. The downside is that building with adobe is time-consuming and physically demanding.

173. Motorhome / Car trailer
It's a home on wheels. A car or trailer with everything you need for life: a bed, a kitchen, a shower, a toilet, a table. With a motorhome, you can travel and live wherever you want. One day you wake up by the sea, the next in a pine forest, the day after at the foot of the mountains. Complete freedom of movement.

174. Digital Nomadism / Digital Nomad
This is a person who works remotely via the internet and is therefore not tied to one place of residence. They could be living and working in Thailand today, in Turkey next month, and traveling around Russia in a campervan next year. All they need to work is a laptop and internet access. The rest is freedom.

175. Hybrid scenario
It's a clever combination of two lifestyles. For example, you buy a plot of land (as in the "Estate" scenario), but instead of a permanent house, you build a mobile home on it (as in the "Nomadism" scenario). You have both your own land (garden, vegetable garden, fire pit) and a mobile home (you can travel at any time). The best of both worlds.

PART 3. MAN, PEOPLE AND HEALTH

176. Instructions for Life on Earth
This is the title of the book you're holding in your hands. The author believes that every person should receive such clear instructions at birth, but they don't. So he decided to write it himself and leave it as a legacy for his descendants and anyone who finds it.

177. Mental levels
These are different "levels" in our consciousness or in society. There is the level of simple, everyday thoughts ("I'm hungry"), and there is the level of lofty philosophical ideas ("What is the meaning of life?"). There is the level of the individual, and then there is the level of the family, the nation, the entire human race. The ability to switch between levels helps us see the bigger picture.

178. Superman
This isn't about a flying Superman from a comic book. This is about an ordinary person who was able to awaken hidden powers within themselves. Someone who, in a critical situation, is able to lift a car to save a child. Someone who persistently pursues a goal when everyone around them gives up. Someone who believes in the impossible and makes it possible through their actions.

179. Institute (of marriage, power)
In this context, it's not a school or a university. "Institution" is an established, historically established system of rules, norms, and traditions. "Institution of marriage" refers to the rules and traditions by which people create families. "Institution of power" refers to how society is governed. Institutions can change slowly over time.

180. Mortgage
This is a very large loan that people take out from a bank to buy an apartment or house. They then repay the bank in equal installments, plus interest, over many years (sometimes 20-30 years). A mortgage is a way to become a homeowner without having to pay the entire amount upfront. But the overpayment over the years can be enormous, sometimes double the value of the apartment.

181. Conscious / Awareness
This is a state where you understand what you're doing at the moment and why. You don't just mechanically scroll through your phone's feed; you tell yourself, "I'm scrolling to take a 15-minute break." You don't just eat automatically; you actually taste the food. Mindfulness is turning on an internal observer that sees your thoughts and actions from the outside.

182. Union of two free people
This is the ideal relationship, according to the author. Not "family" as an obligation or a boring institution, but a voluntary union where two people are better off together, more fulfilled, and more at ease than apart. They stay together only out of love and sincere desire, not because they "should" or "it's customary." It's about equality, respect, and the personal freedom of each person.

183. Boundaries (in relationships)
It's an invisible line that separates you from others and protects your identity. You don't allow others to insult you, boss you around, interfere with your plans without asking, or take advantage of you. The ability to establish and protect your personal boundaries is a sign of healthy self-esteem and self-respect.

184. Psychics, shamans, witches (people with developed abilities)
People who are believed to possess abilities beyond the ordinary: they can see the future, read minds, communicate with spirits, and heal from a distance. Different cultures call them by different names, but the essence is the same: they have developed dormant abilities that everyone possesses to some degree. Science is skeptical, but stories of such people abound.

185. Material and tangible (energy)
It's the process by which something invisible and abstract transforms into something tangible. Your idea becomes a drawing on paper. The drawing becomes a real house. Your thought turns into action, and action into a concrete result. In quantum physics, there's a theory that observation itself transforms invisible energy into materiality.

186. Bio-suit
This is how the author, both jokingly and seriously, refers to our physical body. It's a kind of biological spacesuit in which our soul or consciousness lives on planet Earth. The body can be trained, treated, nourished, and developed, but it doesn't last forever. And who exactly resides inside this suit is the main mystery.

187. Soul
This is what makes a person alive, sentient, and unique. The immaterial part of a person, their inner world. Scientists cannot find the soul, measure it, or dissect it, but almost everyone intuitively senses its existence. Religions say the soul is immortal. Science so far only shrugs its shoulders. But without a soul, the body is simply a complex biological mechanism.

188. Quantum Theory
This is a very complex branch of physics that examines how the world works at the level of the smallest particles—atoms and electrons. Some truly strange and astonishing things happen there. For example, particles can behave differently simply because a human observer is looking at them. This leads to profound thoughts about how our consciousness and the reality around us are connected much more strongly than we usually think.

189. The Power of Thought
This is the idea that our thoughts have energy and can influence our lives and even the world around us. What we think about deeply and passionately tends to come true. What we focus our attention on begins to grow in our lives. If you constantly think about the bad, you will attract the bad. If you think about the good and the desired, you will increase the chances of its manifestation.

190. Archimedes
An ancient Greek scientist (287–212 BC), physicist, mathematician, and engineer. He is famous for the phrase, "Give me a fulcrum, and I will move the Earth." In the book, he symbolizes the principle of leverage—the ability to achieve great results with little effort. In business, the "Archimedes lever" is any system, tool, or method that increases your effectiveness.

191. Gauss (Carl Friedrich Gauss)
The great German mathematician (1777–1855). He is mentioned in the book in connection with the "normal distribution" (Gauss's bell curve)—the law that states that in any large group of people, the distribution will be roughly equal: 5–10% stars, 5–10% laggards, and 80% average. Understanding this law helps avoid wasting energy trying to convince everyone, and instead focus on working with the "stars."

192. Nassim Taleb
A contemporary thinker, essayist, and former trader. He is the author of the concepts of the "Black Swan" (unpredictable events with enormous consequences) and "Antifragility" (the ability of systems to grow stronger after shocks, crises, and mistakes). This book uses his ideas to understand why even strong systems collapse and how to build systems that benefit from, rather than fear, chaos.

193. William Atkinson
American writer (1862–1932), one of the first systematizers of the New Thought movement. Author of Thought Vibration, a basic guide to mind control, thought power, and self-hypnosis. The book is recommended as a practical guide to developing a "superhuman" and working with one's inner state.

194. Tabata
This is a very fast and effective interval training method. You do any exercise at your maximum effort for 20 seconds, then rest for 10 seconds. Repeat this eight times in a row. The entire circuit takes just 4 minutes, but you'll feel as tired as after an hour at the gym. You can do several of these circuits. Developed by Japanese scientist Izumi Tabata.

195. Yoga (headstands/handstands)
This is an ancient system of physical and spiritual practices. The book specifically mentions inverted poses ("stands"), where a person stands on their head or on their hands. This is necessary to improve circulation: blood flows to the brain, which receives better oxygen supply and begins to function more actively. Even simply lying down with your legs up is beneficial.

196. Lunchtime nap
This is a short nap during the day, right after lunch. Just 20-30 minutes of sleep magically restores energy, "reboots" the brain, and allows you to work with a fresh mind. This practice is very popular in hot countries (called a siesta). Productivity soars after such a nap.

197. Discipline (as cutting off the unnecessary)
A famous Bruce Lee quote: Discipline isn't about forcing yourself to do something you hate. It's about consciously removing everything unnecessary from your life that hinders you from moving toward your goals and drains your energy. Don't want to get up at 5 a.m.? And don't if you don't have to. But if you do, remove whatever's keeping you from getting enough sleep.

198. Retraining (habits)
To break an old, bad habit or develop a new, healthy one, you need to regularly repeat the desired action for about 21 days. The brain needs time to create new neural pathways. The first few days will be difficult, then it gets easier, and after three weeks, the new action will become a habit that requires no willpower.

199. Evening planning
A very useful and simple habit for productivity. Every evening, sit down and write out a to-do list for tomorrow, prioritizing your tasks. In the morning, you no longer waste precious time wondering "where to start," but simply get down to the first thing you do. Your brain will subconsciously prepare for the tasks overnight.

200. The 2-Minute Method
A simple way to overcome procrastination and laziness. If you really don't want to start some important but unpleasant task, tell yourself: "I'll do this for just 2 minutes, and then I'll quit." More often than not, after 2 minutes, you're already hooked and don't want to stop. The main psychological barrier is getting started. The 2-Minute Method breaks that barrier.

GUIDE TO THE BOOK

Introduction

Before you is a complete map of the entire double book. All the volumes, parts, sections, chapters, and subchapters of the two books are collected here in a single structure.

In Book 1, these are the chapter titles (by concept).
In Book 2, these are unique titles (stories), but with links to concepts in parentheses.

Use this guide to:

  • Quickly find the chapter you need
  • Understand how the entire book is structured
  • Check when working with text

 

INSTRUCTIONS FOR LIFE ON EARTH

(The book we didn't receive at birth)
(Two books under one cover)

Author: FreemanAV, a resident of planet Earth

  • REVIEW
  • INTRODUCTION        
  • BOOK ONE (FOR ALL)

  • GUIDE TO THE BOOK        
  • CONCLUSION        
  • AFTERWORD FROM THE AUTHOR
  • ADDRESS TO THE READER

SECTION 1. THINKING
(Setting up the main tool)

  • Chapter 1.2Projects = Hypotheses
  • Chapter 1.3Business is a game with rules

CONCLUSION
The Way of the Free Man

AFTERWORD FROM THE AUTHOR
What I realized in the process

---

ADDRESS TO THE READER

CONCLUSION
The Way of the Free Man

We have come a long way together.

Book One (for everyone)— we've laid the foundation. You've learned that entrepreneurship, and life in general, isn't magic, but mathematics. We've overcome the barrier of complexity and realized that big money is found where most people are too lazy to even consider. We've assembled the construction kit, set up promotion, and learned how to maintain the system so it lasts for decades. 63 laws, the hierarchy of business models from kiosk to ecosystems, the public billionaire trap, Black Swans, and Antifragility—all these are now not just words, but working tools. We've covered everything: from assets and liabilities to self-replication, from first contact to a 100+ year strategy.

Book Two (for children and the soul)— We looked into the heart. In parables and fairy tales, you found the same truths, but packaged in images understandable to both a seven-year-old child and a seventy-year-old sage. The story of two brothers and a magic mill, two gardeners and their gardens, two kings and their treasures—these are not just fairy tales and parables. They are the codes by which life is structured.

We've covered business—your personal earning system.
We understood how the world around us works—these aquariums, nesting dolls, and global players.
We remembered who you really are - not just a “citizen”, butinhabitant of planet Earth, a free man by birthright.
We've found FIVE LIFE SCENARIOS—from the Manor to Six Flags—that make you invulnerable.
We've dealt with people: NNNN, the Sun principle, the "Eye for an Eye" strategy, the union of two free people.
We have risen to the level of superman: we have realized that the body is a bio-suit, and inside lies dormant the power capable of lifting machines.
And finally, we took care of the most important thing – health.

What you hold in your hands is not just a collection of chapters. It isoperating systemFor your life. It can be installed on any "computer"—on a young person starting out, on an adult in crisis, on a child who needs to explain complex things in simple terms.

What's next?

And now comes the most interesting part. Now this knowledge needs to be turned into action. Because knowing and not doing is the same as not knowing.

In Section 6 of Book 1there is a clear step-by-step algorithm16 steps from point "zero" to point "freedom." Take it, print it out, hang it on your wall. And start taking it step by step.

In 2-3 years you will look back and not recognize yourself.

And remember the main point we started with: business is only a part of life. Not the most important one. The wheel must be balanced. Family, health, friends, spirituality—they are just as important spokes as your income.

You build a business to live well. You don't live to build a business.

See you at the top.

Don't look at the scale of others' results. Build your own vector of movement. Look and move forward.

AFTERWORD FROM THE AUTHOR
What I realized in the process


You know, when I started writing this book, I was thinking only about adults. About those who are building businesses, searching for themselves, trying to become free. I wanted to give them a map, tools, an understanding of the laws.

I wrote my first book. It was tightly structured, well-organized, and well-organized. 63 laws of business mathematics. Tables, calculations, algorithms. Everything is honest, everything works.

But something interesting happened while I was working on it. I caught myself thinking: how do I explain this to a child? How do I tell my seven-year-old grandson about critical mass, self-replication, the Black Swan? You can't explain it with your fingers; algorithms won't help.

This is how the second book was born.

A collection of short stories, parables, and fairy tales. About a snowball that rolls itself. About a drop that wears away a stone. About a garden that grew on its own, and a fair that drew everyone.

And only when the two books stood next to each other did I understand one simple thing.

The truth is the same for everyone. It's just packaged differently.

The Basic Book (for everyone) contains algorithms and tables.
In the Basic Book for Children - in images and stories.
Both here and there is the same mathematics of life, the same philosophy of freedom, the same laws.

So there is no need to choose.

If you want depth, structure, clear instructions, read on.Book One (for everyone)Return to the spreadsheets, consult the checklists, and build your own systems.

If you have children nearby - or if you yourself are tired and want warmth - openBook Two (for children and the soul)Read stories out loud, explain, smile.

Or better yet, read both. First one, then the other. Or vice versa. Whatever works best for you and your loved ones.

And the last thing.

If you read this book with your parents or grandparents, remember these stories. They will be useful in life.

If you're an adult and read this book for yourself, don't hesitate to reread the children's chapters. Sometimes they contain more wisdom than thick textbooks.

And if you've read this book to your children, you've done a great job. You've planted seeds in them that will grow for many years to come. This is the best investment in the future, in the "right thinking" of our future descendants.

Thank you for walking this path with me.


Sincerely yours, FreemanAV
An author who simply followed his path and decided to leave a map.


“You can’t change your life overnight.
But you can change your thoughts overnight,
that will change your life forever."

"Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic."

"The size of your money and your lifestyle depends on
your sizeSelf-esteem, yoursAmbition And
What are you willing to do for them?».

"Man can be destroyed, but he cannot be defeated."

An appeal to the reader

You're holding in your hands a text that wasn't written for sale or fame. It's simply knowledge, collected bit by bit and packaged into a manual. My name (or pseudonym) doesn't matter. Only the information matters.

Before you close the last page, I want us to agree. This conversation is our last. From now on, the book will live its own life, and I will go on with mine.


For all readers

I am posting this text in the public domain and have no involvement in its further development.

This means that:

  • I do not provide consultations on this book.
  • I do not respond to letters or questions about its content.
  • I don't explain what I "really meant."

If you don't understand something, reread it. Or don't reread it. It's your choice.

I am the "Sun" - I shine a light on everyone!!! And whoever accepts the information - will accept it!!!


For those who know me personally

If you are my relative, friend or acquaintance, this point is for you.

Imagine,I spent my entire life collecting a library. I searched for rare books, translated ancient manuscripts, and rebound crumbling pages. And now I put this bookcase in the common room and said, "This is everything I have—take whatever you want."

I won't read these books aloud to you. I won't write essays for you based on what you've read. Everyone opens the book they need for themselves. Some will find the answer to their question here, some will simply leaf through, and some will close the bookcase and leave—meaning the time hasn't come yet.

Please don't pass my contact information on to anyone who "wants to ask." I'm not a consultant or a guru. I'm just a relative and friend who once sat down and wrote a book.


For everyone without exception

Once you read this text, it becomes yours.

You can do with it whatever you see fit:

  • Store and reread.
  • Give to friends.
  • Rewrite, supplement, criticize.
  • Post anywhere.

My name doesn't have to be included. The information is more important than the name.


And I went...to the next point on my Path.


Sincerely,
FreemanAV (Earth Resident)
